    《因话录》　　[唐] 赵璘

    　　卷一 宫部　　
    　　玄宗柳婕妤，生延王玢。肃宗每见王，则语左右曰：“我与王，兄弟中更相亲，外家皆关中贵族。”柳氏乃尚书右丞范之女，睦州刺史齐物之妹也。 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 兄弟中更相亲 - Indicates a closer relationship than usual among brothers.
    • 外家皆关中贵族 - All the maternal families are nobles of Guanzhong.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玄宗 (Xuanzong) - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (r. 712–756), known for his cultural achievements and later for the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 肃宗 (Suzong) - Emperor Suzong of Tang (r. 756–762), the son of Xuanzong.
    • 婕妤 (Jiayu) - A rank in the imperial harem, below concubine but above ordinary concubines.
    • 延王 (Yan Prince) - A title given to a prince, in this case, Yan Prince Bīn.
    • 尚书右丞 (Right Chancellor of the Board of Works) - A high-ranking official in the Tang government, responsible for overseeing the Board of Works.
    • 睦州 (Muzhou) - A prefecture in Zhejiang province, modern-day Huzhou.
    • 刺史 (Prefect) - A local administrative official responsible for governing a prefecture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玄宗 (Xuanzong) - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 肃宗 (Suzong) - Emperor Suzong of Tang.
    • 柳婕妤 (Liu Jiayu) - The concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 延王玢 (Yan Prince Bīn) - The prince born to Liu Jiayu.
    • 尚书右丞范 (Fan, Right Chancellor of the Board of Works) - Liu Jiayu's father.
    • 睦州刺史齐物 (Qi Wu, Prefect of Muzhou) - Liu Jiayu's brother.

    《Record of Conversations》　　[唐] Zhao Lin

    　　Volume One, Palace Department　　
    　　Liu Jiayu, the concubine of Emperor Xuanzong, gave birth to Yan Prince Bīn. Every time Emperor Suzong saw the prince, he would tell his attendants, "I and the prince are even more intimate among brothers, as all the maternal families are nobles of Guanzhong." Liu Jiayu was the daughter of Fan, the Right Chancellor of the Board of Works, and the sister of Qi Wu, the Prefect of Muzhou. [1]

    肃宗在春宫，尝与诸王従玄宗诣太清宫。有龙见于殿之东梁，玄宗目之，顾问诸王：“有所见乎？”皆曰：“无之。”问太子，太子俯而未对。上问：“头在何处？”曰：“在东上。”抚之曰：“真我儿也。”
    　　安禄山入觐，萧宗屡言其不臣之状，玄宗无言。一日，召太子诸王击球，太子潜欲以鞍马伤之。密谓太子曰：“吾非不疑，但此胡无尾，汝姑置之。”
    　　政和公主，肃宗第三女也，降柳潭。肃宗宴于宫中，女优有弄假官戏，其绿衣秉简者，谓之参军妆。天宝末，蕃将阿布思伏法，其妻配掖庭，善为优，因使隶乐工。是日遂为假官之长。所为妆者，上及侍宴者笑乐。公主独俯首颦眉不视，上问其故，公主遂谏曰：“禁中侍女不少，何必须得此人使阿布思真逆人也，其妻亦同刑人，不合近至尊之座。若果冤横，又岂忍使其妻与群优杂处为笑谑之具哉妾虽至愚，深以为不可。”上亦悯恻，遂罢戏，而免阿布思之妻。由是贤重公主。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 肅宗在春宮 - Indicates that Emperor Suzong was in the position of Crown Prince (春宮), which is a title given to the heir apparent.
    • 従玄宗诣太清宫 - Means 'followed Emperor Xuanzong to the Taqing Palace,' where '诣' means to go or visit.
    • 有龙见于殿之东梁 - Describes a dragon appearing on the eastern beam of the palace hall, which is an unusual and symbolic event.
    • 真我儿也 - Translates to 'truly my son,' indicating approval or recognition.
    • 安禄山入觐 - Means 'An Lushan came to court,' where '觐' refers to the act of a vassal or subject paying homage to the emperor.
    • 吾非不疑，但此胡无尾，汝姑置之 - A statement by Emperor Xuanzong, meaning 'I am not without suspicion, but this barbarian has no tail, so you might as well leave him alone,' which is a metaphorical way of saying he is not a threat.
    • 政和公主，肃宗第三女也 - Introduces Princess Zhenghe as the third daughter of Emperor Suzong.
    • 降柳潭 - Means 'married into the Liu family,' where '降' is used here to mean marriage.
    • 女优有弄假官戏 - Refers to a performance involving actors playing the roles of officials, which was a form of entertainment.
    • 参军妆 - Describes a specific makeup style for the character known as 'Shangjun,' often a comedic role.
    • 阿布思伏法 - Indicates that Abosi was executed according to the law.
    • 配掖庭 - Means 'assigned to the imperial harem,' where '掖庭' is the area within the palace where concubines and servants lived.
    • 善为优 - Means 'skilled in performing,' where '优' refers to performers or entertainers.
    • 隶乐工 - Means 'assigned to the musicians,' where '乐工' refers to musicians or performers.
    • 遂为假官之长 - Indicates that she became the leader of the actors playing officials.
    • 上及侍宴者笑乐 - Means 'the emperor and those attending the banquet laughed and enjoyed themselves.'
    • 俯首颦眉不视 - Describes the princess lowering her head and frowning, not looking up, which shows her disapproval.
    • 合不合近至尊之座 - Questions whether it is appropriate for someone who has committed a crime to be close to the emperor.
    • 若果冤横 - Means 'if indeed wronged,' referring to the possibility that Abosi was unjustly executed.
    • 岂忍使其妻与群优杂处为笑谑之具哉 - A rhetorical question asking if it is right to make the wife of a wronged person a subject of ridicule.
    • 深以为不可 - Means 'deeply feels it is not acceptable,' expressing the princess's strong disapproval.
    • 上亦悯恻 - Means 'the emperor also felt pity and sorrow.'
    • 遂罢戏，而免阿布思之妻 - Indicates that the performance was stopped, and Abosi's wife was released from her assignment.
    • 由是贤重公主 - Means 'from this, the princess was highly regarded,' indicating that her actions earned her respect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 肃宗 - Emperor Suzong of Tang, the son of Emperor Xuanzong and the fourth emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, the father of Emperor Suzong and one of the most famous emperors of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太清宫 - A temple dedicated to the worship of the Taoist deity Laozi, located in Luoyang.
    • 安禄山 - A powerful military leader who later rebelled against the Tang Dynasty, leading to the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 阿布思 - A Turkic general who was executed during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 政和公主 - A princess of the Tang Dynasty, the third daughter of Emperor Suzong.
    • 柳潭 - A noble family to which Princess Zhenghe was married.
    • 女优 - Female performers, often dancers or actresses in traditional Chinese theater.
    • 弄假官戏 - A type of theatrical performance where actors played the roles of officials, often satirical or comedic.
    • 参军妆 - A specific makeup style for the 'Shangjun' role, a comedic character in traditional Chinese theater.
    • 掖庭 - The area within the palace where concubines and servants lived.
    • 乐工 - Musicians or performers in the imperial court.

    Proper Nouns

    • 肃宗 - Emperor Suzong of Tang.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 太清宫 - Taqing Palace.
    • 安禄山 - An Lushan.
    • 萧宗 - Crown Prince Li Heng (later Emperor Suzong).
    • 政和公主 - Princess Zhenghe.
    • 柳潭 - Liu family.
    • 阿布思 - Abosi.
    • 掖庭 - Imperial harem.
    • 乐工 - Musicians or performers in the imperial court.

    Emperor Suzong, while in the position of Crown Prince, once accompanied the various princes to visit the Taqing Palace with Emperor Xuanzong. A dragon was seen on the eastern beam of the palace hall. Emperor Xuanzong pointed at it and asked the princes, "Did you see anything?" They all replied, "No." He then asked the Crown Prince, who lowered his head without answering. The emperor asked, "Where is its head?" The Crown Prince replied, "In the east." The emperor patted him and said, "Truly my son."

    When An Lushan came to court, Crown Prince Li Heng repeatedly spoke of An Lushan's disloyal behavior, but Emperor Xuanzong remained silent. One day, the emperor summoned the Crown Prince and other princes to play ball. The Crown Prince secretly planned to injure An Lushan with saddles and horses. Emperor Xuanzong privately told the Crown Prince, "I am not without suspicion, but this barbarian has no tail, so you might as well leave him alone."

    Princess Zhenghe, the third daughter of Emperor Suzong, was married into the Liu family. During a banquet in the palace, there was a performance involving actors playing the roles of officials. The character dressed in green and holding a tablet was known as the "Shangjun" role. At the end of the Tianbao era, the Turkic general Abosi was executed according to the law, and his wife was assigned to the imperial harem. She was skilled in performing and was thus assigned to the musicians. On that day, she became the leader of the actors playing officials. The performance amused the emperor and those attending the banquet, but Princess Zhenghe alone lowered her head, frowned, and did not look up. The emperor asked her why, and she advised, "There are many attendants in the palace. Why must we have this person? If Abosi truly was a traitor, his wife is also a criminal and should not be close to the emperor's seat. If he was indeed wronged, how could we bear to make his wife a subject of ridicule among the performers? I may be foolish, but I deeply feel this is not acceptable." The emperor also felt pity and sorrow, so he stopped the performance and released Abosi's wife. From this, the princess was highly regarded. [2]

    代宗独孤妃薨，赠贞懿皇后，将葬。尚父汾阳王在邠州，以其子尚主之故，欲致祭。遍问诸従事，皆云；“自古无人臣祭皇后之仪。”汾阳曰：“此事须得柳侍御裁之。”时予外伯祖殿中侍御史，掌汾阳书记，奉使在京，即以书急召之。既至，汾阳迎笑曰：“有切事，须藉侍御为之。”遂说祭事。殿中君初亦对如诸人，既而曰：“礼缘人情，令公勋德不同常人，且又为国姻戚，自令公始，亦谓得宜。”汾阳曰：“正合子仪本意。”殿中君草祭文，其官衔之首称：“驸马都尉郭暧父。”其中叙特恩许致祭之意，辞简礼备，汾阳览之大喜。其文列于左： [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 代宗獨孤妃薨 - Formal way to report the death of an imperial consort.
    • 贈貞懿皇后 - Posthumous title given to the deceased empress.
    • 尚父汾陽王 - Title combining a noble rank and a familial honorific, indicating a high-ranking official and a respected elder.
    • 以其子尚主之故 - Explanation of the reason for the Duke's involvement.
    • 將葬 - Indicates the upcoming funeral.
    • 欲致祭 - Expresses the Duke's intention to perform a ritual.
    • 自古無人臣祭皇后之儀 - Highlights the novelty and potential controversy of the proposed action.
    • 此事須得柳侍御裁之 - Indicates the need for expert advice.
    • 時予外伯祖殿中侍御史 - Introduces the key figure who provides the solution.
    • 掌汾陽書記 - Describes his role.
    • 奉使在京 - Explains his presence in the capital.
    • 即以書急召之 - Describes the urgency of the situation.
    • 既至 - Marks the transition to the next part of the story.
    • 汾陽迎笑曰 - Describes the Duke's reaction.
    • 有切事 - Emphasizes the importance of the issue.
    • 須藉侍御为之 - Reiterates the need for his expertise.
    • 遂说祭事 - Describes the discussion.
    • 殿中君初亦对如诸人 - Shows his initial hesitation.
    • 既而曰 - Marks a change in his opinion.
    • 礼缘人情 - Explains his reasoning.
    • 令公勋德不同常人 - Highlights the Duke's unique status.
    • 且又为国姻戚 - Adds another layer of significance.
    • 自令公始 - Indicates the beginning of a new practice.
    • 亦谓得宜 - Concludes his argument.
    • 汾阳曰 - Marks the Duke's agreement.
    • 正合子仪本意 - Confirms the Duke's approval.
    • 殿中君草祭文 - Describes his task.
    • 其官衔之首称 - Introduces the specific wording.
    • 驸马都尉郭暧父 - Specific title used.
    • 其中叙特恩许致祭之意 - Describes the content of the text.
    • 辞简礼备 - Praises the quality of the text.
    • 汾阳览之大喜 - Describes his reaction.
    • 其文列于左 - Indicates that the text will follow.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 代宗 - Emperor Daizong of Tang, who reigned from 762 to 779.
    • 独孤妃 - Empress Du, a consort of Emperor Daizong.
    • 汾阳王 - The Duke of Fenyang, a high-ranking official and military leader, likely referring to Guo Ziyi.
    • 郭暧 - Prince Consort De, the son of Guo Ziyi, who married a princess.
    • 柳侍御 - Counselor Liu, likely referring to Liu Yan, a prominent official and historian.
    • 殿中侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the Tang court, responsible for various administrative duties.
    • 驸马都尉 - A title given to the husbands of imperial princesses.
    • 特恩 - Special imperial favor or decree.
    • 致祭 - A ritual of offering sacrifices or tributes, often performed to honor the deceased.
    • 官衔 - Official titles or ranks.
    • 祭文 - A text used in rituals to honor the deceased.

    Proper Nouns

    • 代宗 - Emperor Daizong of Tang.
    • 独孤妃 - Empress Du.
    • 贞懿皇后 - Posthumous title of Empress Du.
    • 汾阳王 - The Duke of Fenyang (Guo Ziyi).
    • 郭暧 - Prince Consort De.
    • 柳侍御 - Counselor Liu (likely Liu Yan).
    • 殿中侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the Tang court.
    • 驸马都尉 - A title given to the husbands of imperial princesses.
    • 特恩 - Special imperial favor or decree.
    • 致祭 - A ritual of offering sacrifices or tributes.
    • 官衔 - Official titles or ranks.
    • 祭文 - A text used in rituals to honor the deceased.

    The Empress Du of Emperor Daizong passed away. She was posthumously honored as Empress Zhenyi and is about to be buried. The Duke of Fenyang, the Honorable Father, was in Bi Prefecture, and because his son, Prince Consort De, is a consort of the imperial family, he wished to pay tribute. However, there is no precedent for a subject paying tribute to a queen. The Duke asked his attendants, and they all said, "There is no precedent for a subject paying tribute to a queen." The Duke said, "This matter needs to be decided by Counselor Liu." At that time, my maternal great-grandfather, Counselor of the Palace, was serving and was in charge of the Duke's correspondence. He was on a mission in the capital and immediately wrote to summon him. After he arrived, the Duke greeted him with a smile and said, "There is an urgent matter that requires your expertise." He then explained the matter of the tribute. Counselor Liu initially agreed with the others, but then he said, "Rituals are based on human feelings. Your Excellency's merit and virtue are different from those of ordinary people, and moreover, you are related to the imperial family. Starting from Your Excellency, it is also appropriate." The Duke said, "It perfectly aligns with your original intention." Counselor Liu wrote the tribute text, and the first part of his official title reads, "Prince Consort De's father." The middle part explains the special favor allowing the tribute. The words are concise and the rituals are complete. The Duke was very pleased after reading it. The text is listed below: [3]

    维某年月日，驸马都尉郭暧父，关内河东副元帅、司徒、兼中书令、汾阳郡王臣子仪，谨遣上都进奏院官傅涛，敢昭告于贞懿皇后行宫：伏惟德曜坤灵，明齐月魄，母仪万国，化洽六宫，光辅圣人，赞成阴教，载荣史策，式播讴谣。奄违圣日，上仙灵界，遐迩痛愤，宫闱哀慕。臣幸忝诸亲，男尚贵主，天人之美，鞠育所钟，姻戚光荣，宗族咸戴。今园陵礼备，祖载及期，臣限守方镇，不获陪侍行宫，瞻望灵驾，不胜摧慕。伏荷皇恩，眷以国戚。许申祭礼，超越等夷，古今所绝，独开圣造，无任惶恐铭戴之至。谨献牲牢庶羞之奠。尚飨！ [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 維某年月日 - Formal opening formula meaning 'On a certain day in a certain month of a certain year,' used to indicate the date of the document.
    • 驸马都尉 - Title given to the husband of an imperial princess, indicating his high status.
    • 关内河东副元帅 - Military title indicating the highest rank in the region of Guanxi and Hedong.
    • 司徒、兼中书令 - High-ranking official titles, indicating the holder's dual roles in both civil and military administration.
    • 汾阳郡王 - Noble title indicating the holder's status as a prince of Fenyang.
    • 敢昭告于 - Respectful formula meaning 'dare to inform respectfully to,' used to address a deity or a deceased person.
    • 德曜坤灵 - Phrase praising the virtue and beauty of the deceased, meaning 'virtue shines like the sky, beauty like the earth.'
    • 明齐月魄 - Phrase praising the clarity and brightness of the deceased, meaning 'brightness equals the moon.'
    • 母仪万国 - Phrase praising the deceased's role as a model mother, meaning 'model mother for all nations.'
    • 化洽六宫 - Phrase praising the deceased's influence, meaning 'influence reaches all palaces.'
    • 光辅圣人 - Phrase praising the deceased's support for the emperor, meaning 'brightly supporting the sage.'
    • 赞成阴教 - Phrase praising the deceased's role in women's education, meaning 'assisting in the education of women.'
    • 载荣史策 - Phrase meaning 'recorded in history,' indicating the deceased's importance.
    • 式播讴谣 - Phrase meaning 'spread in songs and poems,' indicating the widespread praise of the deceased.
    • 奄违圣日 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly departed from the world,' indicating the sudden death of the deceased.
    • 上仙灵界 - Phrase meaning 'ascended to the spiritual realm,' indicating the deceased's elevation to a divine status.
    • 遐迩痛愤 - Phrase meaning 'far and near grieve and anger,' indicating the widespread sorrow and anger.
    • 宫闱哀慕 - Phrase meaning 'the palace mourns and misses,' indicating the palace's deep sorrow.
    • 幸忝诸亲 - Phrase meaning 'lucky to be among the relatives,' indicating the writer's close relationship with the deceased.
    • 男尚贵主 - Phrase meaning 'my son is married to a princess,' indicating the writer's high social status.
    • 天人之美 - Phrase meaning 'beauty of heaven and earth,' indicating the deceased's exceptional qualities.
    • 鞠育所钟 - Phrase meaning 'nurtured and cherished,' indicating the deceased's care and upbringing.
    • 姻戚光荣 - Phrase meaning 'family and relatives are honored,' indicating the writer's family's prestige.
    • 宗族咸戴 - Phrase meaning 'all the clan members respect,' indicating the writer's clan's admiration.
    • 园陵礼备 - Phrase meaning 'the mausoleum ceremony is complete,' indicating the completion of the burial rites.
    • 祖载及期 - Phrase meaning 'the ancestral carriage arrives on time,' indicating the timely arrival of the funeral procession.
    • 限守方镇 - Phrase meaning 'limited by the duties of a regional governor,' indicating the writer's inability to attend the ceremony.
    • 不获陪侍行宫 - Phrase meaning 'unable to accompany and serve at the imperial palace,' indicating the writer's absence from the ceremony.
    • 瞻望灵驾 - Phrase meaning 'look forward to the spirit carriage,' indicating the writer's anticipation of the funeral procession.
    • 不胜摧慕 - Phrase meaning 'cannot bear the grief and longing,' indicating the writer's deep sorrow.
    • 伏荷皇恩 - Phrase meaning 'deeply grateful for the imperial favor,' indicating the writer's gratitude to the emperor.
    • 眷以国戚 - Phrase meaning 'graciously treated as a royal relative,' indicating the writer's high status.
    • 许申祭礼 - Phrase meaning 'allowed to perform the sacrificial rites,' indicating the writer's permission to conduct the funeral.
    • 超越等夷 - Phrase meaning 'exceeding the common people,' indicating the writer's special status.
    • 古今所绝 - Phrase meaning 'unprecedented in ancient and modern times,' indicating the uniqueness of the situation.
    • 独开圣造 - Phrase meaning 'only created by the sage,' indicating the writer's belief in the emperor's wisdom.
    • 无任惶恐铭戴之至 - Phrase meaning 'extremely fearful and grateful,' indicating the writer's deep respect and gratitude.
    • 谨献牲牢庶羞之奠 - Phrase meaning 'respectfully offer the sacrificial food and wine,' indicating the writer's offering to the deceased.
    • 尚飨 - Formal closing formula meaning 'may the spirit enjoy the offering,' used to conclude the document.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 驸马都尉 - Title given to the husband of an imperial princess, indicating his high status.
    • 关内河东副元帅 - Military title indicating the highest rank in the region of Guanxi and Hedong.
    • 司徒、兼中书令 - High-ranking official titles, indicating the holder's dual roles in both civil and military administration.
    • 汾阳郡王 - Noble title indicating the holder's status as a prince of Fenyang.
    • 贞懿皇后 - Title of the deceased, indicating her status as a queen with virtues of loyalty and integrity.
    • 园陵 - The mausoleum where the deceased is buried, indicating the grandeur of the burial.
    • 行宫 - The imperial palace, indicating the place where the funeral ceremony is held.
    • 牲牢庶羞之奠 - The sacrificial food and wine offered to the deceased, indicating the traditional Chinese funeral customs.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郭暧 - The name of the writer's son, who is married to a princess.
    • 子仪 - The name of the writer, indicating his high status as a noble and official.
    • 傅涛 - The name of the official sent to inform the deceased.
    • 贞懿皇后 - The title of the deceased, indicating her status as a queen with virtues of loyalty and integrity.

    On a certain day in a certain month of a certain year, the father of Princess Guo Ai, who holds the titles of Vice Marshal of Guanxi and Hedong, Grand Tutor, and Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, and Prince of Fenyang, respectfully sends the official Fu Tao from the Imperial Court to inform the Hall of the Deceased Queen of Loyalty and Integrity: May her virtue shine like the sky, her brightness equal the moon, her model motherhood for all nations, her influence reaching all palaces, her bright support for the sage, her assistance in the education of women, her name recorded in history, her praise spread in songs and poems. Suddenly, she departed from the world and ascended to the spiritual realm, causing grief and anger far and near, and the palace mourns and misses her deeply. I am fortunate to be among the relatives, my son is married to a princess, her beauty of heaven and earth, nurtured and cherished, our family and relatives are honored, all the clan members respect her. Now the mausoleum ceremony is complete, the ancestral carriage arrives on time, but I am limited by the duties of a regional governor and cannot accompany and serve at the imperial palace, look forward to the spirit carriage, and cannot bear the grief and longing. I am deeply grateful for the imperial favor, graciously treated as a royal relative, allowed to perform the sacrificial rites, exceeding the common people, unprecedented in ancient and modern times, only created by the sage, extremely fearful and grateful. Respectfully offer the sacrificial food and wine. May the spirit enjoy the offering. [4]

    代宗以郭尚父勋高，兼连姻帝室，常呼为大臣而不名。每中使内人往来，必询其门内休戚。尚父二爱姬，尝竞宠争长，互论其公私佐助之功，忿媢不相面，尚父不能禁。上知之，赐金帛及簪镮，命宫人载酒以和之。方饮，令选人歌以送酒。一姬怒未解，歌未发，遽引蒲。置觞于席前曰：“酒尽不须歌。”
    　　郭暧尝与升平公主琴瑟不调，暧骂公主：“倚乃父为天子耶我父嫌天子不作。”公主恚啼，奔车奏之。上曰：“汝不知，他父实嫌天子不作。使不嫌，社稷岂汝家有也。”因泣下，但命公主还。尚父拘暧，自诣朝堂待罪。上召而慰之曰：“谚云：‘不痴不聋，不作阿家阿翁。’小儿女子闺帏之言，大臣安用听？”锡赉以遣之。尚父杖暧数十而已。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 兼连姻帝室 - Means 'also connected by marriage to the imperial family,' indicating a familial relationship between De Guo and the imperial family.
    • 门内休戚 - Means 'the joys and sorrows within the household.'
    • 爱姬 - Refers to beloved concubines or mistresses.
    • 竞宠争长 - Means 'competing for favor and status.'
    • 忿媢不相面 - Means 'resentful and not facing each other,' indicating a state of deep resentment and unwillingness to reconcile.
    • 尚父不能禁 - Means 'De Guo could not prevent it,' indicating his inability to control the situation.
    • 赐金帛及簪镮 - Means 'bestowed gold, silk, and jewelry,' referring to gifts given to resolve the conflict.
    • 命宫人载酒以和之 - Means 'ordered palace attendants to bring wine to reconcile them.'
    • 选人歌以送酒 - Means 'selected singers to perform songs to accompany the wine.'
    • 遽引蒲 - Means 'quickly pulled out a reed,' likely referring to a musical instrument.
    • 置觞于席前 - Means 'placed the cup before the seat,' indicating the act of pouring wine.
    • 酒尽不须歌 - Means 'no need for singing when the wine is finished,' indicating a refusal to engage in the reconciliation effort.
    • 郭暧尝与升平公主琴瑟不调 - Means 'De Guo Ai and Princess Shengping had a strained relationship,' referring to their marital discord.
    • 骂公主 - Means 'scolded the princess.'
    • 倚乃父为天子耶 - Means 'relying on your father being the emperor, is that it?'
    • 我父嫌天子不作 - Means 'my father regrets not being the emperor.'
    • 公主恚啼 - Means 'the princess was angry and wept.'
    • 奔车奏之 - Means 'rushed to the palace to report it.'
    • 谚云 - Means 'a saying goes.'
    • 不痴不聋，不作阿家阿翁 - A proverb meaning 'if one is not deaf or dumb, one cannot be a good father-in-law or mother-in-law,' implying that a father-in-law or mother-in-law should sometimes ignore trivial matters.
    • 小儿女子闺帏之言 - Means 'the words of children and women in the inner chambers.'
    • 大臣安用听 - Means 'why should ministers listen to such things?'
    • 锡赉以遣之 - Means 'bestowed gifts and sent him away.'
    • 尚父杖暧数十而已 - Means 'De Guo struck De Guo Ai dozens of times and then let him go.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 代宗 - Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty, known for his reign during a period of significant political and military challenges.
    • 郭尚父 - De Guo (郭子仪), a renowned general and statesman who played a crucial role in the Tang Dynasty's military and political affairs.
    • 升平公主 - Princess Shengping, a member of the Tang imperial family, known for her marriage to De Guo Ai.
    • 郭暧 - De Guo Ai, a son of De Guo, who had a strained relationship with his wife, Princess Shengping.
    • 中使内人 - Imperial messengers and palace attendants, who often carried messages and performed tasks on behalf of the emperor.
    • 爱姬 - Beloved concubines or mistresses, often influential figures in the lives of powerful men.
    • 忿媢不相面 - Indicates a state of deep resentment and unwillingness to reconcile, common in historical accounts of personal conflicts.
    • 赐金帛及簪镮 - Gifts of gold, silk, and jewelry, often used to resolve conflicts or show favor.
    • 命宫人载酒以和之 - A traditional method of reconciliation involving the sharing of wine and entertainment.
    • 选人歌以送酒 - Singers were often employed to entertain and soothe tensions.
    • 遽引蒲 - Refers to a musical instrument, likely a reed instrument, used in traditional Chinese music.
    • 置觞于席前 - Placing the wine cup before the seat, a gesture of respect and hospitality.
    • 酒尽不须歌 - A refusal to engage in the reconciliation effort, indicating a lack of willingness to participate.
    • 郭暧尝与升平公主琴瑟不调 - A common phrase used to describe a strained marital relationship.
    • 骂公主 - Scolding the princess, indicating a public display of anger.
    • 倚乃父为天子耶 - A rhetorical question implying that the princess's father is the emperor, suggesting a sense of privilege.
    • 我父嫌天子不作 - A statement expressing regret or dissatisfaction with not being the emperor.
    • 公主恚啼 - The princess's anger and weeping, indicating a strong emotional reaction.
    • 奔车奏之 - Rushing to the palace to report the incident, indicating a sense of urgency.
    • 谚云 - A proverb or saying, often used to convey wisdom or moral lessons.
    • 不痴不聋，不作阿家阿翁 - A proverb suggesting that a father-in-law or mother-in-law should sometimes ignore trivial matters to maintain harmony.
    • 小儿女子闺帏之言 - The words of children and women in the inner chambers, often considered trivial or insignificant.
    • 大臣安用听 - Why should ministers listen to such trivial matters, indicating a dismissive attitude.
    • 锡赉以遣之 - Bestowing gifts and sending someone away, often used to resolve conflicts or show leniency.
    • 尚父杖暧数十而已 - De Guo striking De Guo Ai dozens of times and then letting him go, indicating a form of punishment and reconciliation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 代宗 - Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 郭尚父 - De Guo (郭子仪), a prominent general and statesman during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 升平公主 - Princess Shengping, a member of the Tang imperial family.
    • 郭暧 - De Guo Ai, a son of De Guo.

    Emperor Dezong, recognizing the high rank of De Guo (De Guo) and his connections through marriage to the imperial family, often referred to him as a minister without using his name. Whenever imperial messengers and palace attendants visited, they would inquire about the joys and sorrows within his household. Two of De Guo's beloved concubines, who competed for his favor and status, would argue over their public and private contributions to his success, becoming resentful and refusing to face each other, which De Guo could not prevent. The emperor learned of this and bestowed gifts of gold, silk, and jewelry, ordering palace attendants to bring wine to reconcile them. As they drank, he commanded singers to perform to accompany the wine. However, one concubine, still angry, pulled out a reed instrument and placed the wine cup before the seat, saying, "No need for singing when the wine is finished."

    De Guo Ai, a son of De Guo, had a strained relationship with Princess Shengping. One day, he scolded her, saying, "Relying on your father being the emperor, is that it? My father regrets not being the emperor." The princess, angered and weeping, rushed to the palace to report the incident. The emperor responded, "You do not understand; his father indeed regrets not being the emperor. If he did not regret it, would the empire belong to your family?" He then wept and simply ordered the princess to return. De Guo Ai was detained by De Guo, who went to the palace to await punishment. The emperor summoned him and comforted him, saying, "A saying goes: 'If one is not deaf or dumb, one cannot be a good father-in-law or mother-in-law.' The words of children and women in the inner chambers, why should ministers listen to such things?" He bestowed gifts and sent him away. De Guo struck De Guo Ai dozens of times and then let him go. [5]

    德宗初嗣位，深尚礼法。谅暗中，召韩王食马齿羹，不设盐酪。皇姨有寡居者，时节入宫。妆饰稍过，上见之极不悦。异日如礼，乃加敬焉。
    　　德宗初登勤政楼，外无知者。望见一人衣绿乘驴戴帽至楼下，仰视久之，俯而东去。上立遣宣示京尹，令以物色求之。尹召万年捕贼官李镕，使促求访。李尉伫立思之曰：“必得。”及出，召干事、所由于春明门外数里内，应有诸司旧职事使艺人，悉搜罗之。而绿衣者果在其中。诘之，对曰：“某天宝教坊乐工也。上皇时，数登此。每来，鸱必集楼上，号随驾老鸱。某自罢居城外，更不复见。今群鸱盛集，又觉景象宛如昔时。心知圣人在上，悲喜且欲泣下。”以此奏闻。敕尽收此辈，却系教坊。李尉亦为京尹所擢用，后至郡守。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 深尚礼法 - Means 'highly valued ritual and law,' indicating a strong emphasis on proper conduct and legal adherence.
    • 召韩王食马齿羹，不设盐酪 - A specific act of humility and respect, where the new emperor invites the Prince of Han to eat a simple dish without seasoning or dairy products.
    • 皇姨有寡居者，时节入宫 - Describes the visit of the emperor's aunt to the palace during festivals.
    • 妆饰稍过，上见之极不悦 - Highlights the emperor's disapproval of excessive adornment.
    • 异日如礼，乃加敬焉 - Shows the emperor's respect for appropriate behavior.
    • 德宗初登勤政楼，外无知者 - Indicates that the people were unaware of the emperor's ascension to the throne.
    • 望见一人衣绿乘驴戴帽至楼下，仰视久之，俯而东去 - Describes a person dressed in green, riding a donkey and wearing a hat, who stops and looks up at the emperor's new residence before continuing eastward.
    • 上立遣宣示京尹，令以物色求之 - The emperor immediately ordered the capital's governor to search for the person based on his description.
    • 尹召万年捕贼官李镕，使促求访 - The governor called upon the official responsible for capturing criminals, Li Rong, to expedite the search.
    • 李尉伫立思之曰：“必得” - Li Rong stood thinking and confidently stated that he would find the person.
    • 及出，召干事、所由于春明门外数里内，应有诸司旧职事使艺人，悉搜罗之 - Upon leaving, he gathered all the officials and artists from various departments within several miles of Chunming Gate and searched them thoroughly.
    • 而绿衣者果在其中 - The person dressed in green was indeed among them.
    • 诘之，对曰：“某天宝教坊乐工也。上皇时，数登此。每来，鸱必集楼上，号随驾老鸱。某自罢居城外，更不复见。今群鸱盛集，又觉景象宛如昔时。心知圣人在上，悲喜且欲泣下。” - When questioned, the person replied that he was a musician from the Imperial Music Bureau during the Tianbao era, who often visited this place during the reign of the previous emperor. He explained that when he came, crows always gathered on the building, calling it the 'old crow following the carriage.' Since he moved to live outside the city, he no longer saw this. Now, seeing many crows gather again, he felt as if the past had returned, and he was moved to tears.
    • 以此奏闻 - This information was reported to the emperor.
    • 敕尽收此辈，却系教坊 - The emperor ordered that all such musicians be collected and returned to the Imperial Music Bureau.
    • 李尉亦为京尹所擢用，后至郡守 - Li Rong was promoted by the capital's governor and later became a prefect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 德宗 - Refers to Emperor Dezong of Tang, who reigned from 779 to 805 AD.
    • 韩王 - The Prince of Han, a member of the imperial family.
    • 马齿羹 - A simple dish made from mallow leaves, symbolizing humility and simplicity.
    • 皇姨 - The emperor's aunt.
    • 勤政楼 - A building in the imperial palace where the emperor would conduct official business.
    • 京尹 - The governor of the capital city, responsible for maintaining order and security.
    • 万年捕贼官 - An official responsible for capturing criminals.
    • 春明门 - One of the gates of the capital city, Chang'an.
    • 教坊 - The Imperial Music Bureau, responsible for music and entertainment in the imperial court.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, during which the Tang Dynasty reached its peak.
    • 上皇 - The previous emperor, referring to Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 鸱 - Crows, often associated with omens and significant events in Chinese culture.
    • 随驾老鸱 - A term used to describe the crows that gathered on the building during the reign of the previous emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 韩王 - The Prince of Han.
    • 马齿羹 - A simple dish made from mallow leaves.
    • 皇姨 - The emperor's aunt.
    • 勤政楼 - A building in the imperial palace.
    • 京尹 - The governor of the capital city.
    • 万年捕贼官 - An official responsible for capturing criminals.
    • 春明门 - One of the gates of the capital city, Chang'an.
    • 教坊 - The Imperial Music Bureau.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 上皇 - The previous emperor, referring to Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 鸱 - Crows.
    • 随驾老鸱 - A term used to describe the crows that gathered on the building during the reign of the previous emperor.

    Emperor Dezong ascended the throne and highly valued ritual and law. During the mourning period, he invited the Prince of Han to eat a simple dish of mallow leaves without seasoning or dairy products. The emperor's aunt, who was widowed, would visit the palace during festivals. If her attire was slightly extravagant, the emperor became very displeased. On another occasion, when she dressed appropriately, the emperor showed greater respect.

    When Emperor Dezong first ascended the Hall of Diligent Governance, the people were unaware of it. They saw a man dressed in green, riding a donkey and wearing a hat, stop and look up at the hall for a long time before continuing eastward. The emperor immediately ordered the capital's governor to search for the person based on his description. The governor called upon the official responsible for capturing criminals, Li Rong, to expedite the search. Li Rong stood thinking and confidently stated that he would find the person. Upon leaving, he gathered all the officials and artists from various departments within several miles of Chunming Gate and searched them thoroughly. The person dressed in green was indeed among them. When questioned, the person replied, "I was a musician from the Imperial Music Bureau during the Tianbao era. During the reign of the previous emperor, I often visited this place. Each time I came, crows would gather on the building, calling it the 'old crow following the carriage.' Since I moved to live outside the city, I no longer saw this. Now, seeing many crows gather again, I felt as if the past had returned, and I was moved to tears." This information was reported to the emperor. The emperor ordered that all such musicians be collected and returned to the Imperial Music Bureau. Li Rong was promoted by the capital's governor and later became a prefect. [6]

    德宗尝暮秋猎于苑中。是日天色微寒，上谓近臣曰：“九月衣衫，二月衣袍，与时候不相称，欲递迁一月，何如？”左右皆拜谢。翌日，命翰林议之，而后下诏。李赵公吉甫，时为承旨，以圣人能上顺天时，下尽物理，表请宣示万方，编之于令。李相程初为学士，独不署名。具状奏曰：“臣谨按《月令》，十月始裘。《月令》是玄宗皇帝删定，不可改易。”上乃止。由是与吉甫不协。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 與時候不相稱 - Means 'does not match the season'; indicates that the clothing is inappropriate for the current weather.
    • 編之於令 - Means 'to include it in the regulations'; suggests that the change would become official policy.
    • 上順天時，下盡物理 - Means 'follow the heavenly timing and fulfill the principles of nature'; justifies the emperor's decision.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 德宗 - Refers to Emperor Dezong of Tang (742-805), the 10th emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 翰林 - Refers to the Hanlin Academy, a prestigious institution of scholars and advisors in the imperial court.
    • 李趙公吉甫 - Refers to Li Jifu (758-814), a prominent statesman and scholar during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李相程 - Refers to Premier Li Cheng, another high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 《月令》 - Refers to the *Lüli* (Rites of Zhou), a classical text that outlines the proper rituals and practices for each month of the year.
    • 玄宗皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (685-762), the 8th emperor of the Tang Dynasty, known for his cultural and administrative reforms.

    Proper Nouns

    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 李趙公吉甫 - Li Jifu.
    • 李相程 - Premier Li Cheng.
    • 《月令》 - *Lüli* (Rites of Zhou).
    • 玄宗皇帝 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.

    Emperor Dezong once hunted in the imperial garden during late autumn. On that day, the weather was slightly chilly, and the emperor said to his close attendants, "The clothes worn in September and the robes in February do not match the season; I wish to advance the timing by one month. What do you think?" All around him bowed and expressed their gratitude. The next day, he ordered the Hanlin Academy to discuss the matter, and then issued an edict. Li Jifu, who was then the chief of the Hanlin Academy, submitted a memorial stating that the emperor could follow the heavenly timing and fulfill the principles of nature, and requested that the change be announced to the world and included in the regulations. Premier Li Cheng, who was initially a scholar, refused to sign the memorial. He submitted a formal report saying, "I respectfully follow the Lüli (Rites of Zhou), which states that fur coats begin in October. The Lüli was edited by Emperor Xuanzong and should not be changed." The emperor then stopped the proposal. From this point on, he did not get along with Li Jifu. [7]

    德宗躬亲庶政，中外除授，无不留神。余伯父自监察里行浙东观察判官，特授高陵县令。裴尚书武，亦自鄜坊监察宰栎阳。二人同制。后数日，因游苑中，有执役者，上问：“何处人？”云：“是高陵百姓。”上曰：“汝是高陵人也，我近为汝拣得一好长官，知否？”
    　　宪宗初，征柳宗元、刘禹锡，至京。俄而以柳为柳州刺史，刘为播州刺史。柳以刘须侍亲，播州最为恶处，请以柳州换。上不许。宰相对曰：“禹锡有老亲。”上曰：“但要与恶郡，岂系母在？”裴晋公进曰：“陛下方侍太后，不合发此言。”上有愧色。既而语左右曰：“裴度终爱我切。”刘遂改授连州。
    　　宪宗知权文公甚真。后权长孺知盐铁福建院，赃污盈积，有司以具狱奏。上曰：“必致极法。”崔相群救之云：“是德舆族子。”上曰：“德舆必不合有子弟犯赃，若德舆在，自犯赃，朕且不赦，况其宗従也？”及知其母年高，乃免死，杖一百，长流康州。 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 躬亲庶政 - Personnel attendance to all state affairs; '躬亲' means 'personally attend to' and '庶政' refers to all state affairs.
    • 特授 - Specially appointed; '特' means 'specially' and '授' means 'appointed'.
    • 里行 - Junior official; a term for a junior official.
    • 亦 - Also; '亦' means 'also'.
    • 宰 - Became the magistrate; '宰' means 'became the magistrate'.
    • 同制 - Under the same decree; '同' means 'same' and '制' means 'decree'.
    • 征 - Summoned; '征' means 'summoned'.
    • 至京 - To the capital; '至' means 'to' and '京' means 'capital'.
    • 恶处 - Worst place; '恶' means 'worst' and '处' means 'place'.
    • 不许 - Did not agree; '不' means 'not' and '许' means 'agree'.
    • 老亲 - Elderly parents; '老' means 'elderly' and '亲' means 'parents'.
    • 方侍太后 - Serving the Empress Dowager; '方' means 'serving' and '太后' means 'Empress Dowager'.
    • 不合发此言 - Inappropriate to say such things; '不合' means 'inappropriate' and '发此言' means 'say such things'.
    • 不合有子弟犯赃 - Must not have had a son who committed embezzlement; '不合' means 'must not' and '犯赃' means 'committed embezzlement'.
    • 朕且不赦 - I would not pardon him; '朕' means 'I' and '赦' means 'pardon'.
    • 及知其母年高 - When it was known that his mother was old; '及知' means 'when it was known' and '年高' means 'old'.
    • 杖一百 - Flogged one hundred strokes; '杖' means 'flogged' and '一百' means 'one hundred'.
    • 长流 - Exiled for life; '长' means 'for life' and '流' means 'exiled'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang, the ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang, the ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 柳宗元 - Liu Zongyuan, a famous Tang Dynasty writer and philosopher.
    • 刘禹锡 - Liu Yuxi, a famous Tang Dynasty writer and poet.
    • 裴武 - Pei Wǔ, a Tang Dynasty official.
    • 裴晋公 - Counselor Pei Jin, a prominent figure in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 权文公 - Quan Wengong, a Tang Dynasty official.
    • 权长孺 - Quan Changru, a Tang Dynasty official.
    • 高陵县 - Gao陵 County, a county in Shaanxi Province.
    • 栎阳县 - Yòuyáng County, a county in Shaanxi Province.
    • 播州 - Baozhou, a prefecture in Guizhou Province.
    • 柳州 - Liuzhou, a prefecture in Guangxi Province.
    • 连州 - Liánzhou, a prefecture in Guangdong Province.
    • 康州 - Kangzhou, a prefecture in Guangdong Province.
    • 监察 - Inspector or censor, a type of official responsible for oversight.
    • 观察判官 - Official responsible for overseeing local administration.
    • 尚书 - Counselor or minister, a high-ranking official.
    • 太监 - Eunuch or宦官, a castrated male servant in the imperial court.
    • 宗従 - Relatives or clan members.
    • 赃污 - Embezzlement or corruption.
    • 恶郡 - Bad or undesirable prefecture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 柳宗元 - Liu Zongyuan.
    • 刘禹锡 - Liu Yuxi.
    • 裴武 - Pei Wǔ.
    • 裴晋公 - Pei Jin.
    • 权文公 - Quan Wengong.
    • 权长孺 - Quan Changru.
    • 高陵县 - Gao陵 County.
    • 栎阳县 - Yòuyáng County.
    • 播州 - Baozhou.
    • 柳州 - Liuzhou.
    • 连州 - Liánzhou.
    • 康州 - Kangzhou.
    • 监察 - Inspector.
    • 观察判官 - Official responsible for overseeing local administration.
    • 尚书 - Counselor or minister.
    • 太监 - Eunuch or宦官.
    • 宗従 - Relatives or clan members.
    • 赃污 - Embezzlement or corruption.
    • 恶郡 - Bad or undesirable prefecture.
    • 杖一百 - Flogged one hundred strokes.
    • 长流 - Exiled for life.

    Emperor Dezong personally attended to all state affairs, paying close attention to appointments both inside and outside the court. My uncle, who served as an inspector in the eastern part of Zhejiang, was specially appointed as the magistrate of Gao陵 County. Counselor Pei Wǔ, also served as an inspector in Fufang and became the magistrate of Yòuyáng. Both were appointed under the same decree. Several days later, while touring the imperial gardens, an official approached and the emperor asked, "Where are you from?" The official replied, "I am from Gao陵." The emperor said, "You are from Gao陵, and I have recently chosen a good magistrate for you. Do you know?"

    In the early years of Emperor Xianzong, Liu Zongyuan and Liu Yuxi were summoned to the capital. Soon after, Liu Zongyuan was appointed as the governor of Liuzhou, while Liu Yuxi was appointed as the governor of Baozhou. Liu Zongyuan requested to be transferred from Liuzhou because Liu Yuxi needed to serve his parents, and Baozhou was considered the worst place. The emperor did not agree. The chancellor said, "Liu Yuxi has elderly parents." The emperor responded, "I only want to give him a bad prefecture, why should it matter if his mother is still alive?" Counselor Pei Jin said, "Your Majesty is serving the Empress Dowager, it is inappropriate to say such things." The emperor looked embarrassed. Later, he told those around him, "Counselor Pei truly cares for me." Liu Yuxi was then reassigned to Liánzhou.

    Emperor Xianzong knew Quan Wengong very well. Later, Quan Changru, who was in charge of the salt and iron administration in Fujian, accumulated a large amount of corruption. The authorities reported this to the emperor, who said, "This must be punished to the fullest extent." Counselor Cui Xianguan interceded, saying, "He is a relative of Deyu." The emperor replied, "Deyu must not have had a son who committed embezzlement; if Deyu himself had committed embezzlement, I would not pardon him, let alone his relatives?" When it was known that his mother was old, he was spared execution, but flogged one hundred strokes and exiled to Kangzhou for life. [8]

    文宗将有事南郊，礼前，本司进相扑人。上曰：“我方清斋，岂合观此事！”左右曰：“旧例皆有，已在门外祗候。”上曰：“此应是要赏物，可向外扑了。”即与赏物令去。又赏观斗鸡，优人称叹“大好鸡”，上曰：“鸡既好，便赐汝。”
    　　文宗赐翰林学士章服，续有待诏欲先赐本司者，以名上。上曰：“赐君子小人不同日，且待别日。”
    　　文宗欲以韦宣州温为翰林学士。韦以先父遗命，恳辞。上后谓次对官曰：“韦温，朕每欲用之，皆辞诉，又安用韦温？”声色俱厉。户部崔侍郎蠡进曰：“韦温禀其父遗命耳。”上曰：“温父不令其子在翰林，是乱命也。岂谓之理乎？”崔曰：“凡人子能遵理命，已是至孝，况能禀乱命而不改者，此则尤可嘉之，陛下不可怪也。”上曰：“然。”乃止。 [9]

    Final Translation

    Emperor Wenzong was about to perform a ritual at the southern suburbs. Before the ritual, officials presented wrestlers. The emperor said, "I am in a state of ritual purity; how can I watch such a thing!" The attendants replied, "This is a traditional practice, and the wrestlers are already waiting outside." The emperor said, "They must be here for rewards; let them compete." He then awarded them prizes and dismissed them. Later, he watched a cockfight, and the entertainers praised the roosters, saying, "Very good chickens." The emperor said, "If the chickens are good, I will reward you."

    Emperor Wenzong granted official robes to the scholars of the imperial academy. Subsequently, a pending official wanted to be granted first and reported his name to the emperor. The emperor said, "Bestow favors on gentlemen and commoners on different days; wait for another day."

    Emperor Wenzong wanted to appoint Wei Wen from Xuanzhou as a scholar of the imperial academy. Wei Wen, following his father's last wishes, earnestly declined. Later, the emperor told the officials who were called to the palace, "Wei Wen, I have always wanted to use him, but he always refuses. What good is Wei Wen to me?" He spoke with a stern voice and angry face. Cui Li, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Revenue, said, "Wei Wen is following his father's last wishes." The emperor said, "Wei Wen's father did not want his son to be in the imperial academy; that is an unreasonable command. How can it be considered reasonable?" Cui Li replied, "A son who follows a reasonable command is already filial; especially if he follows an unreasonable command without changing, this is praiseworthy, and the emperor should not be surprised." The emperor said, "Indeed." And so, he stopped. [9]

    文宗时，有正塔僧履险若平，换塔杪一柱，倾都奔走，皆以为神。上闻之曰：“此塔固由人工所成，当时匠者，岂亦有神？”沙门后果以妖妄伏法。
    　　文宗对翰林诸学士，因论前代文章。裴舍人数道陈拾遗名，柳舍人璟目之，裴不觉。上顾柳曰：“他字伯玉，亦应呼陈伯玉。”
    　　武宗时，李崖州尝面奏处士王龟志业堪为谏官。上曰：“龟是谁子？”对曰：“王起之子。”曰：“凡言处士者，当是山野之人。王龟父大僚，安得居山野不自合有官？”李无以对。又将赐杜悰之子无逸衣，所司条列数目，其衫色未奉进旨，上久之言曰：“我不可赐其白衫，年小未有官，又难假其服色，但赐青衣无衫可也。” [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 履险若平 - Describes a monk who walks on dangerous parts of the tower as if they were flat.
    • 换塔杪一柱 - Means 'replaced a column at the top of the tower.'
    • 倾都奔走 - Means 'the whole city rushed to see it.'
    • 皆以为神 - Means 'all thought it was a miracle.'
    • 此塔固由人工所成 - Means 'this tower was certainly built by human effort.'
    • 当时匠者，岂亦有神 - Means 'the craftsmen of the time, could they also be gods?'
    • 沙门后果以妖妄伏法 - Means 'later, the monk was punished for practicing sorcery.'
    • 裴舍人数道陈拾遗名 - Means 'Pei the Secretary repeatedly mentioned the name of Chen Shiyi.'
    • 柳舍人璟目之 - Means 'Liu the Secretary looked at him.'
    • 他字伯玉，亦应呼陈伯玉 - Means 'His courtesy name is Boyu, so he should be called Chen Boyu.'
    • 李崖州尝面奏处士王龟志业堪为谏官 - Means 'Li Yanzhou once spoke in person to the emperor that the recluse Wang Gui was capable of serving as a censor.'
    • 龟是谁子 - Means 'Who is Wang Gui's father?'
    • 王起之子 - Means 'He is the son of Wang Qi.'
    • 凡言处士者，当是山野之人 - Means 'Generally speaking, a recluse should be a person from the countryside.'
    • 王龟父大僚 - Means 'Wang Gui's father is a high official.'
    • 安得居山野不自合有官 - Means 'How could he live in the countryside without holding an official position?'
    • 李无以对 - Means 'Li had no answer.'
    • 又将赐杜悰之子无逸衣 - Means 'He also intended to grant clothing to Du Cong's son Wu Yi.'
    • 所司条列数目，其衫色未奉进旨 - Means 'The officials listed the items, but the color of the robe had not been approved.'
    • 我不可赐其白衫，年小未有官，又难假其服色 - Means 'I cannot give him a white robe; he is young and has no official position, so it would be inappropriate to grant him this attire.'
    • 但赐青衣无衫可也 - Means 'So I will just grant him a blue robe without a shirt.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 文宗 - Emperor Wenzong of Tang (reigned 826-840).
    • 武宗 - Emperor Wuzong of Tang (reigned 840-846).
    • 裴舍人 - Pei Shiren, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 柳舍人 - Liu Shiren, another high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陈拾遗 - Chen Shiyi, a poet and scholar of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李崖州 - Li Yanzhou, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 王龟 - Wang Gui, a recluse during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 杜悰 - Du Cong, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 无逸 - Wu Yi, the son of Du Cong.

    Proper Nouns

    • 文宗 - Emperor Wenzong of Tang.
    • 武宗 - Emperor Wuzong of Tang.
    • 裴舍人 - Pei Shiren.
    • 柳舍人 - Liu Shiren.
    • 陈拾遗 - Chen Shiyi.
    • 李崖州 - Li Yanzhou.
    • 王龟 - Wang Gui.
    • 杜悰 - Du Cong.
    • 无逸 - Wu Yi.

    During the reign of Emperor Wenzong, there was a monk who walked on the dangerous parts of the tower as if they were flat. He replaced a column at the top of the tower, and the entire city rushed to see him, all thinking he was a miracle. The emperor heard about this and said, "This tower was certainly built by human effort. Could the craftsmen of the time also be gods?" Later, the monk was punished for practicing sorcery.

    Emperor Wenzong spoke with the scholars of the Hanlin Academy and discussed the writings of previous dynasties. Pei Shiren repeatedly mentioned the name of Chen Shiyi. Liu Shiren looked at him, but Pei did not notice. The emperor turned to Liu and said, "His courtesy name is Boyu, so he should be called Chen Boyu."

    During the reign of Emperor Wuzong, Li Yanzhou once spoke in person to the emperor that the recluse Wang Gui was capable of serving as a censor. The emperor asked, "Who is Wang Gui's father?" Li replied, "He is the son of Wang Qi." The emperor said, "Generally speaking, a recluse should be a person from the countryside. Wang Gui's father is a high official. How could he live in the countryside without holding an official position?" Li had no answer. The emperor also intended to grant clothing to Du Cong's son Wu Yi. The officials listed the items, but the color of the robe had not been approved. After a while, the emperor said, "I cannot give him a white robe; he is young and has no official position, so it would be inappropriate to grant him this attire. So I will just grant him a blue robe without a shirt." [10]

    宣宗朝，两省官对。上曰：“卿等皆朕诤臣，切须各务公道，但无私党。所论事，必与卿行。若苟近私，虽直无益。”
    　　大中七年冬，诏来年正月一日，御含元殿受朝贺。璘时为左补阙，请权御宣政殿。疏奏之明日，闻上谓宰臣曰：“有谏官疏，来年御含元殿事如何莫须罢否？”宰臣魏公謩奏曰：“元年大庆，正殿称贺，亦是常仪，况当无事之时。陛下肆觐百辟，朝廷盛礼，不可废阙。”上曰：“近华州奏，光化贼劫下邽县。又关辅久无雨雪，皆朕之忧。岂谓之无事须与他罢。假如权御宣政，亦何不可也？”宰臣奉诏，方欲宣下，而日官奏太阳当亏，遂罢之。其后宰相因奏对，以遗补多阙，请更除八人。上曰：“谏官但要职业修举，亦岂在多只如张道符、牛丛、赵璘辈三数人足矣！使朕闻所未闻。” [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卿等皆朕诤臣 - Formal address meaning 'you are all my loyal and remonstrating ministers,' emphasizing the relationship between the emperor and his officials.
    • 切须各务公道 - Means 'you must each uphold justice,' stressing the importance of impartiality.
    • 但无私党 - Means 'but without private factions,' highlighting the need for loyalty to the state rather than personal alliances.
    • 所论事，必与卿行 - Means 'the matters you discuss, I will certainly act upon,' indicating the emperor's commitment to acting on advice.
    • 若苟近私，虽直无益 - Means 'if you act out of personal interest, even if your advice is correct, it will be of no benefit,' warning against self-serving behavior.
    • 大中七年冬 - Refers to the winter of the seventh year of the Dazhong era (853 CE) during Emperor Xuanzong's reign.
    • 御含元殿受朝贺 - Means 'to preside over the court ceremony at the Hanyuan Hall,' referring to a grand imperial ritual.
    • 御宣政殿 - Means 'to preside over the court at the Xuanzheng Hall,' another imperial hall.
    • 元年大庆 - Refers to a grand celebration in the first year of an emperor's reign, often marking a new era.
    • 正殿称贺 - Means 'to offer congratulations in the main hall,' a formal ritual.
    • 肆觐百辟 - Means 'to meet with all the feudal lords,' referring to the emperor's audience with his subjects.
    • 朝廷盛礼 - Means 'the grand rituals of the court,' emphasizing the importance of ceremonial propriety.
    • 不可废阙 - Means 'cannot be omitted or neglected,' stressing the necessity of maintaining tradition.
    • 遗补多阙 - Means 'there are many vacancies among the remonstrators,' referring to the shortage of officials in this role.
    • 更除八人 - Means 'to appoint eight more people,' suggesting an increase in the number of officials.
    • 谏官但要职业修举 - Means 'the duty of the remonstrators is to fulfill their roles diligently,' emphasizing the importance of their function.
    • 博学求之，无不利于事也 - Means 'broadly seek knowledge from them, and nothing will be unhelpful in matters,' encouraging learning from all sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宣宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (r. 846–859 CE), a period known for its cultural and political reforms.
    • 两省官对 - Refers to a meeting between officials from the two main administrative bodies, the Shangshu Province and the Menxia Province, which were central to the Tang dynasty's governance.
    • 诤臣 - Refers to loyal and remonstrating ministers who advise the emperor and correct his mistakes.
    • 含元殿 - One of the three main halls in the Tang imperial palace, used for grand ceremonies.
    • 宣政殿 - Another important hall in the imperial palace, often used for daily audiences.
    • 大中七年 - The seventh year of the Dazhong era (853 CE), a period during Emperor Xuanzong's reign.
    • 华州奏 - Refers to a report from Huazhou, a region in Shaanxi, indicating a local event or issue.
    • 光化贼劫下邽县 - Refers to a bandit attack on Xiaogui County in Guanghua, a region in Shaanxi, highlighting a security concern.
    • 关辅久无雨雪 - Refers to a prolonged drought in the Guanzhong region, which was a significant agricultural area.
    • 遗补 - Refers to officials who fill vacancies in the remonstrating role, ensuring that the emperor receives advice from a sufficient number of sources.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宣宗 - The reign title of Emperor Xuanzong (r. 846–859 CE).
    • 含元殿 - One of the three main halls in the Tang imperial palace.
    • 宣政殿 - Another important hall in the imperial palace.
    • 大中七年 - The seventh year of the Dazhong era (853 CE).
    • 华州 - A region in Shaanxi, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 光化 - A region in Shaanxi, mentioned in the context of a bandit attack.
    • 下邽县 - A county in Shaanxi, the site of the bandit attack.
    • 关辅 - The Guanzhong region, a significant agricultural area in Shaanxi.
    • 赵璘 - A historical figure, likely a remonstrator or official during Emperor Xuanzong's reign.
    • 张道符 - A historical figure, likely a remonstrator or official during Emperor Xuanzong's reign.
    • 牛丛 - A historical figure, likely a remonstrator or official during Emperor Xuanzong's reign.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, officials from the two main administrative bodies met with the emperor. The emperor said, "You are all my loyal and remonstrating ministers; you must each uphold justice and avoid private factions. The matters you discuss, I will certainly act upon. If you act out of personal interest, even if your advice is correct, it will be of no benefit."

    In the seventh year of the Dazhong era during winter, an imperial decree was issued that the following year on the first day of the first month, the emperor would preside over the court ceremony at the Hanyuan Hall. At that time, Sui Lin, who was a left remonstrator, requested that the ceremony be held at the Xuanzheng Hall instead. The next day, after submitting his remonstration, Sui heard the emperor say to the chancellor, "There is a remonstration from a remonstrating official. Should the ceremony at the Hanyuan Hall for next year be canceled?" The chancellor Wei Gong Mo replied, "The grand celebration in the first year of an emperor's reign, offering congratulations in the main hall, is a customary ritual, especially during times of peace. Your Majesty meeting with all the feudal lords, the grand rituals of the court, cannot be omitted." The emperor said, "Recently, Huazhou reported that bandits from Guanghua attacked Xiaogui County. Additionally, the Guanzhong region has been without rain or snow for a long time, both of which are my concerns. How can I say there is nothing to worry about and cancel the ceremony? If we were to temporarily hold the ceremony at the Xuanzheng Hall, what harm would that cause?" The chancellor, upon receiving the imperial decree, was just about to announce it when the astrologer reported that the sun would be eclipsed, and the ceremony was thus canceled. Later, the chancellor, during a meeting with the emperor, mentioned that there were many vacancies among the remonstrators and requested to appoint eight more. The emperor said, "The duty of the remonstrators is to fulfill their roles diligently. It is not about numbers; even three or four people like Zhang Daofu, Niu Cong, and Zhao Lin are enough! They must inform me of matters I have not yet heard." [11]

    卷二 商部上
    　　商为臣，凡自王公，至有秩已上，皆入此部郭汾阳在汾州，尝奏一州县官，而敕不下。判官张昙言于同列，以令公勋德，而请一吏致阻，是宰相之不知体，甚也。汾阳王闻之，谓寮属曰：“自艰难以来，朝廷姑息方镇武臣，求无不得。以是方镇跋扈，使朝廷疑之，以致如此。今子仪奏一属官不下，不过是所请不当圣意。上恩亲厚，不以武臣待子仪，诸公可以见贺矣！”闻者服其公忠焉。王在河中，禁无故走马，犯者死。南阳夫人乳母之子抵禁，都虞候杖杀。诸子泣告于王，言虞候纵横之状，王叱而遣之。明日，对宾僚吁叹者数四。众皆不晓，徐问之，王曰：“某之诸子，皆奴材也。”遂告以故曰：“伊不赏父之都虞候，而惜母之阿奶儿，非奴材而何？” [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 商为臣 - Formal title or role, likely meaning 'Merchant as an official' or 'Merchant in service.'
    • 凡自王公，至有秩已上，皆入此部 - Formal statement indicating that all officials from high-ranking nobles to those with ranks are included in this department.
    • 郭汾阳在汾州 - Refers to Guo Ziyi, a prominent general during the Tang Dynasty, stationed in Fenyang.
    • 尝奏一州县官，而敕不下 - Indicates that Guo Ziyi once recommended a county official, but the imperial decree did not come down.
    • 判官张昙言于同列 - Means 'the magistrate Zhang Tan spoke to his colleagues.'
    • 以令公勋德，而请一吏致阻，是宰相之不知体，甚也 - A critique suggesting that the chancellor's refusal to approve the recommendation shows a lack of respect for Guo Ziyi's status.
    • 闻之，谓寮属曰 - Means 'hearing this, he told his subordinates.'
    • 自艰难以来，朝廷姑息方镇武臣，求无不得 - Indicates that since the difficult times, the court has been overly lenient with regional military officials, granting them whatever they asked for.
    • 以是方镇跋扈，使朝廷疑之，以致如此 - Explains that this leniency has led to the regional military officials becoming arrogant and causing the court to distrust them.
    • 今子仪奏一属官不下，不过是所请不当圣意 - Means 'now that General Guo has recommended a subordinate who was not approved, it is probably because the request did not align with the emperor's wishes.'
    • 上恩亲厚，不以武臣待子仪，诸公可以见贺矣 - Expresses gratitude for the emperor's favor and reassures his subordinates that the court does not treat him as just a military official.
    • 闻者服其公忠焉 - Means 'those who heard this admired his fairness and loyalty.'
    • 王在河中，禁无故走马，犯者死 - Indicates that Guo Ziyi, while in Hezhong, banned unauthorized horse riding, and violators were punished by death.
    • 南阳夫人乳母之子抵禁，都虞候杖杀 - Means 'the son of the wet nurse of Lady Nanyang was caught violating the ban and was beaten to death by the military commander.'
    • 诸子泣告于王，言虞候纵横之状 - Means 'his sons wept and told him about the military commander's arbitrary actions.'
    • 王叱而遣之 - Means 'he scolded them and sent them away.'
    • 明日，对宾僚吁叹者数四 - Means 'the next day, he sighed several times in front of his guests.'
    • 众皆不晓，徐问之 - Means 'everyone was puzzled, so they asked him slowly.'
    • 某之诸子，皆奴材也 - Means 'my sons are all incompetent.'
    • 伊不赏父之都虞候，而惜母之阿奶儿，非奴材而何 - Means 'he does not appreciate his father's military commander and is concerned about his mother's wet nurse's son, what kind of incompetent is he?'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郭汾阳 - Refers to Guo Ziyi, a famous general during the Tang Dynasty known for his loyalty and military prowess.
    • 汾州 - Refers to Fenyang, a city in Shanxi Province where Guo Ziyi was stationed.
    • 王公 - Refers to high-ranking nobles or officials.
    • 有秩已上 - Refers to officials with ranks or positions.
    • 判官 - Refers to a magistrate or local official.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the chancellor or prime minister of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河中 - Refers to Hezhong, a region in Shanxi Province.
    • 南阳夫人 - Refers to Lady Nanyang, likely a high-ranking lady in Guo Ziyi's household.
    • 都虞候 - Refers to a military commander responsible for maintaining discipline.
    • 阿奶儿 - Refers to a wet nurse's son, indicating a lower social status.
    • 奴材 - A derogatory term meaning 'incompetent' or 'useless.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 郭汾阳 - Guo Ziyi
    • 汾州 - Fenyang
    • 王公 - High-ranking nobles or officials
    • 有秩已上 - Officials with ranks or positions
    • 判官 - Magistrate or local official
    • 宰相 - Chancellor or prime minister
    • 河中 - Hezhong
    • 南阳夫人 - Lady Nanyang
    • 都虞候 - Military commander
    • 阿奶儿 - Wet nurse's son
    • 奴材 - Incompetent or useless

    卷二 商部上

    The merchant serves as an official, and all officials from high-ranking nobles to those with ranks are included in this department. Guo Ziyi, stationed in Fenyang, once recommended a county official, but the imperial decree did not come down. The magistrate Zhang Tan spoke to his colleagues, saying that the chancellor's refusal to approve the recommendation showed a lack of respect for Guo Ziyi's status. Hearing this, Guo Ziyi told his subordinates, "Since the difficult times, the court has been overly lenient with regional military officials, granting them whatever they asked for. This leniency has led to the regional military officials becoming arrogant, causing the court to distrust them, and resulting in this situation. Now that General Guo has recommended a subordinate who was not approved, it is probably because the request did not align with the emperor's wishes. The emperor's favor and closeness, not treating me as just a military official, allow you all to celebrate!" Those who heard this admired his fairness and loyalty. While in Hezhong, Guo Ziyi banned unauthorized horse riding, and violators were punished by death. The son of the wet nurse of Lady Nanyang was caught violating the ban and was beaten to death by the military commander. Guo Ziyi's sons wept and told him about the military commander's arbitrary actions. He scolded them and sent them away. The next day, he sighed several times in front of his guests. Everyone was puzzled, so they asked him slowly. He said, "My sons are all incompetent." He then explained, "He does not appreciate his father's military commander and is concerned about his mother's wet nurse's son, what kind of incompetent is he?" [12]

    余外伯祖殿中侍御史柳君，掌汾阳书记时，有高堂之庆。王每因军中大宴，常戒左右曰：“柳侍御太夫人就棚，可先告。”及赵夫人板舆至，王降阶与僚属等立俟，到棚而退。尝谓柳君曰：“子仪早亲戎事，不尽奉养而孤。今日幸忝重寄，恩宠逾分，虽为贵盛，实无侍御之荣。”因呜咽不胜。又曰：“若太夫人许降顾子仪之家，使南阳夫人已下执爨，子仪自捧馔，具供养足矣！”而赵夫人以清素自居，终不一往。
    　　司徒郑真公，每在方镇，崇树公家，陈设器用，无不精备。至于宴犒之事，未尝刻薄。而居常奉身，过于俭素。中外婚嫁，无日无之，凡是礼物，皆经神虑。公与其宗叔太子太傅絪，俱住招国，太傅第在南，出自南祖；司徒第在北，出自北祖。时人谓之“南郑相”、“北郑相”。司徒堂兄文宪公，前后相德宗。亦谓之“大郑相”、“小郑相”焉。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 外伯祖 - Refers to an uncle on the father's side, specifically the father of one's father.
    • 殿中侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for supervising officials and handling legal matters.
    • 高堂之慶 - A euphemism for the birth of a grandchild, indicating joy and celebration.
    • 板舆 - A traditional Chinese sedan chair or carriage, often used by elderly people.
    • 降阶 - To descend from a higher position or status, here used to show respect.
    • 执爨 - To cook, often used in the context of serving food to guests or family members.
    • 捧馔 - To serve food, often used in a formal or ceremonial context.
    • 供养 - To provide food, clothing, and other necessities, often in a filial context.
    • 鸣咽 - To sob or weep softly, often used to express deep emotion.
    • 忝重寄 - To feel unworthy of a significant responsibility or position.
    • 恩宠逾分 - To receive more favor or grace than one deserves.
    • 清素自居 - To live a simple and modest life, often used to describe a person who does not seek luxury or extravagance.
    • 终不一往 - To never go once, indicating a refusal or reluctance to visit.
    • 司徒 - A high-ranking official in ancient China, responsible for various administrative and military duties.
    • 真公 - A respectful title, often used to refer to a senior official.
    • 方镇 - A regional governor or military commander in ancient China.
    • 崇树公家 - To honor and support the public or state affairs.
    • 陈设器用 - To arrange and display utensils or equipment, often in a formal setting.
    • 宴犒之事 - Banquets and rewards, often given to soldiers or officials.
    • 刻薄 - To be stingy or mean, often used to criticize someone for being too frugal.
    • 俭素 - To live a simple and frugal life, often used positively to describe a virtuous lifestyle.
    • 中外婚嫁 - Marriages within and outside the family, often used to describe social connections and alliances.
    • 礼物 - Gifts, often used in the context of social and ceremonial exchanges.
    • 神虑 - To carefully consider or plan, often used to describe meticulous attention to detail.
    • 南祖 - The southern branch of a family or lineage.
    • 北祖 - The northern branch of a family or lineage.
    • 大郑相 - A respectful title for a prominent official, often used to distinguish between different generations or branches of a family.
    • 小郑相 - A respectful title for a prominent official, often used to distinguish between different generations or branches of a family.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 柳君 - Refers to a person named Liu, likely a relative of the author.
    • 汾阳 - Refers to Fenyang, a city in Shanxi Province, which was an important military base during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 王 - Refers to a high-ranking official, likely a military leader or governor.
    • 赵夫人 - Refers to Zhao, the wife of the official mentioned, likely a noblewoman.
    • 太夫人 - Refers to a mother or grandmother, often used in a respectful context.
    • 子仪 - Refers to Li Guangbi, a famous general and statesman during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南阳夫人 - Refers to a noblewoman, likely a relative of the official mentioned.
    • 司徒郑真公 - Refers to Zheng Zhen, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 方镇 - Refers to regional governors or military commanders, who had significant power and influence in their territories.
    • 德宗 - Refers to Emperor Dezong of Tang, who ruled from 779 to 805 AD.
    • 南郑相 - Refers to a prominent official from the southern branch of the Zheng family.
    • 北郑相 - Refers to a prominent official from the northern branch of the Zheng family.
    • 大郑相 - Refers to a prominent official from the Zheng family, likely a senior member.
    • 小郑相 - Refers to a prominent official from the Zheng family, likely a junior member.

    Proper Nouns

    • 柳君 - Liu, a high-ranking official.
    • 汾阳 - Fenyang, a city in Shanxi Province.
    • 王 - A high-ranking official, likely a military leader or governor.
    • 赵夫人 - Zhao, the wife of the official mentioned.
    • 太夫人 - A mother or grandmother, often used in a respectful context.
    • 子仪 - Li Guangbi, a famous general and statesman during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南阳夫人 - A noblewoman, likely a relative of the official mentioned.
    • 司徒郑真公 - Zheng Zhen, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 南郑相 - A prominent official from the southern branch of the Zheng family.
    • 北郑相 - A prominent official from the northern branch of the Zheng family.
    • 大郑相 - A prominent official from the Zheng family, likely a senior member.
    • 小郑相 - A prominent official from the Zheng family, likely a junior member.

    My uncle, the Dean of the Imperial Censorate, Liu Jun, was in charge of the Fenyang military records when he had a son, bringing joy to his family. Whenever there was a grand feast in the army, the high-ranking official would instruct his attendants, saying, "Tell Master Liu that his mother is coming in the sedan chair, and we should be informed first." When Zhao's sedan chair arrived, the official descended the steps and stood with his subordinates to welcome her, then retreated to the pavilion. He once told Liu Jun, "Li Guangbi served in the military early on and could not fully care for his family. Today, I am fortunate to hold such a significant position, receiving more favor than I deserve. Although I am in a position of great honor, it does not bring me the same glory as your family." He then wept softly, unable to control his emotions. He also said, "If your mother agrees to visit Li Guangbi's home, let Lady Nanyang and others prepare the meal, and Li Guangbi will serve the food himself, ensuring everything is taken care of!" However, Zhao chose to live a simple and modest life and never visited once.

    Zheng Zhen, the Duke of Sima, was a high-ranking official who, whenever stationed in a regional command, honored and supported public affairs, arranging and displaying utensils with meticulous care. He never cut corners in hosting banquets and rewarding officials. In his daily life, he lived a simple and frugal lifestyle. Marriages within and outside the family were frequent, and all gifts were carefully considered. He lived alongside his cousin, the Prince of Tai, who resided in the south, representing the southern branch of the family; Zheng Zhen's residence was in the north, representing the northern branch. People referred to them as the "Southern Zheng" and the "Northern Zheng." Zheng Zhen's older brother, the Duke of Wenxian, served as a chancellor to Emperor Dezong. They were also known as the "Senior Zheng" and the "Junior Zheng." [13]

    韩仆射皋为京兆尹，韦相贯之以畿尉趋事。及韦公入相，仆射为吏部尚书，每至中书，韦常异礼，以伸故吏之敬。又仆射为尹时，久旱祈雨，县官读祝文，一心记公之家讳，及称官衔毕，而误呼先相公名，公但惨然，因命重读，亦不之罪。在夏口，尝病小疮，令医傅膏药，药不濡，公问之，医云：“天寒膏硬。”公笑曰：“韩皋实是硬。”竟不以为事，得大贤体矣。初公自贬所量移钱唐，与李庶人不协。后公在鄂州，锜梦万岁楼上挂冰，因自解曰：“冰者寒也，楼者高也，岂韩皋来代我乎？”意甚恶之。其后公果移镇浙右焉。自黄门以来，三世传执一笏，经祖父所执，未尝轻授于仆人之手，归则躬置于卧内一榻，以示敬慎。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    Han Yuese Gao served as the Governor of Jingzhao Prefecture, while Wei Xiangguan, as a Prefectural Magistrate, attended to his duties. When Wei Xiangguan later became chancellor, Han Yuese Gao was appointed as the Minister of Personnel. Whenever Han Yuese Gao visited the chancellery, Wei Xiangguan treated him with special respect, showing deference to his former subordinate. Additionally, during Han Yuese Gao's time as governor, there was a prolonged drought, and a county official was tasked with reading the prayer text. The official was so focused on remembering Han Yuese Gao's family name that he mistakenly called out the name of a former chancellor. Han Yuese Gao merely frowned and ordered the text to be read again, without punishing the official. In Xia Kou, Han Yuese Gao once suffered from a minor wound and had a doctor apply ointment. The ointment was too hard, and Han Yuese Gao asked why. The doctor replied, "It's cold, and the ointment is hard." Han Yuese Gao laughed and said, "Han Gao is indeed hard." He did not make a fuss about it, demonstrating the demeanor of a great sage. Initially, Han Yuese Gao was demoted and reassigned to Qiantang, where he did not get along with Li Shuren. Later, when Han Yuese Gao was stationed in Ezhou, Li Shuren dreamed of hanging ice on a high tower and interpreted it as a sign that Han Yuese Gao would replace him, which he deeply disliked. Indeed, Han Yuese Gao was later reassigned to govern Zheyou. Since the time of his father, Han Yuese Gao's family had passed down a folding fan, which was never given lightly to servants. Upon returning home, he personally placed it on a bed in his room, to show respect and caution. [14]

    族祖天水昭公，以旧相为吏部侍郎。考前进士杜元颖宏词登科，镇南又奏为従事。杜公入相，昭公复掌选。至杜出镇西川，奏宋相申锡为従事。数年，杜以南蛮入寇，贬刺循州，遂卒。宋以宰相被诬，谪佐开州。又数年，昭公始薨。公凡八任铨衡，三领节镇，皆带府号，为尚书，惟不历工部，其兵、吏、太常皆再往。年八十七薨，其间未尝遇重疾，异数寿考，为中朝之首焉。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 族祖天水昭公 - Refers to a respected ancestor named '昭公' from the Tian Shui lineage.
    • 以旧相为吏部侍郎 - Means 'appointed as the Minister of Personnel with the rank of Left Censor.'
    • 考前进士杜元颖宏词登科 - Means 'his son, Du Yuanying, passed the imperial examination with distinction.'
    • 镇南又奏为従事 - Means 'was then recommended for the position of Assistant Governor in the Southern Commandery.'
    • 杜公入相 - Means 'Du Yuanying became Chancellor.'
    • 昭公复掌选 - Means '昭公 resumed his role as Minister of Personnel.'
    • 至杜出镇西川 - Means 'when Du Yuanying was dispatched to govern the Western Sichuan region.'
    • 奏宋相申锡为従事 - Means 'recommended Song Xingxi as his Assistant Governor.'
    • 数年，杜以南蛮入寇，贬刺循州，遂卒 - Means 'after several years, Du Yuanying was demoted to the position of Prefect of Xunzhou due to the invasion by southern barbarians and died there.'
    • 宋以宰相被诬，谪佐开州 - Means 'Song Xingxi, as a former Chancellor, was falsely accused and exiled to assist in Kai Prefecture.'
    • 又数年，昭公始薨 - Means 'after several more years, 昭公 passed away.'
    • 公凡八任铨衡 - Means 'He served eight terms as Minister of Personnel.'
    • 三领节镇 - Means 'He governed three commanderies.'
    • 皆带府号，为尚书 - Means 'All his appointments came with the title of Governor and he held the rank of Secretary.'
    • 惟不历工部，其兵、吏、太常皆再往 - Means 'except for the Ministry of Works, he served twice in the Ministries of War, Personnel, and Rites.'
    • 年八十七薨 - Means 'He passed away at the age of eighty-seven.'
    • 其间未尝遇重疾 - Means 'Throughout his life, he never suffered from a serious illness.'
    • 异数寿考，为中朝之首焉 - Means 'His longevity and numerous honors made him the foremost figure in the central court.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天水 - A place name referring to the Tian Shui region, historically significant for its association with the Sima clan.
    • 吏部侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 宏词登科 - A prestigious category in the imperial examination system.
    • 镇南 - Refers to the Southern Commandery, a region in ancient China.
    • 従事 - An assistant or deputy governor.
    • 入相 - Becoming Chancellor, the highest administrative position in the imperial government.
    • 出镇 - Being dispatched to govern a distant region.
    • 西川 - Refers to the Western Sichuan region.
    • 南蛮入寇 - Refers to invasions by southern barbarians, a common theme in Chinese history.
    • 刺循州 - Being demoted to the position of Prefect of Xunzhou.
    • 宰相被诬 - Being falsely accused as a former Chancellor.
    • 谪佐开州 - Being exiled to assist in Kai Prefecture.
    • 铨衡 - Refers to the Ministry of Personnel, responsible for appointments and promotions.
    • 节镇 - Commanderies, administrative divisions in ancient China.
    • 府号 - Titles associated with governorships.
    • 尚书 - A high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 工部 - The Ministry of Works, responsible for public works and construction.
    • 兵、吏、太常 - Ministries of War, Personnel, and Rites, respectively.
    • 中朝 - The central court, referring to the imperial government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 族祖天水昭公 - The main subject of the passage.
    • 杜元颖 - His son, who passed the imperial examination with distinction.
    • 宋相申锡 - A former Chancellor recommended by Du Yuanying.
    • 杜公 - Du Yuanying, who became Chancellor.
    • 西川 - The Western Sichuan region.
    • 南蛮 - Southern barbarians.
    • 循州 - A prefecture where Du Yuanying was demoted.
    • 开州 - A prefecture where Song Xingxi was exiled.
    • 工部 - The Ministry of Works.
    • 兵、吏、太常 - Ministries of War, Personnel, and Rites.
    • 中朝 - The central court.

    My ancestor, the illustrious Lord of Tian Shui, was appointed as the Minister of Personnel with the rank of Left Censor. His son, Du Yuanying, passed the imperial examination with distinction and was recommended for the position of Assistant Governor in the Southern Commandery. When Du Yuanying became Chancellor, the Lord of Tian Shui resumed his role as Minister of Personnel. Later, when Du Yuanying was dispatched to govern the Western Sichuan region, he recommended Song Xingxi, a former Chancellor, as his Assistant Governor. After several years, Du Yuanying was demoted to the position of Prefect of Xunzhou due to the invasion by southern barbarians and died there. Song Xingxi, as a former Chancellor, was falsely accused and exiled to assist in Kai Prefecture. After several more years, the Lord of Tian Shui passed away. He served eight terms as Minister of Personnel, governed three commanderies, all with the title of Governor and the rank of Secretary, except for the Ministry of Works, where he served twice in the Ministries of War, Personnel, and Rites. He passed away at the age of eighty-seven, having never suffered from a serious illness. His longevity and numerous honors made him the foremost figure in the central court. [15]

    仆射柳元公家行，为士林仪表。居大官，奉继亲薛太夫人，尽孝敬之道，凡事不异布衣时。薛夫人左右仆使，至有连小字呼公者。性严重，居外下辇，常惕惧。在薛夫人之侧，未尝以毅颜待家人，恂恂如小子弟。敦睦内外，当世无比。宗族穷苦无告，因公而存立优泰者，不知其数。在方镇，子弟有事他适，所经境内，人不知之。族子应规，为水部员外郎，求公为市宅，公不与。潜语所亲曰：“柳应规以儒素进身，始入省，便坐新宅，殊不若且税居之为善也。”及水部殁，公抚视孤幼，恩意加厚，特为置居处，诸子皆与身名。族孙立疾病，以儿女托公。及廉察夏口，嫁其孤女，虽箱箧刀尽微物，悉手自阅视以付之。公出自清河崔氏，继外族薛氏，前后与舅能、従同时领方镇、居省闼。又与继舅苹同时为观察使，妻父韩仆射同时居大僚，未尝敢以爵位自高，减卑下之敬，其行己如此。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 仆射 - High-ranking official position in ancient China, often translated as 'Chancellor of the Board of Works' or 'Minister of Works.'
    • 行 - In this context, it means 'to go on a journey' or 'to travel.'
    • 士林仪表 - Refers to a model or exemplar for scholars and officials.
    • 大官 - A high-ranking official.
    • 继亲 - A term used to describe a stepmother or stepfather.
    • 布衣时 - Refers to a time when one was a commoner or ordinary person.
    • 小字 - A nickname or pet name.
    • 敦睦 - To promote harmony and unity.
    • 方镇 - A regional military governor or commander.
    • 身名 - Refers to a person's reputation or standing.
    • 廉察 - An official responsible for supervision and investigation.
    • 箱箧刀尽微物 - Refers to all the small and insignificant items in a box or chest.
    • 阅视 - To examine or inspect.
    • 出自 - To come from or originate from.
    • 领方镇 - To hold the position of a regional military governor.
    • 居省闼 - To reside in the government offices.
    • 观察使 - An official responsible for overseeing and reporting on local conditions.
    • 大僚 - A high-ranking official.
    • 行己 - One's conduct or behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 柳元公 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 薛太夫人 - The stepmother of Li Yuanyong, likely a respected and influential figure.
    • 清河崔氏 - A prominent aristocratic family from Qinghe, known for their political and cultural influence.
    • 夏口 - A region in modern-day Hubei Province, historically significant for its strategic location.
    • 水部员外郎 - A low-ranking official in the Water Department, responsible for water management.
    • 应规 - A family member of Li Yuanyong, likely a relative by marriage or blood.
    • 立 - Another family member, possibly a younger relative.
    • 廉察 - An official responsible for supervision and investigation, often involved in administrative and judicial duties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 柳元公 - Li Yuanyong, a high-ranking official.
    • 薛太夫人 - The stepmother of Li Yuanyong.
    • 清河崔氏 - The Qinghe Cui family, a prominent aristocratic family.
    • 夏口 - A region in modern-day Hubei Province.
    • 水部员外郎 - A low-ranking official in the Water Department.
    • 应规 - A family member of Li Yuanyong.
    • 立 - Another family member of Li Yuanyong.
