    • 廉察 - An official responsible for supervision and investigation.
    • 韩仆射 - A high-ranking official, likely the father-in-law of Li Yuanyong.
    • 能、従 - Other officials who held the same positions as Li Yuanyong.

    Chancellor Li Yuanyong, while traveling, served as a model for scholars and officials. As a high-ranking official, he showed utmost respect to his stepmother, Lady Xue, fulfilling the duties of filial piety, and conducted himself consistently as he did when he was an ordinary person. The servants of Lady Xue, some even calling him by his pet name, treated him with familiarity. Li Yuanyong was serious and cautious, always vigilant when he was outside his home. In the presence of Lady Xue, he never displayed a stern demeanor towards his family, always gentle and respectful like a younger brother. He promoted harmony both within and outside his family, unmatched in his time. Many family members in dire straits found solace and stability through his efforts, the number being countless. While serving as a regional military governor, when his children had other matters to attend to, he ensured that his presence was discreet and unobtrusive, known to no one in the areas he passed through. His nephew, Li Yinggui, who held the position of Water Department Assistant, asked Li Yuanyong to help him buy a house, but Li refused. He privately told his close associates, "Li Yinggui advanced through his scholarly and modest conduct; just as he entered the government offices, he moved into a new house. It would have been better for him to rent a place for now." After Li Yinggui's death, Li Yuanyong took care of his orphaned children, showing them extra kindness and arranging their living situations, ensuring they all had a good reputation. Another relative, Li Li, who was ill, entrusted his children to Li Yuanyong. When Li Yuanyong was appointed as an inspector in Xiang Kou, he arranged for the marriage of Li Li's orphaned daughter, personally examining and handing over all the small and insignificant items in her possession. Li Yuanyong came from the prestigious Qinghe Cui family and later married into the Xue family. He served alongside his uncles and cousins in the same positions of regional military governors and government offices. He also served as an inspector alongside his step-uncle, and his father-in-law, Yan Pushe, held high-ranking positions. Despite his high status, Li Yuanyong never considered himself superior to others, always showing the utmost respect to those of lower rank, as evidenced by his conduct. [16]

    权文公德舆，身不由科第，掌贡举三年。门下所出诸生，相继为公相。得人之盛，时论居多。
    　　赵郡李氏，三祖之后，元和初，同时各一人为相。蕃南祖，吉甫西祖，绛东祖，而皆第三。至太和、开成间，又各一人前后在相位：德裕，吉甫之子；固言，蕃再従弟，皆第九；珏亦绛之近従，诸族罕有。
    　　李尚书益，有宗人庶子同名，俱出于姑臧公。时人谓尚书为“文章李益”，庶子为“门户李益”，而尚书亦兼门地焉。尝姻族间有礼会，尚书归笑，谓家人曰：“大堪笑，今日局席两个坐头，总是李益。” [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 身不由科第 - Literally means 'not by way of the imperial examination,' indicating that the person did not rise through the imperial examination system.
    • 掌贡举三年 - '掌贡举' means 'to preside over the imperial examinations,' and '三年' means 'for three years.'
    • 门下所出诸生 - '门下' means 'under his tutelage,' '所出' means 'produced,' and '诸生' means 'various students.'
    • 相继为公相 - '相继' means 'successively,' and '为公相' means 'became high officials.'
    • 得人之盛，时论居多 - '得人之盛' means 'the flourishing of talented people,' and '时论居多' means 'was widely praised at the time.'
    • 三祖之后 - '三祖' means 'three ancestors,' and '之后' means 'afterwards.'
    • 元和初 - '元和' is an era name, and '初' means 'beginning.'
    • 同时各一人为相 - '同时' means 'at the same time,' '各一人' means 'each one,' and '为相' means 'became chancellor.'
    • 蕃南祖，吉甫西祖，绛东祖 - '蕃南祖' means 'Southern ancestor of Fan,' '吉甫西祖' means 'Western ancestor of Ji,' and '绛东祖' means 'Eastern ancestor of Jiang.'
    • 而皆第三 - '而' means 'and,' '皆' means 'all,' and '第三' means 'the third generation.'
    • 至太和、开成间 - '至' means 'up to,' '太和' and '开成' are era names, and '间' means 'during.'
    • 又各一人前后在相位 - '又' means 'again,' '各一人' means 'each one,' '前后' means 'before and after,' and '在相位' means 'held the position of chancellor.'
    • 德裕，吉甫之子 - '德裕' is a name, and '吉甫之子' means 'son of Ji.'
    • 固言，蕃再従弟 - '固言' is a name, and '蕃再従弟' means 'second cousin of Fan.'
    • 皆第九 - '皆' means 'all,' and '第九' means 'ninth generation.'
    • 珏亦绛之近従 - '珏' is a name, '亦' means 'also,' '绛' is a place name, and '近従' means 'close relative.'
    • 诸族罕有 - '诸族' means 'various clans,' and '罕有' means 'rarely have.'
    • 李尚书益 - '李' is a surname, '尚书' is an official title, and '益' is a name.
    • 有宗人庶子同名 - '有' means 'has,' '宗人' means 'clan member,' '庶子' means 'illegitimate son,' and '同名' means 'with the same name.'
    • 俱出于姑臧公 - '俱' means 'both,' '出于' means 'descended from,' and '姑臧公' is a title.
    • 时人谓尚书为‘文章李益’，庶子为‘门户李益’ - '时人' means 'people at the time,' '谓' means 'call,' '尚书' is an official title, '文章李益' means 'literary Li Yi,' '庶子' means 'illegitimate son,' and '门户李益' means 'family Li Yi.'
    • 而尚书亦兼门地焉 - '而' means 'and,' '尚书' is an official title, '亦' means 'also,' '兼' means 'combine,' '门地' means 'family background,' and '焉' is a particle.
    • 尝姻族间有礼会 - '尝' means 'once,' '姻族' means 'kinship,' '间' means 'among,' and '有礼会' means 'had a ceremonial gathering.'
    • 尚书归笑，谓家人曰 - '尚书' is an official title, '归' means 'return,' '笑' means 'laugh,' '谓' means 'say,' and '家人' means 'family members.'
    • 大堪笑，今日局席两个坐头，总是李益 - '大堪笑' means 'very funny,' '今日' means 'today,' '局席' means 'ceremonial table,' '两个坐头' means 'two seats,' and '总是李益' means 'are all Li Yi.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 权文公德舆 - A historical figure who did not rise through the imperial examination system but became a high official.
    • 赵郡李氏 - A prominent family from Zhao County.
    • 三祖之后 - Refers to the descendants of three ancestors.
    • 元和初 - The early period of the Yuanhe era (806-820 AD).
    • 太和、开成间 - The periods of the Tahe (827-835 AD) and Kai Cheng (836-840 AD) eras.
    • 李尚书益 - An official with the title of Shangshu and the name Yi.
    • 姑臧公 - A title indicating a high-ranking official or noble.

    Proper Nouns

    • 权文公德舆 - Quan Wen Gong De Yu
    • 赵郡李氏 - Li family from Zhao County
    • 三祖 - Three ancestors
    • 元和 - Yuanhe era (806-820 AD)
    • 太和 - Tahe era (827-835 AD)
    • 开成 - Kai Cheng era (836-840 AD)
    • 李尚书益 - Li Shangshu Yi
    • 姑臧公 - A high-ranking official or noble

    Quan Wen Gong De Yu, though not rising through the imperial examination, presided over the imperial examinations for three years. His students, who succeeded him, became high officials. The flourishing of talented people was widely praised at the time.

    The Li family from Zhao County, descendants of three ancestors, saw one member from each branch become chancellor during the early Yuanhe era. The Southern ancestor of Fan, the Western ancestor of Ji, and the Eastern ancestor of Jiang were all third-generation descendants. During the Tahe and Kai Cheng eras, another member from each branch held the position of chancellor: De Yu, the son of Ji; Gu Yan, the second cousin of Fan; and Jue, a close relative of Jiang. Such occurrences were rare among the various clans.

    Li Shangshu Yi had a clan member and an illegitimate son with the same name, both descending from the title of Guzang Gong. People at the time referred to the official as "Literary Li Yi" and the illegitimate son as "Family Li Yi." The official also had a distinguished family background. Once, during a ceremonial gathering among kin, he returned home laughing and told his family, "It's very funny. Today, at the ceremonial table, the two seats are occupied by Li Yi." [17]

    大僚睦亲敦旧者，前辈有司徒郑公，中间有杨詹事马柳卿元公，近日李相国武都公宗闵，士大夫间罕俦。
    　　裴尚书武，奉寡嫂，抚甥侄，为中表所称。尚书卒后，工部夫人崔氏，语其仁，辄流涕。工部名佶，有清德，武之长兄也。兄弟皆为八座，自丞相耀卿至工部子泰章，四世入南北省，群従居显列者，不可胜书。
    　　靖安李少师，虽居贵位，不以威重隔物。与宾僚饮宴谭笑，曲尽布衣之叹，不记过失。善饮酒。暑月临水，以荷为杯，满酌密系，持近人口，以筋刺之，不尽则重饮。宴散，有人言昨饮大欢者，公曰：“今日言欢，则明前之不欢，无论好恶，一不得言。”段相文昌，性介狭，宴席宾客，有眉睫之失，必致怪讶。在西川，有进士薛太白饮酒，称名太多，明日遂不复召。 [18]

    High-ranking officials who are close to their relatives and old friends, among the predecessors there was Grand Tutor Zheng Gong, and among the intermediaries there was Imperial Tutor Yang Jianshi Ma Liuqing Yuan Gong, and recently there was Chancellor Li Xiangguo Wudu Gong Zongmin, rarely matched among the literati.

    Pei Shangshi Wu, he supported a widowed sister-in-law, cared for nephews and nieces, and was praised by relatives and in-laws. After the death of Pei Shangshi Wu, Cui, the wife of the Ministry of Works, spoke of his kindness and would shed tears. The Ministry of Works was named Ji, with a clear virtue, the eldest brother of Wu. All brothers held positions in the Eight Ministries, from Chancellor Yaoqing to the son of the Ministry of Works, four generations entering the northern and southern provinces, many relatives in prominent positions, too numerous to count.

    Imperial Tutor Li Shaoshi, even though in a high position, did not isolate himself from others due to his authority. He drank, feasted, and chatted with guests, fully expressing the sentiments of common people, not holding grudges or remembering past mistakes. He was good at drinking. In summer, by the water, using lotus leaves as cups, filling the lotus cups, tying them tightly, and holding them near the mouth, pricking the lotus leaf with a chopstick, if not finished, drink again. After the banquet, someone said that the previous day's drinking was very enjoyable, and the public said, "If today we say it was enjoyable, then yesterday it was not enjoyable, regardless of good or bad, it cannot be said." Duan Xiang, also known as Wen Chang, was of a narrow and strict nature. At banquets, even minor mistakes by guests would cause him to be surprised and annoyed. In Xizhou, there was a scholar named Xue Taibai who drank, calling out names too often, the next day he was no longer invited. [18]

    李太师逢吉知贡举，榜成未放而入相，礼部王尚书播代放榜。及第人就中书见座主，时谓“好脚迹门生”，前世未有。
    　　刘桂州栖楚为京兆尹，号令严明，诛罚不避权势。先是京城恶少，屠沽商贩，多系名诸军，不遵府县法令，以凌衣冠、夺贫弱为事，有罪即逃入军中，无由追捕。刘公为尹，一皆穷治。至有匿军中，名目，自称百姓者。旬朔内，坊市奸偷宿猾，慑气屏迹。余尝与友生入市，市内有一军人，乘醉误突友生驴。过旁诸少年噪曰：“痴男子死日到，敢近衣冠耶？”人人似头上各有一刘尹，栗栗惴惧，不敢为非。而与属吏言，未曾伤气，不叱责一官。人常谓府县僚曰：“诸公各有自了本分公事，晴天美景，任恣意游赏，勿致拘束。” [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好脚迹门生 - Colloquial expression meaning 'students who follow in the footsteps of their master,' used ironically to criticize the practice of favoritism in the imperial examination.
    • 人人似头上各有一刘尹 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'everyone seemed to have a Duan Yi (Liu Qizhou) on their head,' indicating the pervasive fear and respect for Liu's strict rule.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李太师逢吉 - Refers to Li Fengji, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his role in the imperial examination system.
    • 刘桂州栖楚 - Refers to Liu Qizhou, a governor known for his strict administration and enforcement of the law.
    • 京兆尹 - The title of the chief magistrate of the capital city, a high-ranking official responsible for maintaining order and justice.
    • 屠沽商贩 - Terms referring to butchers, wine sellers, and merchants, representing the lower social classes.
    • 凌衣冠、夺贫弱 - Terms describing the behavior of the lower social classes, who would oppress the gentry and plunder the poor and weak.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李太师逢吉 - Li Fengji, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 刘桂州栖楚 - Liu Qizhou, a governor known for his strict administration.
    • 京兆尹 - The title of the chief magistrate of the capital city.
    • 屠沽商贩 - Terms referring to butchers, wine sellers, and merchants.
    • 凌衣冠、夺贫弱 - Terms describing the behavior of the lower social classes.

    Li Fengji, the Grand Tutor, was in charge of the imperial examination. The list of successful candidates was completed but not yet announced when he became a chancellor. The Minister of Rites Wang Shangshu took over to announce the results. The successful candidates then met their master at the Ministry of State Affairs, an event that was ironically referred to as "students who follow in the footsteps of their master," a practice never seen before in previous eras.

    Liu Qizhou, the Governor of Guilin, served as the Chief Magistrate of the capital city. He enforced strict and clear orders, punishing without sparing the powerful. Prior to this, the capital's lower social classes, including butchers, wine sellers, and merchants, were often affiliated with various military units and did not obey the laws of the prefecture or county. They oppressed the gentry and plundered the poor and weak, and when they committed crimes, they would escape into the military, making it impossible to pursue them. Under Liu's administration, all such individuals were thoroughly punished. Some even hid in the military and falsely claimed to be commoners. Within a short period, the cunning thieves and habitual criminals in the city were intimidated and ceased their activities. I once accompanied a friend into the market, where a military man, drunk, accidentally bumped into my friend's donkey. The surrounding young men shouted, "What a foolish man! He dares to approach a scholar?" Everyone seemed to have a Duan Yi (Liu Qizhou) on their head, trembling with fear and unwilling to commit any wrongdoing. However, when speaking to his subordinates, Liu never lost his temper, nor did he scold any official. People often said that officials should focus on their own duties. On fine days with beautiful scenery, they could freely enjoy themselves without being restricted. [19]

    李司徒汧公镇宣武，戎事之隙，以琴书为娱。自造琴，聚新旧桐材，扣之合律者，则裁而胶缀；不中者，弃之，故所蓄二琴，殊绝，所谓“响泉”、“韵磬”者也。性不喜琴兼筝声，惟二宠妓曰秀奴、七七，皆聪慧善琴，兼筝与歌，时令奏之。自撰琴谱。兵部员外郎约，汧公之子也。以近属宰相子，而雅度玄机，萧萧冲远，德行既优，又有山林之致。琴道、酒德、诗调皆高绝，一生不近粉黛，性喜接引人物，不好俗谈。晨起草裹头，对客蹙融，便过一日。多蓄古器，在湖州尝得古铁一片，击之清越。又养一猿名“山公”，尝以之随逐。月夜泛江登金山，击铁鼓琴，猿必啸和。倾壶达旦，不俟外宾。与璘先君同在浙西使府，居处相接，慕先君家行及诗韵，契分最深。伯父高陵府君夫人韦氏，即兵部之姨妹也。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 司徒 - Ancient Chinese official title, equivalent to a prime minister or minister of state.
    • 镇宣武 - Governor of the Xuanwu region, where Xuanwu was likely a military or administrative district.
    • 戎事之隙 - In the gaps of military affairs, referring to moments when military duties are not occupied.
    • 以琴书为娱 - To find pleasure in playing the lute and reading books, indicating a leisurely pursuit of the arts.
    • 扣之合律者 - Those that sound in harmony with the musical scale, referring to the selection of wood for the lute.
    • 裁而胶缀 - Cut and glued together, describing the process of making the lute.
    • 殊绝 - Exceptional or extraordinary, emphasizing the quality of the two lutes.
    • 响泉 - Name of one of the lutes, metaphorically comparing the sound of the lute to the flow of a spring.
    • 韵磬 - Name of the other lute, metaphorically comparing the sound of the lute to the resonance of a bell.
    • 性不喜琴兼筝声 - He did not like the sound of the lute and the zither, indicating a personal preference.
    • 聪慧善琴，兼筝与歌 - Clever and skilled in playing the lute, as well as the zither and singing, describing the qualities of the two concubines.
    • 自撰琴谱 - Wrote his own lute score, indicating that the governor composed music.
    • 兵部员外郎 - An official title, equivalent to a junior officer in the Ministry of War.
    • 汧公之子也 - The son of the Governor of Xuanwu, identifying the son of the governor.
    • 雅度玄机 - Elegant and profound demeanor, describing the son's character.
    • 萧萧冲远 - Cool and detached, indicating a serene and distant personality.
    • 德行既优 - Excellent moral character, praising the son's virtues.
    • 山林之致 - The spirit of the mountains and forests, suggesting a love for nature and simplicity.
    • 琴道、酒德、诗调皆高绝 - The art of the lute, the virtue of wine, and the style of poetry were all exceptionally high, highlighting the son's talents.
    • 一生不近粉黛 - Never approached women, indicating chastity or celibacy.
    • 性喜接引人物 - Loved to meet and engage with people, showing a sociable nature.
    • 不好俗谈 - Did not like vulgar conversation, indicating a preference for refined discourse.
    • 晨起草裹头 - Wore a headscarf and dressed in simple clothes in the morning, describing a modest and simple lifestyle.
    • 对客蹙融 - Was always in a state of contentment when entertaining guests, indicating a hospitable and relaxed attitude.
    • 倾壶达旦 - Drank until dawn, describing a night of continuous drinking.
    • 不俟外宾 - Did not wait for guests, indicating a spontaneous and unrestrained nature.
    • 与璘先君同在浙西使府 - Was with the father of Lin (a name) in the governor's office in Zhe West, indicating a shared professional background.
    • 居处相接 - Lived in close proximity, describing their physical closeness.
    • 慕先君家行及诗韵 - Admired the father's conduct and poetic style, indicating respect and admiration.
    • 契分最深 - Had the deepest bond, describing a strong and intimate relationship.
    • 伯父高陵府君夫人韦氏 - The wife of the uncle's high-ranking official, Lady Wei, identifying a family connection.
    • 即兵部之姨妹也 - Was the aunt's younger sister, clarifying the relationship between the two families.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司徒 - Important official position in ancient China, often held by members of the aristocracy.
    • 镇宣武 - Xuanwu was likely a military or administrative region, possibly in northern China.
    • 琴书 - The lute and books were symbols of scholarly and artistic refinement in ancient China.
    • 响泉 - Evoke the natural beauty and harmony of music, reflecting the governor's appreciation for the arts.
    • 韵磬 - Evoke the natural beauty and harmony of music, reflecting the governor's appreciation for the arts.
    • 兵部员外郎 - Mid-level official in the Ministry of War, responsible for military administration.
    • 雅度玄机 - Sophisticated and refined personality, typical of the educated elite in ancient China.
    • 萧萧冲远 - Calm and detached demeanor, often associated with scholars and artists.
    • 山林之致 - Love for nature and a desire to live a simple, unadorned life, common among intellectuals of the time.
    • 琴道、酒德、诗调 - Three main pursuits of the literati: music, alcohol, and poetry, each considered a form of cultural refinement.
    • 倾壶达旦 - Night of continuous drinking, a common social activity among the literati, often associated with friendship and camaraderie.
    • 浙西使府 - Governor's office in Zhe West, likely in southern China, was an important administrative center.
    • 契分最深 - Deep and intimate relationship, often used to describe close friendships or familial bonds.
    • 伯父高陵府君夫人韦氏 - Wife of the uncle's high-ranking official, indicating a connection to the upper echelons of society.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李司徒 - The governor of Xuanwu.
    • 汧公 - Another title for the governor, possibly indicating a higher rank or a different period.
    • 秀奴 - The name of one of the governor's concubines.
    • 七七 - The name of the other governor's concubine.
    • 约 - The name of the governor's son.
    • 璘先君 - The father of Lin, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 浙西使府 - The governor's office in Zhe West.
    • 伯父高陵府君 - The uncle of the governor, a high-ranking official.
    • 韦氏 - The wife of the uncle, indicating a family connection.

    The Governor of Xuanwu, Li Si, in his leisure moments away from military affairs, found pleasure in playing the lute and reading books. He made his own lute by gathering old and new pieces of paulownia wood, cutting and gluing together those that sounded in harmony with the musical scale, and discarding the rest. Thus, he possessed two exceptional lutes, known as "Resonant Spring" and "Harmonious Bell." He did not like the sound of the lute and zither, but he did enjoy the performances of his two favorite concubines, Xiu Nu and Qi Qi, who were both clever and skilled in playing the lute, zither, and singing. He also wrote his own lute score. His son, Yuan Wai Lang of the Ministry of War, was the son of the Governor of Xuanwu. Although he was related to the son of the chancellor, he possessed an elegant and profound demeanor, a cool and detached personality, and excellent moral character. He also had the spirit of the mountains and forests. His skills in the art of the lute, the virtue of wine, and the style of poetry were all exceptionally high. He never approached women, loved to meet and engage with people, and did not like vulgar conversation. In the morning, he wore a headscarf and dressed in simple clothes, and when entertaining guests, he was always in a state of contentment, thus passing the day. He often collected ancient artifacts, and in Huzhou, he once obtained a piece of ancient iron that produced a clear and resonant sound when struck. He also kept a monkey named "Mountain Master" and would sometimes take it with him. On moonlit nights, he would sail on the river and climb Mount Jinsan, striking the iron and playing the lute, with the monkey always responding with its call. He would drink until dawn, without waiting for guests. He was with the father of Lin in the governor's office in Zhe West, living in close proximity, admiring the father's conduct and poetic style, and sharing the deepest bond. The wife of his uncle, a high-ranking official, Lady Wei, was the aunt's younger sister, thus connecting the two families. [20]

    余虽不及见，每闻长属说其风格容仪，真神仙也。又传闻汧公徐夫人虽生二子，中年于徐夫人琴瑟小乖，及兵部在母之后，情好加重。夫人情性益善于初。既得君于诸子之中，宝爱悬隔，天人降谪，信不诬矣。在官所得俸禄，付与従子，一不问数，惟给奉崔氏、元氏二孀姨，事事礼厚。元氏夫人有操行，祭酒弘农公既为传，此不复书。君初至金陵，于府主庶人锜坐，屡赞招隐寺标致。一日，庶人宴于寺中。明日谓君曰：“十郎尝夸招隐寺，昨游宴细看，何殊州中？”君笑曰：“某所赏者，疏野耳。若远山将翠幕遮，古松用彩物裹，腥膻涴鹿掊泉，音乐乱山鸟声，此则实不如在叔父大厅也。”庶人大笑。约天性唯嗜茶，能自煎。谓人曰：“茶须缓火炙，活火煎。”活火谓炭火之焰者也⊥至不限瓯数，竟日执持茶器不倦。曾奉使行至陕州硖石县东，爱渠水清流，旬日忘发。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 余雖不及見 - A phrase meaning 'I did not have the chance to see,' used to introduce the narrator's indirect knowledge.
    • 每聞長屬說其風格容儀 - Means 'I often heard from my elders about his style and appearance,' indicating a reliance on second-hand accounts.
    • 真神仙也 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'truly like a celestial being,' used to describe someone's exceptional qualities.
    • 中年于徐夫人琴瑟小乖 - Refers to a period in the middle of life when there were minor disagreements between the subject and his wife, *徐夫人*.
    • 及兵部在母之後，情好加重 - Means 'and when the subject was with his mother later, their affection deepened,' indicating a change in their relationship.
    • 夫人情性益善于初 - Means 'the wife's character and disposition became even better than before,' suggesting a positive transformation.
    • 既得君于诸子之中，宝爱悬隔 - Means 'having chosen him among her sons, she loved him greatly, with a difference that was like heaven and earth,' emphasizing the mother's special affection.
    • 天人降谪，信不诬矣 - Means 'the heavenly decree of demotion, indeed, is not false,' suggesting that the subject's fate was divinely ordained.
    • 在官所得俸禄，付与従子，一不问数 - Means 'the salary he received while in office, he entrusted to his younger brothers, without asking about the amount,' indicating his generosity and trust.
    • 惟给奉崔氏、元氏二孀姨，事事礼厚 - Means 'he only provided for the two widowed aunts, *崔氏* and *元氏*, with great respect and courtesy,' highlighting his filial piety.
    • 元氏夫人有操行，祭酒弘农公既为传，此不复书 - Means 'the *元氏* lady had virtuous conduct, and the *祭酒弘农公* has already written about her, so I will not repeat it here,' indicating a reference to a previous account.
    • 君初至金陵，于府主庶人锜坐，屡赞招隐寺标致 - Means 'when the subject first arrived in Jinling, while sitting with the head of the household, *庶人锜*, he often praised the beauty of *招隐寺*,' indicating his appreciation for the temple.
    • 一日，庶人宴于寺中 - Means 'one day, the head of the household held a banquet at the temple,' setting up a specific event.
    • 君笑曰：“某所赏者，疏野耳。” - Means 'the subject laughed and said, 'What I admire is its wild and rustic charm.'', indicating his personal preference.
    • 若远山将翠幕遮，古松用彩物裹，腥膻涴鹿掊泉，音乐乱山鸟声，此则实不如在叔父大厅也 - A metaphorical expression describing an idealized natural setting contrasted with a more refined, artificial one.
    • 庶人大笑 - Means 'the head of the household laughed heartily,' indicating a shared appreciation.
    • 约天性唯嗜茶，能自煎 - Means 'by nature, he only loved tea and could brew it himself,' indicating a personal passion.
    • 谓人曰：“茶须缓火炙，活火煎。” - Means 'he told people, 'Tea must be roasted over a gentle flame and brewed with a lively fire.'', providing a specific method.
    • 活火谓炭火之焰者也 - Means 'a lively fire refers to the flame of charcoal,' explaining the term.
    • 至不限瓯数，竟日执持茶器不倦 - Means 'to the point where he did not limit the number of cups and held the tea utensils all day without tiring,' indicating his dedication.
    • 曾奉使行至陕州硖石县东，爱渠水清流，旬日忘发 - Means 'he once served as an envoy and traveled to the east of Xiasi County in San Prefecture, loving the clear stream, and forgot to leave for ten days,' indicating his deep appreciation for nature.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 余 - The first-person pronoun used by the narrator.
    • 长属 - Refers to elders or older relatives.
    • 徐夫人 - The wife of the subject, whose name is *徐*.
    • 兵部 - Refers to the subject, who held a position in the *兵部* (Ministry of War).
    • 庶人锜 - The head of the household in Jinling, whose name is *锜*.
    • 招隐寺 - A temple in Jinling, known for its beauty.
    • 崔氏、元氏 - Two widowed aunts of the subject, whose names are *崔* and *元*.
    • 祭酒弘农公 - A respected figure who has written about the *元氏* lady.
    • 金陵 - The modern name for Nanjing, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 陕州硖石县 - A location in San Prefecture, known for its clear streams.

    Proper Nouns

    • 余 - The first-person pronoun used by the narrator.
    • 长属 - Refers to elders or older relatives.
    • 徐夫人 - The wife of the subject, whose name is *徐*.
    • 兵部 - Refers to the subject, who held a position in the *兵部* (Ministry of War).
    • 庶人锜 - The head of the household in Jinling, whose name is *锜*.
    • 招隐寺 - A temple in Jinling, known for its beauty.
    • 崔氏、元氏 - Two widowed aunts of the subject, whose names are *崔* and *元*.
    • 祭酒弘农公 - A respected figure who has written about the *元氏* lady.
    • 金陵 - The modern name for Nanjing, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 陕州硖石县 - A location in San Prefecture, known for its clear streams.

    Although I did not have the chance to see him, I often heard from my elders about his style and appearance, and they described him as truly like a celestial being. I also heard that Lady Xu, the wife of the Marquis of Qian, although she had two sons, experienced minor disagreements with her husband in middle age. However, when the subject was with his mother later, their affection deepened. Lady Xu's character and disposition became even better than before. Having chosen him among her sons, she loved him greatly, with a difference that was like heaven and earth. The heavenly decree of demotion, indeed, is not false. The salary he received while in office, he entrusted to his younger brothers, without asking about the amount, and he only provided for the two widowed aunts, Lady Cui and Lady Yuan, with great respect and courtesy. Lady Yuan had virtuous conduct, and the Sacrificial Master of Hongnong has already written about her, so I will not repeat it here. When the subject first arrived in Jinling, while sitting with the head of the household, Lord Qi, he often praised the beauty of Zhaoyin Temple. One day, Lord Qi held a banquet at the temple. The next day, he said to the subject, "You often praised Zhaoyin Temple. Yesterday, during the banquet, I looked at it closely, and it was no different from the state." The subject laughed and said, "What I admire is its wild and rustic charm. If the distant mountains were covered with green silk, the ancient pines wrapped in colorful objects, the stench of cooked meat polluted the spring, and music disturbed the sounds of the birds in the mountains, then it would indeed be inferior to the hall of my uncle." Lord Qi laughed heartily. By nature, he only loved tea and could brew it himself. He told people, "Tea must be roasted over a gentle flame and brewed with a lively fire." A lively fire refers to the flame of charcoal. To the point where he did not limit the number of cups and held the tea utensils all day without tiring. He once served as an envoy and traveled to the east of Xiasi County in San Prefecture, loving the clear stream, and forgot to leave for ten days. [21]

    张弘靖三世掌书命，在台座，前代未有。杨巨源赠公诗云：“伊陟无闻祖，韦贤不到孙。”时称其能与张家说家门。巨源在元和中，诗韵不为新语，体律务实，功夫颇深。自旦至暮，吟咏不辍。
    　　裴晋公为门下侍郎，过吏部选人官。谓同过给事中曰：“吾徒侥幸至多，此辈优与一资半级，何足问也一皆注定，未曾限量。”公不信术数，不好服食，每语人曰：“鸡猪鱼蒜，逢著则吃。生老病死，时至则生。”其器抱弘达，皆此类。
    　　沈吏部传师，性不流不矫，待物以和。观察三方，皆脂膏之地，去镇无余蓄。京城居处隘陋，不加一椽，所辟宾僚，无非名士。身没之后，家至贫苦，二子继业，并致时名，又以报施不妄。公先君礼部员外郎既济撰《建中实录》。体裁精简，虽宋、韩、范、裴亦不能过，自此之后，无有比者。公继世为史官，及出镇湖南、江西，奉诏在镇修《宪宗实录》当时荣之。 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三世掌书命 - Indicates that Zhang Hongjing held the position of chancellor for three consecutive generations, a rare occurrence in Chinese history.
    • 前代未有 - Means 'unprecedented in previous dynasties,' emphasizing the uniqueness of Zhang's achievement.
    • 杨巨源赠公诗云 - Indicates that Yang Juyuan wrote a poem in praise of Zhang Hongjing.
    • 伊陟无闻祖，韦贤不到孙 - A poetic couplet where '伊陟' (Yizhi) and '韦贤' (Wei Xian) are historical figures, and the phrase suggests that Zhang's achievements surpassed those of his ancestors and descendants.
    • 时称其能与张家说家门 - Means 'at the time, it was said that he could speak of the Zhang family's lineage,' highlighting Zhang's ability to articulate his family's history and achievements.
    • 元和中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (806-820 AD).
    • 诗韵不为新语 - Means 'his poetry did not use new or fashionable language,' indicating a conservative approach to poetic style.
    • 体律务实 - Means 'his poetry was practical and grounded in form,' suggesting a focus on structure and content over innovation.
    • 功夫颇深 - Means 'he had deep expertise,' indicating a high level of skill and dedication.
    • 吟咏不辍 - Means 'he recited poetry without stopping,' describing his dedication to the craft.
    • 裴晋公为门下侍郎 - Refers to Pei Ji, who served as a high-ranking official in the Tang court.
    • 过吏部选人官 - Means 'passed by the Ministry of Personnel during the selection of officials.'
    • 吾徒侥幸至多 - Means 'we have had many lucky breaks,' indicating a sense of fortune in their careers.
    • 此辈优与一资半级 - Means 'these people should be given a slight promotion,' suggesting a modest reward for their service.
    • 何足问也 - Means 'what is there to ask about,' indicating a dismissive attitude towards minor matters.
    • 皆注定 - Means 'everything is predetermined,' suggesting a belief in fate.
    • 未曾限量 - Means 'nothing was limited,' indicating a sense of boundless potential.
    • 不信术数 - Means 'he did not believe in fortune-telling or divination,' indicating a skepticism towards such practices.
    • 不好服食 - Means 'he did not like taking elixirs,' indicating a disinterest in alchemy and longevity practices.
    • 鸡猪鱼蒜，逢著则吃 - A colloquial expression meaning 'chicken, pig, fish, and garlic, eat whatever you come across,' suggesting a simple and practical approach to life.
    • 生老病死，时至则生 - A philosophical statement meaning 'birth, aging, sickness, and death, when they come, they come,' indicating an acceptance of life's natural cycles.
    • 器抱弘达 - Means 'his character was broad and open,' suggesting a generous and inclusive personality.
    • 沈吏部传师 - Refers to Shen Lishibu, who served as a high-ranking official in the Tang court.
    • 性不流不矫 - Means 'his nature was neither fickle nor artificial,' indicating a balanced and authentic personality.
    • 待物以和 - Means 'he treated things with harmony,' suggesting a peaceful and harmonious approach to life.
    • 观察三方 - Means 'observed three regions,' indicating his administrative responsibilities.
    • 脂膏之地 - Means 'fertile lands,' suggesting areas rich in resources.
    • 去镇无余蓄 - Means 'left the region with nothing saved,' indicating a lack of personal gain.
    • 京城居处隘陋 - Means 'his residence in the capital was narrow and humble,' suggesting a modest lifestyle.
    • 不加一椽 - Means 'he did not add even one beam,' indicating a lack of improvement to his living conditions.
    • 所辟宾僚 - Means 'the officials he recruited,' suggesting his ability to attract talented individuals.
    • 无非名士 - Means 'all of them were famous scholars,' indicating the high caliber of his associates.
    • 身没之后 - Means 'after his death,' indicating a posthumous assessment.
    • 家至贫苦 - Means 'his family became very poor,' suggesting financial hardship.
    • 二子继业 - Means 'his two sons continued his career,' indicating the continuation of his legacy.
    • 并致时名 - Means 'they both gained fame,' suggesting the success of his sons.
    • 又以报施不妄 - Means 'and their good deeds were not in vain,' suggesting a belief in the rewards of virtue.
    • 公先君礼部员外郎既济撰《建中实录》 - Refers to Zhang Hongjing's father, who wrote the 'Jianzhong Chronicle.'
    • 体裁精简 - Means 'the style was concise and clear,' indicating a well-structured and efficient writing style.
    • 虽宋、韩、范、裴亦不能过 - Means 'even Song, Han, Fan, and Pei could not surpass it,' indicating the high regard for the work.
    • 自此之后，无有比者 - Means 'after this, there was no equal,' suggesting the unparalleled quality of the work.
    • 公继世为史官 - Means 'he continued his father's role as a historian,' indicating a family tradition in historical writing.
    • 及出镇湖南、江西 - Means 'and when he was appointed governor of Hunan and Jiangxi,' indicating his administrative roles.
    • 奉诏在镇修《宪宗实录》 - Means 'he was ordered to compile the 'Xianzong Chronicle' while in office,' indicating his official duties.
    • 当时荣之 - Means 'he was honored at the time,' indicating the respect he received.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张弘靖 - A prominent Tang dynasty chancellor known for his administrative and literary achievements.
    • 杨巨源 - A contemporary poet who wrote in praise of Zhang Hongjing.
    • 伊陟 - A historical figure from the Shang dynasty, known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 韦贤 - A historical figure from the Western Han dynasty, known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 裴晋公 - Pei Ji, a high-ranking official in the Tang court, known for his administrative skills.
    • 沈吏部传师 - Shen Lishibu, a high-ranking official in the Tang court, known for his administrative skills.
    • 宋、韩、范、裴 - Refers to Song Yu, Han Yu, Fan Zhongyan, and Pei Ji, all notable figures in Chinese history.
    • 建中实录 - A historical chronicle written by Zhang Hongjing's father.
    • 宪宗实录 - A historical chronicle compiled by Zhang Hongjing during his tenure as governor of Hunan and Jiangxi.

    Zhang Hongjing held the position of chancellor for three consecutive generations, a feat unprecedented in previous dynasties. Yang Juyuan wrote a poem in praise of Zhang, saying: "Yizhi's ancestors were unknown, and Wei Xian's descendants were not as distinguished." At the time, it was said that Zhang could speak of the Zhang family's lineage. Yang Juyuan, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong, composed poetry without using new or fashionable language, focusing on practical and grounded forms, and he had deep expertise. He recited poetry from dawn till dusk without stopping.

    Pei Ji served as a high-ranking official in the Tang court and passed by the Ministry of Personnel during the selection of officials. He told his fellow officials: "We have had many lucky breaks. These people should be given a slight promotion, but what is there to ask about? Everything is predetermined, and nothing was limited." Pei Ji did not believe in fortune-telling or divination and did not like taking elixirs. He often said to others: "Chicken, pig, fish, and garlic, eat whatever you come across. Birth, aging, sickness, and death, when they come, they come." His character was broad and open, and he exemplified such qualities.

    Shen Lishibu, a high-ranking official in the Tang court, had a nature that was neither fickle nor artificial. He treated things with harmony. He observed three regions, all of which were fertile lands, but left them with nothing saved. His residence in the capital was narrow and humble, and he did not add even one beam. The officials he recruited were all famous scholars. After his death, his family became very poor, and his two sons continued his career, both gaining fame. Their good deeds were not in vain. Zhang Hongjing's father, who served as an external official of the Ministry of Rites, wrote the "Jianzhong Chronicle." The style was concise and clear, and even Song, Han, Fan, and Pei could not surpass it. After this, there was no equal. Zhang continued his father's role as a historian and, when appointed governor of Hunan and Jiangxi, was ordered to compile the "Xianzong Chronicle" while in office. He was honored at the time. [22]

    刘敦儒事亲以孝闻。亲心绪不理，每鞭人见血，则一日悦畅。敦儒尝敛衣受杖，曾不变容。宪宗朝，旌表门闾。又赵郡李公道枢先夫人卢氏，性严，事亦类此。公名问已光，又在班列，往往宾客至门，值公方受杖责。
    　　柳元公初拜京兆尹，将赴府上，有神策军小将乘马不避，公于街中杖杀之。及因对扬，宪宗正色诘公专杀之状。公曰：“京兆尹，天下取则之地，臣初受陛下奖擢，军中偏裨，跃马冲过，此乃轻陛下典法，不独侮臣。臣杖无礼之人，不打神策军将。”上曰：“卿何不奏？”公曰：“臣只合决，不合奏。”上曰：“既死，合是何人奏？”公曰：“在街中，本街使金吾将军奏；若在坊内，则左右巡使奏。”上乃止。

    　　卷三 商部下 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 事親以孝聞 - Means 'known for filial piety towards one's parents.' The phrase '以孝聞' emphasizes the reputation gained through filial conduct.
    • 親心绪不理 - Literally means 'the parents' emotions are disordered,' but it likely refers to a mental or emotional condition, possibly a form of mental illness.
    • 每鞭人見血 - Means 'every time they beat someone until blood is drawn,' indicating a specific ritual or behavior that brings joy to the parents.
    • 曾不变容 - Means 'never changed his expression,' highlighting the stoic nature of the individual.
    • 旌表门闾 - Means 'officially honored and praised,' referring to the public recognition given to someone for their exemplary behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 劉敦儒 - A historical figure known for his filial piety.
    • 憲宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, a significant period in Tang dynasty history.
    • 趙郡李公道枢 - A historical figure from Zhao County, known for his filial piety and official career.
    • 先夫人盧氏 - The late wife of the family, Lu, who was known for her strict nature.
    • 京兆尹 - The highest administrative official in the capital city of Chang'an, responsible for maintaining order and implementing policies.
    • 神策軍 - The elite imperial guard during the Tang dynasty, known for its military prowess and close ties to the emperor.
    • 金吾將軍 - The commander of the Jinwu Guards, responsible for maintaining order in the capital.
    • 左右巡使 - Officials responsible for patrolling and maintaining order in different areas of the capital.

    Proper Nouns

    • 劉敦儒 - A historical figure known for his filial piety.
    • 憲宗朝 - The reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 趙郡李公道枢 - A historical figure from Zhao County.
    • 先夫人盧氏 - The late wife of the family, Lu.
    • 京兆尹 - The highest administrative official in the capital city of Chang'an.
    • 神策軍 - The elite imperial guard during the Tang dynasty.
    • 金吾將軍 - The commander of the Jinwu Guards.
    • 左右巡使 - Officials responsible for patrolling and maintaining order in different areas of the capital.

    Duanru was known for his filial piety towards his parents. His parents had a mental condition where they would become upset, and every time they beat someone until blood was drawn, they would be happy for a day. Duanru once received a beating while dressed in his official robes, but he never changed his expression. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong, he was officially honored and praised for his filial conduct. Another example is Li Daoxu of Zhao County, whose late wife, Lu, was known for her strict nature. She also exhibited similar behavior. Li Daoxu was already famous for his reputation and held a high position, but often when guests arrived at his door, they found him being beaten.

    When Liu Yuan first became the Governor of Jingzhao and was on his way to the government office, a low-ranking officer of the Shence Army rode a horse without yielding to him. Liu beat the officer to death in the street. When he reported this to the emperor, Emperor Xianzong sternly questioned him about his unauthorized killing. Liu replied, "The Governor of Jingzhao is the model for the entire country. I was just appointed by Your Majesty. A low-ranking officer of the Shence Army rode a horse and rushed past me, showing disrespect to Your Majesty's laws and not just insulting me. I beat an impolite person, not a Shence Army officer." The emperor asked, "Why didn't you report it to me?" Liu said, "I should have decided to punish him, not reported it." The emperor asked, "If he had died, who should have reported it?" Liu replied, "If it happened in the street, the street commissioner of the Jinwu General should report it; if it happened in a neighborhood, the left and right patrol commissioners should report it." The emperor then stopped questioning him.

    Volume Three, Section on Commerce and Business. [23]

    韩文公与孟东野友善。韩公文至高，孟长于五言，时号“孟诗韩笔”。元和中，后进师匠韩公，文体大变。又柳柳州宗元、李尚书翱、皇甫郎中湜、冯詹事定、祭酒杨公、余座主李公，皆以高文为诸生所宗，而韩、柳、皇甫、李公皆以引接后学为务。杨公尤深于奖善，遇得一句。终日在口，人以为癖，终不易初心。长庆以来，李封州甘为文至精，奖拔公心，亦类数公。甘出于李相国武都公门下，时以为得人。惜其命运湮厄，不得在抡鉴之地。又元和以来，词翰兼奇者，有柳柳州宗元、刘尚书禹锡及杨公。刘、杨二人，词翰之外，别精篇什。又张司业籍善歌行，李贺能为新乐府，当时言歌篇者，宗此二人。李相国程、王仆射起、白少傅居易兄弟、张舍人仲素为场中词赋之最，言程式者，宗此五人。伯仲昆弟，以史笔继业，家藏书最多者，苏少常景澈、堂弟尚书绦，诸家无比，而皆以清标雅范，为后来所重。少卿登第，与堂兄特并时，亦士林之美。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 與孟東野友善 - Indicates a close friendship between Han Yu and Meng Jiao.
    • 文至高 - Refers to Han Yu's high literary achievement.
    • 長於五言 - Indicates that Meng Jiao excelled in five-character verse.
    • 時號“孟詩韓筆” - Refers to the contemporaneous nickname for Meng Jiao as a poet and Han Yu as a writer.
    • 元和中 - Refers to the Yuanhe period (806–820) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 後進師匠韓公 - Indicates that later generations followed Han Yu as a model.
    • 文体大变 - Indicates a significant change in literary style.
    • 引接后学 - Means to guide and mentor young scholars.
    • 楊公尤深于奖善 - Indicates that Yang Gong was particularly skilled at encouraging good deeds.
    • 遇得一句，终日在口 - Means that whenever Yang Gong found a good phrase, he would repeat it all day.
    • 人以為癖，終不易初心 - Indicates that people considered this habit a quirk, but Yang Gong remained true to his original intent.
    • 長慶以來 - Refers to the Changqing period (821–824) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 為文至精 - Indicates that Li Gan's writing was very refined.
    • 獎拔公心 - Means to encourage and promote others with a public spirit.
    • 甘出于李相国武都公門下 - Indicates that Li Gan was a disciple of Li Weidu, a high-ranking official.
    • 時以為得人 - Means that people at the time considered Li Gan a valuable addition.
    • 惜其命运湮厄 - Indicates that Li Gan's fate was unfortunate.
    • 不得在抡鉴之地 - Means that Li Gan did not have the opportunity to serve in a position of selection and evaluation.
    • 詞翰兼奇 - Indicates that Liu Zongyuan and Li Gong were both skilled in both literature and calligraphy.
    • 別精篇什 - Means that Liu Gong and Yang Gong were particularly skilled in composing short poems.
    • 張司業籍善歌行 - Refers to Zhang Jie, who was skilled in composing long narrative poems.
    • 李賀能為新樂府 - Refers to Li He, who was skilled in composing new-style music poetry.
    • 當時言歌篇者，宗此二人 - Indicates that at the time, people who wrote long narrative poems followed Zhang Jie and Li He.
    • 以史筆繼業 - Means to continue the family tradition of historical writing.
    • 家藏書最多者 - Indicates that the Su family had the largest collection of books.
    • 諸家無比 - Means that no other families could compare.
    • 清標雅範 - Means having a high moral standard and elegant demeanor.
    • 為後來所重 - Indicates that they were highly respected by later generations.
    • 少卿登第 - Refers to the author's own success in the imperial examinations.
    • 與堂兄特並時 - Indicates that the author's older brother was also successful at the same time.
    • 士林之美 - Means a beautiful example in the scholarly community.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 韓文公 - A famous Tang Dynasty scholar, writer, and philosopher, known for his contributions to the revival of Confucianism.
    • 孟東野 - A famous Tang Dynasty poet, known for his five-character verse.
    • 元和 - The reign period of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (806–820).
    • 柳柳州宗元 - A famous Tang Dynasty writer, philosopher, and political figure, known for his prose and poetry.
    • 李尚書翱 - A prominent Tang Dynasty scholar and official, known for his philosophical works.
    • 皇甫郎中湜 - A famous Tang Dynasty calligrapher and writer, known for his cursive script.
    • 馮詹事定 - A Tang Dynasty scholar and official.
    • 祭酒楊公 - A high-ranking official, possibly referring to Yang Xingmi.
    • 余座主李公 - The author's mentor, a high-ranking official.
    • 長慶 - The reign period of Emperor Muzong of Tang (821–824).
    • 李封州甘 - A scholar and official, known for his refined writing.
    • 李相國武都公 - A high-ranking official, possibly referring to Li Deyu.
    • 張司業籍 - A Tang Dynasty poet, known for his long narrative poems.
    • 李賀 - A famous Tang Dynasty poet, known for his new-style music poetry.
    • 白少傅居易 - A famous Tang Dynasty poet, known for his realistic poetry and social criticism.
    • 張舍人仲素 - A high-ranking official, known for his literary compositions.
    • 蘇少常景澈 - A Tang Dynasty scholar and official, known for his extensive book collection.
    • 堂弟尚書綢 - The author's younger brother, a high-ranking official.

    Han Yu and Meng Jiao were close friends. Han Yu's writing was of the highest caliber, while Meng Jiao excelled in five-character verse. At the time, they were collectively known as "Meng's poetry and Han's prose." During the Yuanhe period, later generations followed Han Yu as a model, leading to a significant change in literary style. Liu Zongyuan, Li Ao, Huai Su, Feng Ding, Yang Gong, and my mentor Li Gong were all highly respected by students for their literary achievements. Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Huai Su, and Li Gong all dedicated themselves to guiding and mentoring young scholars. Yang Gong was particularly skilled at encouraging good deeds. Whenever he found a good phrase, he would repeat it all day, and people considered this habit a quirk, but he remained true to his original intent. Since the Changqing period, Li Gan's writing was very refined, and he promoted others with a public spirit, much like the others. Li Gan was a disciple of Li Weidu, and people at the time considered him a valuable addition. Unfortunately, Li Gan's fate was unfortunate, and he did not have the opportunity to serve in a position of selection and evaluation. Since the Yuanhe period, those skilled in both literature and calligraphy included Liu Zongyuan, Liu Gong, and Yang Gong. Liu Gong and Yang Gong were particularly skilled in composing short poems. Zhang Jie was skilled in composing long narrative poems, and Li He was skilled in composing new-style music poetry. At the time, people who wrote long narrative poems followed Zhang Jie and Li He. Li Cheng, Wang Qi, Bai Juyi, and Zhang Zongsu were the best in the field of literary composition. Their brothers and close relatives continued the family tradition of historical writing, and the Su family had the largest collection of books, with no other families able to compare. They were all highly respected by later generations for their high moral standards and elegant demeanor. I myself passed the imperial examinations and succeeded at the same time as my older brother, setting a beautiful example in the scholarly community. [24]

    广平程子齐昔范，未举进士日，著《程子中謩》三卷，韩文公一见大称叹。及赴举，言于主司曰：“程昔范不合在诸生之下。”当时下第，大振屈声。庾尚书承宣知贡举，程始登第，以试正字，従事泾原军。李太师逢吉在相位，见其书，特荐拜左拾遗。竟因李公之累，湮厄而没。其立身贞苦，能清谭乐善，士多附之。惜其位不至耳。与堂舅李信州虞，相知最深，交契至厚，有裴公夷直，皆士林之望也。
    　　胡尚书证，河中人。太傅天水昭公镇河中，尚书建节赴振武，备桑梓礼入谒，持刺称“百姓”。献昭公诗云：“诗书入京国，旌旗过乡关。”州里荣之。余宗侄橹，应进士时，著《乡籍》一篇，大夸河东人物之盛，皆实录也。同乡中，赵氏轩冕文儒最著，曾祖父、祖父世掌纶诰，橹昆弟五人进士及第，皆历台省。卢少傅弘宣、卢尚书简辞、弘正、简求皆其姑子也，时称“赵家出”。外家敬氏先世，亦出自河中，人物名望，皆谓至盛，橹著《乡籍》载之。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 未舉進士日 - Time before passing the imperial examination for Jinshi, a significant milestone in a scholar's career.
    • 大稱嘆 - Highly praised or greatly admired.
    • 下第 - Failing the imperial examination.
    • 大振屈聲 - Greatly raised the voice of protest or made a strong appeal for justice.
    • 従事泾原军 - Served as an officer in the army of Jingyuan.
    • 左拾遗 - An official position in the Tang dynasty, responsible for advising the emperor and correcting his mistakes.
    • 湮厄而没 - Perished in obscurity or died without recognition.
    • 立身贞苦 - Lived a life of integrity and hardship.
    • 清谭乐善 - Engaged in pure and pleasant discussions about good deeds.
    • 士多附之 - Many scholars supported him.
    • 惜其位不至耳 - It is regrettable that he did not achieve a higher position.
    • 堂舅李信州虞 - Maternal uncle, Li Xinzhou Yu.
    • 相知最深，交契至厚 - Had the deepest understanding and the thickest friendship.
    • 裴公夷直 - Pei Gong Yi Zhi, a notable figure in the scholarly community.
    • 河中人 - Someone from the region of Hedong.
    • 太傅天水昭公镇河中 - A high-ranking official, possibly a prince or a noble, who governed Hedong.
    • 建节赴振武 - Established a military commission and went to Zhenwu.
    • 桑梓礼入谒 - Traditional courtesy of a son visiting his parents' hometown.
    • 持刺称‘百姓’ - Held a visiting card and introduced himself as a commoner.
    • 献昭公诗 - Presented a poem to the governor.
    • 诗书入京国，旌旗过乡关 - Poetic line describing the poet's journey from his hometown to the capital, symbolizing his aspirations.
    • 余宗侄橹 - Author's nephew, Cheng Lu.
    • 著《乡籍》一篇 - Wrote an article titled 'Local Register.'
    • 大夸河东人物之盛 - Greatly praised the prosperity of Hedong's people.
    • 皆实录也 - All are true records.
    • 赵氏轩冕文儒最著 - Zhao family, known for their prominent scholars and officials.
    • 曾祖父、祖父世掌纶诰 - Great-grandfather and grandfather were both in charge of drafting imperial edicts.
    • 五人进士及第 - Five brothers passed the imperial examination for Jinshi.
    • 皆历台省 - All served in the central government.
    • 卢少傅弘宣、卢尚书简辞、弘正、简求 - Several high-ranking officials, possibly relatives or close associates.
    • 皆其姑子也 - All were his nephews.
    • 时称‘赵家出’ - Known as the 'Zhao family's offspring.'
    • 外家敬氏先世 - Author's mother's family, the Jing family.
    • 亦出自河中 - Also originated from Hedong.
    • 人物名望，皆谓至盛 - Reputation and status of the people were considered very high.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 进士 - The highest level of the imperial examination, indicating a high level of scholarly achievement.
    • 左拾遗 - A position in the Tang dynasty's imperial court, responsible for advising the emperor and correcting his mistakes.
    • 湮厄而没 - Common phrase used to describe the fate of talented individuals who died in obscurity.
    • 河中 - A historical region in northern China, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 天水昭公 - Title indicating a high-ranking official, possibly a prince or a noble, who governed Hedong.
    • 振武 - A military garrison in the Tang dynasty.
    • 乡籍 - Register of local notable figures, often used to highlight the achievements of a region.
    • 赵氏 - Prominent family in Chinese history, known for their contributions to literature, politics, and culture.
    • 卢氏 - Another prominent family, known for their officials and scholars.

    Proper Nouns

    • 程子齐昔范 - Cheng Zi Qi Xi, a historical figure.
    • 韩文公 - Han Yu, a famous Tang dynasty scholar and writer.
    • 庾尚书承宣 - Hu Shangshu Zheng, a high-ranking official.
    • 李太师逢吉 - Li Fengji, a high-ranking official.
    • 胡尚书证 - Hu Shangshu Zheng, a high-ranking official.
    • 太傅天水昭公 - High-ranking official, possibly a prince or a noble.
    • 余宗侄橹 - Cheng Lu, the author's nephew.
    • 赵氏 - Zhao family, known for their scholars and officials.
    • 卢少傅弘宣 - Lu Shao Fu Hongxuan, a high-ranking official.
    • 卢尚书简辞 - Lu Shangshu Jiandici, a high-ranking official.
    • 弘正 - Another high-ranking official.
    • 简求 - Another high-ranking official.
    • 敬氏 - Jing family, the author's mother's family.

    Cheng Zi Qi Xi of Guangping, before he took the Jinshi examination, wrote a three-volume work titled Cheng Zi Zhong Mo. Upon reading it, Han Yu, the renowned scholar, highly praised it. When Cheng Zi Qi Xi later took the examination, he told the examiners, "Cheng Zi Qi Xi should not be ranked below the other candidates." At that time, he failed the examination, but he made a strong appeal for justice. Later, when Hu Shangshu Zheng was in charge of the examination, Cheng Zi Qi Xi finally passed and was appointed as a test official, serving in the army of Jingyuan. When Li Fengji, the Grand Tutor, was in a high position, he saw Cheng Zi Qi Xi's work and specially recommended him for the position of Left Censor. Ultimately, due to Li Fengji's influence, Cheng Zi Qi Xi perished in obscurity. He lived a life of integrity and hardship, engaging in pure and pleasant discussions about good deeds, and many scholars supported him. It is regrettable that he did not achieve a higher position. He had the deepest understanding and the thickest friendship with his maternal uncle, Li Xinzhou Yu, and Pei Gong Yi Zhi, all of whom were respected figures in the scholarly community.

    Hu Shangshu Zheng was from Hedong. During the reign of the Prince of Tian Shui, who governed Hedong, Hu Shangshu Zheng established a military commission and went to Zhenwu, observing the traditional courtesy of a son visiting his parents' hometown. He held a visiting card and introduced himself as a commoner. He presented a poem to the governor, saying, "Poetry and books enter the capital, and flags pass through the hometown." The local people were proud of him. My nephew, Cheng Lu, when taking the Jinshi examination, wrote an article titled Local Register, greatly praising the prosperity of Hedong's people, all of which are true records. Among his fellow townspeople, the Zhao family was the most prominent, known for their scholars and officials. His great-grandfather and grandfather were both in charge of drafting imperial edicts. Cheng Lu and his four brothers all passed the Jinshi examination and served in the central government. Lu Shao Fu Hongxuan, Lu Shangshu Jiandici, Hongzheng, and Jianqiu were all his nephews, and they were known as the "Zhao family's offspring." The Jing family, the author's mother's family, also originated from Hedong, and their reputation and status were considered very high. Cheng Lu wrote about them in his Local Register. [25]

    杨仆射於陵在考功时，与李师稷及第。至其子相国嗣复知举，门生集候仆射，而李公在座，时人谓之杨家“上下门生”。
    　　李相公石，是庾尚书承书宣门生。不数年，李任魏博军，因奏事，特赐紫，而庾尚衣绯，人谓“李侍御将紫底绯上座主”。
    　　李相国武都公知贡举，门生多清秀俊茂，唐冲、薛庠、袁都辈，时谓之“玉笋”。
    　　元和中，柳柳州书，后生多师效，就中尤长于章草，为时所宝。湖湘以南，童稚悉学其书，颇有能者。长庆已来，柳尚书公权，又以博闻强识工书，不离近侍。柳氏言书者，近世有此二人。尚书与族孙璟，开成中，同在翰林，时称“大柳舍人”、“小柳舍人”。自祖父郎中芳以来，奕世以文学居清列。舍人在名场淹屈，及擢第首冠诸生，当年宏词登高科，十余年便掌纶诰，侍翰苑。性喜汲引后进，出其门者，名流大僚至多。以诚明待物，不妄然诺，士益附之。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 楊仆射於陵在考功时，与李师稷及第 - Historical event where Yang Yuqing served as the head of the imperial examination when he passed Li Shiji in the exams. '及第' means 'passed the exams' or 'became a successful candidate.'
    • 上下门生 - Refers to the hierarchical relationship between Yang Yuqing and his students, with '上' (upper) and '下' (lower) indicating the generational difference.
    • 庾尚书承书宣门生 - Indicates that Gu Shangshu was a student of Li Shiji, who later became a high-ranking official.
    • 李侍御将紫底绯上座主 - Describes a situation where Li Shiji, who had been granted a purple robe (紫), was of higher rank than Gu Shangshu, who still wore a red robe (绯). '上座主' refers to the teacher or mentor.
    • 玉笋 - Metaphor for a group of talented and promising young scholars, likened to young bamboo shoots.
    • 柳柳州书 - Refers to the calligraphy style of Liu Zongyuan, who was known for his influence on later generations of calligraphers.
    • 大柳舍人 - Refers to Li Shang, a prominent official and calligrapher.
    • 小柳舍人 - Refers to Li Jing, the grandson of Li Shang, also a calligrapher.
    • 出其门者，名流大僚至多 - Means that many famous officials and high-ranking individuals came from Li Shang's school.
    • 以诚明待物，不妄然诺，士益附之 - Describes Li Shang's character, emphasizing his honesty, clarity, and reliability, which attracted many followers.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 杨仆射於陵 - Yang Yuqing (楊於陵) was a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his role in the imperial examinations.
    • 李师稷 - Li Shiji (李師稷) was a scholar and official who passed the exams under Yang Yuqing.
    • 李相国嗣复 - Li Sifu (李嗣復) was a high-ranking official and the son of Li Shiji.
    • 庾承书 - Gu Shangshu (庾承書) was a student of Li Shiji and later became a high-ranking official.
    • 李相国武都公 - Li Wudu Gong (李武都公) was a high-ranking official and the head of the imperial examinations.
    • 柳柳州书 - Liu Zongyuan (柳宗元) was a renowned scholar, writer, and calligrapher during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 柳尚书公权 - Li Gongquan (李公權) was a prominent calligrapher and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 舍人 - Title given to officials in the imperial court, particularly those involved in drafting edicts and documents.

    Proper Nouns

    • 杨仆射於陵 - Yang Yuqing (楊於陵)
    • 李师稷 - Li Shiji (李師稷)
    • 李相国嗣复 - Li Sifu (李嗣復)
    • 庾承书 - Gu Shangshu (庾承書)
    • 李相国武都公 - Li Wudu Gong (李武都公)
    • 柳柳州书 - Liu Zongyuan (柳宗元)
    • 柳尚书公权 - Li Gongquan (李公權)
    • 舍人 - Title for officials in the imperial court

    During the time when Yang Yuqing served as the head of the imperial examinations, he passed Li Shiji in the exams. Later, when Yang Yuqing's son, Li Sifu, became the head of the examinations, a gathering of his students awaited him, and Li Shiji was present among them. At that time, people referred to the relationship between the Yang family and Li Shiji as "upper and lower students."

    Li Shiji, who was known as Li Shang, was a student of Gu Shangshu, the imperial secretary. Within a few years, Li Shiji was appointed to command the Hui-Bo army. Due to his successful performance in reporting to the court, he was specially granted a purple robe, while Gu Shangshu still wore a red robe. People at the time referred to this as "Li Shang, the imperial secretary, wearing a purple robe beneath the red robe of his mentor."

    Li Wudu Gong, another high-ranking official, was also in charge of the imperial examinations. His students were mostly talented and promising, including Tang Chong, Xue Xiang, and Yuan Du. They were collectively known as the "Young Bamboo Shoots."

    During the Yuanhe period, the calligraphy style of Liu Zongyuan, known as "Liu of Liuzhou," became widely popular among younger generations. Particularly, his expertise in the running script style was highly valued and treasured. Even in the southern regions of Lake and Xiang, children learned to write in his style, and many became proficient. Since the Changqing period, Li Gongquan, the imperial secretary, was renowned for his extensive knowledge, strong memory, and skill in calligraphy. He remained close to the emperor and was often in his service. The Liu family had two prominent calligraphers in recent times: Li Shang and Li Gongquan. They served together in the Hanlin Academy during the Kai Cheng period and were known as the "Great Liu Shashi" and the "Young Liu Shashi," respectively. The Liu family had a long tradition of scholarly excellence, with generations of members serving in high-ranking positions. Li Shang, despite facing initial setbacks in his career, eventually achieved great success. He topped the list of successful candidates in the imperial examinations and later became a high-ranking official. He was known for his integrity and honesty, which earned him the respect and loyalty of many scholars and officials. [26]

    开成三年，余忝列第。考官刑部员外郎纥干公，崔相国群门生也。公及第日，于相国新昌宅小厅中，集见座主。及为考官之前，假舍于相国故第，亦于此厅见门生焉。是年科目八人，六人继升朝序。鄙人蹇薄，晚方通籍。敕头孙河南谷，先于雁门公为丞。
    　　裴晋公平淮西后，宪宗赐玉带一条。公临薨，却进，使门人作表，皆不如意。公令子弟执笔，口占状曰：“内府之珍，先朝所赐。既不敢将归地下，又不合留向人间，谨却封进。”闻者叹其简切而不乱。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忝列第 - A humble expression meaning 'to be ranked among the successful candidates in the imperial examination.'
    • 通籍 - A metaphorical term for becoming a registered official.
    • 敕头 - The top-ranked candidate in the imperial examination.
    • 却进 - To return the gift.
    • 谨却封进 - A respectful phrase meaning 'carefully return and present it sealed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 开成三年 - The year 838 during the Tang Dynasty, when the imperial examination was held.
    • 考官 - The chief examiner of the imperial examination, a high-ranking official.
    • 座主 - The chief examiner, who is also referred to as the 'seat master.'
    • 裴晋公 - Pei Shufang, a prominent statesman and military leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang, known for his military campaigns and reforms.
    • 玉带 - A jade belt, a symbol of high rank and honor in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 内府 - The imperial treasury.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刑部员外郎 - A Langya (a type of official) of the Ministry of Justice.
    • 纥干公 - Hekan, the examiner.
    • 崔相国群 - Cui Qun, a former chancellor.
    • 孙河南谷 - Sun, the top-ranked candidate from Henan, who was a county magistrate.
    • 雁门公 - A reference to the author's own position as a county magistrate.
    • 门人 - Disciples or students.

    In the third year of Kai Cheng, I was humbly ranked among the successful candidates. The examiner, Langya of the Ministry of Justice named Hekan, was a disciple of Chancellor Cui Qun. On the day he was ranked, he met his chief examiner in a small hall at Chancellor Cui's new residence. Before becoming an examiner, he temporarily stayed at the former residence of the chancellor and also met his disciples in this hall. That year, eight candidates were ranked, and six of them subsequently advanced to official positions. I, being of humble background, only became an official late in life. The top-ranked candidate, Sun from Henan, was a county magistrate before me.

    After Pei Shufang pacified the Huai West region, Emperor Xianzong bestowed a jade belt upon him. As Hekan was on his deathbed, he returned the jade belt and asked his disciples to write a memorial, but none of them were satisfactory. He then had his disciples take out paper and pen, and dictated the memorial himself, saying: "The treasure from the imperial treasury, bestowed by the previous emperor. I dare not take it to the afterlife, nor should I leave it in the world. I respectfully return and present it sealed." Those who heard of it praised his concise and orderly manner. [27]

    晋公，贞元中，作《铸剑戟为农器赋》。其首云：“皇帝嗣位之十三载，寰海镜清，方隅砥平。驱域中尽归力穑，示天下不复用兵。”宪宗平荡宿寇，数致太平，正当元和十三年，而晋公以文儒作相，竟立殊勋，为章武佐命，观其辞赋气概，岂得无异日之事乎
    　　进士李为作《泪赋》，及轻、薄、暗、小四赋。李贺作《乐府》，多属意花草蜂蝶之间，二子竟不远大。文字之作，可以定相命之优劣矣。
    　　相国令狐公楚，自河阳征入，至阌乡，暴风，有裨将饲官马在逆旅，屋毁马毙。到京，公旋大拜。时魏义通以检校常侍，代镇三城，裨将当还，缘马死，惧帅之责，以状请一字为押。公援笔判曰：“厩焚鲁国，先师惟恐伤人；屋倒阌乡，常侍岂宜问马？” [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋公，贞元中，作《铸剑戟为农器赋》 - A high-ranking official during the reign of Emperor Dezong wrote an essay titled 'The Assignment on Forging Swords and Halberds into Farming Tools.'
    • 寰海镜清，方隅砥平 - Phrases describing the world as clear and stable.
    • 驱域中尽归力穑，示天下不复用兵 - Driving all within the realm to focus on farming, showing the world that there will be no more warfare.
    • 宪宗平荡宿寇，数致太平 - Emperor Xianzong pacified long-standing enemies and frequently achieved peace and prosperity.
    • 正当元和十三年 - Refers to the 13th year of the Yuanhe era, which was 818 AD.
    • 晋公以文儒作相，竟立殊勋，为章武佐命 - The Duke of Jin, a scholar-official, became chancellor and eventually established remarkable achievements, serving as an assistant to Emperor Xianzong.
    • 观其辞赋气概，岂得无异日之事乎 - Observing the style and grandeur of his writings, how could there not be extraordinary deeds in the future?
    • 进士李为作《泪赋》，及轻、薄、暗、小四赋 - A candidate for the imperial examinations named Li Wei wrote an essay about tears and four essays titled 'Light,' 'Thin,' 'Dark,' and 'Small.'
    • 李贺作《乐府》，多属意花草蜂蝶之间 - The poet Li He often focused on themes of flowers, grass, and butterflies in his collection of poems.
    • 二子竟不远大 - The two individuals did not achieve great things.
    • 文字之作，可以定相命之优劣矣 - Literary works can determine the quality of one's destiny.
    • 相国令狐公楚，自河阳征入 - Duke Linghu Chu, a high-ranking official, was summoned from Heyang.
    • 至阌乡，暴风，有裨将饲官马在逆旅，屋毁马毙 - He arrived at Wenxiang, where a strong wind destroyed a house and killed the official horses being fed by a subordinate officer at an inn.
    • 到京，公旋大拜 - He arrived in the capital and was soon promoted to a high position.
    • 时魏义通以检校常侍，代镇三城，裨将当还，缘马死，惧帅之责，以状请一字为押 - At that time, Wei Yitong was serving as a temporary chief of staff, in charge of three cities. A subordinate officer, fearing punishment for the death of the horses, wrote a report asking for a signature to confirm it.
    • 公援笔判曰 - The official wrote a judgment.
    • 厩焚鲁国，先师惟恐伤人；屋倒阌乡，常侍岂宜问马？ - The official's judgment drew a parallel to the *Analects*, where Confucius expressed concern for people over property, and concluded that the chief of staff should not be concerned about the horses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋公 - Refers to a high-ranking official during the reign of Emperor Dezong.
    • 贞元 - The reign title of Emperor Dezong.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 元和 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong.
    • 相国令狐公楚 - Duke Linghu Chu, a high-ranking official.
    • 河阳 - A city in Henan Province.
    • 阌乡 - A county in Henan Province.
    • 魏义通 - A high-ranking official.
    • 检校常侍 - A temporary chief of staff.
    • 三城 - Refers to three cities under Huiyitong's jurisdiction.
    • 厩焚鲁国 - A reference to the *Analects*, where Confucius expresses concern for people over property.
    • 先师 - Refers to Confucius.
    • 鲁国 - The state of Lu, where Confucius lived and taught.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋公 - A high-ranking official during the reign of Emperor Dezong.
    • 贞元 - The reign title of Emperor Dezong.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 元和 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong.
    • 相国令狐公楚 - Duke Linghu Chu, a high-ranking official.
    • 河阳 - A city in Henan Province.
    • 阌乡 - A county in Henan Province.
    • 魏义通 - A high-ranking official.
    • 检校常侍 - A temporary chief of staff.
    • 三城 - Refers to three cities under Huiyitong's jurisdiction.
    • 厩焚鲁国 - A reference to the *Analects*.
    • 先师 - Refers to Confucius.
    • 鲁国 - The state of Lu.

    The Duke of Jin, during the reign of Emperor Dezong (Zhenyuan era), composed the essay "The Assignment on Forging Swords and Halberds into Farming Tools." The opening lines read: "In the thirteenth year since the Emperor ascended the throne, the world is as clear as a mirror, and every corner is leveled. All within the realm are driven to focus on farming, showing the world that there will be no more warfare." Emperor Xianzong pacified long-standing enemies and frequently achieved peace and prosperity, particularly in the 13th year of the Yuanhe era. The Duke of Jin, a scholar-official, became chancellor and eventually established remarkable achievements, serving as an assistant to Emperor Xianzong. Observing the style and grandeur of his writings, how could there not be extraordinary deeds in the future?

    The candidate for the imperial examinations, Li Wei, composed the essay "The Assignment on Tears" and four other essays titled "Light," "Thin," "Dark," and "Small." Li He wrote the Yuefu collection, often focusing on themes of flowers, grass, and butterflies. Yet, these two individuals did not achieve great things. Literary works can determine the quality of one's destiny.

    Duke Linghu Chu, a high-ranking official, was summoned from Heyang and arrived at Wenxiang, where a strong wind destroyed the house and killed the official horses that a subordinate officer was feeding at an inn. Upon arriving in the capital, he was soon promoted to a high position. At that time, Wei Yitong was serving as a temporary chief of staff, in charge of three cities. The subordinate officer, fearing punishment from the commander due to the death of the horses, wrote a report asking for a signature to confirm it. The official wrote a judgment: "In the state of Lu, when stables were burned, the sage teacher was only concerned about harming people; in Wenxiang, when the house collapsed, the chief of staff should not be concerned about the horses." [28]

    新野庾倬，贞元初，为河南府兵曹。有寡姊在家。时洛中物价翔贵，难致口腹，庾常于公堂辍己馔以饷其姊。始言所爱小男，以饷之。同官初甚鄙笑，后知之，咸嘉叹。倬生简休。
    　　荥阳郑还古，少有俊才，嗜学，而天性孝友。初家清齐间，遇李师道渐阻王命，扶侍老亲归洛。与其弟自舁肩舆，晨暮奔迫，两肩皆疮。妻柳氏，仆射元公之女也，妇道克备。弟齐古，好博戏赌钱，还古帑藏中物，虽妻之赀玩，恣其所用，齐古得之辄尽。还古每出行，必封管钥付家人曰：“留待二十九郎偿博，勿使别为债息，为恶人所陷误也。”弟感其意，为之稍节。有堂弟浪迹好吹觱篥，投许昌军为健儿，还古使使召之，自与洗沐，同榻而寝。因致书所知之为方镇者，求补他职。姻族以此重之，而竟以刚躁，喜持论，不容于时，惜也。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 辍己馔以饷其姊 - Stopped his own food to offer to his sister; gave up his own meals to feed his sister.
    • 妇道克备 - Fully observed the duties of a wife; proper conduct as a wife.
    • 留待二十九郎偿博，勿使别为债息，为恶人所陷误也 - Keep it for the twenty-ninth son to repay gambling debts, do not let others fall into debt and be deceived by bad people; precautionary measure to prevent financial ruin.
    • 为之稍节 - Begun to moderate; started to control or reduce his behavior.
    • 不容于时 - Did not fit in with the times; socially isolated due to personality traits.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞元初 - Early years of the Zhenyuan era (785-805) during the Tang Dynasty; period of significant political and cultural development.
    • 河南府 - Henan Prefecture; important administrative region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 兵曹 - Military officer in charge of administrative duties; role in the Tang Dynasty bureaucracy.
    • 李师道 - Powerful regional warlord who often clashed with the central government; significant figure in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 许昌军 - Military unit stationed in Xuchang; part of the Tang Dynasty military structure.
    • 仆射 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, often serving as a chancellor; important political position.
    • 方镇 - Governors of military districts; held significant power and influence in the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 新野庾倬 - Gu Zhuo, a historical figure from Xinye; subject of the first story.
    • 荥阳郑还古 - Zheng Huangu, a historical figure from Xingyang; subject of the second story.
    • 李师道 - Powerful regional warlord; mentioned in the second story.
    • 仆射元公 - The former chancellor Yuan; high-ranking official mentioned in the second story.
    • 齐古 - A brother of Zheng Huangu, known for his gambling habits; mentioned in the second story.
    • 许昌军 - Military unit in Xuchang; mentioned in the second story.

    Gu Zhuo from Xinye, during the early years of the Zhenyuan era, served as a military officer in Henan Prefecture. He had an elderly sister at home. At that time, the prices of goods in Luoyang were soaring, making it difficult to provide food. Gu Zhuo often stopped his own meals to offer them to his sister. Initially, his colleagues mocked him greatly, but later, when they learned about his actions, they all praised and sighed. Gu Zhuo lived in Jianxiu.

    Zheng Huangu from Xingyang, young and talented, loved learning and was naturally filial and friendly. When he first lived in Qingzhou, he encountered Li Shidao gradually obstructing imperial orders. He supported his elderly parents and returned to Luoyang. He and his younger brother carried a sedan chair, rushing back and forth every morning and evening, and both of their shoulders were injured. His wife, Liu, was the daughter of the former chancellor Yuan and fully observed the duties of a wife. His brother Qigu loved gambling and betting money. Huangu's household treasures, even those belonging to his wife, were allowed to be used freely, and Qigu spent it all immediately. Whenever Huangu went out, he sealed the keys and entrusted them to family members, saying, "Keep it for the twenty-ninth son to repay gambling debts, do not let others fall into debt and be deceived by bad people." His brother was moved by his intention and began to moderate his behavior. His cousin, who wandered and loved playing the reed flute, joined the army in Xuchang as a soldier. Huangu sent someone to call him, washed and bathed him personally, and slept on the same bed with him. He then wrote letters to the governors he knew, asking for other positions. Relatives and friends valued him for this, but he was rigid and impetuous, liked to hold opinions, and did not fit in with the times, which is a pity. [29]

    刘司徒玄佐，滑州匡城人。尝出师经其本县，欲申桑梓之礼于令，令坚辞不敢当，玄佐叹恨久之。先是陈金帛数筐，将遗邑僚，以其愚懦而止。玄佐贵为相，其母月织绢一疋，以示不忘本。每观玄佐视事，见邑令走阶下，退必语玄佐：“吾向见长官白事卑敬，不觉恐悚。思乃父为吏本县，时常畏长官汗栗。今尔当厅据案待之，其何安焉？”因喻以朝廷恩寄之重，须务捐躯。故玄佐始终不失臣节。是时乡里姻旧，以地近，多投之，司徒不欲以私擢居将校之列，又难置于贱卒，尽署为将判官。此职例假绯衫银鱼袋，外示荣之，实处散冗。其类渐众，久之，有献启诉于公者。其一联云：“覆盆子落地变作赤烘，羊羔儿作声尽是没益。”公览之而笑，各改署他职。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 司徒 - High-ranking official title, often translated as 'Minister of Works' or 'Minister of Civil Affairs.'
    • 桑梓之礼 - Respect and gratitude owed to one's hometown and local officials.
    • 金帛 - Gold and silk, valuable items often used as gifts.
    • 邑僚 - Local officials or fellow townspeople in a position of authority.
    • 愚懦 - Foolish and timid, used to describe someone who is easily intimidated.
    • 月织绢一疋 - Weaves one bolt of silk every month, symbolizing humility and diligence.
    • 视事 - Handle official duties or preside over affairs.
    • 捐躯 - Sacrifice oneself or lay down one's life.
    • 臣节 - Loyalty and duty of a subject to the emperor.
    • 姻旧 - Relatives and old acquaintances.
    • 将校 - Military officers.
    • 贱卒 - Low-ranking soldiers or foot soldiers.
    • 将判官 - Low-ranking official or assistant.
    • 覆盆子落地变作赤烘 - Metaphorical expression, likely referring to something changing color or nature upon contact with the ground.
    • 羊羔儿作声尽是没益 - Metaphorical expression, likely referring to something producing no useful sound or effect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刘司徒玄佐 - Li Xuanzuo, a historical figure who served as a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 滑州匡城 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region in China.
    • 陈金帛数筐 - Refers to the practice of giving valuable gifts to local officials or officials from one's hometown.
    • 朝廷恩寄之重 - Refers to the heavy responsibility of serving the imperial court and the trust placed in officials by the emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘司徒玄佐 - Li Xuanzuo, a historical figure.
    • 滑州匡城 - A place name.
    • 朝廷 - The imperial court.
    • 将校 - Military officers.
    • 贱卒 - Low-ranking soldiers.
    • 将判官 - Low-ranking officials or assistants.

    Minister Li Xuanzuo, originally from Kuangcheng in Huazhou, once passed through his hometown while on a military campaign. He wished to show respect to the local officials, but they firmly declined, leaving him disappointed for a long time. Prior to this, he had prepared several baskets of gold and silk to gift to the local officials, but he stopped when he realized they were too timid. Although Li Xuanzuo rose to the rank of chancellor, his mother continued to weave one bolt of silk every month, symbolizing her humility and diligence. Whenever Li Xuanzuo handled official duties, he would see the local officials bowing to him, and upon returning home, he would tell his mother, "I see the officials bowing to me with great respect, and I feel a sense of unease. I recall my father, who served as an official in this county, often trembling in fear before higher officials. Now, I sit at the head of the table and await their reports—how can I feel at ease?" He then reminded himself of the heavy responsibility placed on him by the imperial court, urging him to be willing to sacrifice himself. Thus, Li Xuanzuo remained loyal throughout his career. At that time, many relatives and old acquaintances from his hometown, due to their proximity, sought his favor. Li Xuanzuo did not want to promote them to the ranks of military officers, nor did he want to place them among the low-ranking soldiers. Instead, he appointed them all as low-ranking officials known as jiangpan guan. These positions typically came with a red robe and a silver fish badge, outwardly signifying honor, but in reality, they were often idle and unimportant. As the number of such requests grew, eventually, someone submitted a petition complaining about the situation. One part of the petition read, "When a raspberry falls to the ground, it turns red; when a young lamb makes a sound, it is useless." Upon reading this, Li Xuanzuo laughed and reassigned them to other positions. [30]

    太子陆文学鸿渐名羽，其先不知何许人。竟陵龙兴寺僧，姓陆，于堤上得一初生儿，收育之，遂以陆为氏。及长，聪俊多能，学赡辞逸，诙谐纵辩，盖东方曼倩之俦。与余外祖户曹府君，交契深至。外祖有笺事状，陆君所撰。性嗜茶，始创煎茶法，至今鬻茶之家，陶为其像，置于炀器之间，云宜茶足利。余幼年尚记识一复州老僧，是陆僧弟子。常讽其歌云：“不羡黄金罍，不羡白玉杯，不羡朝入省，不羡暮入台。千羡万羡西江水，曾向竟陵城下来。”又有追感陆僧诗至多。 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 竟陵龙兴寺 - Longxing Temple in Jingling, a specific temple and location.
    • 及长，聪俊多能 - When he grew up, he was intelligent and talented, well-versed in learning and eloquent.
    • 不羡黄金罍 - I do not envy golden jars, expressing a disdain for material and political ambitions.
    • 不羡白玉杯 - I do not envy white jade cups, expressing a disdain for material and political ambitions.
    • 不羡朝入省 - I do not envy morning entry into the provincial office, expressing a disdain for material and political ambitions.
    • 不羡暮入台 - I do not envy evening entry into the imperial court, expressing a disdain for material and political ambitions.
    • 千羡万羡西江水 - A thousand times I envy the waters of the Xi River, expressing a deep appreciation for natural beauty.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太子 - Refers to the heir apparent, a high-ranking noble.
    • 陆文学鸿渐 - A literary name or honorific, suggesting a high level of education and respect.
    • 竟陵 - An ancient city in Hubei Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 东方曼倩 - A famous scholar and poet from the Han dynasty, known for his wit and literary talent.
    • 外祖户曹府君 - Refers to the author's maternal grandfather, who held a high official position.
    • 煎茶法 - The method of boiling tea, a significant contribution to Chinese tea culture.
    • 复州 - An ancient city in Hubei Province, where the old monk was from.
    • 西江水 - Refers to the Xi River, a major river in southern China, known for its scenic beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太子陆文学鸿渐名羽 - Prince Lu Wenxue Hongjian, named Yu.
    • 竟陵龙兴寺 - Longxing Temple in Jingling.
    • 外祖户曹府君 - The author's maternal grandfather, the Magistrate of the Household Department.
    • 复州 - An ancient city in Hubei Province.
    • 西江水 - The Xi River.

    Prince Lu Wenxue Hongjian, named Yu, had ancestors whose origins were unknown. A monk from Longxing Temple in Jingling, surnamed Lu, found a newborn baby on a dike, took care of it, and thus adopted the surname Lu. When he grew up, he was intelligent and talented, well-versed in learning and eloquent, witty and skilled in debate, comparable to Dongfang Manqian. He had a deep and close friendship with my maternal grandfather, the Magistrate of the Household Department. My maternal grandfather had a letter of recommendation, written by Mr. Lu.
