He had a fondness for tea and was the first to create the method of boiling tea. Today, in households that sell tea, they make clay figurines of him and place them among the tea utensils, saying they bring good fortune in tea. I still remember meeting an old monk from Fuzhou who was a disciple of the monk Lu. He often recited this song: "I do not envy golden jars, white jade cups, morning entry into the provincial office, or evening entry into the imperial court. A thousand times I envy the waters of the Xi River, which once flowed down to Jingling City." There are also many poems that mourn and commemorate the monk Lu. [31]

    崔吏部枢夫人，太尉西平王女也。西平生日，中堂大宴，方食，有小婢附崔氏妇耳语久之，崔氏妇颔之而去。有顷，复至，王问曰：“何事？”女对曰：“大家昨夜小不安适，使人往候。”王掷筋怒曰：“我不幸有此女，大奇事！汝为人妇，岂有阿家体候不安，不检校汤药，而与父作生日，吾有此女，何用作生日为？”遽遣走檐子归，身亦续至崔氏家问疾，且拜谢教训子女不至。姻族闻之，无不愧叹。故李夫人妇德克备，治家整肃，贵贱皆不许时世妆梳。勋臣之家，特数西平礼法。
    　　裴澥为陕府录事参军。李汧公勉除长史充观察，始至官，属吏谒讫。令别召裴录事，坐与之语，面约云：“少顷有宴，便请随判官同赴。”及宴，凡三召不至，公极怒。明晨召澥让之曰：“某忝公之官长，以素闻公名，兼朝中新友，话公美事，思接従容。故超礼分，面约赴燕，遂累召不来。 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 婦德克備 - Virtues of a wife were fully developed; indicates exemplary behavior.
    • 治家整肅 - Managed her household with strict discipline; describes her management style.
    • 身亦續至崔氏家問疾 - He also went to Lady Cui's home to inquire about her illness; shows personal involvement.
    • 拜謝教訓子女不至 - Apologized and thanked for the lack of proper instruction in raising children; indicates a formal apology and expression of gratitude.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 崔吏部樞 - Cui the Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel; indicates a high-ranking official.
    • 太尉西平王 - The Duke of Xiping, who held the title of Taifu (Grand Commandant); indicates a noble and powerful figure.
    • 中堂 - The central hall, often used for important gatherings and banquets.
    • 小婢 - A young maid, typically a servant in a noble household.
    • 大家 - A respectful term for a noble or high-ranking person.
    • 檐子 - A sedan chair, used for transportation, especially for women.
    • 姻族 - Relatives and friends, indicating the broader social network.
    • 婦德 - Virtues of a wife, including moral and ethical conduct.
    • 勳臣 - Meritorious officials, those who have served the state with distinction.

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔吏部樞 - Cui the Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel.
    • 太尉西平王 - The Duke of Xiping.
    • 李夫人 - Lady Li.
    • 裴澥 - Pei Xia.
    • 陝府錄事參軍 - Recorder of the Ministry of Personnel in Shaanxi.
    • 李汧公勉 - Li Qian Gong Mian.
    • 長史 - Chief Secretary.
    • 觀察 - Inspector.

    Lady Cui, the wife of Cui the Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel, was the daughter of the Duke of Xiping. On the Duke's birthday, there was a grand banquet in the central hall. During the meal, a young maid whispered to Lady Cui for a long time, and she nodded and left. Shortly after, she returned, and the Duke asked, "What was the matter?" She replied, "Your highness was slightly unwell last night, and someone went to inquire." The Duke angrily threw down his chopsticks and said, "It is a great misfortune that I have such a daughter! As a wife, how can you neglect to check on your mother-in-law's condition and not bring medicine, while attending a birthday celebration for your father? If I have such a daughter, what is the point of celebrating my birthday?" He immediately sent the sedan chair back and went to Lady Cui's home to inquire about her illness, apologizing and thanking her for the lack of proper instruction in raising children. All relatives and friends heard about it and were both ashamed and moved. Thus, Lady Li's virtues as a wife were fully developed, and she managed her household with strict discipline. Neither the noble nor the common people were allowed to follow fashionable hairstyles and clothing. Among the families of meritorious officials, the Xiping family was especially noted for its propriety and etiquette.

    Pei Xia served as the Recorder of the Ministry of Personnel in Shaanxi. Li Qian Gong Mian was appointed as the Chief Secretary and Inspector. Upon his arrival at the office, he had all the officials pay their respects. He then ordered Pei Xia to be called separately and sat down to speak with him, making a face-to-face agreement: "In a short while, there will be a banquet. Please attend together with the Chief Secretary." When the banquet began, Pei Xia was summoned three times but did not come. Li Qian Gong Mian was extremely angry. The next morning, he called Pei Xia and rebuked him, saying, "I am honored to serve as your official superior, and I have heard of your name. Moreover, we are new friends in court, and I have spoken about your good deeds. I thought we could meet informally. Therefore, I went beyond the usual etiquette and personally invited you to the banquet, but you did not come despite repeated summonses." [32]

    何相忽之甚也？”澥正色言曰：“中丞细思之，未知谁失必也正名，各司其局，古人所守，某敢忘之中丞使府，自有宾僚，某走吏也，安得同之？”汧公曰：“老夫过矣，请吾子归所止。”澥既退，汧公遽命驾访之，拜请置在宾席。澥之子充为太常寺太祝，年甚少。时京司书考官之清高者，例得上考。充之同侪以例，皆止中考，诉于卿长，曰：“此旧例也。”充曰：“奉常职重地高，不同他寺。大卿在具瞻之地，作事当出于人。本设考课，为奖勤劳，则书岂系于官秩。若一一以官高下为优劣，则卿合书上上考，少卿合上中考，丞合中上考，主簿合中考，协律合下考，某等合吃杖矣！”卿笑且惭，遂特书上考。 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 何相忽之甚也 - Rhetorical question meaning 'How could you treat me so carelessly?' or 'How could you be so neglectful of me?'
    • 正色言曰 - Means 'spoke with a serious expression' or 'spoke sternly.'
    • 必也正名，各司其局 - Phrase from Confucian thought, meaning 'to correct names' or 'to clarify the proper names and roles.'
    • 走吏也，安得同之 - Means 'a mere clerk' or 'a low-ranking official'; rhetorical question emphasizing low status.
    • 老夫过矣，请吾子归所止 - Formal apology and request, showing the humility of the speaker.
    • 遽命驾访之，拜请置在宾席 - Describes a series of actions, showing the change in the speaker's status.
    • 年甚少 - Means 'very young' or 'quite young.'
    • 京司书考官之清高者 - Means 'officials of the capital who are responsible for evaluating the performance of others and are considered high-ranking and respected.'
    • 例得上考，例止中考 - Means 'by custom, they should receive the highest evaluation'; 'by custom, they only received a middle evaluation.'
    • 具瞻之地 - Means 'a place of public scrutiny' or 'a position where one is subject to public opinion.'
    • 作事当出于人 - Means 'actions should be exemplary and lead others.'
    • 书岂系于官秩 - Means 'evaluations should not be tied to rank' or 'performance should not be determined by rank.'
    • 若一一以官高下为优劣 - Means 'if evaluations were based solely on rank, then...'
    • 少卿合上中考，丞合中上考，主簿合中考，协律合下考 - Describes hypothetical evaluations based on rank.
    • 某等合吃杖矣 - Means 'we would deserve to be beaten' or 'we would deserve punishment.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中丞 - Refers to a high-ranking official, specifically the Censor-in-Chief, responsible for overseeing officials and maintaining moral standards.
    • 汧公 - Refers to a noble or high-ranking official, likely a prince or a high-ranking minister.
    • 奉常 - Refers to the Minister of Rites, responsible for religious and ceremonial affairs.
    • 少卿 - Refers to the Deputy Minister of Rites.
    • 丞 - Refers to the head of a department or bureau.
    • 主簿 - Refers to a clerk or secretary responsible for administrative tasks.
    • 协律 - Refers to a music official responsible for musical matters.
    • 太常寺 - Refers to the Temple of the Supreme Ancestor, a central institution responsible for religious and ceremonial functions.
    • 太祝 - Refers to a junior official in the Temple of the Supreme Ancestor, responsible for ritual and ceremonial duties.
    • 上考、中考、下考 - Refers to the evaluation system used in the imperial bureaucracy, where officials are evaluated and ranked based on their performance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中丞 - The Censor-in-Chief.
    • 汧公 - A high-ranking official, possibly a prince or a high-ranking minister.
    • 奉常 - The Minister of Rites.
    • 少卿 - The Deputy Minister of Rites.
    • 丞 - The head of a department or bureau.
    • 主簿 - A clerk or secretary.
    • 协律 - A music official.
    • 太常寺 - The Temple of the Supreme Ancestor.
    • 太祝 - A junior official in the Temple of the Supreme Ancestor.

    How could you treat me so carelessly?" Hua said sternly, "The Censor-in-Chief should reflect carefully. I do not know who has erred, but it is necessary to correct names and roles, each taking charge of their own domain, as the ancients taught. I dare not forget this. In your office, there are already guests and officials, I am merely a low-ranking clerk, how could I be treated equally?" The Prince of Qian said, "I have indeed been mistaken. Please return to your place." After Hua withdrew, the Prince of Qian immediately ordered his carriage to visit him, bowed and asked to be seated at the guest table. Hua's son, Chong, was a junior official in the Temple of the Supreme Ancestor, very young. At the time, high-ranking officials in the capital who were responsible for evaluating the performance of others and were considered respected, by custom, should receive the highest evaluation. Chong's peers, by custom, only received a middle evaluation. They complained to the head of the department, saying, "This is an old custom." Chong said, "The Minister of Rites holds a high and important position, different from other departments. The Deputy Minister of Rites is in a place of public scrutiny, and his actions should set an example. The purpose of the evaluation system is to reward diligence, so evaluations should not be tied to rank. If evaluations were based solely on rank, then the Minister of Rites should receive an upper-middle evaluation, the Deputy Minister of Rites should receive a middle-high evaluation, the head of the department should receive a middle-high evaluation, the clerk should receive a middle evaluation, the music official should receive a low evaluation, and we would deserve to be beaten!" The head of the department laughed and felt ashamed, and thus specially gave Chong an upper evaluation. [33]

    崔相国群为华州刺史。郑县陆镇以名与崔公近讳音同，请假。崔视事后，遍问官属，怪镇不在列，左右以回避对。公曰：“县尉旨授官也，不可以刺史私避，而使之罢不治事。”召之令出。镇因陈牒，请权改名瑱。公判准状，仍戒之曰：“公庭可以従权，簿书则当仍旧，台省中无陆瑱名也。”其知大体如此。
    　　柳元公善张尚书正甫。元公之子仲郢，尝遇张于途，去盖下马而拜，张止之不获。他日张言于元公曰：“寿郎相逢，其谦太过。”元公作色不应。久之，张起去。元公谓客曰：“张正甫与公绰往还，欲使儿于街中骑马冲公绰耶此人亦不足与语。”张闻之，拜谢。元公为西川従事，尝纳一姬，同院知之。或征出其妓者，言之数四，元公曰：“士有一妻一妾，以主中馈，备洒扫。公绰买妾，非妓也。” [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 崔相国群为华州刺史 - Cui Qun, the prime minister, served as the governor of Huazhou.
    • 郑县陆镇以名与崔公近讳音同，请假 - Lu Zhen, the magistrate of Zheng County, requested leave because his name sounded similar to that of Cui Gong, which was considered disrespectful.
    • 崔视事后，遍问官属，怪镇不在列 - Cui Gong inspected his office and questioned his subordinates about why Lu Zhen was absent.
    • 县尉旨授官也，不可以刺史私避，而使之罢不治事 - The county magistrate's position is an official appointment and should not be avoided privately by the governor, leading to neglect of duties.
    • 召之令出 - Summon Lu Zhen and order him to return to his post.
    • 镇因陈牒，请权改名瑱 - Lu Zhen submitted a petition asking to temporarily change his name to Tian.
    • 公判准状，仍戒之曰 - Cui Gong approved the request and warned him.
    • 公庭可以従权，簿书则当仍旧，台省中无陆瑱名也 - In public settings, it is permissible to use a temporary name, but official records should retain the original name, as there is no record of Lu Tian in the central government.
    • 柳元公善张尚书正甫 - Liu Yuan Gong was friendly with Zhang Zheng Fu, who held the position of Shangshu (Minister).
    • 元公之子仲郢，尝遇张于途，去盖下马而拜，张止之不获 - Liu Yuan Gong's son, Zhong Ying, met Zhang Zheng Fu on the road and dismounted to bow, but Zhang could not stop him.
    • 他日张言于元公曰 - On another day, Zhang spoke to Liu Yuan Gong about the incident.
    • 寿郎相逢，其谦太过 - Zhong Ying was too humble when he met Zhang.
    • 元公作色不应 - Liu Yuan Gong responded angrily and silently.
    • 张起去 - Zhang stood up and left.
    • 张正甫与公绰往还，欲使儿于街中骑马冲公绰耶此人亦不足与语 - A sarcastic remark by Liu Yuan Gong, implying that Zhang wanted his son to ride a horse and collide with Gong Chuo, and that Zhang is not worth talking to.
    • 张闻之，拜谢 - Zhang heard the remark and bowed to apologize.
    • 元公为西川从事，尝纳一姬，同院知之 - Liu Yuan Gong, while serving as an official in Western Sichuan, had taken a concubine, and his colleagues knew about it.
    • 或征出其妓者，言之数四，元公曰 - Some colleagues repeatedly asked him to bring out the concubine, and Liu Yuan Gong responded.
    • 士有一妻一妾，以主中馈，备洒扫 - A man should have one wife and one concubine to manage household affairs and clean the house.
    • 公绰买妾，非妓也 - Gong Chuo bought a concubine, not a prostitute.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 崔相国群 - Cui Qun, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 华州 - A prefecture in Shaanxi Province.
    • 郑县 - A county in Huazhou.
    • 柳元公 - Liu Yuan Gong, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张尚书正甫 - Zhang Zheng Fu, a minister during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 西川 - A region in Sichuan Province.
    • 姬 - A concubine, a secondary wife in ancient China.
    • 中馈 - Refers to the management of household affairs.
    • 洒扫 - Refers to cleaning and maintaining the household.
    • 公绰 - Gong Chuo, a historical figure known for his integrity and principles.

    Proper Nouns

    • 崔相国群 - Cui Qun, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 华州 - A prefecture in Shaanxi Province.
    • 郑县 - A county in Huazhou.
    • 柳元公 - Liu Yuan Gong, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 张尚书正甫 - Zhang Zheng Fu, a minister during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 西川 - A region in Sichuan Province.
    • 公绰 - Gong Chuo, a historical figure known for his integrity and principles.

    Cui Qun, the prime minister, served as the governor of Huazhou. Lu Zhen, the magistrate of Zheng County, requested leave because his name sounded similar to that of Cui Gong, which was considered disrespectful. After inspecting his office, Cui Gong questioned his subordinates and was surprised that Lu Zhen was absent. His attendants explained that Lu Zhen had requested leave to avoid the name. Cui Gong said, "The position of county magistrate is an official appointment and should not be avoided privately by the governor, leading to neglect of duties." He summoned Lu Zhen and ordered him to return to his post. Lu Zhen submitted a petition asking to temporarily change his name to Tian. Cui Gong approved the request and warned him, saying, "In public settings, it is permissible to use a temporary name, but official records should retain the original name, as there is no record of Lu Tian in the central government." Thus, Cui Gong demonstrated his understanding of the broader principles.

    Liu Yuan Gong was friendly with Zhang Zheng Fu, who held the position of Shangshu (Minister). Liu Yuan Gong's son, Zhong Ying, met Zhang on the road and dismounted to bow, but Zhang could not stop him. On another day, Zhang spoke to Liu Yuan Gong about the incident, saying that Zhong Ying was too humble when he met Zhang. Liu Yuan Gong responded angrily and silently, and Zhang stood up and left. Liu Yuan Gong sarcastically remarked, "Zhang Zheng Fu and Gong Chuo are close friends; does he want his son to ride a horse and collide with Gong Chuo? This person is not worth talking to." Zhang heard the remark and bowed to apologize.

    While serving as an official in Western Sichuan, Liu Yuan Gong had taken a concubine, and his colleagues knew about it. Some colleagues repeatedly asked him to bring out the concubine, and Liu Yuan Gong responded, "A man should have one wife and one concubine to manage household affairs and clean the house. Gong Chuo bought a concubine, not a prostitute." [34]

    范陌卢仲元，家于寿之安丰。其妻清河崔氏，率更令谦侄女也。崔氏兄即，有薄田百亩，在洛城之东，守道力田以自给，未尝干人。常躬耕得金一瓶，计百两，不言于人，密埋于居室内。临终，其妻李氏，以家贫子幼，身后冻馁为忧。崔屏人，语妻以埋金之事，指其记处。戒云：“慎勿言于人，他日卢郎中来，可告也。”未几，卢赴调，经洛中，吊崔氏之孤讫。李使婢传语曰：“新妇有哀迫之事，须面见姑夫。”卢许之。既见，乃述亡夫之意。卢悲泣久之，曰：“惟嫂之命。”李氏仍密遣所使之谨厚者，持金付之。卢遂罢选，持金鬻于扬州。时遇金贵，两获八千。复市南货入洛，为崔孤置田宅，兼为剖分家事，既毕而归。逾年方选。竟未尝言于人，惟密亲有知者。卢君生既字子严，清望重器，为世名臣，信阴德之报也。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 守道力田以自给 - Means 'worked diligently in the fields to sustain himself,' emphasizing self-reliance.
    • 信阴德之报也 - Means 'it is indeed a reward for his hidden virtue,' indicating moral belief.
    • 屏人 - Means 'sent away everyone,' indicating privacy.
    • 慎勿言于人 - Means 'be careful not to tell anyone,' reinforcing secrecy.
    • 临终 - Means 'on his deathbed,' indicating the end of his life.
    • 身后冻馁为忧 - Means 'worried about freezing and starving after death,' expressing concern for his family's future.
    • 不言于人 - Means 'never sought help from others,' highlighting his independence.
    • 密埋于居室内 - Means 'secretly buried inside the house,' suggesting a hidden action.
    • 惟嫂之命 - Means 'only as my elder sister commands,' showing respect.
    • 密遣所使之谨厚者 - Means 'secretly sent a trustworthy messenger,' indicating trustworthiness.
    • 持金鬻于扬州 - Means 'took the gold to sell in Yangzhou,' indicating a specific action.
    • 金贵 - Means 'gold was expensive,' indicating market conditions.
    • 两获八千 - Means 'received eight thousand for two taels,' indicating a transaction.
    • 市南货入洛 - Means 'bought southern goods and brought them to Luoyang,' indicating trade.
    • 为崔孤置田宅 - Means 'provided land and housing for Cui's orphan,' indicating charity.
    • 兼为剖分家事 - Means 'also managed the family affairs,' indicating responsibility.
    • 竟未尝言于人 - Means 'never told anyone about it,' indicating secrecy.
    • 惟密亲有知者 - Means 'only close relatives knew about it,' indicating confidentiality.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 范陌 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location.
    • 寿之安丰 - A district within Shouzhou, indicating a specific region.
    • 清河崔氏 - A family name, likely referring to a noble family.
    • 率更令 - A high-ranking official title, possibly from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 扬州 - A city in Jiangsu Province, known for its economic importance.
    • 洛 - A place name, likely referring to Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 范陌卢仲元 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Lu Zhongyuan from Fanmo.
    • 清河崔氏 - A family name, likely referring to a noble family.
    • 率更令谦 - A high-ranking official title, possibly from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 扬州 - A city in Jiangsu Province, known for its economic importance.
    • 洛 - A place name, likely referring to Luoyang, the capital of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 卢君生 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Lu Junsheng.
    • 子严 - A nickname, likely referring to Lu Junsheng.

    Lu Zhongyuan from Fanmo lived in Anfeng of Shouzhou. His wife was Cui, a niece of Rategeng Ling Qian, from Qinghe. Cui's brother had a small plot of land, about one hundred acres, east of Luoyang. He worked diligently in the fields to sustain himself and never sought help from others. One day, while tilling the soil, he found a bottle of gold, totaling one hundred taels, and kept it secret, burying it inside his home. On his deathbed, his wife Li was worried about their poverty and the young children, fearing they would freeze and starve after his death. Cui sent away everyone and told his wife about the buried gold, pointing out where it was hidden. He instructed her, "Be careful not to tell anyone. When Lu Langzhong comes, you can tell him." Shortly after, Lu went on a journey and passed through Luoyang, paying his respects to Cui's orphan. Li sent a servant to convey a message: "I have a sad and urgent matter and must meet my brother-in-law in person." Lu agreed to see her. When they met, she recounted her late husband's wishes. Lu wept for a long time and said, "Only as my elder sister commands." Li then secretly sent a trustworthy messenger to deliver the gold to Lu. Lu then resigned from his post and traveled to Yangzhou to sell the gold. At that time, gold was expensive, and he received eight thousand coins for two taels. He bought southern goods and brought them back to Luoyang, providing land and housing for Cui's orphan and managing the family affairs. After completing these tasks, he returned home. It was only after more than a year that he resumed his official duties. Throughout this, he never told anyone about it, and only close relatives knew about it. Lu Junsheng, also known as Ziyan, had a clear reputation and was highly respected, becoming a famous minister of the world, indeed a reward for his hidden virtue. [35]

    有读《萧氏集》，问功曹是谁子孙，及有后否。余应之曰：“梁高祖武皇帝，父讳顺之，《齐书》有传。武帝受禅，武尊文帝。文帝第三子恢，封鄱阳王，薨谥忠烈。恢生宜丰侯循。循生唐太子太保造。造生武威大将军夙。夙生雅州都督善义。善义生左卫录事参军元恭。元恭生密县主簿旻。旻生杨府功曹讳颖士，字茂挺，门人谥曰文元先生。先生一子存，字伯诚，为金部员外郎，谅直有功曹之风。时裴延龄为户部尚书，恃恩奸佞，与张滂不叶。金部恶延龄之为人，弃官归庐山，以山水自娱，识者甚高之。终于检校仓部郎中。生三子，皆无禄早世，无后。惟次子东，従事邑南，有二子，今皆流落江湖，假吏州县。功曹以其子妻门人柳君讳澹，字中庸，即余之外王父也。韩文公少时，常受萧金部知赏。 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有读《萧氏集》 - There is a reader of the *Xiao Collection*. Introduces the context of reading a specific collection of works.
    • 问功曹是谁子孙 - Asks who the *Gongcao* (a title of an official) is the descendant of. Direct question about lineage.
    • 及有后否 - And whether he has descendants. Extends the question to include the existence of descendants.
    • 余应之曰 - I replied, saying. Introduces the speaker's response.
    • 梁高祖武皇帝 - Emperor Wu of the Liang dynasty, the founding emperor. Proper title and name.
    • 父讳顺之 - His father was named Shun. Use of '讳' indicates respect for the name.
    • 《齐书》有传 - There is a biography in *The Book of Qi*. Refers to a historical record.
    • 武帝受禅 - Emperor Wu accepted the throne through abdication. Describes a historical event.
    • 武尊文帝 - Emperor Wu honored Emperor Wen. Indicates a respectful act.
    • 文帝第三子恢 - The third son of Emperor Wen, named Hui. Provides genealogical information.
    • 封鄱阳王 - Was enfeoffed as Prince of Poyang. Describes a political action.
    • 薨谥忠烈 - Died and was posthumously honored with the title 'Zhonglie'. Indicates a posthumous title.
    • 恢生宜丰侯循 - Hui's son was named Xun and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Yifeng. Continues the genealogy.
    • 循生唐太子太保造 - Xun's son was named Zao and served as Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince of Tang. Provides another genealogical link.
    • 造生武威大将军夙 - Zao's son was named Su and served as General of Wuwei. Continues the lineage.
    • 夙生雅州都督善义 - Su's son was named Shan Yi and served as the Governor of Yazhou. Provides another genealogical link.
    • 善义生左卫录事参军元恭 - Shan Yi's son was named Yuan Gong and served as a Military Aide in the Left Guard. Continues the lineage.
    • 元恭生密县主簿旻 - Yuan Gong's son was named Min and served as the Magistrate of Mì County. Provides another genealogical link.
    • 旻生杨府功曹讳颖士 - Min's son was named Ying Shi and served as the *Gongcao* of Yangfu. Continues the lineage.
    • 字茂挺 - His courtesy name was Maoting. Provides additional personal information.
    • 门人谥曰文元先生 - His disciples posthumously honored him with the title 'Wen Yuan Master'. Indicates a posthumous title.
    • 先生一子存 - Master had one son named Cun. Provides genealogical information.
    • 字伯诚 - His courtesy name was Bocheng. Provides additional personal information.
    • 为金部员外郎 - Served as an Assistant Minister of Finance. Describes a political position.
    • 谅直有功曹之风 - He was honest and straightforward, possessing the virtues of the *Gongcao*. Describes his character.
    • 时裴延龄为户部尚书 - At that time, Pei Yanling served as the Minister of Finance. Provides historical context.
    • 恃恩奸佞 - Relying on favors, he was deceitful and cunning. Describes Pei Yanling's character.
    • 与张滂不叶 - He did not get along with Zhang Pang. Indicates a conflict.
    • 金部恶延龄之为人 - The Ministry of Finance disliked Pei Yanling's character. Describes a negative sentiment.
    • 弃官归庐山 - He resigned and returned to Mount Lu. Describes a personal decision.
    • 以山水自娱 - He enjoyed himself with mountains and waters. Describes a lifestyle.
    • 识者甚高之 - Those who understood him highly regarded him. Indicates a positive evaluation.
    • 终于检校仓部郎中 - He died as an Inspector of the Grain Department. Describes his final position.
    • 生三子，皆无禄早世，无后 - He had three sons, all of whom died young without descendants. Provides genealogical information.
    • 惟次子东 - Only his second son, Dong, survived. Continues the lineage.
    • 従事邑南 - Served in the southern part of the county. Describes a political position.
    • 有二子 - He had two sons. Provides genealogical information.
    • 今皆流落江湖 - They are now wandering in the rivers and lakes. Describes their current situation.
    • 假吏州县 - They are temporary officials in the prefectures and counties. Describes their current positions.
    • 功曹以其子妻门人柳君讳澹 - The *Gongcao* married his son to a disciple named Liu Dan. Describes a marital arrangement.
    • 字中庸 - His courtesy name was Zhongyong. Provides additional personal information.
    • 即余之外王父也 - He was my maternal grandfather. Provides a personal connection.
    • 韩文公少时，常受萧金部知赏 - When Han Wengong was young, he often received recognition from Xiao Jinfu. Describes a historical relationship.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 萧氏集 - The *Xiao Collection*, a compilation of works by the Xiao family.
    • 功曹 - A title of an official responsible for various administrative duties.
    • 梁高祖武皇帝 - Emperor Wu of the Liang dynasty, the founding emperor.
    • 《齐书》 - *The Book of Qi*, a historical record.
    • 受禅 - Accepting the throne through abdication, a common practice in Chinese dynastic transitions.
    • 鄱阳王 - Prince of Poyang, a title of nobility.
    • 忠烈 - A posthumous title indicating loyalty and valor.
    • 唐太子太保 - Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince of Tang, a high-ranking official.
    • 武威大将军 - General of Wuwei, a military rank.
    • 雅州都督 - Governor of Yazhou, a political position.
    • 左卫录事参军 - A Military Aide in the Left Guard, a military position.
    • 密县主簿 - Magistrate of Mì County, a local administrative position.
    • 杨府功曹 - *Gongcao* of Yangfu, a local administrative position.
    • 文元先生 - A posthumous title indicating respect and scholarly achievement.
    • 金部员外郎 - Assistant Minister of Finance, a financial position.
    • 裴延龄 - Pei Yanling, a historical figure known for his deceitful and cunning nature.
    • 户部尚书 - Minister of Finance, a high-ranking official.
    • 张滂 - Zhang Pang, a historical figure known for his conflict with Pei Yanling.
    • 庐山 - Mount Lu, a famous mountain in China known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 韩文公 - Han Wengong, a historical figure known for his early recognition by Xiao Jinfu.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁高祖武皇帝 - Emperor Wu of the Liang dynasty, the founding emperor.
    • 《齐书》 - *The Book of Qi*, a historical record.
    • 鄱阳王 - Prince of Poyang, a title of nobility.
    • 唐太子太保 - Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince of Tang, a high-ranking official.
    • 武威大将军 - General of Wuwei, a military rank.
    • 雅州都督 - Governor of Yazhou, a political position.
    • 左卫录事参军 - A Military Aide in the Left Guard, a military position.
    • 密县主簿 - Magistrate of Mì County, a local administrative position.
    • 杨府功曹 - *Gongcao* of Yangfu, a local administrative position.
    • 裴延龄 - Pei Yanling, a historical figure known for his deceitful and cunning nature.
    • 户部尚书 - Minister of Finance, a high-ranking official.
    • 张滂 - Zhang Pang, a historical figure known for his conflict with Pei Yanling.
    • 韩文公 - Han Wengong, a historical figure known for his early recognition by Xiao Jinfu.

    There was a reader of the Xiao Collection who asked about the descendants of the Gongcao and whether he had any. I replied: "Emperor Wu of the Liang dynasty, the founding emperor, had a father named Shun, whose biography is recorded in The Book of Qi. Emperor Wu accepted the throne through abdication and honored Emperor Wen. The third son of Emperor Wen, named Hui, was enfeoffed as Prince of Poyang and posthumously honored with the title 'Zhonglie'. Hui's son, named Xun, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Yifeng. Xun's son, named Zao, served as Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince of Tang. Zao's son, named Su, served as General of Wuwei. Su's son, named Shan Yi, served as the Governor of Yazhou. Shan Yi's son, named Yuan Gong, served as a Military Aide in the Left Guard. Yuan Gong's son, named Min, served as the Magistrate of Mì County. Min's son, named Ying Shi, served as the Gongcao of Yangfu and was posthumously honored with the title 'Wen Yuan Master'. Master had one son named Cun, whose courtesy name was Bocheng. He served as an Assistant Minister of Finance and was honest and straightforward, possessing the virtues of the Gongcao. At that time, Pei Yanling served as the Minister of Finance, relying on favors and being deceitful and cunning, and did not get along with Zhang Pang. The Ministry of Finance disliked Pei Yanling's character, and he resigned and returned to Mount Lu, enjoying himself with mountains and waters. Those who understood him highly regarded him. He died as an Inspector of the Grain Department and had three sons, all of whom died young without descendants. Only his second son, Dong, survived and served in the southern part of the county, having two sons who are now wandering in the rivers and lakes and serving as temporary officials in the prefectures and counties. The Gongcao married his son to a disciple named Liu Dan, whose courtesy name was Zhongyong, and he was my maternal grandfather. When Han Wengong was young, he often received recognition from Xiao Jinfu." [36]

    及自袁州入为国子祭酒，途经江州，因游庐山，过金部山居，访知诸子凋谢，惟二女在。因赋诗曰：“中郎有女能传业，伯道无儿可主家，今日匡山过旧隐，空将衰泪对烟霞。”留百缣以拯之。或传功曹为李林甫所召，时在禫制中，谒见，林甫薄之，不复用。萧遂作《伐樱桃树赋》以刺。此盖不与者所诬也。功曹孝爱著于士林，李吏部华称其冒难葬亲，岂有越礼之事此事且下萧公数等者不为。余尝闻外族长老说，林甫闻功曹名，欲见之，知在艰棘。后闻禫制已毕，令功曹所厚之人导意，请于萧君所居侧僧舍一见，遂许之。林甫出中书至寺，自以宰辅之尊，意谓功曹便于下马处趋见。功曹乃于门内哭以待之。林甫不得已前吊。由此怒其恃才敢与宰相敌礼，竟不问。后余见今丞相崔公铉，说正同。崔公外祖母柳夫人，亦余族姨，即李北海之外孙也。柳夫人聪明强记，且得于其外族，可为实录。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及自袁州入为国子祭酒 - Formal phrase meaning 'when he entered the position of Grand Master of the Imperial College from Yizhou.' Indicates a significant career advancement.
    • 途经江州 - Means 'passed through Jiangzhou on his way,' indicating a specific route.
    • 因游庐山 - Means 'and thus visited Mount Lu,' suggesting a leisurely visit after a journey.
    • 过金部山居 - Means 'visited his residence in Jinbu Mountain,' indicating a stop at a specific location.
    • 访知诸子凋谢 - Means 'to find out that all his sons had passed away,' conveying a somber discovery.
    • 惟二女在 - Means 'only two daughters were left,' emphasizing the remaining family members.
    • 因赋诗曰 - Means 'then composed a poem saying,' indicating a poetic response to the situation.
    • 中郎有女能传业 - Phrase meaning 'the Middle Rang has a daughter who can carry on the family business,' referring to the continuation of a family legacy.
    • 伯道无儿可主家 - Phrase meaning 'Bo Dao has no son to manage the household,' using a historical reference to express the lack of male heirs.
    • 今日匡山过旧隐 - Means 'today, passing by my old retreat in Mount Kuang,' indicating a return to a previous place.
    • 空将衰泪对烟霞 - Means 'only shedding tears of grief against the mist and clouds,' expressing deep sorrow.
    • 留百缣以拯之 - Means 'left a hundred bolts of silk to help them,' indicating a generous act of charity.
    • 或传功曹为李林甫所召 - Means 'it was rumored that Gongcao was summoned by Li Linfu,' introducing a controversial event.
    • 时在禫制中 - Means 'at a time when he was in mourning,' indicating a period of ritual observance.
    • 谒见 - Means 'to pay a visit,' indicating a formal meeting.
    • 林甫薄之，不复用 - Means 'Li Linfu looked down on him and did not rehire him,' expressing a negative reaction.
    • 萧遂作《伐樱桃树赋》以刺 - Means 'Xiao then wrote the 'Essay on Felling the Cherry Tree' to criticize,' indicating a literary response to the situation.
    • 此盖不与者所诬也 - Means 'this is probably a false accusation by those who opposed him,' expressing a defense.
    • 功曹孝爱著于士林 - Means 'Gongcao's filial piety and love were well-known among the literati,' highlighting his reputation.
    • 李吏部华称其冒难葬亲 - Means 'Li, the Minister of Personnel, praised him for risking danger to bury his parents,' indicating a positive assessment.
    • 岂有越礼之事 - Means 'how could there be any violation of propriety,' expressing a denial of impropriety.
    • 此事且下萧公数等者不为 - Means 'this matter is below the level of what Xiao Gong and others would do,' indicating a comparison to more serious issues.
    • 余尝闻外族长老说 - Means 'I have heard from the elders of my clan,' introducing a personal anecdote.
    • 林甫闻功曹名，欲见之 - Means 'Li Linfu heard of Gongcao's name and wanted to meet him,' indicating an interest in meeting him.
    • 知在艰棘 - Means 'knowing he was in mourning,' indicating awareness of his status.
    • 后闻禫制已毕 - Means 'later, hearing that the mourning period was over,' indicating a change in circumstances.
    • 令功曹所厚之人导意 - Means 'ordered someone close to Gongcao to convey his intentions,' indicating a mediated approach.
    • 请于萧君所居侧僧舍一见 - Means 'to ask for a meeting at a temple near Xiao's residence,' indicating a specific location for the meeting.
    • 遂许之 - Means 'he agreed,' indicating a positive response.
    • 林甫出中书至寺 - Means 'Li Linfu left the Zhongshu Province and went to the temple,' indicating his journey.
    • 自以宰辅之尊 - Means 'thinking of his own high status as a chancellor,' indicating his self-perception.
    • 意谓功曹便于下马处趋见 - Means 'thinking that Gongcao would come to meet him where he got off his horse,' indicating an expectation.
    • 功曹乃于门内哭以待之 - Means 'but Gongcao was already inside the temple, weeping in anticipation,' indicating a different action.
    • 林甫不得已前吊 - Means 'Li Linfu had no choice but to go forward and pay his respects,' indicating a reluctant action.
    • 由此怒其恃才敢与宰相敌礼 - Means 'he became angry at Gongcao for showing off his talent and treating the chancellor with disrespect,' indicating a negative reaction.
    • 竟不问 - Means 'and ultimately did not question him further,' indicating a final decision.
    • 后余见今丞相崔公铉 - Means 'later, I met the current chancellor Cui Gong Xuan,' introducing another personal anecdote.
    • 说正同 - Means 'he said the same thing,' indicating agreement.
    • 崔公外祖母柳夫人 - Means 'Cui Gong's maternal grandmother, Lady Liu,' introducing a family connection.
    • 亦余族姨 - Means 'is also my cousin,' indicating a familial relationship.
    • 即李北海之外孙也 - Means 'she is the granddaughter of Li Beihai,' indicating a historical connection.
    • 柳夫人聪明强记 - Means 'Lady Liu is intelligent and has a strong memory,' highlighting her qualities.
    • 且得于其外族 - Means 'and she also gained recognition from her own clan,' indicating additional support.
    • 可为实录 - Means 'can be considered a reliable record,' indicating trustworthiness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 袁州 - Refers to Yizhou, a prefecture in modern-day Hunan Province.
    • 国子祭酒 - Refers to the Grand Master of the Imperial College, a high-ranking official responsible for education.
    • 江州 - Refers to Jiangzhou, a region in modern-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 庐山 - Refers to Mount Lu, a famous mountain in Jiangxi Province known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
    • 金部山居 - Refers to a specific residence in Jinbu Mountain, likely a place of retreat or residence.
    • 中郎 - Refers to a title of a low-ranking official, often used in a respectful sense.
    • 伯道 - Refers to a historical figure, Bo Dao, who is often cited in Chinese literature as an example of someone without a son.
    • 匡山 - Refers to Mount Kuang, another name for Mount Lu, emphasizing the connection to the author's past.
    • 烟霞 - Refers to mist and clouds, often used in poetry to evoke a sense of natural beauty and tranquility.
    • 百缣 - Refers to a large quantity of silk, a valuable commodity in ancient China.
    • 功曹 - Refers to a local government official, often responsible for administrative duties.
    • 李林甫 - Refers to Li Linfu, a prominent and controversial chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 禫制 - Refers to the period of mourning after a funeral, a significant ritual in Chinese culture.
    • 《伐樱桃树赋》 - Refers to an essay or poem titled 'Essay on Felling the Cherry Tree,' likely a literary work criticizing Li Linfu.
    • 李吏部华 - Refers to Li, the Minister of Personnel, a high-ranking official responsible for appointments.
    • 越礼 - Refers to violating social norms or propriety, a serious offense in traditional Chinese society.
    • 萧公 - Refers to Xiao Gong, a respected official or scholar.
    • 崔公铉 - Refers to Cui Gong Xuan, a chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 柳夫人 - Refers to Lady Liu, the maternal grandmother of Cui Gong Xuan and a respected figure.
    • 李北海 - Refers to Li Beihai, a historical figure known for his integrity and wisdom.

    When he entered the position of Grand Master of the Imperial College from Yizhou, he passed through Jiangzhou on his way, and thus visited Mount Lu, stopping at his residence in Jinbu Mountain. Upon learning that all his sons had passed away, he found only two daughters left. He then composed a poem saying:

    "The Middle Rang has a daughter who can carry on the family business,
    Bo Dao has no son to manage the household.
    Today, passing by my old retreat in Mount Kuang,
    I only shed tears of grief against the mist and clouds."

    He left a hundred bolts of silk to help them. It was rumored that Gongcao was summoned by Li Linfu, at a time when he was in mourning. He paid a visit, but Li Linfu looked down on him and did not rehire him. Xiao then wrote the "Essay on Felling the Cherry Tree" to criticize. This is probably a false accusation by those who opposed him. Gongcao's filial piety and love were well-known among the literati; Li, the Minister of Personnel, praised him for risking danger to bury his parents. How could there be any violation of propriety? This matter is below the level of what Xiao Gong and others would do.

    I have heard from the elders of my clan that Li Linfu heard of Gongcao's name and wanted to meet him, knowing he was in mourning. Later, hearing that the mourning period was over, he ordered someone close to Gongcao to convey his intentions, asking for a meeting at a temple near Xiao's residence. Gongcao agreed. Li Linfu left the Zhongshu Province and went to the temple, thinking of his own high status as a chancellor, and expecting that Gongcao would come to meet him where he got off his horse. But Gongcao was already inside the temple, weeping in anticipation. Li Linfu had no choice but to go forward and pay his respects. He became angry at Gongcao for showing off his talent and treating the chancellor with disrespect, and ultimately did not question him further.

    Later, I met the current chancellor Cui Gong Xuan, who said the same thing. Cui Gong's maternal grandmother, Lady Liu, is also my cousin, and she is the granddaughter of Li Beihai. Lady Liu is intelligent and has a strong memory, and she also gained recognition from her own clan. She can be considered a reliable record. [37]

    余座主陇西公为台丞，奏今孔尚书温、丞相徐公商为监察。及孔为中丞，陇西公淹恤在外多年，除宗正少卿归朝。而孔徐二公并时为丞相，每宴集，时人以为盛事。亦可太息于宦途也。 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 座主 - Term for the examiner or supervisor of a student, particularly in the imperial examination system.
    • 台丞 - Title for a high-ranking official, often referring to a vice-minister.
    • 中丞 - Title for a high-ranking official, often referring to a vice-minister or a high-ranking censor.
    • 淹恤 - Term meaning to be detained or delayed, often due to illness or other reasons.
    • 宗正少卿 - Title for a junior official in charge of the royal family's affairs.
    • 宴集 - Term for a banquet or gathering.
    • 宦途 - Term for the career path of a civil servant, often with connotations of the ups and downs of official life.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陇西公 - Refers to a high-ranking official from the region of Longxi (陇西), a place name in ancient China.
    • 孔尚书温 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Kong Wen, who held the position of Shangshu (尚书), a high-ranking minister.
    • 丞相徐公商 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Xu Shang, who held the position of Chancellor (丞相).
    • 监察 - Refers to a high-ranking official in charge of supervision and inspection.
    • 中丞 - Refers to a high-ranking official in charge of supervision and inspection.
    • 宗正少卿 - Refers to a junior official in charge of the royal family's affairs.
    • 宴集 - Refers to a banquet or gathering, often held to celebrate achievements or to foster relationships.
    • 宦途 - Refers to the career path of a civil servant, often with connotations of the ups and downs of official life.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陇西公 - A high-ranking official from the region of Longxi.
    • 孔尚书温 - A high-ranking official named Kong Wen, who held the position of Shangshu.
    • 丞相徐公商 - A high-ranking official named Xu Shang, who held the position of Chancellor.
    • 监察 - A high-ranking official in charge of supervision and inspection.
    • 中丞 - A high-ranking official in charge of supervision and inspection.
    • 宗正少卿 - A junior official in charge of the royal family's affairs.
    • 宴集 - A banquet or gathering.
    • 宦途 - The career path of a civil servant.

    My examiner, the Lord of Longxi, served as a vice-minister and recommended Kong Wen, the Shangshu, and Xu Shang, the Chancellor, to serve as high-ranking censors. When Kong Wen became a vice-minister, the Lord of Longxi was detained abroad for many years and was later appointed as a junior official in charge of the royal family's affairs before returning to court. Meanwhile, Kong Wen and Xu Shang both served as Chancellors at the same time, and every time they attended banquets, contemporaries considered it a grand event. One can also sigh at the nature of official careers. [38]

    唐尚书特，太和六年，尉渭南，为亦兆府试进士官。杜丞相悰时为京兆尹，将托亲知闻等第召公従容，兼命茶酒。及语举人，则趋而下阶，俯伏不对，杜公竟不敢言而止。是年上等内近三十余人，数年内皆及第无缺落者，前后莫比。
    　　权实子范为殿中侍御史知巡。有小吏従市求取者，事发，笞臀十数。他日复有如此者，白于台长，杖背十五。同列疑其罪同罚异。权对曰：“前吏所取者，名属左军。台之威令不振久矣，百司尚有不禀奉者，况凭禁军之势耶！彼受贿于此辈，且是知抑豪强，可以末减。后吏则挟台之威，恐吓百姓，杖背全命，犹为至轻。”
    　　张杰夫前自襄州従事至京，先到台中。三院多张之亲友，为求马价，同列有或怒或嗤而不署文字者。权独先署，谓众曰：“某向不与张君熟，且闻其在穷丧马，正当求禄求知之际，不可使徒行。且一缗何足为轻重若使小？生荐所不知之人，实不従众署状。” [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 太和六年 - The sixth year of the Taihe era during the Tang Dynasty (832 AD).
    • 尉渭南 - Served as the magistrate of Weinan.
    • 亦兆府試進士官 - Examination of candidates for the jinshi degree at the Yizhou Prefecture.
    • 托親知聞等第 - To ask a close friend to inquire about the results.
    • 従容 - To visit casually or chat informally.
    • 茶酒 - Tea and wine, often served as hospitality.
    • 趨而下階 - To hurry down the steps as a sign of respect or deference.
    • 俯伏不对 - To bow down and not respond, indicating extreme politeness or reluctance to speak.
    • 竟不敢言而止 - Finally dared not speak and stopped, implying a failure to act.
    • 上等内近三十余人 - Nearly thirty people in the top category.
    • 数年内皆及第无缺落者 - All passed within a few years without any failures.
    • 前后莫比 - No one before or after could match this.
    • 权实子范为殿中侍御史知巡 - Quan Shizifan served as the Inspector of the Dianzhong Shiyushi.
    • 凭禁军之势 - Relying on the power of the imperial guards.
    • 末减 - Mild punishment or reduced penalty.
    • 杖背十五 - Fifteen strokes on the back.
    • 台之威令不振久矣 - The authority of the central government has been weak for a long time.
    • 百司尚有不禀奉者 - Many officials still do not obey.
    • 挟台之威 - To rely on the power of the central government.
    • 恐吓百姓 - To intimidate the common people.
    • 至轻 - Extremely light or minimal.
    • 先到台中 - Arrived at the central government first.
    • 同列有或怒或嗤而不署文字者 - Some of his colleagues were angry or ridiculed him and refused to sign the document.
    • 不与张君熟 - Not very familiar with Zhang Jun.
    • 穷丧马 - In dire straits, losing his horse.
    • 求禄求知 - Seeking a position and knowledge.
    • 徒行 - To walk or travel on foot.
    • 一缗 - A unit of currency, equivalent to 1,000 coins.
    • 小生荐所不知之人 - It is inappropriate to recommend someone I don't know well.
    • 实不従众署状 - In fact, I did not follow the crowd in signing the document.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐尚书特 - A high-ranking official named Te during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 杜丞相悰 - Du Cong, a chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 京兆尹 - The governor of the capital region.
    • 亦兆府 - The Yizhou Prefecture, an administrative division in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 进士 - The highest degree in the imperial examination system.
    • 禁军 - The imperial guards, a special military force loyal to the emperor.
    • 殿中侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the central government, responsible for inspection and supervision.
    • 台中 - The central government or the imperial court.
    • 台之威令 - The authority and command of the central government.
    • 百司 - All the officials and departments under the central government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐尚书特 - A high-ranking official named Te during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 杜丞相悰 - Du Cong, a chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 京兆尹 - The governor of the capital region.
    • 亦兆府 - The Yizhou Prefecture.
    • 进士 - The highest degree in the imperial examination system.
    • 禁军 - The imperial guards.
    • 殿中侍御史 - A high-ranking official in the central government.
    • 台中 - The central government or the imperial court.

    During the sixth year of the Taihe era, Te, a high-ranking official of the Tang Dynasty, served as the magistrate of Weinan and was responsible for the examination of candidates for the jinshi degree at the Yizhou Prefecture. At that time, Du Cong, a chancellor, was the governor of the capital region. He intended to ask a close friend to inquire about the results and invite Te to visit, offering him tea and wine. When the friend mentioned the candidates to Te, Te hurried down the steps, bowed down, and did not respond. Du Cong finally dared not speak and stopped. That year, nearly thirty people were in the top category, and all passed within a few years without any failures. No one before or after could match this success.

    Quan Shizifan served as the Inspector of the Dianzhong Shiyushi. One day, a low-ranking official was caught seeking something from the market and was punished with ten to fifteen strokes on the buttocks. Another day, another official was caught doing the same thing and was reported to the head of the central government, who punished him with fifteen strokes on the back. His colleagues were confused about the different punishments. Quan explained, "The first official was seeking something from the left military unit. The authority of the central government has been weak for a long time, and many officials still do not obey. How much more so, then, for someone relying on the power of the imperial guards! The first official received a lighter punishment because he was known to suppress the powerful and could be given a reduced penalty. The second official relied on the power of the central government to intimidate the common people, so even a minimal punishment was too light."

    Zhang Jiefu, who had served as an official in Xiangzhou, arrived in the capital first. Many of his friends and relatives sought financial help from him. Some of his colleagues were angry or ridiculed him and refused to sign a document. Quan was the only one to sign it first and said to the others, "I am not very familiar with Zhang Jun, but I heard that he was in dire straits and had lost his horse, and he was at a time when he was seeking a position and knowledge. It would be inappropriate to let him walk. Moreover, what is the significance of a thousand coins? It is inappropriate to recommend someone I do not know well, and in fact, I did not follow the crowd in signing the document." [39]

    刑部郎中元沛妻刘氏，全白之妹，贤而有文学。著《女仪》一篇，亦曰《直训》。夫人既寡居，奉玄元之教，受道箓于吴筠先生，精苦寿考。长子固，早有名，官历省郎、刺史、国子司业。次子察，进士及第，累佐使府，后高卧庐山。察之长子潾，好道不仕；次子充，进士及第，亦尚灵玄矣。

    　　卷四 角部　　
    　　（角为人，凡不仕者皆入此部）
    　　元和初，南岳道士田良逸、蒋含弘，皆道业绝高，远近钦敬，时号田蒋。田以虚无为心，和煦待物，不事浮饰，而天格清峻，人见者褊吝尽去。吕侍郎渭、杨侍郎凭，相继廉问湖南，皆北面师事。潭州大旱，祈祷不获，或请邀致先生。杨公曰：“田先生岂为人祈雨者耶？”不得已迎之。先生蓬发弊衣，欣然就辇到郡，亦终无言，即日降雨。所居岳观，内建黄箓坛场，法具已陈，而天阴晦。弟子请先生祈晴，先生亦无言，岸帻垂发而坐。及行斋，左右代整冠履，扶而升坛，天即开霁。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 刑部郎中 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Justice, responsible for legal matters.
    • 全白之妹 - Refers to the sister of Quan Bai, a proper noun that needs to be rendered consistently.
    • 賢而有文學 - Describes a person as both virtuous and well-versed in literature.
    • 著《女儀》一篇，亦曰《直訓》 - Indicates that the person wrote a treatise titled *Nüyi* (Ladies' Conduct), also known as *Zhixun* (Direct Teachings).
    • 奉玄元之教 - Refers to following the teachings of the Taoist deity Xuan Yuan (Laozi).
    • 受道箓于吴筠先生 - Means 'received the Taoist talisman from Master Wu Jun,' indicating formal initiation into Taoism.
    • 精苦寿考 - Describes a life of intense devotion and longevity.
    • 长子固，早有名，官历省郎、刺史、国子司业 - Indicates that the eldest son, Gu, was well-known early on and held various high-ranking positions, including provincial official, governor, and vice president of the Imperial Academy.
    • 次子察，进士及第，累佐使府，后高卧庐山 - Describes the second son, Cha, as having passed the imperial examination, served in various administrative roles, and later retired to Mount Lu.
    • 察之长子潾，好道不仕 - Indicates that the eldest grandson, Lin, was devoted to the Dao but did not seek official positions.
    • 次子充，进士及第，亦尚灵玄矣 - Describes the second grandson, Chong, as having passed the imperial examination and also being devoted to the spiritual and mystical aspects of Taoism.
    • 卷四 角部 - Indicates the fourth volume, section on 'Jiao' (Horn), which is part of a larger text or anthology.
    • 元和初 - Refers to the early years of the Yuanhe era (806–820) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南岳道士田良逸、蒋含弘 - Refers to Taoist priests Tian Liangyi and Jiang Hanhong from Mount Heng (Nanyue), a famous Taoist mountain.
    • 道业绝高 - Describes their spiritual attainments as extremely high.
    • 远近钦敬 - Means 'respected by people far and near,' indicating widespread admiration.
    • 时号田蒋 - Means 'they were known as Tian and Jiang,' indicating their fame.
    • 田以虚无为心 - Describes Tian as having a mindset focused on the Taoist concept of *wu* (nothingness).
    • 和煦待物 - Means 'treated all things with kindness and warmth.'
    • 不事浮饰 - Indicates that he did not engage in superficial or ostentatious behavior.
    • 天格清峻 - Describes his character as clear and lofty.
    • 人见者褊吝尽去 - Means 'those who met him had all their narrow-mindedness and greed dispelled.'
    • 吕侍郎渭、杨侍郎凭 - Refers to two officials, Lü Wei and Yang Ping, who held the rank of *Shilang* (Attendant).
    • 相继廉问湖南 - Means 'they successively served as governors of Hunan Province.'
    • 北面师事 - Means 'they treated him as a teacher,' indicating a high level of respect.
    • 潭州大旱 - Refers to a severe drought in Tan Prefecture (modern-day Changsha).
    • 祈祷不获 - Means 'prayers for rain were unsuccessful.'
    • 或请邀致先生 - Means 'someone suggested inviting the master.'
    • 田先生岂为人祈雨者耶 - Means 'Is Master Tian someone who prays for rain for others?'
    • 蓬发弊衣 - Describes him as having unkempt hair and ragged clothing.
    • 欣然就辇到郡 - Means 'he happily went by carriage to the prefectural capital.'
    • 亦终无言 - Means 'he also remained silent.'
    • 即日降雨 - Means 'it rained that very day.'
    • 所居岳观 - Refers to the Taoist temple on Mount Heng where he lived.
    • 内建黄箓坛场 - Means 'an inner yellow talismanic altar was set up.'
    • 法具已陈 - Means 'the ritual implements were already arranged.'
    • 天阴晦 - Means 'the sky was overcast and dark.'
    • 弟子请先生祈晴 - Means 'his disciples asked him to pray for clear weather.'
    • 岸帻垂发而坐 - Means 'he sat with his headband tilted and hair hanging down.'
    • 及行斋 - Means 'when he began the ritual.'
    • 左右代整冠履 - Means 'his attendants helped him adjust his hat and shoes.'
    • 扶而升坛 - Means 'they helped him ascend the altar.'
    • 天即开霁 - Means 'the sky immediately cleared up.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刑部郎中 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty's Ministry of Justice, responsible for legal matters.
    • 全白之妹 - Quan Bai was a historical figure, and her sister is mentioned here. The exact identity of Quan Bai is not necessary to translate, but her name should be rendered consistently.
    • 《女仪》 - A treatise on women's conduct, also known as *Zhixun* (Direct Teachings).
    • 玄元之教 - Refers to Taoism, specifically the teachings of Laozi (Xuan Yuan), the founder of Taoism.
    • 道箓 - A Taoist talisman that signifies formal initiation into the Taoist order.
    • 吴筠先生 - Master Wu Jun was a famous Taoist priest during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和初 - The early years of the Yuanhe era (806–820) during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 南岳 - Mount Heng (Nanyue) in Hunan Province, a famous Taoist mountain.
    • 田良逸、蒋含弘 - Two famous Taoist priests from Mount Heng.
    • 潭州 - Tan Prefecture, the modern-day city of Changsha in Hunan Province.
    • 黄箓坛场 - A Taoist ritual altar used for specific ceremonies, such as the *Huanglu* ritual.

    The Wife of Yuan Pei, Criminal Ministry Censor, and Her Descendants

    The wife of Yuan Pei, a censor in the Criminal Ministry, was the sister of Quan Bai. She was virtuous and well-versed in literature, and she wrote a treatise titled Nüyi (Ladies' Conduct), also known as Zhixun (Direct Teachings). After her husband's death, she observed widowhood and followed the teachings of the Taoist deity Xuan Yuan. She received the Taoist talisman from Master Wu Jun and lived a life of intense devotion, achieving longevity. Her eldest son, Gu, was well-known early on and held various high-ranking positions, including provincial official, governor, and vice president of the Imperial Academy. Her second son, Cha, passed the imperial examination and served in various administrative roles before retiring to Mount Lu. Cha's eldest son, Lin, was devoted to the Dao but did not seek official positions. Cha's second son, Chong, also passed the imperial examination and was devoted to the spiritual and mystical aspects of Taoism.

    Volume 4, Section on "Jiao" (Horn)
    (Those who do not seek official positions are included in this section)

    In the early years of the Yuanhe era, the Taoist priests Tian Liangyi and Jiang Hanhong of Mount Heng (Nanyue) were renowned for their exceptionally high spiritual attainments. They were respected by people far and near and were known as Tian and Jiang. Tian had a mindset focused on the Taoist concept of wu (nothingness), treated all things with kindness and warmth, and did not engage in superficial or ostentatious behavior. His character was clear and lofty, and those who met him had all their narrow-mindedness and greed dispelled. Lü Wei and Yang Ping, both officials holding the rank of Shilang (Attendant), successively served as governors of Hunan Province and treated Tian as their teacher.

    During a severe drought in Tan Prefecture, prayers for rain were unsuccessful, and someone suggested inviting the master. Yang Gong remarked, "Is Master Tian someone who prays for rain for others?" Reluctantly, he was invited. The master arrived in the prefectural capital with unkempt hair and ragged clothing, happily going by carriage. He remained silent and, that very day, it rained. At his temple on Mount Heng, an inner yellow talismanic altar was set up, and the ritual implements were already arranged. The sky was overcast and dark. His disciples asked him to pray for clear weather, but he also remained silent, sitting with his headband tilted and hair hanging down. When he began the ritual, his attendants helped him adjust his hat and shoes and helped him ascend the altar. Immediately, the sky cleared up. [40]

    尝有村姥，持一碧绢襦来奉先生，先生对众便著之，在坐者窃笑，先生不以介意。杨公尝迎先生至潭州，先生方洗足，使到，乘小舟便行，侍者以履袜追及于衙门，先生即于门外坐砖阶著袜，旁若无人。杨再拜，亦不止之。喜饮酒，而言不及吉凶是非。及杨自京尹谪临驾尉，使使候先生，兼遗银器，先生受之，便悉付门人，作法会。使还，先生曰：“报汝阿本郎，不久即归，勿忧也。”未几，杨果移杭州长史。良逸未尝干人，人至亦不逆，不记人官位姓名第。与吕渭分最深。后郎中吕温刺衡州，因来候之，左右先告以使君是侍郎之子。 [41]

    There was once an old village woman who brought a green silk robe to present to the master. In front of everyone, the master put it on, and those present secretly laughed, but the master did not take it to heart. Mr. Yang once welcomed the master to Tan Prefecture. While washing his feet, Mr. Yang arrived, and the master took a small boat and went. The attendants caught up with him at the government office with his shoes and socks, and the master immediately sat on the brick steps outside the door and put on his socks, as if no one was there. Mr. Yang bowed twice, but the master did not stop him. The master enjoyed drinking but did not speak about good or bad, right or wrong. When Mr. Yang was demoted from the capital prefect to the prefecture of Linjiang, he sent an envoy to visit the master, bringing silverware as a gift. The master accepted it and immediately gave it all to his disciples, holding a religious ceremony. When the envoy returned, the master said, "Tell your father, Mr. Ben, that I will return soon, do not worry." Not long after, Mr. Yang was indeed transferred to the position of Chief Clerk in Hangzhou. Mr. Liangyi never sought to influence others; he did not refuse anyone who came, and he did not remember people's official positions, names, or ranks. He had the closest relationship with Mr. Lü Wei. Later, Mr. Lü Wen, the Assistant Magistrate, was appointed to the prefecture of Hengzhou, so he came to visit him. The attendants first informed him that the envoy was the son of a minister. [41]

    及温入，下床抚其背曰：“尔是吕渭儿子耶？”温泫然降阶，田亦不止，其真朴如此。良逸母为喜王寺尼，尼众皆呼先生为“小师”。尝日负薪两束奉母，或有故不及往，即弟子代送之。或传寺尼晨起见一虎在田媪门外，走以告，媪曰：“此应是小师使送柴来，不足畏也。”蒋君混元之气，虽不及田，而修持趣尚亦相类。兄事于田，号为莫逆。蒋始善符术，自晦其道，人莫知之。后居九贞观，曾命弟子至县市斋物，不及期还，语其故云：“于山口见一猛兽当路，良久不去，以故迟滞。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及温入 - Temporal phrase meaning 'when Wen entered' or 'upon Wen's arrival.'
    • 下床抚其背 - Literally 'got off the bed and patted his back,' a gesture of affection or reassurance.
    • 尔是吕渭儿子耶 - Direct question meaning 'Are you the son of Lu Wei?' with a tone of surprise or recognition.
    • 泫然降阶 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'tearfully descended the steps,' indicating emotional response.
    • 田亦不止 - Means 'Tian did not stop' or 'Tian also did not stop,' likely referring to continued interaction or action.
    • 其真朴如此 - Means 'such is his true simplicity' or 'such is his genuine plainness,' summarizing the character's qualities.
    • 尝日负薪两束奉母 - Means 'he would often carry two bundles of firewood to serve his mother,' describing a habitual act of filial piety.
    • 或有故不及往 - Means 'if there was an obstacle preventing him from going,' introducing a conditional scenario.
    • 即弟子代送之 - Means 'then his disciples would deliver it on his behalf,' showing a supportive community.
    • 或传寺尼晨起见一虎在田媪门外 - Means 'it was said that a nun at the temple saw a tiger outside the door of Lady Tian in the morning,' introducing a supernatural element.
    • 走以告 - Means 'she ran to tell,' indicating urgency and concern.
    • 此应是小师使送柴来 - Means 'this should be Xiaoshi sending firewood,' interpreting the tiger as a sign of the young man's actions.
    • 不足畏也 - Means 'there is no need to fear it,' expressing reassurance.
    • 蒋君混元之气 - Means 'Jiang Jun's mixed and primal energy,' referring to a mystical or spiritual quality.
    • 虽不及田 - Means 'though not as great as Tian,' indicating a comparison.
    • 而修持趣尚亦相类 - Means 'but his cultivation and interests were also similar,' highlighting shared characteristics.
    • 兄事于田 - Means 'treated Tian as an elder brother,' indicating a respectful relationship.
    • 号为莫逆 - Means 'called the closest of friends,' emphasizing a deep bond.
    • 蒋始善符术 - Means 'Jiang initially excelled in divination and magic,' introducing a skill.
    • 自晦其道 - Means 'hid his ways,' indicating secrecy or modesty.
    • 人莫知之 - Means 'no one knew about it,' emphasizing the hidden nature of his abilities.
    • 后居九贞观 - Means 'later he lived at Jiuzhen Temple,' providing a new setting.
    • 曾命弟子至县市斋物 - Means 'once he sent his disciples to the county to buy food,' describing a practical task.
    • 不及期还 - Means 'they did not return on time,' introducing a delay.
    • 语其故云 - Means 'he explained the reason, saying,' indicating an explanation.
    • 于山口见一猛兽当路 - Means 'they saw a fierce beast blocking the path at the mountain pass,' describing a dangerous encounter.
    • 良久不去 - Means 'it stayed for a long time,' indicating persistence.
    • 以故迟滞 - Means 'due to this, they were delayed,' explaining the cause of the delay.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吕渭 - Refers to Lu Wei, likely a historical figure or a respected person.
    • 喜王寺 - Refers to Xiwang Temple, a specific temple in the context.
    • 尼众 - Refers to a group of nuns, indicating a religious community.
    • 小师 - A respectful title for a junior master or a younger but respected individual.
    • 混元之气 - Refers to a mystical or spiritual energy, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist practices.
    • 九贞观 - Refers to Jiuzhen Temple, a specific location where Jiang Jun lived.
    • 符术 - Refers to divination and magic, often associated with Daoist practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吕渭 - A historical or respected figure.
    • 喜王寺 - A specific temple.
    • 小师 - A respectful title.
    • 九贞观 - A specific temple.
    • 符术 - A skill or practice.

    When Wen arrived, he got off the bed and patted his back, saying, "Are you the son of Lu Wei?" Wen, moved to tears, descended the steps, and Tian did not stop either. Such was his true simplicity. Liang Yi's mother was a nun at Xiwang Temple, and the nuns all called him "Xiaoshi" (Junior Master). He would often carry two bundles of firewood to serve his mother, and if there was an obstacle preventing him from going, his disciples would deliver it on his behalf. It was said that a nun at the temple saw a tiger outside the door of Lady Tian in the morning and ran to tell her. Lady Tian said, "This should be Xiaoshi sending firewood; there is no need to fear it." Jiang Jun possessed a mixed and primal energy, though not as great as Tian's, but his cultivation and interests were also similar. He treated Tian as an elder brother and was called the closest of friends. Jiang Jun initially excelled in divination and magic, hiding his ways, and no one knew about it. Later, he lived at Jiuzhen Temple, and once he sent his disciples to the county to buy food. They did not return on time, and he explained the reason, saying, "They saw a fierce beast blocking the path at the mountain pass, and it stayed for a long time, so they were delayed." [42]

    ”蒋曰：“我在此庇伊已多时，何敢如此。”即以一符置所见处，明日兽踣符下。蒋闻之曰：“我本以符却之，使其不来，岂知不能自脱。既以害物，安用术为？”取符焚之，自此绝不复留意。有欧阳平者，行业亦高，又兄事蒋君，于田君即邻于入室。欧阳曾一夕梦三金炉自天而下，若有所召。既寤，潜告人曰：“二先生不久去矣，我继之。”俄而田君蜕去，蒋次之，欧阳亦逝。桐柏山陈寡言、徐灵府、冯云翼三人，皆田之弟子也。衡山周混沌，蒋之门人也。陈徐在东南，品第比田蒋，而冯在欧阳之列。周自幼入道，科法清严，今为南岳首冠。
    　　道士陶天活者，安南人。居濒海，海溢，家人悉惊走避水。天活始生，其母挈去不得，举族悲念。洎水退而归，其婴儿在桑之交枝，无恙，抱之啼乳如常，遂以《天活》为名。及长，聪慧简率，真气内充。自元和至大和，为供奉道士，朝野归向。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 庇伊 - Colloquial term meaning 'to protect' or 'to shelter'; used in a context of supernatural intervention.
    • 符却之 - Technical term referring to the use of a talisman or charm to repel evil spirits or forces.
    • 既以害物，安用术为 - Rhetorical question meaning 'if it has already harmed something, what use is the skill?'
    • 兄事蒋君 - Means 'to treat Jang Jun like a brother'; indicates a close and respectful relationship.
    • 邻于入室 - Means 'to be close to being a disciple' or 'to be nearly a student'.
    • 蜕去 - Metaphorical term meaning 'to leave the world' or 'to pass away'.
    • 真气内充 - Means 'inner vitality is abundant'; describes a state of spiritual or physical health.
    • 品第比田蒋 - Means 'to be ranked alongside Tian and Jiang'; indicates a high level of respect or status.
    • 聪慧简率 - Means 'clever and straightforward'; describes a person's character.
    • 朝野归向 - Means 'the court and the common people look up to'; indicates widespread admiration and respect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蒋曰 - Refers to Jang, a character in the text who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 田君 - Refers to Tian Jun, another character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 欧阳平 - Refers to Ouyang Ping, a character who is also likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 桐柏山 - Refers to Tongbai Mountain, a location in China known for its Daoist temples and practices.
    • 陈寡言 - Refers to Chen Guanyan, a character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 徐灵府 - Refers to Xu Lingfu, a character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 冯云翼 - Refers to Feng Yunyi, a character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 衡山周混沌 - Refers to Zhou Hunchun from Mount Heng, a character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 陶天活 - Refers to Tao Tianhuo, a character who is likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 安南 - Refers to Annam, an ancient name for Vietnam, indicating the character's origin.
    • 元和至大和 - Refers to the years 806-835 CE, a period during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 供奉道士 - Refers to a high-ranking Daoist priest who serves in the imperial court.
    • 朝野归向 - Indicates widespread admiration and respect from both the court and the common people.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蒋 - Jang, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 田君 - Tian Jun, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 欧阳平 - Ouyang Ping, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 桐柏山 - Tongbai Mountain, a location in China.
    • 陈寡言 - Chen Guanyan, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 徐灵府 - Xu Lingfu, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 冯云翼 - Feng Yunyi, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 衡山周混沌 - Zhou Hunchun from Mount Heng, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 陶天活 - Tao Tianhuo, a Daoist practitioner.
    • 安南 - Annam, an ancient name for Vietnam.
    • 元和至大和 - The years 806-835 CE.
    • 供奉道士 - A high-ranking Daoist priest.
    • 朝野归向 - Widespread admiration and respect from both the court and the common people.

    Jiang said, "I have been protecting it for a long time, how could I dare to do such a thing?" He immediately placed a talisman at the place where he had seen the beast, and the next day, the beast fell dead at the talisman's feet. Jiang heard of this and said, "I originally used the talisman to drive it away, to make it not come, but I did not know it could not free itself. Since it has already harmed something, what use is the skill?" He took the talisman and burned it, and from then on, he no longer paid attention to it. There was a man named Ouyang Ping, whose industry was also high, and he treated Jiang Jun like a brother, and was nearly a disciple of Tian Jun. Ouyang once dreamed of three golden braziers descending from the sky, as if summoning him. After waking up, he secretly told others, "The two masters will not be long for this world, I will follow them." Soon, Tian Jun passed away, followed by Jiang, and then Ouyang also died. Chen Guanyan, Xu Lingfu, and Feng Yunyi, three men from Tongbai Mountain, were all disciples of Tian. Zhou Hunchun from Mount Heng was a disciple of Jiang. Chen and Xu were in the southeast, ranked alongside Tian and Jiang, while Feng was in the same category as Ouyang. Zhou entered Daoism since childhood, with clear and strict methods, and is now the leading figure of the Southern Mountain.

    The Daoist Tao Tianhuo was from Annam. He lived near the sea, and when the sea overflowed, his family fled in fear to avoid the water. When Tao was born, his mother could not take him away, and the entire family mourned. When the water receded and they returned, the infant was found safe on the intersecting branches of a mulberry tree, unharmed, and he cried and nursed as usual. Thus, he was named 'Tianhuo.' As he grew up, he was clever and straightforward, with abundant inner vitality. From the Yuanhe to the Dahan period, he served as a high-ranking Daoist priest, and was admired and respected by both the court and the common people. [43]

    江南多名僧。贞元、元和以来，越州有清江、清昼，婺州有乾俊、乾辅，时谓之“会稽二清”，“东阳二乾”。
    　　吴兴僧昼，字皎然，工律诗。尝谒韦苏州，恐诗体不合，乃于舟中抒思，作古体十数篇为贽。韦公全不称赏，昼极失望。明日写其旧制献之，韦公吟讽，大加叹咏。因语昼云：“师几失声名，何不但以所工见投，而猥希老夫之意。人名有所得，非卒能致。”昼大伏其鉴别之精。
    　　元和以来，京城诸僧及道士，尤多大德之号。偶因势进，则得补署，遂以为头衔。各因所业谈论，取本教所业，以符大德之目，此犹近于理。至有号文章大德者。夫文章之称，岂为缁徒设耶讹亦甚矣！有似昔岁，德宗搜访怀才抱器不求闻达者。有人于昭应县逢一书生，奔驰入京，问求何事。答云：“将应不求闻达科。”此科亦岂可应耶号欺聋俗，皆此类也。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 江南多名僧 - The southern region has many renowned monks.
    • 贞元、元和以来 - Since the reigns of Emperor Dezong (779-805) and Emperor Xianzong (805-820).
    • 越州有清江、清昼 - Yuezhou has monks named Qingjiang and Qingzhou.
    • 婺州有乾俊、乾辅 - Wuzhou has monks named Qianjun and Qianfu.
    • 时谓之“会稽二清”，“东阳二乾” - They were called the 'Two Qings of Kuaiji' and the 'Two Qians of Dongyang.'
    • 吴兴僧昼，字皎然，工律诗 - A monk from Wuxing named Zhuo, with the courtesy name Jiao Ran, was skilled in composing regulated verse.
    • 尝谒韦苏州，恐诗体不合 - He once visited Wei Suozhou, fearing that his poetic style might not align with his.
    • 乃于舟中抒思，作古体十数篇为贽 - So he wrote several dozen ancient-style poems on a boat as a gift.
    • 韦公全不称赏，昼极失望 - Wei Suozhou did not praise them at all, and Zhuo was extremely disappointed.
    • 明日写其旧制献之 - The next day, he wrote his old works and presented them.
    • 韦公吟讽，大加叹咏 - Wei Suozhou recited them and greatly praised them.
    • 因语昼云：“师几失声名，何不但以所工见投，而猥希老夫之意” - He then told Zhuo, 'You almost lost your reputation. Why not just present what you are good at, instead of trying to please me?'
    • 昼大伏其鉴别之精 - Zhuo greatly admired his keen discernment.
    • 元和以来，京城诸僧及道士 - Since the reign of Emperor Xianzong (805-820), various monks and Taoist priests in the capital.
    • 尤多大德之号 - Especially numerous were those with the title of 'Great Virtue.'
    • 偶因势进，则得补署 - Sometimes they gained positions due to favorable circumstances.
    • 遂以为头衔 - Thus, they took it as a title.
    • 各因所业谈论，取本教所业，以符大德之目 - Each based their discussions on their respective professions, taking their own religious practices to match the title of 'Great Virtue.'
    • 此犹近于理 - This is still somewhat reasonable.
    • 至有号文章大德者 - There were even those who called themselves 'Great Virtue in Literature.'
    • 夫文章之称，岂为缁徒设耶 - The title of 'literary excellence' was not meant for Buddhist monks.
    • 讹亦甚矣 - This is a great error.
    • 有似昔岁，德宗搜访怀才抱器不求闻达者 - It is similar to when Emperor Dezong sought out talented individuals who did not seek fame.
    • 有人于昭应县逢一书生，奔驰入京，问求何事 - Someone in Zhaoying County met a scholar who rushed to the capital, asking what he was seeking.
    • 答云：“将应不求闻达科” - The scholar replied, 'I am going to take the examination for those who do not seek fame.'
    • 此科亦岂可应耶 - Could such an examination exist?
    • 号欺聋俗，皆此类也 - Such titles deceive the ignorant, and all such cases are like this.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贞元、元和 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Dezong (779-805) and Emperor Xianzong (805-820).
    • 越州、婺州、吴兴 - Refers to Yuezhou, Wuzhou, and Wuxing, respectively, which are regions in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 清江、清昼、乾俊、乾辅 - Names of famous monks during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 韦苏州 - Wei Suozhou, a prominent poet of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和 - The era name of Emperor Xianzong (805-820).
    • 大德 - A title often given to high-ranking monks or Taoist priests.
    • 文章大德 - A title that suggests literary excellence, but is criticized as inappropriate for monks.
    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong (779-805), known for his efforts to recruit talented individuals.
    • 昭应县 - A county in present-day Shaanxi Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 贞元 - The era name of Emperor Dezong (779-805).
    • 元和 - The era name of Emperor Xianzong (805-820).
    • 越州 - Yuezhou, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 婺州 - Wuzhou, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 吴兴 - Wuxing, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 清江 - A monk from Yuezhou.
    • 清昼 - A monk from Yuezhou.
    • 乾俊 - A monk from Wuzhou.
    • 乾辅 - A monk from Wuzhou.
    • 韦苏州 - Wei Suozhou, a prominent poet of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong (779-805).
    • 昭应县 - A county in present-day Shaanxi Province.

    The southern region has many renowned monks. Since the reigns of Emperor Dezong (779-805) and Emperor Xianzong (805-820), Yuezhou has monks named Qingjiang and Qingzhou, while Wuzhou has monks named Qianjun and Qianfu. They were called the 'Two Qings of Kuaiji' and the 'Two Qians of Dongyang.'

    A monk from Wuxing named Zhuo, with the courtesy name Jiao Ran, was skilled in composing regulated verse. He once visited Wei Suozhou, fearing that his poetic style might not align with his. So he wrote several dozen ancient-style poems on a boat as a gift. Wei Suozhou did not praise them at all, and Zhuo was extremely disappointed. The next day, he wrote his old works and presented them. Wei Suozhou recited them and greatly praised them. He then told Zhuo, 'You almost lost your reputation. Why not just present what you are good at, instead of trying to please me?' Zhuo greatly admired his keen discernment.

    Since the reign of Emperor Xianzong (805-820), various monks and Taoist priests in the capital, especially numerous were those with the title of 'Great Virtue.' Sometimes they gained positions due to favorable circumstances, thus taking it as a title. Each based their discussions on their respective professions, taking their own religious practices to match the title of 'Great Virtue.' This is still somewhat reasonable. There were even those who called themselves 'Great Virtue in Literature.' The title of 'literary excellence' was not meant for Buddhist monks. This is a great error. It is similar to when Emperor Dezong sought out talented individuals who did not seek fame. Someone in Zhaoying County met a scholar who rushed to the capital, asking what he was seeking. The scholar replied, 'I am going to take the examination for those who do not seek fame.' Could such an examination exist? Such titles deceive the ignorant, and all such cases are like this. [44]

    昔北齐封阳休之为王，休之曰：“我非奴非獠，何故封我为王使林远及陶隐居之徒，当必为休之耻矣。”
    　　有文淑僧者，公为聚众谭说，假托经论所言，无非淫秽鄙亵之事。不逞之徒，转相鼓扇扶树。愚夫冶妇，乐闻其说，听者填咽。寺舍瞻礼崇奉，呼为“和尚”。教坊效其声调，以为歌曲。其氓庶易诱，释徒苟知真理，及文义稍精，亦甚嗤鄙之。近日庸僧以名系功德使，不惧台省府县，以士流好窥其所为，视衣冠过于仇雠，而淑僧最甚，前后杖背，流在边地数矣。
    　　元和中，僧鉴虚本为不知肉味，作僧素无道行。及有罪伏诛，后人遂作鉴虚煮肉法，大行于世。不妨他僧为之，置于鉴虚耳。亦犹才命论称张燕公，《革华传》称韩文公，《老牛歌》称白乐天，《佛骨诗》称郑司徒，皆后人所诬也。故其辞多鄙浅。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 封王 - Formal title granted by the emperor, equivalent to being made a king or prince.
    • 非奴非獠 - Phrase indicating that the person is neither a slave nor a barbarian, emphasizing their status.
    • 何故 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why' or 'for what reason.'
    • 林遠及陶隱居之徒 - Refers to the literati and recluses, such as Lin Yuan and Tao Yuanming, who were respected scholars and poets.
    • 轉相鼓扇扶樹 - Means to spread and promote something enthusiastically.
    • 愚夫冶妇 - Refers to the common people, particularly those who are less educated.
    • 寺舍瞻礼崇奉 - Means to show respect and veneration in temples.
    • 和尚 - Term for a Buddhist monk.
    • 教坊效其声调 - Means that the music hall imitates the tone and style.
    • 氓庶易诱 - Refers to the common people who are easily swayed.
    • 释徒苟知真理 - Means that Buddhist monks who understand the true teachings.
    • 文义稍精 - Refers to those with a deeper understanding of the text.
    • 杖背 - Punishment involving beating the back.
    • 流在边地 - Means to be exiled to the frontier regions.
    • 不知肉味 - Metaphor for someone who does not eat meat, often used to describe a person who is virtuous or ascetic.
    • 作僧素无道行 - Means that the monk was a novice with no moral conduct.
    • 伏诛 - To be executed.
    • 煮肉法 - Method of cooking meat.
    • 不妨他僧为之 - Means that other monks could have done it as well.
    • 置于鉴虚耳 - Means that the act is attributed to Jianxu.
    • 才命论 - Treatise on talent and destiny.
    • 革华传 - Biography or account.
    • 老牛歌 - Song about an old ox.
    • 佛骨诗 - Poem about the relics of the Buddha.
    • 郑司徒 - High-ranking official.
    • 皆后人所诬也 - Means that these were all fabrications by later people.
    • 鄙浅 - Means shallow and vulgar.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi dynasty, a short-lived Chinese dynasty that ruled northern China from 550 to 577 AD.
    • 阳休之 - Historical figure from the Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 林远及陶隐居之徒 - Refers to the literati and recluses, such as Lin Yuan and Tao Yuanming, who were respected scholars and poets.
    • 文淑僧 - Historical figure, a Buddhist monk.
    • 鉴虚 - Historical figure, a Buddhist monk.
    • 张燕公 - Zhang Yan, a historical figure.
    • 韩文公 - Han Yu, a famous Tang dynasty scholar and poet.
    • 白乐天 - Bai Juyi, a famous Tang dynasty poet.
    • 郑司徒 - Zheng Sishu, a high-ranking official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 阳休之 - Yang Xiuzhi.
    • 林远 - Lin Yuan.
    • 陶隐居 - Tao Yuanming.
    • 文淑僧 - Wen Shu.
    • 鉴虚 - Jianxu.
    • 张燕公 - Zhang Yan.
    • 韩文公 - Han Yu.
    • 白乐天 - Bai Juyi.
    • 郑司徒 - Zheng Sishu.

    During the Northern Qi dynasty, Yang Xiuzhi was granted the title of king, but he said, "I am neither a slave nor a barbarian, so why am I being made a king? This would certainly be a disgrace for scholars like Lin Yuan and Tao Yuanming."

    There was a Buddhist monk named Wen Shu who gathered crowds to spread corrupt and vulgar teachings, pretending to speak about the scriptures. The wicked people spread and promoted these teachings, and the common people, especially the less educated, enjoyed hearing them. The temples showed great respect and veneration for Wen Shu, calling him "Buddhist monk." The music hall imitated his tone and style, turning it into songs. The common people were easily swayed, but Buddhist monks who understood the true teachings and those with a deeper understanding of the text were greatly ashamed of it. Recently, a monk in charge of religious duties, who did not fear the authorities and looked down on the educated class, was the most extreme case. He was beaten and exiled to the frontier several times.

    During the Yuanhe period, there was a monk named Jianxu who was a novice with no moral conduct. After he was executed for a crime, later people created a method of cooking meat attributed to him, which became popular. Other monks could have done it as well, but it was attributed to Jianxu. Similarly, the Treatise on Talent and Destiny attributed to Zhang Yan, the Biography of Han Yu, the Song of the Old Ox attributed to Bai Juyi, and the Poem of the Buddha's Relics attributed to Zheng Sishu were all fabrications by later people. Therefore, the language used in these fabrications is often shallow and vulgar. [45]

    卢子严说，早年随其懿亲郑常侍东之同游宣州当涂，隐居山岩，即陶贞白炼丹所也。垆迹犹在，后为佛舍。有僧甚高洁，好事因说其先师，名彦范，姓刘，虽为沙门，早究儒学，邑人呼为刘九经。颜鲁公、韩晋公、刘忠州、穆监宁、独孤常州皆与之善，各执经受业者数十人。年八十，犹精强，僧行不亏。性颇嗜酒，饮亦未尝及乱。学徒有携壶至者，欣然而受之。每进三数杯，则讲说方锐。所居有小圃，自植茶，为鹿所损，人劝以垣隔之，诸名士悉乐为运石共成。穆兵部贽，事之最谨。尝得美酒，密以小瓷壶置于怀中。累石之际，因白师曰：“有少好酒，和尚饮否？”彦范笑而倾饮。满似酣，则语穆曰：“不用般石，且来听书。”遂与剖析微奥，至多不倦。 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卢子严说 - Biographical introduction indicating the account is from Lu Ziyang's perspective.
    • 随其懿亲 - Literally 'followed by his esteemed relative,' referring to a close family member.
    • 同游宣州当涂 - Indicates a shared journey to Dangtu in Anhui Province.
    • 隐居山岩 - Refers to living in seclusion in a mountain cave or rocky area.
    • 即陶贞白炼丹所也 - Locative phrase meaning 'the place where Tao Zhenbai practiced alchemy.'
