    • 垆迹犹在 - 'The traces of the furnace still exist,' referring to the remnants of alchemical activity.
    • 后为佛舍 - Indicates that the place later became a Buddhist temple.
    • 有僧甚高洁 - Describes a monk as highly virtuous and pure.
    • 好事因说其先师 - 'A well-meaning person told about his former teacher.'
    • 虽为沙门 - Indicates that the person was a Buddhist monk.
    • 早究儒学 - 'Had studied Confucianism early on.'
    • 邑人呼为刘九经 - 'The local people called him 'Liu Jiujing,' likely referring to his mastery of nine Confucian classics.'
    • 各执经受业者数十人 - 'Dozens of students studied under him.'
    • 年八十，犹精强 - Indicates that he was still vigorous at the age of eighty.
    • 僧行不亏 - 'His monastic conduct was without flaw.'
    • 性颇嗜酒 - Indicates that he had a strong liking for alcohol.
    • 饮亦未尝及乱 - 'He never drank to the point of disorder.'
    • 学徒有携壶至者，欣然而受之 - Describes how students who brought wine were welcomed by him.
    • 每进三数杯，则讲说方锐 - 'After drinking a few cups, he became more vigorous in his teachings.'
    • 所居有小圃，自植茶，为鹿所损 - Describes a small garden where he planted tea, which was damaged by deer.
    • 人劝以垣隔之 - 'People advised him to build a wall to protect the garden.'
    • 诸名士悉乐为运石共成 - Indicates that many scholars were happy to help build the wall.
    • 穆兵部贽，事之最谨 - Refers to Mu Bingbu Zhi, who served him with great diligence.
    • 尝得美酒，密以小瓷壶置于怀中 - 'He once obtained good wine and secretly placed it in a small porcelain bottle in his robe.'
    • 累石之际，因白师曰 - Describes a moment when he told his teacher while building the wall.
    • 有少好酒，和尚饮否 - 'I have a little good wine; will the monk drink some?'
    • 满似酣，则语穆曰 - 'When he was slightly tipsy, he said to Mu.'
    • 不用般石，且来听书 - 'There's no need to move the stones; come and listen to a lecture instead.'
    • 遂与剖析微奥，至多不倦 - 'He then analyzed profound and subtle teachings with him, never tiring of it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陶贞白 - Refers to Tao Zhenbai, a famous alchemist and Taoist master from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 刘九经 - Likely refers to Liu Jiujing, a title given to someone who had mastered nine of the Confucian classics, indicating great erudition.
    • 颜鲁公、韩晋公、刘忠州、穆监宁、独孤常州 - These are proper names of notable historical figures, possibly referring to important officials or scholars of the time.
    • 僧行不亏 - Refers to the strict adherence to monastic rules and conduct, a key aspect of Buddhist practice.
    • 博学求之 - Indicates a pursuit of broad knowledge, a common theme in classical Chinese education.
    • 穆兵部贽 - Refers to Mu Bingbu Zhi, likely a high-ranking official in the Ministry of War (兵部), who served the monk with great diligence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 卢子严 - The narrator of the account.
    • 郑常侍东之 - The narrator's esteemed relative.
    • 陶贞白 - A famous alchemist and Taoist master.
    • 刘九经 - A title given to someone who had mastered nine of the Confucian classics.
    • 颜鲁公 - Likely refers to Yan Zhenqing, a famous calligrapher and statesman.
    • 韩晋公 - Likely refers to Han Yu, a famous literary figure and statesman.
    • 刘忠州 - Likely refers to Liu Zongyuan, a famous literary figure.
    • 穆监宁 - Likely refers to Mu Zhenning, a high-ranking official.
    • 独孤常州 - Likely refers to Dugu Changzhou, a high-ranking official.
    • 穆兵部贽 - Likely refers to Mu Bingbu Zhi, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of War.

    Lu Ziyang recounts that in his youth, he accompanied his esteemed relative, Zheng Changshi Dongzhi, on a journey to Dangtu in Anhui Province. They lived in seclusion in a mountain cave, which was the site where Tao Zhenbai practiced alchemy. The traces of the furnace still exist, and the place later became a Buddhist temple. There was a monk who was highly virtuous and pure. A well-meaning person told about his former teacher, named Yanfan, with the surname Liu. Although he was a Buddhist monk, he had studied Confucianism early on, and the locals called him Liu Jiujing. Notable figures such as Yan Zhenqing, Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Mu Zhenning, and Dugu Changzhou were all his friends, and dozens of students studied under him. At the age of eighty, he was still vigorous, and his monastic conduct was without flaw. He had a strong liking for alcohol but never drank to the point of disorder. When students brought wine, he welcomed them and accepted it. After drinking a few cups, he became more vigorous in his teachings. He had a small garden where he planted tea, which was damaged by deer. People advised him to build a wall to protect the garden, and many scholars were happy to help build the wall together. Mu Bingbu Zhi served him with great diligence. Once, he obtained good wine and secretly placed it in a small porcelain bottle in his robe. While building the wall, he told his teacher, "I have a little good wine; will the monk drink some?" Yanfan smiled and drank it. When he was slightly tipsy, he said to Mu, "There's no need to move the stones; come and listen to a lecture instead." He then analyzed profound and subtle teachings with him, never tiring of it. [46]

    郑君更征其遗事，僧叹息久之曰：“近日尊儒重道，都无前辈之风。”因出一纸，穆兵部与书，倾寒暄之仪极卑敬。其略曰：“某偶忝名宦，皆因善诱。自居班列，终日尘屑却思。昔岁临清涧，荫长松，接侍座下，获闻微言。未知何时复遂此事遥瞻水中月，岭上云，但驰攀想而已。和尚薄于滋味，深于酒德，所食仅同婴儿，所饮或如少壮。持尊体有所不安，中夜思之，实怀忧恋。”其诚切如此，月日之下，但云门人姓名，状上和尚法座前，不言官位，当时嗜学事师，可谓至矣。又云，有耆宿僧总持，彦范之友也。为人清苦，一生未曾干人。惟自垦山，种田数亩给衣食。或遇丰岁多麦，傍有滞穗，度知其主必不收者，拾之以归。若可惜者，则求而积之，召主以付，不至则置于路口，其独行如此。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鄭君更征其遺事 - 鄭君 refers to a person named Zheng, 更征 means to further inquire, and 遺事 refers to past events. Together, this phrase means Zheng further inquired about past events.
    • 傾寒暄之儀極卑敬 - 傾 means to devote oneself, 寒暄 refers to casual conversation, 儀 means formality, and 卑敬 means humility and respect. Together, this phrase means to devote oneself to the formality of casual conversation with great humility and respect.
    • 某偶忝名宦 - 某 is a humble reference to oneself, 偶 means occasionally, 忝 means to humbly accept, and 名宦 refers to a prestigious official position. Together, this phrase means 'I occasionally humbly accept a prestigious official position.'
    • 自居班列 - 自居 means to place oneself, and 班列 refers to the order of officials. Together, this phrase means to place oneself in the order of officials.
    • 塵屑 - A metaphor for trivial or mundane matters.
    • 昔歲臨清涧，荫长松，接侍座下，獲聞微言 - 昔歲 means past years, 臨清涧 means to be near a clear stream, 荫长松 means to be shaded by tall pines, 接侍座下 means to receive service under one's seat, and 获聞微言 means to hear subtle or profound words. Together, this phrase describes a past experience of being near a clear stream, shaded by tall pines, and receiving subtle teachings.
    • 未知何时复遂此事 - 未知 means not knowing, 何时 means when, 复遂 means to again achieve, and 此事 means this matter. Together, this phrase means 'I do not know when this matter will again be achieved.'
    • 遥瞻水中月，岭上云 - A metaphor for distant and unattainable things, like looking at the moon in the water or clouds on the mountain.
    • 但驰攀想而已 - 但 means merely, 驰攀 means to rush and climb, and 想 means to think. Together, this phrase means 'merely rushing and climbing in thought.'
    • 和尚薄于滋味，深于酒德 - 和尚 refers to a monk, 薄于滋味 means to have a light taste for flavors, and 深于酒德 means to have a deep appreciation for the virtue of wine. Together, this phrase means the monk has a light taste for flavors but a deep appreciation for the virtue of wine.
    • 所食仅同婴儿，所饮或如少壮 - 所食 means what is eaten, 仅同婴儿 means just like an infant, 所饮 means what is drunk, and 或如少壮 means sometimes like a young adult. Together, this phrase means the monk eats like an infant and drinks like a young adult.
    • 持尊体有所不安 - 持尊体 means to hold one's body, and 有所不安 means to feel uneasy. Together, this phrase means the monk feels uneasy in his body.
    • 中夜思之，实怀忧恋 - 中夜 means midnight, 思之 means to think about it, 实怀 means truly holds, and 忧恋 means worry and longing. Together, this phrase means 'at midnight, I think about it and truly hold worry and longing.'
    • 其诚切如此 - 其 means its, 诚切 means sincere and earnest, and 如此 means like this. Together, this phrase means 'its sincerity is like this.'
    • 月日之下，但云门人姓名，状上和尚法座前 - 月日之下 means under the date, 但云 means merely states, 门人 means disciple, 姓名 means name, 状上 means to present a report, and 和尚法座前 means before the monk's dharma seat. Together, this phrase means 'under the date, merely states the disciple's name, presenting a report before the monk's dharma seat.'
    • 不言官位 - 不言 means does not mention, and 官位 means official position. Together, this phrase means 'does not mention the official position.'
    • 当时嗜学事师，可谓至矣 - 当时 means at that time, 嗜学 means to be fond of learning, 事师 means to serve a teacher, 可谓 means can be said, and 至矣 means to the utmost. Together, this phrase means 'at that time, to be fond of learning and serving a teacher can be said to be to the utmost.'
    • 有耆宿僧总持，彦范之友也 - 有 means there is, 耆宿 means an elder monk, 僧 means monk, 总持 is the monk's name, and 彦范之友 means a friend of Yan Fan. Together, this phrase means 'there is an elder monk named Zongchi, who is a friend of Yan Fan.'
    • 为人清苦 - 为人 means to be as a person, and 清苦 means to be clean and frugal. Together, this phrase means 'to be as a person who is clean and frugal.'
    • 一生未曾干人 - 一生 means a lifetime, 未曾 means never, and 干人 means to interfere with others. Together, this phrase means 'a lifetime without ever interfering with others.'
    • 惟自垦山，种田数亩给衣食 - 惟 means only, 自垦山 means to cultivate mountains oneself, 种田 means to farm, 数亩 means several acres, and 给衣食 means to provide clothing and food. Together, this phrase means 'only cultivating mountains oneself, farming several acres to provide clothing and food.'
    • 或遇丰岁多麦，傍有滞穗，度知其主必不收者，拾之以归 - 或遇 means sometimes encountering, 丰岁 means a bountiful year, 多麦 means much wheat, 傍有 means beside there are, 滞穗 means leftover ears of grain, 度知 means to estimate and know, 其主 means its owner, 必不收者 means certainly not to collect, and 拾之以归 means to pick it up and bring it home. Together, this phrase means 'sometimes encountering a bountiful year with much wheat, beside there are leftover ears of grain, estimating and knowing that its owner certainly will not collect, picking it up and bringing it home.'
    • 若可惜者，则求而积之，召主以付，不至则置于路口 - 若可惜者 means if it is worth saving, 则 means then, 求而积之 means to seek and accumulate it, 召主 means to call the owner, 以付 means to give it to them, 不至 means if they do not come, and 则置于路口 means then place it at the road side. Together, this phrase means 'if it is worth saving, then seek and accumulate it, call the owner to give it to them, if they do not come, then place it at the road side.'
    • 其独行如此 - 其 means its, 独行 means solitary conduct, and 如此 means like this. Together, this phrase means 'its solitary conduct is like this.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鄭君 - A person named Zheng, possibly a historical figure or a contemporary of the author.
    • 穆兵部 - A person named Mu, who held the position of Minister of War (兵部).
    • 和尚 - Refers to a monk, possibly a historical figure or a contemporary of the author.
    • 总持 - The name of an elder monk, Zongchi, who is described as a friend of Yan Fan.
    • 彦范 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, who was a friend of the monk Zongchi.
    • 耆宿僧 - Refers to an elder monk, emphasizing wisdom and experience.
    • 清苦 - A term describing a simple and frugal lifestyle, often associated with monks and scholars.
    • 自垦山 - Refers to the practice of cultivating land oneself, often associated with self-sufficiency and simplicity.
    • 丰岁 - A bountiful year, often associated with good harvests and prosperity.
    • 滞穗 - Leftover ears of grain, often associated with the idea of charity and helping those in need.
    • 路口 - The side of the road, often associated with the idea of leaving things for others to find.

    Proper Nouns

    • 鄭君 - A person named Zheng, possibly a historical figure or a contemporary of the author.
    • 穆兵部 - A person named Mu, who held the position of Minister of War (兵部).
    • 和尚 - Refers to a monk, possibly a historical figure or a contemporary of the author.
    • 总持 - The name of an elder monk, Zongchi, who is described as a friend of Yan Fan.
    • 彦范 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, who was a friend of the monk Zongchi.

    Zheng further inquired about past events, and the monk sighed for a long time before saying, "Recently, there is respect for Confucianism and the Way, but none of the spirit of the predecessors." He then produced a piece of paper, which was a letter from Mu, the Minister of War, to the monk. The letter was filled with the utmost humility and respect in the form of casual conversation. It read: "I occasionally humbly accept a prestigious official position, all due to good guidance. Placing myself in the order of officials, I spend my days on trivial matters, yet I often think of the past. In past years, I was near a clear stream, shaded by tall pines, receiving service under your seat, and hearing subtle teachings. I do not know when this matter will again be achieved, like looking at the moon in the water or clouds on the mountain, merely rushing and climbing in thought. The monk has a light taste for flavors but a deep appreciation for the virtue of wine, eating like an infant and drinking like a young adult. Holding his body, he feels uneasy, and at midnight, I think about it and truly hold worry and longing." Its sincerity is like this. Under the date, it merely states the disciple's name, presenting a report before the monk's dharma seat, without mentioning the official position. At that time, to be fond of learning and serving a teacher can be said to be to the utmost. It also mentions that there is an elder monk named Zongchi, who is a friend of Yan Fan. He is clean and frugal, a lifetime without ever interfering with others. He only cultivates mountains himself, farming several acres to provide clothing and food. Sometimes encountering a bountiful year with much wheat, beside there are leftover ears of grain, estimating and knowing that its owner certainly will not collect, picking it up and bringing it home. If it is worth saving, then seek and accumulate it, call the owner to give it to them, if they do not come, then place it at the road side. Its solitary conduct is like this. [47]

    ○角部之次
    　　元和十五年，淮南裨将谭可则，因防边为吐蕃所掠。初到蕃中，蕃人未知宪宗弃天下，日夜惧王师复河湟，不安寝食。可则既至，械系之置地牢中，绝其饮食，考问累至。可则具告以大行升遐，蕃人尚未之信。其傍有知书者，可则因略记遗诏示之，乃信焉。蕃法刻木为印，每有急事，则使人驰马赴赞府牙帐，日行数百里，使者上马如飞，号为“马使”。报得可则审宪皇崩问之状。先是，每得华人，其无所能者，便充所在役使，辄黥其面。粗有文艺者，则Ａ其臂，以候赞普之命。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 角部之次 - Refers to a specific order or category, possibly related to military or administrative divisions.
    • 元和十五年 - The year 820 AD, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 淮南裨将谭可则 - A subordinate general named Tan Keze from Huainan.
    • 防边为吐蕃所掠 - Indicates that Tan Keze was guarding the border and was captured by the Tibetans.
    • 械系之置地牢中 - Indicates that Tan Keze was shackled and placed in a dungeon.
    • 绝其饮食 - Indicates that Tan Keze was deprived of food and water.
    • 考问累至 - Indicates that Tan Keze was subjected to repeated interrogations.
    • 大行升遐 - A euphemism for the death of an emperor, specifically referring to the death of Emperor Xianzong.
    • 刻木为印 - Indicates that the Tibetans used wooden seals as official stamps.
    • 急事 - Indicates urgent matters.
    • 赞府牙帐 - Refers to the headquarters of a Tibetan military commander.
    • 日行数百里 - Indicates that the messenger traveled hundreds of miles in a day.
    • 上马如飞 - Indicates that the messenger rode his horse as fast as possible.
    • 马使 - Refers to a messenger who rides a horse.
    • 得华人 - Indicates that the Tibetans captured Han Chinese.
    • 无所能者 - Indicates that the captured Han Chinese had no special skills.
    • 充所在役使 - Indicates that the captured Han Chinese were put to work in various roles.
    • 黥其面 - Indicates that the captured Han Chinese were branded on their faces.
    • 粗有文艺者 - Indicates that the captured Han Chinese had some level of cultural or literary skills.
    • 候赞普之命 - Indicates that the captured Han Chinese were waiting for the order of the Tibetan ruler.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元和十五年 - The year 820 AD, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 淮南 - A region in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 裨将 - A subordinate general in the Tang military.
    • 谭可则 - A historical figure, a subordinate general from Huainan.
    • 吐蕃 - The Tibetan Empire, a powerful state in Central Asia during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 赞府 - A title for a Tibetan military commander.
    • 赞普 - The title for the Tibetan emperor or king.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元和十五年 - The year 820 AD, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 淮南 - A region in present-day Anhui Province, China.
    • 谭可则 - A historical figure, a subordinate general from Huainan.
    • 吐蕃 - The Tibetan Empire.
    • 赞府 - A title for a Tibetan military commander.
    • 赞普 - The title for the Tibetan emperor or king.

    In the year of Yuanhe, the fifteenth year of Emperor Xianzong's reign, a subordinate general named Tan Keze from Huainan was captured by the Tibetans while guarding the border. Upon arriving in Tibetan territory, the Tibetans were unaware that Emperor Xianzong had passed away, and they feared the Tang army would reclaim the Hexi Corridor, causing them great anxiety and sleepless nights. After Tan Keze was captured, he was shackled and placed in a dungeon, deprived of food and water, and subjected to repeated interrogations. Tan Keze fully informed them of Emperor Xianzong's death, but the Tibetans did not believe him. There was a literate person nearby, and Tan Keze briefly recorded the imperial edict and showed it to them, and they then believed him. According to Tibetan law, wooden seals were used as official stamps, and for urgent matters, messengers would ride horses to the headquarters of a Tibetan military commander, traveling hundreds of miles in a day. The messengers would ride their horses as fast as possible, and they were called "horse messengers." The messengers reported the news of Tan Keze's confirmation of Emperor Xianzong's death. Previously, whenever the Tibetans captured Han Chinese who had no special skills, they were put to work in various roles and branded on their faces. Those with some level of cultural or literary skills had their arms marked, waiting for the order of the Tibetan ruler. [48]

    得华人补为吏者，则呼为“舍人”可则以晓文字，将以为知汉书舍人，可则不愿。其旧舍人有姓崔者，本华人，可则尝于灵武相识。其人大为蕃帅所信，为言之，得免可则前后数逃归，辄为候者所得。蕃帅虽不杀，以皮鞭榜之，凡数百，竟得脱。凡在蕃六年，及归，诣阙自陈，敕付神策军前驰使。未及进用，为军中沙汰，因配在浙东，止得散将而已，竟无官—成四年，余于越州遇之，见其步履不快。云于蕃中走时冻损足。视其臂，一字尚存。译云：“天子家臣。”可则亦细言河湟可复之状。听其语，犹微染戎音。 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 得华人补为吏者，则呼为“舍人” - When a Chinese person is appointed as an official, they are called '舍人' (a title for a low-ranking official).
    • 可则以晓文字，将以为知汉书舍人 - The person could read and write, which would qualify them as a '知汉书舍人' (an official who knows the Han dynasty's records).
    • 其旧舍人有姓崔者，本华人，可则尝于灵武相识 - There was an old '舍人' named Cui, who was originally a Chinese person, and the subject had met him in Lingwu.
    • 其人大为蕃帅所信，为言之，得免 - Cui was highly trusted by the '蕃帅' (a foreign military leader) and was able to intercede on behalf of the subject, resulting in his release.
    • 可则前后数逃归，辄为候者所得 - The subject attempted to escape multiple times but was always caught by guards or scouts.
    • 蕃帅虽不杀，以皮鞭榜之，凡数百，竟得脱 - The '蕃帅' did not kill the subject but instead beat him with a leather whip hundreds of times, and eventually, the subject managed to escape.
    • 凡在蕃六年，及归，诣阙自陈，敕付神策军前驰使 - The subject spent six years in captivity and, upon returning, went to the imperial court to report his experiences. The emperor ordered him to be sent to the '神策军' (a military force) as a messenger.
    • 未及进用，为军中沙汰，因配在浙东，止得散将而已，竟无官 - Before he could be promoted, he was demoted due to a military purge and was assigned to the '浙东' (Zhe Dong region) as a low-ranking officer.
    • 成四年，余于越州遇之，见其步履不快。云于蕃中走时冻损足。视其臂，一字尚存。译云：“天子家臣。” - In the fourth year of the Cheng era, the subject met someone in Yuezhou (a place name) who walked slowly and had a scar on his arm. The person explained that he had been beaten and frozen in captivity, and the scar read '天子家臣' (a servant of the emperor).
    • 可则亦细言河湟可复之状。听其语，犹微染戎音 - The subject also spoke in detail about the possibility of reclaiming the '河湟' (a region) and that his speech still had a slight foreign accent.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 华人 - Refers to Chinese people, distinguishing them from foreigners.
    • 吏 - Refers to officials or bureaucrats.
    • 舍人 - A title for a low-ranking official, often used in the context of the Han dynasty.
    • 蕃帅 - Refers to a foreign military leader, likely from a neighboring country or tribe.
    • 灵武 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 神策军 - A military force, likely referring to a specific military unit in the Tang dynasty.
    • 浙东 - A region name, likely referring to a specific area in the eastern part of China.
    • 河湟 - A region name, likely referring to a specific area in the northwest of China.
    • 天子家臣 - Refers to a servant of the emperor, indicating a high-ranking position.

    Proper Nouns

    • 华人 - Chinese people.
    • 吏 - Officials.
    • 舍人 - A low-ranking official.
    • 蕃帅 - A foreign military leader.
    • 灵武 - A place name.
    • 神策军 - A military force.
    • 浙东 - A region name.
    • 河湟 - A region name.
    • 天子家臣 - A servant of the emperor.

    A Chinese person who was appointed as an official was called a "舍人" (a low-ranking official). He could read and write, which qualified him as a "知汉书舍人" (an official who knew the Han dynasty's records), but he did not wish to hold that position. There was an old "舍人" named Cui, who was originally a Chinese person, and the subject had met him in Lingwu. Cui was highly trusted by the "蕃帅" (a foreign military leader) and was able to intercede on behalf of the subject, resulting in his release. The subject attempted to escape multiple times but was always caught by guards or scouts. The "蕃帅" did not kill him but instead beat him with a leather whip hundreds of times, and eventually, the subject managed to escape. He spent six years in captivity and, upon returning, went to the imperial court to report his experiences. The emperor ordered him to be sent to the "神策军" (a military force) as a messenger. Before he could be promoted, he was demoted due to a military purge and was assigned to the "浙东" (Zhe Dong region) as a low-ranking officer. In the fourth year of the Cheng era, the subject met someone in Yuezhou (a place name) who walked slowly and had a scar on his arm. The person explained that he had been beaten and frozen in captivity, and the scar read "天子家臣" (a servant of the emperor). The subject also spoke in detail about the possibility of reclaiming the "河湟" (a region) and that his speech still had a slight foreign accent. [49]

    ○谐戏附
    　　玄宗问黄幡绰：“是勿儿得人怜！”对曰：“自家儿得人怜。”上又尝登苑北楼，望渭水，见一醉人临水卧。问左右：“是何人？”左右不知，将遣使问之。幡绰曰：“是年满令史。”上问曰：“汝何以知？”对曰：“更一转入流。”上笑而止。上又与诸王会食，宁王对御坐喷一口饭，直及龙颜。上曰：“宁哥何故错喉？”幡绰曰：“此非错喉，是喷嚏。”
    　　周愿常奉使魏州，节度使田季安引之连辔。路周一驴极肥，季安指示愿曰：“此物大王世充。”应声答曰：“总是小窦建德。”李尚书选，性严毅，不好戏笑。时愿知江西盐铁留后事，将至。李公戒従事曰：“周生好谐谑，忝僭无礼，幸诸贤稍庄以待之。”及愿至，数宴。李公寒温外，不与之言，周亦无由得发。一日，馔亲宾，愿亦预焉。李公有故人子弟来投，落拓不事。李公遍问旧时别墅，及家童有技者，图书有名者，悉云卖却。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 是勿儿得人怜 - Playful phrase, likely a pun or term of endearment, meaning 'Why is this child so lovable?' or 'This child is so lovable.'
    • 自家儿得人怜 - Direct response, meaning 'Because he is my own child.'
    • 更一转入流 - Cryptic response, meaning 'He will become a regular official after one more promotion.'
    • 宁哥何故错喉 - Playful question, 'Why did Ning'er (a nickname for the prince) make a mistake in his throat?' A humorous way of asking why he spat.
    • 总是小窦建德 - Pun, 'He is just a little Sin Jian-de,' referring to the historical figure Sin Jian-de, a rebel leader, in a humorous context.
    • 忝僭无礼 - Formal expression meaning 'to be unworthy and presumptuous,' often used to apologize for one's own rudeness.
    • 落拓不事 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'lacking ambition and not doing anything meaningful.'
    • 悉云卖却 - Means 'all said they had sold them.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玄宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a prominent ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄幡绰 - A famous court jester known for his wit and humor.
    • 宁王 - Refers to Prince Ning, a member of the imperial family.
    • 田季安 - A military governor (Jiedushi) of Hui Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 周愿 - A Tang Dynasty official and historian.
    • 李尚书选 - A Tang Dynasty official known for his strict demeanor.
    • 小窦建德 - A reference to Sin Jian-de, a rebel leader during the Sui Dynasty, used here in a humorous context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 黄幡绰 - A famous court jester.
    • 宁王 - Prince Ning.
    • 田季安 - Jiedushi of Hui Prefecture.
    • 周愿 - A Tang Dynasty official and historian.
    • 李尚书选 - A Tang Dynasty official.
    • 小窦建德 - Historical figure, Sin Jian-de.

    Translation

    附录：谐戏

    玄宗问黄幡绰：“为什么这个孩子这么招人喜欢！”黄幡绰答道：“因为他是我自己的孩子。”玄宗又曾登上苑北楼，远眺渭水，看见一个醉汉躺在水边。玄宗问左右侍从：“这是什么人？”左右侍从不知，打算派人去询问。黄幡绰说：“他是一位满年资的令史。”玄宗问：“你怎么知道的？”黄幡绰答道：“他再过一次转正就能升官了。”玄宗听后笑了，不再追究。

    玄宗又与各位亲王共餐，宁王对着皇帝的座位喷了一口饭，直接喷到了皇帝的脸上。玄宗问：“宁哥为什么喷饭？”黄幡绰说：“这不是喷饭，是打喷嚏。”

    周愿曾奉命出使魏州，节度使田季安与他并辔而行。路上遇到一匹非常肥壮的驴，田季安指着驴对周愿说：“这头驴是大王世充的。”周愿应声答道：“这头驴是小窦建德的。”

    李尚书选性情严厉，不喜欢开玩笑。当时周愿担任江西盐铁留后，即将到任。李公告诫属下：“周生喜欢开玩笑，有时会僭越无礼，希望各位稍微庄重些对待他。”等到周愿到任后，李公多次设宴款待。除了寒暄，李公很少与周愿交谈，周愿也没有机会发话。有一天，李公宴请亲朋好友，周愿也参加了。李公有一位老朋友的子弟前来投奔，此人无所事事。李公问及这位子弟过去住过的别墅，以及家中有技能的仆人和有名气的图书，这位子弟都说已经卖掉了。 [50]

    李责曰：“郎君未官家贫，产业従卖，何至卖及书籍古画？”惆怅之久。复问曰：“有一本虞永兴手写《尚书》，此犹在否？”其人惭惧，不敢言卖，云：“暂将典钱。”愿忽言曰：“《尚书》大屯。”李公忘却先拒其谈谐之事，遂问曰：“《尚书》何屯？”愿曰：“已遭《尧典》《舜典》，又被此儿郎典。”李公兴怒之意大开，自此更不拒周。一日后，洪之属邑民产一子，有三首，李公览状恶之，久不怿，愿曰：“留待长大，令试幞头。”
    　　李纾侍郎好谐戏，又服用华鲜。尝朝回，以同列入坊门，有负贩者呵不避。李骂云：“头钱价奴兵，辄冲官长。”负者顾而言曰：“八钱价措大，漫作威风。”纾乐采异语，使仆者诱之至家，为设酒馔，徐问八钱之义。负者答曰：“只是衣短七耳。”同列以为破的，纾甚惭。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 郎君未官家贫，产业従卖 - Colloquial expression indicating that the young man, due to his family's poverty, has had to sell his property.
    • 惆怅之久 - Phrase expressing prolonged sadness or regret.
    • 有一本虞永兴手写《尚书》 - Refers to a handwritten copy of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) by Yu Yongxing, a calligrapher.
    • 此犹在否 - Rhetorical question asking if something still exists or is available.
    • 暂将典钱 - Colloquial expression meaning to temporarily pawn something for money.
    • 《尚书》大屯 - Playful or sarcastic remark, possibly implying that the *Shangshu* is a burden.
    • 《尚书》何屯 - Rhetorical question asking what burden the *Shangshu* represents.
    • 已遭《尧典》《舜典》，又被此儿郎典 - Humorous or sarcastic remark, suggesting that the *Shangshu* has already been pawned once and now again by a young man.
    • 头钱价奴兵，辄冲官长 - Colloquial expression implying that a lowly person (worth only a few coins) dares to collide with a higher-ranking official.
    • 八钱价措大，漫作威风 - Colloquial expression implying that a scholar (worth only eight coins) is trying to act important or authoritative.
    • 头钱价奴兵 - Colloquial expression implying that a lowly person (worth only a few coins) is like a soldier.
    • 八钱价措大 - Colloquial expression implying that a scholar (worth only eight coins) is like a commoner.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李责 - A character in the story, likely a high-ranking official or scholar.
    • 虞永兴 - A famous calligrapher during the Tang Dynasty, known for his work on the *Shangshu*.
    • 《尚书》 - The *Shangshu* (Book of Documents), one of the Five Classics of Confucianism.
    • 洪之属邑 - A place name, likely a county or district under the jurisdiction of a larger region.
    • 李纾侍郎 - A character in the story, likely a high-ranking official or scholar.
    • 负贩者 - A street vendor or peddler, often seen as a lowly person in classical Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李责 - A character in the story, likely a high-ranking official or scholar.
    • 虞永兴 - A famous calligrapher during the Tang Dynasty, known for his work on the *Shangshu*.
    • 《尚书》 - The *Shangshu* (Book of Documents), one of the Five Classics of Confucianism.
    • 洪之属邑 - A place name, likely a county or district under the jurisdiction of a larger region.
    • 李纾侍郎 - A character in the story, likely a high-ranking official or scholar.
    • 负贩者 - A street vendor or peddler, often seen as a lowly person in classical Chinese literature.

    Li Zhe said, "Since you are not yet an official and your family is poor, you have sold your property, but why would you sell your books and ancient paintings as well?" He felt sad for a long time. Then he asked, "Do you still have a copy of the Shangshu (Book of Documents) written by Yu Yongxing?" The person felt ashamed and afraid, and did not dare to admit that he had sold it. He said, "I have temporarily pawned it for money." Zou Wanyuan suddenly said, "The Shangshu is a great burden." Li Zhe forgot about his earlier refusal to engage in witty conversation, and asked, "What burden is the Shangshu?" Zou Wanyuan replied, "It has already been pawned once for the Yao Dian and Shun Dian, and now again by this young man." Li Zhe's anger grew, and he no longer resisted Zou Wanyuan. The next day, a man from a county under the jurisdiction of Hong gave birth to a child with three heads. Li Zhe saw the report and was displeased, and remained unhappy for a long time. Zou Wanyuan said, "Wait until he grows up, and let him try on a hat."

    Li Shu, the Censor-in-Chief, enjoyed witty banter and wore luxurious clothes. Once, after returning from court, he entered the market with his colleagues. A peddler shouted at him without yielding. Li Shu cursed, "A worthless soldier worth only a few coins, how dare you collide with an official?" The peddler turned around and said, "A scholar worth only eight coins, acting so important." Li Shu enjoyed hearing unusual sayings, and had his servant lure the peddler to his home, where he set up a feast. He slowly asked the meaning of "eight coins." The peddler replied, "It just means that my clothes are seven inches too short." Li Shu's colleagues thought this was a clever answer, and Li Shu felt very ashamed. [51]

    京兆庞尹及第后，従事寿春。有江淮举人，姓严，是《登科记》误本，倒书庞、严姓名，遂赁舟丐食。就谒时，郡中止有一判官，亦更不问其氏，便诣门投刺，称従侄。庞之族人甚少，览刺极喜，延纳殷勤，便留款曲，兼命对举匕筋。久之，语及族人，都非庞氏之事，庞方讶之。因问止竟：“郎君何姓？”曰：“某姓严。”庞抚掌大笑曰：“君误矣！余自姓庞，预君何事？”揖之令去。其人尚拜谢叔父，従容而退。
    　　姚岘有文学，而好滑稽，遇机即发。姚仆射南仲，廉察陕郊。岘初释艰服候见，以宗従之旧，延于中堂。吊讫，未语及他事。陕当两京之路，宾客谒无时。门外忽有投刺者云：“李过庭。”仆射曰：“过庭之名甚新，未知谁家子弟？”客将左右，皆称不知，又问岘：“知之否？”岘初犹俯首颦眉，顷之自不可忍，敛手言曰：“恐是李趋儿。”仆射久方悟而大笑。 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 従事 - A term indicating a person holding an official position, equivalent to 'serving as an official' or 'working as an administrator.'
    • 舉人 - Refers to candidates who passed the provincial examinations in the imperial examination system.
    • 倒書 - Means 'miswritten' or 'reversed,' indicating a mistake in writing.
    • 投刺 - A formal greeting card or letter of introduction used in ancient China.
    • 従侄 - Refers to a distant relative, specifically a nephew or cousin.
    • 延納殷勤 - Means 'to welcome warmly and hospitably.'
    • 留款曲 - Means 'to entertain with great courtesy and politeness.'
    • 命對舉匕筋 - Means 'to invite to eat together,' where '舉匕筋' refers to using chopsticks and spoons.
    • 俯首颦眉 - A gesture indicating thoughtfulness or hesitation, often translated as 'lowering one’s head and frowning.'
    • 従容而退 - Means 'to leave gracefully and calmly.'
    • 滑稽 - In this context, it refers to wit and humor, often used to describe someone who is clever and quick-witted.
    • 廉察 - Refers to the act of inspecting or supervising, often in an official capacity.
    • 候見 - Means 'to wait and see' or 'to meet formally.'
    • 宗従之舊 - Refers to a long-standing relationship within the same clan or family.
    • 拜謝叔父 - Means 'to bow and thank the uncle,' indicating a show of respect and gratitude.
    • 従容 - Means 'gracefully' or 'calmly.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京兆庞尹及第后，従事寿春 - This refers to a person named Pang, who passed the imperial examinations and was appointed to serve in Shouchun (a place name).
    • 江淮举人 - Refers to a candidate from the Jianghuai region who took the provincial examinations.
    • 《登科记》 - A historical record of successful candidates in the imperial examinations.
    • 李过庭 - A person whose name is mentioned, but no specific historical figure is known.
    • 姚岘 - A person known for his literary talent and wit.
    • 姚仆射南仲 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Yao Nanzhong, who held the position of Shao Fu (a high-ranking minister).
    • 陕郊 - Refers to the area around Shaanxi, indicating the region where Yao Nanzhong served.
    • 李趋儿 - A name mentioned as a possible identity of the visitor, but it is unclear who this person is.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庞尹 - A person named Pang.
    • 严 - A person named Yan.
    • 《登科记》 - A historical record of successful candidates.
    • 李过庭 - A person whose name is mentioned.
    • 姚岘 - A person known for his literary talent and wit.
    • 姚仆射南仲 - A high-ranking official named Yao Nanzhong.

    After passing the imperial examinations, Pang, the magistrate of Jingzhao, was appointed to serve in Shouchun. There was a candidate from the Jianghuai region named Yan, whose name was mistakenly written in reverse in the Dengkeji (Record of Successful Candidates). He then rented a boat and begged for food. When he visited the official, there was only one judge in the prefecture, who did not ask about his surname and welcomed him warmly, inviting him to the hall and introducing himself as a distant relative. Pang had few relatives, and he was very pleased to see the candidate, welcoming him warmly and entertaining him with great courtesy, even inviting him to eat together. After some time, when they talked about family matters, it turned out that the candidate was not related to Pang. Pang was surprised and asked, "What is your name, young master?" The candidate replied, "I am surnamed Yan." Pang clapped his hands and laughed, saying, "You are mistaken! I am surnamed Pang. What business do I have with you?" He bowed and dismissed the candidate, who still bowed and thanked him as an uncle before leaving gracefully.

    Yao Xian was known for his literary talent and his fondness for humor and wit, often making jokes when the opportunity arose. Yao Nanzhong, a high-ranking official, was inspecting the area around Shaanxi. When Yao Xian first paid a formal visit, he was warmly received in the main hall due to their long-standing family ties. After the formal greeting, no other topics were discussed. The area around Shaanxi was on the route between the two capitals, and visitors could come at any time. Suddenly, a visitor introduced himself as Li Guoteng. Yao Nanzhong said, "The name Guoteng is quite new; I don't know which family he belongs to." His attendants and those around him all said they did not know. He then asked Yao Xian, "Do you know him?" Yao Xian initially lowered his head and frowned, but soon could not hold back his laughter and said, "I think it might be Li Quci." Yao Nanzhong eventually realized the joke and laughed. [52]

    卢尚书弘宣，与弟卢衢州简辞同在京。一日衢州早出，尚书问：“有何除改？”答曰：“无大除改，惟皮遐叔蜀中刺史。”尚书不知皮是遐叔姓，谓是宗人，低头久之曰：“我弭当家，没处得卢皮遐来。”衢州为辩之，皆大笑。
    　　有人说李寰建节晋州，表兄武恭，性诞妄。又称好道，及蓄古物。遇寰生日，无饷遣，乃箱擎一故皂袄子与寰云：“此是李令公收复京师时所服，愿尚书功业一似西平。”寰以书谢。后闻知恭生日，箱擎一破腻脂幞头饷恭曰：“知兄深慕高贞，求得一洪崖先生初得仙时幞头，愿兄得道，一如洪崖。”宾僚无不大笑。余尝读谢绰宗《拾遗录》云：“江夏王义恭，性爱古物，常遍就朝士求之。侍中何勖已有所送，而王征索不已，何甚不平。尝出行于道，遇狗枷败犊鼻，乃命左右取之还，以箱擎送之。笺曰：‘承复须古物，今奉李斯狗枷，相如犊鼻。’”此颇与寰、恭相类耳。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 與弟盧衢州簡辭同在京 - Classical structure indicating a shared location and position; means 'along with his younger brother, Lu Jianci, who was in charge of Quzhou, they were both in the capital.'
    • 有何除改 - Colloquial way of asking if there have been any promotions or changes in position.
    • 無大除改 - Means 'no major promotions or changes,' indicating that there were no significant appointments.
    • 惟皮遐叔蜀中刺史 - Emphasizes that this is the only change; means 'only Pi Xiat叔 was appointed as the governor of Shu.'
    • 我弭當家 - Ambiguous phrase likely meaning 'I'm not sure what you mean' or 'I don't understand what you're saying.'
    • 沒處得盧皮遐來 - Ambiguous phrase possibly meaning 'I don't know where to find Lu Pi Xiat叔' or 'I can't find Lu Pi Xiat叔.'
    • 李寰建節晉州 - Means 'Li Huan was appointed as the military governor of Jin Prefecture.'
    • 表兄武恭 - Means 'his cousin, Wu Gong,' where '表兄' indicates a cousin by marriage.
    • 性誕妄 - Means 'he was eccentric' or 'he had a wild nature.'
    • 好道 - Means 'he loved the Dao' or 'he was interested in Daoist practices.'
    • 及蓄古物 - Means 'and he collected ancient objects.'
    • 遇寰生日 - Means 'on Li Huan's birthday.'
    • 無饋遣 - Means 'he did not send any gifts.'
    • 箱擎一故皂襖子 - Means 'he sent a box containing an old black robe.'
    • 此是李令公收復京師時所服 - Means 'this is the robe that Li Linggong wore when he recaptured the capital.'
    • 願尚書功業一似西平 - Means 'I hope your achievements will be as great as those of Xiping.'
    • 後聞知恭生日 - Means 'later, when Wu Gong had his birthday.'
    • 箱擎一破膩脂幞頭 - Means 'he sent a box containing an old, greasy cap.'
    • 知兄深慕高貞 - Means 'knowing that you deeply admire high virtue and purity.'
    • 求得一洪崖先生初得仙時幞頭 - Means 'I have obtained a cap that the Mr. Hongya wore when he first became an immortal.'
    • 願兄得道，一如洪崖 - Means 'I hope you achieve immortality just like Mr. Hongya.'
    • 賓僚無不大笑 - Means 'all the guests and officials laughed heartily.'
    • 謝綽宗《拾遺錄》 - Means 'Xie Chaozong's 'Collected Oddities.'
    • 江夏王義恭 - Means 'King Yigong of Jiangxia.'
    • 性愛古物 - Means 'he loved ancient objects.'
    • 常遍就朝士求之 - Means 'he often asked court officials for them.'
    • 侍中何勖已有所送 - Means 'the Secretary of State, He Xu, had already sent him something.'
    • 而王徵索不已 - Means 'but the king continued to ask for more.'
    • 何甚不平 - Means 'He Xu was very unhappy.'
    • 嘗出行於道 - Means 'once, while traveling on the road.'
    • 遇狗枷敗牐鼻 - Means 'he encountered an old dog collar and a worn-out apron.'
    • 乃命左右取之還 - Means 'he then ordered his attendants to bring them back.'
    • 以箱擎送之 - Means 'he sent them in a box.'
    • 箋曰：承復須古物，今奉李斯狗枷，相如牐鼻 - Means 'in the note he wrote: 'Knowing that you still want ancient objects, I am sending you the dog collar of Li Si and the apron of Sima Xiangru.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 盧尚書弘宣 - Refers to Lu Hongxuan, a high-ranking official.
    • 盧衢州簡辭 - Refers to Lu Jianci, who was the prefect of Quzhou.
    • 皮遐叔 - Refers to Pi Xiat叔, a historical figure.
    • 蜀中刺史 - Refers to the governor of Shu, an important administrative position.
    • 李寰 - Refers to Li Huan, a military governor.
    • 晉州 - Refers to Jin Prefecture, an important region.
    • 武恭 - Refers to Wu Gong, a historical figure.
    • 洪崖先生 - Refers to Mr. Hongya, a legendary figure in Daoist tradition.
    • 江夏王義恭 - Refers to King Yigong of Jiangxia, a historical figure.
    • 李斯 - Refers to Li Si, a famous chancellor of the Qin dynasty.
    • 相如 - Refers to Sima Xiangru, a famous literary figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 謝綽宗《拾遺錄》 - Refers to a historical text compiled by Xie Chaozong.
    • 皮遐叔 - Historical figure whose name is mentioned in the text.
    • 洪崖先生 - Legendary figure in Daoist tradition, often associated with immortality.
    • 李斯 - Famous chancellor of the Qin dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese law and administration.
    • 相如 - Famous literary figure from the Han dynasty, known for his poetry and prose.

    Proper Nouns

    • 盧尚書弘宣 - Lu Hongxuan, a high-ranking official.
    • 盧衢州簡辭 - Lu Jianci, the prefect of Quzhou.
    • 皮遐叔 - Pi Xiat叔, a historical figure.
    • 李寰 - Li Huan, a military governor.
    • 武恭 - Wu Gong, a historical figure.
    • 洪崖先生 - Mr. Hongya, a legendary figure in Daoist tradition.
    • 江夏王義恭 - King Yigong of Jiangxia, a historical figure.
    • 李斯 - Li Si, a famous chancellor of the Qin dynasty.
    • 相如 - Sima Xiangru, a famous literary figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 謝綽宗 - Xie Chaozong, the author of 'Collected Oddities.'

    Lu Shangshu Hongxuan, along with his younger brother, Lu Jianci, the prefect of Quzhou, were both in the capital. One day, when Jianci was about to leave early, Shangshu asked, "Have there been any promotions or changes?" Jianci replied, "There are no major promotions or changes, only Pi Xiat叔 was appointed as the governor of Shu." Shangshu did not know that Pi was Xiat叔's surname and thought he was a relative, so he lowered his head for a long time and said, "I don't understand what you mean; I can't find Lu Pi Xiat叔." Jianci then explained, and they all laughed heartily.

    It is said that Li Huan was appointed as the military governor of Jin Prefecture, and his cousin, Wu Gong, was eccentric and loved the Dao. He also collected ancient objects. On Li Huan's birthday, Wu Gong did not send any gifts, but instead sent a box containing an old black robe and said, "This is the robe that Li Linggong wore when he recaptured the capital. I hope your achievements will be as great as those of Xiping." Li Huan thanked him in a letter. Later, when Wu Gong had his birthday, he sent a box containing an old, greasy cap and said, "Knowing that you deeply admire high virtue and purity, I have obtained a cap that the Mr. Hongya wore when he first became an immortal. I hope you achieve immortality just like Mr. Hongya." All the guests and officials laughed heartily.

    I once read Xie Chaozong's "Collected Oddities," which states: "King Yigong of Jiangxia loved ancient objects and often asked court officials for them. The Secretary of State, He Xu, had already sent him something, but the king continued to ask for more. He Xu was very unhappy. Once, while traveling on the road, he encountered an old dog collar and a worn-out apron. He then ordered his attendants to bring them back and sent them in a box. In the note he wrote: 'Knowing that you still want ancient objects, I am sending you the dog collar of Li Si and the apron of Sima Xiangru.'" This is quite similar to the cases of Li Huan and Wu Gong. [53]

    衢州视事际，有妇人姓翁，陈牒论田产，称“阿公阿翁在日”，坐客笑之。因征其类。余尝目睹者，王屋有梓人女曰阿家，京中有阿辅，洪州有阿姑，蜀中有阿母，洛中有阿伯、阿郎，皆因其姓，亦堪笑也。

    　　卷五 徵部
    　　（徵为事，凡不为其人与物而泛说者，皆入此部）
    　　尚书省东南隅通衢有小桥，相承目为“拗项桥”。言侍御史及殿中诸郎，久次者至此，心拗项而望南宫也。
    　　都堂南门东道，有古槐垂阴至广。相传夜深闻丝竹之音，省中即有人人相者，俗谓之“音声树”。祠部呼为冰厅，言其清且冷也。
    　　尚书省二十四司印，故事：悉纳直厅，每郎官交直时，吏人悬之于臂以相授，颇觉为烦。杨虔州虞卿任吏部员外郎，始置柜加钥以贮之，人以为便，至今不改。柜初成，州戎时为吏部郎中，大书其上，戏作考词状：“当有千有万，忍俊不禁考上下。” [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 衢州视事际 - At the time of handling affairs in Quzhou, where '视事' refers to official duties.
    • 陈牒论田产 - Presenting a petition concerning land property, where '陈牒' means to submit a formal document.
    • 称‘阿公阿翁在日’ - Calling out 'Grandfather and Grandfather were alive,' where '在日' means 'when they were alive.'
    • 坐客笑之 - The seated guests laughed at her, where '坐客' refers to the audience or listeners.
    • 因征其类 - Therefore, he asked for similar examples, where '征' means to seek or inquire.
    • 余尝目睹者 - I have personally seen, where '余' is the first-person pronoun 'I' and '尝' means 'once' or 'ever.'
    • 王屋有梓人女曰阿家 - In Wangwu, there is a carpenter's daughter named Ah Jia, where '梓人' refers to a carpenter.
    • 京中有阿辅 - In the capital, there is Ah Fu, where '阿辅' is a name.
    • 洪州有阿姑 - In Hongzhou, there is Ah Gu, where '阿姑' is a name.
    • 蜀中有阿母 - In Shu, there is Ah Mu, where '阿母' is a name.
    • 洛中有阿伯、阿郎 - In Luoyang, there are Ah Bo and Ah Lang, where '阿伯' and '阿郎' are names.
    • 皆因其姓，亦堪笑也 - All of them are named after their surnames, and it is also laughable, where '堪笑' means 'worthy of laughter.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 衢州 - A place name, the current Quzhou, Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 陈牒 - A formal document submitted to an official, often used in legal or administrative contexts.
    • 阿公阿翁 - Common terms of respect for elderly male relatives, 'Grandfather' and 'Grandfather.'
    • 王屋 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 梓人 - A carpenter or woodworker.
    • 京中 - Refers to the capital city, likely referring to the capital of the time.
    • 洪州 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 蜀中 - A place name, likely referring to the region of Sichuan.
    • 洛中 - A place name, likely referring to the region of Luoyang.

    Proper Nouns

    • 衢州 - The place name Quzhou.
    • 王屋 - The place name Wangwu.
    • 京中 - The capital city.
    • 洪州 - The place name Hongzhou.
    • 蜀中 - The region of Sichuan.
    • 洛中 - The region of Luoyang.
    • 阿家 - A name.
    • 阿辅 - A name.
    • 阿姑 - A name.
    • 阿母 - A name.
    • 阿伯 - A name.
    • 阿郎 - A name.

    At the time of handling affairs in Quzhou, there was a woman surnamed Wang who presented a petition concerning land property, calling out, "Grandfather and Grandfather were alive." The seated guests laughed at her. Therefore, he asked for similar examples. I have personally seen: in Wangwu, there is a carpenter's daughter named Ah Jia; in the capital, there is Ah Fu; in Hongzhou, there is Ah Gu; in Shu, there is Ah Mu; in Luoyang, there are Ah Bo and Ah Lang. All of them are named after their surnames, and it is also laughable.

    Volume 5: Miscellaneous Department
    (The Miscellaneous Department deals with matters that are not specific to individuals or objects but are broadly discussed.)

    In the southeastern corner of the Shangshu Province, there is a small bridge on a major thoroughfare, which has been traditionally called the "Crooked-Neck Bridge." It is said that when the officials of the Cishi and Dianzhong bureaus, who have served for a long time, reach this point, they crane their necks to look toward the Southern Palace.

    On the eastern path of the southern gate of the main hall, there is an ancient槐tree whose shade extends very wide. It is said that at night, the sound of music can be heard, and within the province, there are people who can divine the future, commonly known as the "Sound Tree." The Cibu Bureau calls it the Ice Hall, saying that it is both clear and cold.

    In the Shangshu Province, the seals of the twenty-four departments have a tradition: they are all kept in the straight office. When the officials exchange their duties, the clerks hang them on their arms to pass them on, which is quite cumbersome. Yang Yuanzhou, serving as the Director of the Personnel Bureau, first placed them in a cabinet with a lock to store them, which people found convenient, and it has not been changed since. When the cabinet was first made, the state commander, who was then the Director of the Personnel Bureau, wrote a large inscription on it, jokingly creating a test paper: "There are thousands and tens of thousands, unable to resist laughing, pass or fail." [54]

    秘书省内有落星石，薛少保画鹤，贺监草书，郎余令画凤，相传号为“四绝”。元和中，韩公武为秘书郎，挟弹中鹤一眼，时谓之“五绝”。又省之东，即右威卫，荒秽摧毁，其大厅逼校书院，南对御史台，有人嘲之曰：“门缘御史塞，庙被校书侵。”
    　　御史台三院，一曰台院。其僚曰侍御史，众呼为“端公”。见宰相及台长，则曰“某姓侍御”。知杂事，谓之“杂端”。见台长，则曰“知杂侍御”。虽他官高秩兼之，其侍御号不改。见宰相，则曰“知杂某姓某官”。台院非知杂者，乃俗号“散端”。二曰殿院。其僚曰殿中侍御史，众呼为“侍御”。见宰相及台长“杂端”则曰“某姓殿中”。最新入，知右巡，已次知左巡，号“两巡使”，所主繁剧。及迁向上，则又入推，益为劳屑，惟其中间，则入清闲。故台中谚曰：“免巡未推，只得自知。”言其畅适也。厅有壁画小？山水甚工，云是吴道玄真迹。三曰察院。 [55]

    Within the Secretariat, there is a meteorite stone, and paintings of cranes by Xue Shaobao, calligraphy by He Zhizhang, and paintings of peacocks by Lang Yuling, which are collectively known as the "Four Treasures." During the Yuanhe period, Han Gongwu, serving as a secretary, shot one eye of a crane with a crossbow, and this incident was later referred to as the "Five Treasures." To the east of the Secretariat lies the Right Wing Guards, which are in ruins and overgrown with weeds. Its main hall is close to the Imperial Library and faces the Yushi Tai (Imperial Censorate) to the south. Someone mocked it by saying, "The gate is blocked by the Yushi, and the temple is invaded by the Xiangshu."

    The Yushi Tai has three departments: the first is the Taiyuan (Main Department). Its officials are called Shi Yu (Imperial Censors), commonly referred to as "Duan Gong." When they meet with the Chancellor or the head of the Yushi Tai, they address them as "Mr. Shi Yu of the surname." Those who handle miscellaneous affairs are called "Za Duan." When they meet the head of the Yushi Tai, they address them as "Za Duan Shi Yu." Even if other high-ranking officials also hold this position, their title remains unchanged. When they meet the Chancellor, they address them as "Za Duan Mr. of the surname and official rank." Those in the Taiyuan department who do not handle miscellaneous affairs are commonly called "San Duan." The second department is the Dianyuan (Palace Department). Its officials are called Dian Zhong Shi Yu (Imperial Censors of the Palace), commonly referred to as "Shi Yu." When they meet the Chancellor or the head of the Yushi Tai, they address them as "Mr. Dian Zhong of the surname." The newest arrivals are assigned to the right patrol, followed by the left patrol, and are known as "Liang Xun Shi" (Two Patrol Officials). Their duties are numerous and complex. When they advance in rank, they enter the department responsible for investigations and trials, which is even more laborious. However, during the intervals between these duties, they enjoy a period of leisure. Hence, there is a saying in the Yushi Tai: "Avoid the patrols and the trials, and you will only know yourself." This refers to the ease and comfort of their leisure time. The hall has murals of small landscapes that are very skillful, and it is said to be the true work of Wu Daozi. The third department is the Chaoyuan (Investigation Department). [55]

    其僚曰监察御史，众呼亦曰“侍御”。见宰相及台长“杂端”，则曰“某姓监察”。若三院同见台长，则通曰“三院侍御”，而主簿纪其所行之事。每公堂食会，杂事不至，则无所检辖，惟相揖而已。杂事至，则尽用宪府之礼，杂端在南揖，主簿在北揖，两院则分坐，虽举匕筋，皆绝谈笑。食毕，则主簿持黄卷揖曰：“请举事。”于是台院白杂端曰：“举事。”则举曰：“某姓侍御有某过，请准条。”主簿书之。若举时差错，则最小殿中举院长，最小侍御史举殿院长。若杂端失笑，则三院皆笑，谓之“烘堂”，悉免罚矣。凡见黄卷罚直遇赦悉免，台长到诸院，凡官吏所罚，亦悉免。御史历三院虽至美，而月满殿中推鞫之劳，惮于转两院。 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其僚曰监察御史 - Formal title for an official; 'His colleagues are called Inspector of Justice.'
    • 众呼亦曰“侍御” - Informal term for the same official; 'People also call him 'Inspector of Justice.'
    • 见宰相及台长“杂端” - Specific title or role within the Censorate; 'When he meets the Prime Minister and the head of the Censorate.'
    • 则曰“某姓监察” - Formal address; 'He says, 'Inspector of Justice surnamed [name].' indicating a formal address.'
    • 若三院同见台长 - Three departments of the Censorate; 'If all three departments meet the head of the Censorate together.'
    • 则通曰“三院侍御” - Collective address; 'They collectively call themselves 'Inspectors of Justice from all three departments.'
    • 而主簿纪其所行之事 - Clerk responsible for record-keeping; 'And the clerk records their actions.'
    • 每公堂食会 - Formal gathering in the official residence; 'At each official banquet in the hall.'
    • 杂事不至 - Assistant or subordinate official; 'If the assistant is not present.'
    • 则无所检辖 - Lack of supervision; 'There is no one to oversee.'
    • 惟相揖而已 - Formal greeting; 'They only bow to each other.'
    • 杂事至 - Presence of the assistant; 'If the assistant arrives.'
    • 则尽用宪府之礼 - Strict rules of the Censorate; 'They then follow the strict rules of the Censorate.'
    • 杂端在南揖 - Specific position during the ritual; 'The '杂端' bows from the south.'
    • 主簿在北揖 - Another specific position during the ritual; 'The clerk bows from the north.'
    • 两院则分坐 - Officials from two departments sit separately; 'The officials from the two departments sit separately.'
    • 虽举匕筋 - Act of eating; 'Even when eating with chopsticks and spoons.'
    • 皆绝谈笑 - Remain completely silent; 'They remain completely silent.'
    • 食毕 - End of the formal dining session; 'After finishing the meal.'
    • 主簿持黄卷揖曰 - Clerk holds a formal document; 'The clerk holds a yellow scroll and bows, saying.'
    • “请举事” - Request to proceed with business; 'Please proceed with the business.'
    • 于是台院白杂端曰 - Officials of the Censorate inform the '杂端'; 'Then the officials of the Censorate inform the '杂端,' saying.'
    • “举事” - Repeat the request; 'Proceed with the business.'
    • 则举曰 - Formal presentation of a matter; 'They then raise the issue.'
    • “某姓侍御有某过” - Specific fault or mistake; 'Inspector of Justice surnamed [name] has a fault.'
    • 请准条 - Request for adherence to established rules; 'Please follow the regulations.'
    • 主簿书之 - Documentation of the issue; 'The clerk records it.'
    • 若举时差错 - Mistake in the procedure; 'If there is an error in raising the issue.'
    • 则最小殿中举院长 - Smallest official from the Palace Department; 'The smallest official from the Palace Department raises the issue.'
    • 最小侍御史举殿院长 - Smallest Inspector of Justice; 'The smallest Inspector of Justice raises the issue.'
    • 若杂端失笑 - Breach of decorum; 'If the '杂端' laughs.'
    • 则三院皆笑 - Shared reaction; 'Then all three departments laugh.'
    • 谓之“烘堂” - Collective laugh that breaks the formality; 'It is called '烘堂'.'
    • 悉免罚矣 - Exempt from punishment; 'They are all exempt from punishment.'
    • 凡见黄卷罚直遇赦悉免 - Penalty recorded in the yellow scroll is waived upon amnesty; 'Any penalty recorded in the yellow scroll is waived upon amnesty.'
    • 台长到诸院 - Head of the Censorate visits the various departments; 'When the head of the Censorate visits the various departments.'
    • 凡官吏所罚 - Penalties imposed on officials; 'Any penalties imposed on officials.'
    • 亦悉免 - Comprehensive exemption; 'Are also waived.'
    • 御史历三院虽至美 - Inspectors of Justice who have served in all three departments are highly esteemed; 'Inspectors of Justice who have served in all three departments are highly esteemed.'
    • 而月满殿中推鞫之劳 - Weary from the monthly investigations in the Palace Department; 'But they are weary from the monthly investigations in the Palace Department.'
    • 惮于转两院 - Reluctant to transfer to the other two departments; 'They are reluctant to transfer to the other two departments.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 监察御史 - Specific title for an official in the Censorate, responsible for supervising government officials and ensuring compliance with laws and regulations.
    • 侍御 - Informal term for the same official, reflecting a more personal or less formal relationship.
    • 杂端 - Specific title or role within the Censorate, possibly referring to a senior official or a specific position.
    • 三院 - The three departments of the Censorate, which were part of the administrative structure of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 主簿 - The title of the clerk responsible for record-keeping and administrative tasks.
    • 宪府 - The Censorate or the office of censors, a key institution for oversight and accountability in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄卷 - A formal document or record used in official proceedings.
    • 烘堂 - A term for a collective laugh that breaks the formality of a situation, often resulting in leniency or exemption from punishment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 监察御史 - Inspector of Justice.
    • 侍御 - Informal term for Inspector of Justice.
    • 杂端 - A specific title or role within the Censorate.
    • 三院 - The three departments of the Censorate.
    • 主簿 - Clerk

    The colleagues of the Inspector of Justice are called "Inspector of Justice," and people also refer to them as "Inspector of Justice." When they meet the Prime Minister and the head of the Censorate, known as "杂端," they say, "Inspector of Justice surnamed [name]." If all three departments meet the head of the Censorate together, they collectively call themselves "Inspectors of Justice from all three departments," and the clerk records their actions. At each official banquet in the hall, if the assistant is not present, there is no one to oversee, and they only bow to each other. If the assistant arrives, they then follow the strict rules of the Censorate, with the "杂端" bowing from the south and the clerk bowing from the north. The officials from the two departments sit separately, even when eating with chopsticks and spoons, they remain completely silent. After finishing the meal, the clerk holds a yellow scroll and bows, saying, "Please proceed with the business." Then the officials of the Censorate inform the "杂端," saying, "Proceed with the business." They then raise the issue, saying, "Inspector of Justice surnamed [name] has a fault, please follow the regulations." The clerk records it. If there is an error in raising the issue, the smallest official from the Palace Department raises the issue, and the smallest Inspector of Justice raises the issue. If the "杂端" laughs, then all three departments laugh, it is called "烘堂," and they are all exempt from punishment. Any penalty recorded in the yellow scroll is waived upon amnesty, and when the head of the Censorate visits the various departments, any penalties imposed on officials are also waived. Inspectors of Justice who have served in all three departments are highly esteemed, but they are weary from the monthly investigations in the Palace Department and are reluctant to transfer to the other two departments. [56]

    以向下侍御史，便领推也，多不愿为，以此台中以殿中转两院为戏谑之辞。每出入行步，侍御史在柱里，殿察两院在柱外，有时殿中入柱里，则共咍之曰：“著去也。”三院御史主簿有事白端公，就其厅。若有中路白事，谓之“篸端。”篸端有罚。殿中已免巡，遇正知巡者假故，则向上人又权知，谓之“蘸巡”。台官有亲受除拜喜庆之事，则谒院长、杂端、台长，谓之“取贺”。凡此皆因胥徒走卒之言，遂成故事。院长每上堂了各报，诸御史皆立于南廊，便服靸鞋以俟院长。立定，院长方出。相揖而序行，至殿院门，揖殿中。又序行，至食堂门，揖侍御史，凡入门至食，凡数揖。大抵揖者，古之肃拜也。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 便领推也 - Means 'to take charge of investigations.'
    • 多不愿为 - Indicates that many do not wish to undertake this role.
    • 以此台中以殿中转两院为戏谑之辞 - Suggests that the phrase 'from the Palace to the two courts' has become a term of mockery within the office.
    • 篸端 - A specific type of report with a penalty.
    • 蘸巡 - Temporary patrol duty.
    • 取贺 - Visiting to congratulate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 侍御史 - A lower-ranking judicial official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 殿察两院 - Refers to the two courts of the palace.
    • 胥徒走卒 - Clerks and runners, lower-ranking officials who assist in administrative tasks.
    • 揖 - A formal bow, a gesture of respect in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 侍御史 - A lower-ranking judicial official.
    • 殿察两院 - The two courts of the palace.
    • 篸端 - A specific type of report.
    • 蘸巡 - Temporary patrol duty.
    • 胥徒走卒 - Clerks and runners.
    • 揖 - A formal bow.

    In the lower ranks of the judicial officials (侍御史), they are responsible for taking charge of investigations (便领推也). Many do not wish to undertake this role (多不愿为), and within the office, the phrase "from the Palace to the two courts" (以此台中以殿中转两院) has become a term of mockery (为戏谑之辞).

    Every day, the officials come and go (每出入行步). The lower-ranking judicial officials stand in the inner part of the building (侍御史在柱里), while the officials of the two courts stand outside (殿察两院在柱外). Sometimes, the Palace officials enter the inner part of the building (有时殿中入柱里), and they all laugh at him (共咍之曰：“著去也。”).

    The officials of the three courts and their clerks (三院御史主簿) report matters to the chief official (有事白端公) in his hall (就其厅). If there is an urgent matter to report along the way (若有中路白事), it is called "篸端" (谓之“篸端”), and there is a penalty for such reports (篸端有罚).

    The Palace officials are exempt from patrols (殿中已免巡). When the regular patrol officials are absent due to leave or illness (遇正知巡者假故), the higher-ranking officials temporarily take over (则向上人又权知), and this is called "蘸巡" (谓之“蘸巡”).

    When officials receive appointments or celebrate personal events (台官有亲受除拜喜庆之事), they visit the chief official, the deputy, and the head of the office (则谒院长、杂端、台长), and this is called "取贺" (谓之“取贺”).

    All these terms and practices are derived from the language of the clerks and runners (凡此皆因胥徒走卒之言), and thus have become established customs (遂成故事).

    The chief official reports to each person when he finishes his duties (院长每上堂了各报). All the judicial officials stand in the south corridor (诸御史皆立于南廊) in casual clothes and slippers (便服靸鞋) waiting for the chief official (以俟院长). When they are all standing, the chief official then comes out (立定，院长方出). They bow to each other and line up (相揖而序行). When they reach the palace court gate, they bow to the Palace officials (至殿院门，揖殿中). They line up again and bow to the judicial officials at the dining hall gate (又序行，至食堂门，揖侍御史). They bow several times when entering the hall and going to the dining area (凡入门至食，凡数揖). In general, bowing is the ancient form of respectful greeting (大抵揖者，古之肃拜也). [57]

    台中无不揖，其酒，无起谢之礼，但云揖酒而已，酒最合敬，以恐烦却损。往往自台拜他官执事，亦误作台揖，人皆笑之。每赴朝，序行至待漏院偃息，则有卧揖。上门有马揖。凡院长在厅院内，御史欲往他院，必先白。决罚又必先白。察院有都厅，院长在本厅，诸人皆会话于都厅，亦曰察院南院，会昌初，监察御史郑路所葺。礼察厅，谓之“松厅”，南有古松也。刑察厅谓之“魇厅”，寝于此多魇。兵察常主院中茶，茶必市蜀之佳者，贮于陶器，以防暑湿。御史躬亲缄启，故谓之“茶瓶厅。”吏察主院中入朝人次第名籍，谓之“朝簿厅。”吏察之上，则馆驿使。馆驿使之上，则监察使。监察使，同僚之冠也，谓之“院长”。台中敬长，三院皆有长。察院风彩尤峻。凡三院御史初拜，未朝谢，先谒院长。院长辞疾不见，则不得谢及上矣。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 台中无不揖 - Formal expression meaning 'in the office, there is no bowing' or 'in the office, one does not bow,' indicating a specific protocol.
    • 其酒，无起谢之礼，但云揖酒而已 - Formal expression meaning 'for wine, there is no need to stand up and thank; one simply bows to the wine,' indicating a specific ritual.
    • 酒最合敬，以恐烦却损 - Formal expression meaning 'wine is the most suitable for showing respect, because standing up and thanking might be too cumbersome and disrespectful.'
    • 往往自台拜他官执事，亦误作台揖，人皆笑之 - Formal expression meaning 'often, when officials from the office greet other officials, they mistakenly perform the office bow, and people laugh at them.'
    • 每赴朝，序行至待漏院偃息，则有卧揖 - Formal expression meaning 'every time going to court, when in line at the waiting hall, there is a lying bow.'
    • 上门有马揖 - Formal expression meaning 'at the gate, there is a horse bow.'
    • 凡院长在厅院内，御史欲往他院，必先白 - Formal expression meaning 'when the head of the office is in the hall, if a censor wants to go to another hall, they must first report.'
    • 决罚又必先白 - Formal expression meaning 'for punishment decisions, they must also first report.'
    • 察院有都厅，院长在本厅，诸人皆会话于都厅，亦曰察院南院，会昌初，监察御史郑路所葺 - Formal expression meaning 'the inspection office has a general hall; when the head of the office is in this hall, all people gather to speak in the general hall, also called the southern inspection office, built initially during the Kaiyuan era by the censor Zheng Lu.'
    • 礼察厅，谓之“松厅”，南有古松也 - Formal expression meaning 'the etiquette inspection hall is called the 'Pine Hall,' because there is an ancient pine tree to the south.'
    • 刑察厅谓之“魇厅”，寝于此多魇 - Formal expression meaning 'the criminal inspection hall is called the 'Nightmare Hall,' because many people have nightmares when sleeping there.'
    • 兵察常主院中茶，茶必市蜀之佳者，贮于陶器，以防暑湿 - Formal expression meaning 'the military inspection hall usually manages the tea in the office, and the tea must be bought from Sichuan, stored in pottery to prevent heat and dampness.'
    • 御史躬亲缄启，故谓之“茶瓶厅” - Formal expression meaning 'the censor personally seals and opens the tea, so it is called the 'Tea Bottle Hall.'
    • 吏察主院中入朝人次第名籍，谓之“朝簿厅” - Formal expression meaning 'the administrative inspection hall manages the list of people entering the court in order, so it is called the 'Court Register Hall.'
    • 吏察之上，则馆驿使 - Formal expression meaning 'above the administrative inspection hall, there is the host of the guesthouse.'
    • 馆驿使之上，则监察使 - Formal expression meaning 'above the host of the guesthouse, there is the censor.'
    • 监察使，同僚之冠也，谓之“院长” - Formal expression meaning 'the censor is the leader among peers, called the 'Head of the Office.'
    • 台中敬长，三院皆有长 - Formal expression meaning 'in the office, respect for the leader is important; each of the three halls has a leader.'
    • 察院风彩尤峻 - Formal expression meaning 'the inspection office has particularly strict rules.'
    • 凡三院御史初拜，未朝谢，先谒院长 - Formal expression meaning 'when censors from the three halls first pay their respects, they do not thank the court first but first visit the head of the office.'
    • 院长辞疾不见，则不得谢及上矣 - Formal expression meaning 'if the head of the office declines to see them due to illness, they cannot thank the court or ascend to the hall.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 台中 - Refers to the office or administrative center, likely the Censorate (御史台) in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 揖 - A formal bow or gesture of respect.
    • 待漏院 - A waiting hall where officials wait before entering the court.
    • 会昌初 - Refers to the early Kaiyuan era, a period in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 监察御史郑路 - Refers to Zheng Lu, a censor during the Kaiyuan era.
    • 松厅 - The Pine Hall, named for an ancient pine tree.
    • 魇厅 - The Nightmare Hall, named for the prevalence of nightmares.
    • 茶瓶厅 - The Tea Bottle Hall, named for the censor's personal management of tea.
    • 朝簿厅 - The Court Register Hall, named for managing the list of people entering the court.
    • 馆驿使 - The host of the guesthouse, responsible for lodging and hospitality.
    • 监察使 - The censor, the highest-ranking official in the Censorate.
    • 院长 - The head of the office, the leader among peers.

    Proper Nouns

    • 台中 - The office or administrative center.
    • 待漏院 - The waiting hall.
    • 会昌初 - The early Kaiyuan era.
    • 监察御史郑路 - Zheng Lu, a censor during the Kaiyuan era.
    • 松厅 - The Pine Hall.
    • 魇厅 - The Nightmare Hall.
    • 茶瓶厅 - The Tea Bottle Hall.
    • 朝簿厅 - The Court Register Hall.
    • 馆驿使 - The host of the guesthouse.
    • 监察使 - The censor.
    • 院长 - The head of the office.

    In the office, there is no bowing; for wine, there is no need to stand up and thank; one simply bows to the wine. Wine is the most suitable for showing respect, because standing up and thanking might be too cumbersome and disrespectful. Often, when officials from the office greet other officials, they mistakenly perform the office bow, and people laugh at them. Every time going to court, when in line at the waiting hall, there is a lying bow. At the gate, there is a horse bow. When the head of the office is in the hall, if a censor wants to go to another hall, they must first report. For punishment decisions, they must also first report. The inspection office has a general hall; when the head of the office is in this hall, all people gather to speak in the general hall, also called the southern inspection office, built initially during the Kaiyuan era by the censor Zheng Lu. The etiquette inspection hall is called the "Pine Hall," because there is an ancient pine tree to the south. The criminal inspection hall is called the "Nightmare Hall," because many people have nightmares when sleeping there. The military inspection hall usually manages the tea in the office, and the tea must be bought from Sichuan, stored in pottery to prevent heat and dampness. The censor personally seals and opens the tea, so it is called the "Tea Bottle Hall." The administrative inspection hall manages the list of people entering the court in order, so it is called the "Court Register Hall." Above the administrative inspection hall, there is the host of the guesthouse. Above the host of the guesthouse, there is the censor. The censor is the leader among peers, called the "Head of the Office." In the office, respect for the leader is important; each of the three halls has a leader. The inspection office has particularly strict rules. When censors from the three halls first pay their respects, they do not thank the court first but first visit the head of the office. If the head of the office declines to see them due to illness, they cannot thank the court or ascend to the hall. [58]

    北省班谏议，在给事中上。中书舍人，在给事中下。裴尚书休为谏议大夫，形质短小。诸舍人戏之曰：“如此短，何得向上立？”裴对曰：“若怪即曳向下着。”众皆大笑。后除舍人。 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 如此短，何得向上立 - Colloquial expression questioning why someone of short stature could stand above others.
    • 若怪即曳向下着 - Humorous retort, suggesting that if they find it strange, he will be pulled down.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北省 - Refers to the Northern Office, a part of the imperial administration.
    • 班谏议 - Title for a censor.
    • 给事中 - Title for an official.
    • 中书舍人 - Title for an official.
    • 裴尚书休 - Historical figure, a censor.
    • 谏议大夫 - Title for a censor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 裴尚书休 - Historical figure, a censor.
    • 谏议大夫 - Title for a censor.
    • 给事中 - Title for an official.
    • 中书舍人 - Title for an official.

    The censor of the Northern Office, Cai Jinyi, ranked above the position of "给事中." The "中书舍人" ranked below the position of "给事中." Pei Shangshu Xiu, as a censor, was of short stature. The other officials joked with him, saying, "How can someone so short stand above us?" Pei replied humorously, "If you find it strange, just pull me down." The others all laughed. Later, he was appointed as a "中书舍人." [59]

    或问东津先生曰：“昔人立法，将以利人邪？”曰：“利之。”曰：“何以后世反为害也？”曰：“因其利而奸生，则反害也。燧人钻木，致民火食，以熟百物，安知后世有咸阳焚烧宫室，三月不绝之毒。”伏羲画八卦，造书契，安知后世有假鬻文字，以市道欺诳时俗之弊后稷播百谷，安知后世有榷酤闭籴茶盐求利之苦轩辕制车服戎器，安知后世有华泽靡丽相尚，及穷兵黩武之弊，制宫室，安知后世有甲第别馆，凌云耀日唐虞建官列爵，安知后世有入金纳粟，冒取公侯禹平水土，别九州，安知后世有山林川泽之税，开津堰埭之难周公制嫁娶之礼，安知后世以依炫姻援，而求富贵孔子删《诗书》，定礼乐，垂五常之教，安知后世有掠儒之名，而盗声华，叨尊显凡此触类浇讹，流荡纪纲，大坏其本，岂圣人之过耶其由圣人启之耶其后世为治者，引而炽之耶呜呼！马肝之说，余不忍言。 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 或問 - Formal expression meaning 'someone asked' or 'one inquired,' used to introduce a dialogue.
    • 昔人立法 - Means 'ancient people established laws,' referring to the creation of legal systems by past rulers or sages.
    • 將以利人邪 - Rhetorical question meaning 'was it intended to benefit people?' The use of '邪' at the end softens the tone, making it more conversational.
    • 因其利而奸生 - Means 'because of the benefits, evil arises,' indicating that the unintended consequences of laws can lead to corruption.
    • 燧人钻木 - Refers to the mythological figure Suiren (燧人), who is credited with inventing fire by drilling wood, symbolizing human progress.
    • 致民火食 - Means 'to enable people to cook food with fire,' highlighting the practical benefit of fire.
    • 熟百物 - Means 'to cook all things,' emphasizing the transformative power of fire in food preparation.
    • 安知后世有 - Rhetorical phrase meaning 'how could one know that in later generations there would be,' introducing examples of negative outcomes.
    • 咸阳焚烧宫室 - Refers to the burning of the Qin palace in Xianyang, a historical event symbolizing destruction and chaos.
    • 三月不绝之毒 - Means 'a poison that did not cease for three months,' describing the prolonged suffering caused by the fire.
    • 伏羲画八卦 - Refers to Fuxi (伏羲), the mythical founder of Chinese civilization, who is said to have created the eight trigrams (八卦).
    • 造书契 - Means 'to create written characters,' referring to the invention of writing.
    • 假鬻文字 - Means 'to sell fake writings,' indicating the exploitation of writing for profit.
    • 市道欺诳时俗 - Means 'to deceive the customs of the time through market practices,' describing the manipulation of society through deceit.
    • 后稷播百谷 - Refers to Houji (后稷), the mythical founder of agriculture, who is credited with cultivating grains.
    • 榷酤闭籴茶盐求利之苦 - Means 'the suffering caused by monopolizing wine, grain, tea, and salt for profit,' referring to the exploitation of essential goods.
    • 轩辕制车服戎器 - Refers to the Yellow Emperor (轩辕), who is credited with inventing vehicles, clothing, and military equipment.
    • 华泽靡丽相尚 - Means 'to compete in luxurious and beautiful things,' describing the pursuit of material wealth and status.
    • 穷兵黩武 - Means 'excessive military campaigns,' referring to the overuse of military force.
    • 制宫室 - Means 'to build palaces and houses,' referring to the construction of dwellings.
    • 甲第别馆 - Means 'magnificent mansions and separate buildings,' describing luxurious residences.
    • 凌云耀日 - Means 'towering into the clouds and shining like the sun,' describing grand and impressive structures.
    • 唐虞建官列爵 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Yao (唐) and Emperor Shun (虞), who are credited with establishing government and ranks.
    • 入金纳粟 - Means 'to buy ranks by paying gold or grain,' describing the practice of purchasing official positions.
    • 冒取公侯 - Means 'to falsely claim titles of nobility,' referring to the fraudulent acquisition of high status.
    • 禹平水土 - Refers to Yu the Great (禹), who is credited with controlling floods and transforming the land.
    • 别九州 - Means 'to divide the world into nine provinces,' referring to the organization of territory.
    • 山林川泽之税 - Means 'taxes on mountains, forests, rivers, and lakes,' describing the exploitation of natural resources.
    • 开津堰埭之难 - Means 'the difficulties of opening channels and building dams,' referring to the challenges of water management.
    • 周公制嫁娶之礼 - Refers to the Duke of Zhou (周公), who is credited with establishing marriage rituals.
    • 依炫姻援 - Means 'to rely on炫耀 relationships and alliances,' describing the manipulation of social connections for personal gain.
    • 孔子删《诗书》，定礼乐，垂五常之教 - Refers to Confucius, who is credited with editing the *Book of Songs* and *Book of Documents*, establishing rites and music, and teaching the Five Constants (仁义礼智信).
    • 掠儒之名 - Means 'to steal the name of Confucian scholars,' referring to the misuse of scholarly authority.
    • 盗声华 - Means 'to steal fame and reputation,' describing the appropriation of prestige.
    • 叨尊显 - Means 'to falsely claim high status,' referring to the fraudulent acquisition of respect and honor.
    • 触类浇讹 - Means 'to corrupt through similar examples,' describing the spread of corruption.
    • 流荡纪纲 - Means 'to undermine the rules and order,' referring to the erosion of social norms.
    • 大坏其本 - Means 'to greatly damage the root,' describing the fundamental destruction of something.
    • 岂圣人之过耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is it the fault of the sages?' The use of '耶' softens the tone, making it more conversational.
    • 其由圣人启之耶 - Means 'Is it because the sages initiated it?' The use of '耶' again softens the tone.
    • 其后世为治者，引而炽之耶 - Means 'Is it because those who governed in later generations intensified it?' The use of '耶' again softens the tone.
    • 马肝之说 - Refers to a metaphorical expression, possibly alluding to the idea that even good things can have hidden dangers.
    • 余不忍言 - Means 'I cannot bear to speak of it,' expressing reluctance to discuss the topic further.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 燧人 - The mythical figure credited with inventing fire by drilling wood.
    • 伏羲 - The mythical founder of Chinese civilization, credited with creating the eight trigrams.
    • 后稷 - The mythical founder of agriculture, credited with cultivating grains.
    • 轩辕 - The Yellow Emperor, credited with inventing vehicles, clothing, and military equipment.
    • 唐虞 - The reigns of Emperor Yao and Emperor Shun, credited with establishing government and ranks.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, credited with controlling floods and transforming the land.
    • 周公 - The Duke of Zhou, credited with establishing marriage rituals.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, credited with editing the *Book of Songs* and *Book of Documents*, establishing rites and music, and teaching the Five Constants.
    • 咸阳焚烧宫室 - The historical event of the burning of the Qin palace in Xianyang, symbolizing destruction and chaos.
    • 五常 - The Five Constants (仁义礼智信), a set of moral principles in Confucianism.
    • 马肝之说 - A metaphorical expression, possibly alluding to the idea that even good things can have hidden dangers.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The opening dialogue introduces the question and sets the tone for the subsequent discussion.
    • The responses are concise and direct, setting up the contrast between intended benefits and unintended consequences.
    • This response explains the cause-and-effect relationship between benefits and corruption.
    • This long sentence introduces the example of fire and its unintended consequences, requiring careful segmentation.
    • This long sequence of examples requires careful segmentation to maintain clarity and readability.
    • This final sequence of rhetorical questions requires careful segmentation to maintain the flow and impact of the argument.
    • This closing statement expresses the speaker's reluctance to discuss the topic further.

    A scholar asked Mr. Dongjin, "Did ancient people establish laws with the intention of benefiting people?" He replied, "To benefit them." The scholar then asked, "Why did these laws later become harmful?" He responded, "Because of the benefits, evil arises, and thus they become harmful. Suiren drilled wood to create fire, enabling people to cook food and cook all things. How could one know that in later generations there would be the burning of the Qin palace in Xianyang, with a poison that did not cease for three months? Fuxi drew the eight trigrams and created written characters. How could one know that in later generations there would be the selling of fake writings, deceiving the customs of the time through market practices? Houji cultivated grains. How could one know that in later generations there would be the suffering caused by monopolizing wine, grain, tea, and salt for profit? The Yellow Emperor invented vehicles, clothing, and military equipment. How could one know that in later generations there would be the competition in luxurious and beautiful things, as well as excessive military campaigns? He also built palaces and houses. How could one know that in later generations there would be magnificent mansions and separate buildings, towering into the clouds and shining like the sun? Emperor Yao and Emperor Shun established government and ranks. How could one know that in later generations there would be the buying of ranks by paying gold or grain, falsely claiming titles of nobility? Yu the Great controlled floods and divided the world into nine provinces. How could one know that in later generations there would be taxes on mountains, forests, rivers, and lakes, as well as the difficulties of opening channels and building dams? The Duke of Zhou established marriage rituals. How could one know that in later generations there would be reliance on炫耀 relationships and alliances to seek wealth and status? Confucius edited the Book of Songs and Book of Documents, established rites and music, and taught the Five Constants. How could one know that in later generations there would be the stealing of the name of Confucian scholars, stealing fame and reputation, and falsely claiming high status? All these examples corrupt through similar means, undermining the rules and order, greatly damaging the root. Is it the fault of the sages? Is it because the sages initiated it? Is it because those who governed in later generations intensified it? Alas! The metaphor of the horse's liver, I cannot bear to speak of it. [60]

    所可叹者，伊、周初以公忠，放主操政，以全国家，安社稷。而莽、卓、操、懿以降，行滔天之心，援此为法，尤可悲也。桀、纣、幽、厉，身遭放弑，常与万世之君，必为龟镜。则伊、周一时公忠，反误后世乱臣贼子；桀、纣、幽、厉，一时淫虐，而有益万世明君矣。善为政者，有才必用，用必当才，任之而不私之，非才则不任。故使人无弃无滥，天下无一人叹不遇而怀过望之事者。皋陶曰：‘知人则哲，能官人。 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions
