    • 所可叹者 - Introduces a statement of lament or regret; means 'what is to be lamented is.'
    • 放主操政 - Means 'to assume control over the sovereign,' indicating a situation where a minister takes over the governance.
    • 行滔天之心 - Means 'to harbor ambitions as vast as the sea,' indicating extreme and often malevolent intentions.
    • 援此为法 - Means 'to take this as a precedent,' suggesting that these figures used the actions of Yi Yin and Zhou Gong to justify their own actions.
    • 尤可悲也 - Means 'especially lamentable,' emphasizing the tragic nature of the situation.
    • 常与万世之君，必为龟镜 - Means 'they are always taken as examples for future generations,' suggesting that the fates of these rulers serve as lessons for future rulers.
    • 则伊、周一时公忠，反误后世乱臣贼子 - Means 'thus, the temporary public loyalty of Yi Yin and Zhou Gong misled later traitors and villains,' highlighting the negative impact of their actions on future generations.
    • 桀、纣、幽、厉，一时淫虐，而有益万世明君矣 - Means 'Jie, Zhou, You, and Li, though tyrannical in their time, benefited future wise rulers,' suggesting that the downfall of these rulers provided valuable lessons for future generations.
    • 有才必用，用必当才 - Means 'use talents when they are available, and use them appropriately,' emphasizing the importance of utilizing talent effectively.
    • 任之而不私之 - Means 'appoint them but do not favor them,' indicating that while talents should be appointed, they should not be given undue favoritism.
    • 非才则不任 - Means 'do not appoint those who lack talent,' emphasizing the importance of meritocracy.
    • 故使人无弃无滥 - Means 'thus, no one is wasted or overused,' highlighting the balanced approach to talent management.
    • 天下无一人叹不遇而怀过望之事者 - Means 'there is no one in the world who laments not being recognized or harbors excessive expectations,' suggesting a society where everyone feels valued and recognized.
    • 知人则哲，能官人 - Means 'to know people is wisdom, and to appoint them is governance,' emphasizing the importance of understanding and effectively managing people.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伊、周 - Yi Yin and Zhou Gong, legendary wise ministers who served the Shang and Zhou dynasties, respectively.
    • 莽、卓、操、懿 - Wang Mang, Dong Zhuo, Cao Cao, and Sima Yi, four notorious figures in Chinese history known for their treacherous and power-hungry behavior.
    • 桀、纣、幽、厉 - Jie of Xia, Zhou of Shang, King You of Zhou, and King Li of Zhou, four notorious rulers in Chinese history known for their tyranny and misrule.
    • 皋陶 - A legendary figure known for his wisdom and justice, often associated with the early Xia dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伊 - Yi Yin, a legendary wise minister of the Shang dynasty.
    • 周 - Zhou Gong, a legendary wise minister of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 莽 - Wang Mang, a usurper who briefly established the Xin dynasty.
    • 卓 - Dong Zhuo, a warlord who controlled the Han court during the late Eastern Han period.
    • 操 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and statesman who laid the foundation for the Cao Wei state.
    • 懿 - Sima Yi, a prominent statesman and military leader who played a key role in the fall of the Cao Wei state.
    • 桀 - Jie, the last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 纣 - Zhou, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 幽 - King You of Zhou, a ruler of the Zhou dynasty known for his misrule.
    • 厉 - King Li of Zhou, a ruler of the Zhou dynasty known for his misrule.
    • 皋陶 - Gao Yao, a legendary figure known for his wisdom and justice.

    What is to be lamented is that Yi Yin and Zhou Gong, initially acting with public loyalty, took control of the sovereign to preserve the nation and stabilize the state. However, from Wang Mang, Dong Zhuo, Cao Cao, and Sima Yi onward, those with ambitions as vast as the sea took this as a precedent, which is especially lamentable. Jie, Zhou, You, and Li, who were exiled or killed, are always taken as examples for future generations. Thus, the temporary public loyalty of Yi Yin and Zhou Gong misled later traitors and villains, while Jie, Zhou, You, and Li, though tyrannical in their time, benefited future wise rulers. A good ruler or administrator must use talents when they are available, and use them appropriately, appointing them but not favoring them, and not appointing those who lack talent. Thus, no one is wasted or overused, and there is no one in the world who laments not being recognized or harbors excessive expectations. Gao Yao said: "To know people is wisdom, and to appoint them is governance." [61]

    ’《传》曰：‘及其使人也器之。’臧僖伯云：‘国家之败，由官邪也。’班固称宣帝：‘信赏必罚，综核名实，政事、文学、法理之士，咸精其能。至于技巧工匠器械，自元、成间鲜能及之。亦足以知吏称其职，民安其业者也。’光武不许馆陶公主子为郎。前代明哲之主，及霸业之君，国朝列圣，躲亲庶政，用才使器，著在史籍者多矣。以君上之尊，深居高视，犹于小官小吏，一赏一罚，重慎如此。况辅弼之臣，岂得容易苟且哉苻秦入寇，谢安石用其兄子玄为帅以拒之，郗超虽素与不善，闻而叹曰：‘安为重举亲，明也。玄心不负举，才也。’人皆以为不然。 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 《传》曰 - Formulaic expression meaning 'It is said in the *Zhuangzi*' or 'The *Zhuangzi* states.'
    • 及其使人也器之 - Complex phrase meaning 'When employing people, he uses them according to their abilities.'
    • 国家之败，由官邪也 - Statement meaning 'The downfall of a state is due to corrupt officials.'
    • 信赏必罚，综核名实 - Phrase meaning 'Rewarding with certainty and punishing with severity, thoroughly examining names and realities.'
    • 政事、文学、法理之士，咸精其能 - Phrase meaning 'Officials, scholars, and jurists all excel in their abilities.'
    • 技巧工匠器械，自元、成间鲜能及之 - Phrase meaning 'Craftsmen and their tools, since the Yuan and Cheng reigns, few can match them.'
    • 亦足以知吏称其职，民安其业者也 - Phrase meaning 'This is also sufficient to show that officials are competent in their duties and the people are content in their occupations.'
    • 光武不许馆陶公主子为郎 - Statement meaning 'Emperor Guangwu did not allow the son of Princess Guantao to become a court official.'
    • 明哲之主，及霸业之君 - Phrase meaning 'Wise and enlightened rulers, as well as those who achieved hegemony.'
    • 国朝列圣，躲亲庶政 - Phrase meaning 'The emperors of our dynasty, avoiding involvement in minor affairs.'
    • 用才使器 - Phrase meaning 'Using people according to their talents and abilities.'
    • 著在史籍者多矣 - Phrase meaning 'There are many recorded in historical records.'
    • 深居高视 - Phrase meaning 'Living in seclusion and looking down from a height.'
    • 一赏一罚，重慎如此 - Phrase meaning 'Every reward and punishment, so cautious and serious.'
    • 况辅弼之臣，岂得容易苟且哉 - Phrase meaning 'How much more so for the ministers who assist the ruler, how could they act carelessly?'
    • 苻秦入寇，谢安石用其兄子玄为帅以拒之 - Statement meaning 'When Former Qin invaded, Xie An appointed his nephew Xuan as the commander to resist them.'
    • 郗超虽素与不善，闻而叹曰 - Statement meaning 'Xishi, although he had always been at odds with Xuan, heard this and said.'
    • 安为重举亲，明也 - Phrase meaning 'An is wise to heavily rely on his relatives.'
    • 玄心不负举，才也 - Phrase meaning 'Xuan's heart does not辜负 the appointment, it is his talent.'
    • 人皆以为不然 - Phrase meaning 'Everyone thought otherwise.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《传》 - Refers to the *Zhuangzi*, a classic text of Daoism.
    • 臧僖伯 - Historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period, known for his advice to Duke Huan of Lu.
    • 班固 - Historian and writer of the Han dynasty, author of the *Book of Han*.
    • 宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han, known for his reforms and emphasis on Confucian principles.
    • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han, founder of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 馆陶公主 - Princess of the Han dynasty.
    • 明哲之主 - Refers to wise and enlightened rulers.
    • 霸业之君 - Refers to rulers who achieved hegemony.
    • 国朝列圣 - Refers to the emperors of the current dynasty.
    • 苻秦 - Former Qin, a short-lived but powerful state during the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
    • 谢安石 - Xie An, a prominent statesman and military leader of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 郗超 - Xishi, a politician and strategist of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 元、成间 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Yuan and Emperor Cheng of Han.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《传》 - *Zhuangzi*
    • 臧僖伯 - Historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period
    • 班固 - Historian and writer of the Han dynasty
    • 宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han
    • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han
    • 馆陶公主 - Princess of the Han dynasty
    • 明哲之主 - Wise and enlightened rulers
    • 霸业之君 - Rulers who achieved hegemony
    • 国朝列圣 - Emperors of the current dynasty
    • 苻秦 - Former Qin
    • 谢安石 - Xie An
    • 郗超 - Xishi
    • 元、成间 - Reigns of Emperor Yuan and Emperor Cheng of Han

    It is said in the Zhuangzi: "When employing people, he uses them according to their abilities." Zang Xi Bo said: "The downfall of a state is due to corrupt officials." Ban Gu praised Emperor Xuan: "Rewarding with certainty and punishing with severity, thoroughly examining names and realities, officials, scholars, and jurists all excel in their abilities. As for craftsmen and their tools, since the Yuan and Cheng reigns, few can match them. This is also sufficient to show that officials are competent in their duties and the people are content in their occupations." Emperor Guangwu did not allow the son of Princess Guantao to become a court official. From ancient times, wise and enlightened rulers, as well as those who achieved hegemony, and the emperors of our dynasty, who avoid involvement in minor affairs, use people according to their talents and abilities. There are many recorded in historical records. Even with the supreme authority of the ruler, living in seclusion and looking down from a height, they are still so cautious and serious about every reward and punishment for minor officials. How much more so for the ministers who assist the ruler, how could they act carelessly? When Former Qin invaded, Xie An appointed his nephew Xuan as the commander to resist them. Xishi, although he had always been at odds with Xuan, heard this and said, "An is wise to heavily rely on his relatives." "Xuan's heart does not辜负 the appointment, it is his talent." Everyone thought otherwise. [62]

    超曰：‘吾尝与玄共府，见其使才，虽履屐间亦得其任。是以知之。’玄果立功于淮淝，破苻秦百万之众。郗嘉宾之言，亦可谓至公矣。今之人徒私于所亲，无安石之明；徒憎所不善，无嘉宾之论，可悲矣！李林甫奸邪阴贼，妨嫉贤能。至于善守章程，深得宰相之体。当时虽正直有名之士，多被构害。而守寻常仕进者，名获其分。至今人有以右座呼之，而不名也。非其在下有感之者乎韩晋公节制三吴，多历年所取宾佐僚属，随其所长，无不得人。尝有故旧子弟，投之与语，更无能解。召之与宴而观之，毕席端坐不旁视，不与比坐交言。数日，署以随军令监库门。使人视之，每早入，惟端坐至夕，警察吏卒之徒，无敢滥出入者，竟获其力。” [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 雖履屐間亦得其任 - Figurative phrase meaning 'even in the smallest tasks, he was able to find the right person for the job.'
    • 竟獲其力 - Means 'he ultimately proved his worth.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 謝安 - Refers to Xie An (謝安), a prominent politician and military leader during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 郗賓客 - Refers to Xī Bīnghuá (郗嘉宾), a respected official known for his impartiality.
    • 李林甫 - Refers to Lǐ Línfǔ (李林甫), a notorious chancellor of the Tang dynasty known for his cunning and treachery.
    • 韓滉 - Refers to Hàn Jìngōng (韓滉), a Tang dynasty chancellor and governor-general of the Three Wu regions.
    • 淮淝 - Refers to the Battle of Fei River (淝水之战), a significant battle during the Jin dynasty.
    • 苻秦 - Refers to the Former Qin dynasty, led by Fu Jian (苻坚).
    • 三吳 - Refers to the Three Wu regions, a geographical area in southern China.
    • 右座 - A title or position, often used to denote a high-ranking official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 謝安 - Xie An (謝安)
    • 郗賓客 - Xī Bīnghuá (郗嘉宾)
    • 李林甫 - Lǐ Línfǔ (李林甫)
    • 韓滉 - Hàn Jìngōng (韓滉)
    • 淮淝 - Battle of Fei River (淝水之战)
    • 苻秦 - Former Qin dynasty (前秦)
    • 三吳 - Three Wu regions (三吳)
    • 右座 - A title or position

    超曰：“吾尝与玄共府，见其使才，虽履屐间亦得其任。是以知之。”玄果立功于淮淝，破苻秦百万之众。郗嘉宾之言，亦可谓至公矣。今之人徒私于所亲，无安石之明；徒憎所不善，无嘉宾之论，可悲矣！李林甫奸邪阴贼，妨嫉贤能。至于善守章程，深得宰相之体。当时虽正直有名之士，多被构害。而守寻常仕进者，名获其分。至今人有以右座呼之，而不名也。非其在下有感之者乎？韩晋公节制三吴，多历年所取宾佐僚属，随其所长，无不得人。尝有故旧子弟，投之与语，更无能解。召之与宴而观之，毕席端坐不旁视，不与比坐交言。数日，署以随军令监库门。使人视之，每早入，惟端坐至夕，警察吏卒之徒，无敢滥出入者，竟获其力。 [63]

    裴光德垍在中书。有故人，官亦不卑，自远相访。裴公给恤优厚，従容款洽，在其第无所不为。乘间求京府判司。裴公曰：“公诚佳士，但此官与公不相当，不敢以故人之私，而隳朝廷纲纪。他日有瞎眼宰相怜公者，不妨却得，某必不可。”其执守如此。呜呼，上不慎名器，是以厮养待贤也；下不慎名器，是以婴孩奉君，而削弱朝廷也。遂致猥滥讹弊，流荡可忧，不知谁之咎也。 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 裴光德垍在中书 - Pei Guangde, likely a historical figure, holds a position in the central government or imperial secretariat.
    • 有故人，官亦不卑 - Has an old friend whose position is not lowly, indicating a respectable official position.
    • 自远相访 - Visits from afar, indicating a long-distance visit.
    • 裴公给恤优厚 - Pei Guangde provides generous support.
    • 从容款洽，在其第无所不为 - Interacts in a composed and friendly manner, doing anything and everything in his residence.
    • 乘间求京府判司 - Takes advantage of an opportunity to request a local administrative position in the capital.
    • 公诚佳士 - You are indeed a fine man.
    • 但此官与公不相当 - But this position is not suitable for you.
    • 不敢以故人之私，而隳朝廷纲纪 - I dare not because of the favor of an old friend, and destroy the rules and regulations of the court.
    • 他日有瞎眼宰相怜公者 - One day, if there is a blind prime minister who pities you.
    • 不妨却得 - It is not wrong to obtain.
    • 某必不可 - I certainly cannot.
    • 其执守如此 - His adherence is like this.
    • 上不慎名器 - The ruler is not careful with titles and positions.
    • 是以厮养待贤也 - Therefore, treating the wise like servants.
    • 下不慎名器 - The subjects are not careful with titles and positions.
    • 是以婴孩奉君，而削弱朝廷也 - Therefore, serving the ruler like infants, and weakening the court.
    • 遂致猥滥讹弊 - Thus leading to abuse, overuse, corruption, and decay.
    • 流荡可忧 - Wandering and drifting, which is worrisome.
    • 不知谁之咎也 - Do not know whose fault it is.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 裴光德垍 - Likely refers to Pei Guangde, a historical figure known for his integrity and adherence to principles.
    • 中书 - Refers to the central government or the imperial secretariat, a key administrative body in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 京府判司 - A local administrative position in the capital, responsible for various governmental duties.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister, the highest-ranking official in the government.
    • 名器 - Refers to titles and positions, emphasizing the importance of proper governance and respect for official roles.
    • 厮养 - Refers to servants, indicating a low social status.
    • 婴孩 - Refers to infants, symbolizing a lack of maturity or capability.
    • 朝廷 - Refers to the court or the government, highlighting the central authority.
    • 猥滥讹弊 - Refers to the widespread abuse, overuse, corruption, and decay in governance.
    • 流荡可忧 - Refers to the state of wandering and drifting, which is a cause for concern.
    • 不知谁之咎也 - Refers to the uncertainty about who is to blame for the current state of affairs.

    Proper Nouns

    • 裴光德垍 - Likely refers to Pei Guangde, a historical figure.
    • 中书 - Refers to the central government or the imperial secretariat.
    • 京府判司 - A local administrative position in the capital.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister.
    • 名器 - Refers to titles and positions.
    • 厮养 - Refers to servants.
    • 婴孩 - Refers to infants.
    • 朝廷 - Refers to the court or the government.
    • 猥滥讹弊 - Refers to abuse, overuse, corruption, and decay.
    • 流荡可忧 - Refers to wandering and drifting, which is worrisome.
    • 不知谁之咎也 - Refers to the uncertainty about responsibility.

    Pei Guangde, while serving in the central government, had an old friend who held a respectable official position and came to visit from afar. Pei Guangde provided him with generous support and treated him with composed and friendly hospitality, doing everything for him during his stay. The friend took the opportunity to request a local administrative position in the capital. Pei Guangde said, "You are indeed a fine man, but this position is not suitable for you. I dare not grant it because of the favor of an old friend, and destroy the rules and regulations of the court. One day, if there is a blind prime minister who pities you, it is not wrong to obtain it, but I certainly cannot." His adherence to principles was like this. Alas, when the ruler is not careful with titles and positions, it leads to treating the wise like servants; when the subjects are not careful with titles and positions, it leads to serving the ruler like infants, and weakening the court. Thus, it leads to abuse, overuse, corruption, and decay, wandering and drifting, which is worrisome, and it is uncertain whose fault it is. [64]

    王并州璠，自河南尹拜右丞相。除目才到，少尹侯继有宴，以书邀之。王判书后云：“新命虽闻，旧衔尚在。遽为招命，堪入笑林。”洛中以为话柄。故事：少尹与大尹游宴礼隔，虽除官，亦须候正敕也。
    　　有人撰集《怪异记》传云：“玄宗令道士叶静能书符，不见国史。”不知叶静能，中宗朝坐妖妄伏法。玄宗时，有道术者，乃法善也。谈话之误差尚可，若著于文字，其误甚矣。
    　　又有人检陆法言《切韵》，见其音字，遂云：“此吴儿，真是翻字太僻。”不知法言是河南陆，非吴郡也。 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 王并州璠，自河南尹拜右丞相 - Indicates a promotion in rank and position; Wang Binzhou Fan, from the position of Governor of Henan, was appointed as the Right Chancellor.
    • 除目才到，少尹侯继有宴，以书邀之 - Describes a situation where the appointment notice arrives just before a social event; Just as the official notice of his appointment arrived, the Deputy Governor Hou Ji was hosting a banquet and invited him with a letter.
    • 新命虽闻，旧衔尚在，遽为招命，堪入笑林 - Uses a metaphor to criticize the haste and inappropriateness of the invitation; Although the new appointment has been heard, the old title is still in place; hastily issuing an invitation is enough to be laughed at.
    • 洛中以为话柄 - Indicates that the incident became a subject of gossip or ridicule; People in Luoyang took it as a topic of conversation.
    • 故事：少尹与大尹游宴礼隔，虽除官，亦须候正敕也 - Explains the customary protocol and the need for official confirmation; According to tradition, there is a protocol difference between the Deputy Governor and the Governor during social gatherings; even if appointed, one must wait for the official decree.
    • 有人撰集《怪异记》传云：“玄宗令道士叶静能书符，不见国史” - Introduces a historical claim that is disputed; Someone compiled a book called *The Strange Tales* and wrote that Emperor Xuanzong ordered the Taoist monk Ye Jingneng to write talismans, which is not recorded in official history.
    • 不知叶静能，中宗朝坐妖妄伏法。玄宗时，有道术者，乃法善也 - Clarifies the historical facts and corrects the misconception; It is not known that Ye Jingneng was executed during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong for sorcery; during Emperor Xuanzong's reign, the person who had magical abilities was named Fa Shan.
    • 谈话之误差尚可，若著于文字，其误甚矣 - Emphasizes the importance of accuracy in written records; Errors in conversation are still acceptable, but if they are written down, the mistakes are much more serious.
    • 又有人检陆法言《切韵》，见其音字，遂云：“此吴儿，真是翻字太僻” - Describes a critique of the phonetic system; Another person checked Lu Fayan's *Qieyun* and saw the phonetic characters, and said, 'This is a Wu person, truly flipping characters too obscure.'
    • 不知法言是河南陆，非吴郡也 - Corrects the misunderstanding about Lu Fayan's origin; It is not known that Lu Fayan was from Henan, not Wu.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 右丞相 - The title of Right Chancellor, a high-ranking official in the imperial government.
    • 河南尹 - The Governor of Henan, a regional administrative position.
    • 少尹 - The Deputy Governor, a lower-ranking official who assists the Governor.
    • 洛中 - Refers to Luoyang, the capital city during the Tang Dynasty, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a prominent ruler known for his patronage of the arts and culture.
    • 叶静能 - A Taoist monk who was executed for sorcery during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong.
    • 法善 - A Taoist monk with magical abilities during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 陆法言 - A scholar who compiled the *Qieyun*, a comprehensive dictionary of Chinese phonetics.
    • 切韵 - The *Qieyun*, a phonetic dictionary that standardized the pronunciation of Chinese characters.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王并州璠 - Wang Binzhou Fan, a historical figure.
    • 侯继 - Hou Ji, a historical figure.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 叶静能 - Ye Jingneng, a historical figure.
    • 法善 - Fa Shan, a historical figure.
    • 陆法言 - Lu Fayan, a historical figure.
    • 切韵 - *Qieyun*, a historical text.

    Wang Binzhou Fan, who was appointed as the Right Chancellor from his position as Governor of Henan, received his official notice just as the Deputy Governor Hou Ji was hosting a banquet and invited him with a letter. Wang responded by writing, "Although the new appointment has been heard, the old title is still in place; hastily issuing an invitation is enough to be laughed at." People in Luoyang took this as a topic of conversation. According to tradition, there is a protocol difference between the Deputy Governor and the Governor during social gatherings; even if appointed, one must wait for the official decree.

    Someone compiled a book called The Strange Tales and wrote that Emperor Xuanzong ordered the Taoist monk Ye Jingneng to write talismans, which is not recorded in official history. It is not known that Ye Jingneng was executed during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong for sorcery; during Emperor Xuanzong's reign, the person who had magical abilities was named Fa Shan. Errors in conversation are still acceptable, but if they are written down, the mistakes are much more serious.

    Another person checked Lu Fayan's Qieyun and saw the phonetic characters, and said, "This is a Wu person, truly flipping characters too obscure." It is not known that Lu Fayan was from Henan, not Wu. [65]

    又有书生，读经书甚精熟，不知近代事。因说骆宾王，遂云：“某识其孙李少府者，兄弟太多。”意谓骆宾是诸王封号也。今人往往说旧云：“准皇太子教。”不知皇太子合称令。称教，乃王公也。此皆不知根本之误也。
    　　人道尚右，以右为尊。礼先宾客，故西让客，主人在东，盖自卑也。今之人，或以东让客，非礼也。盖缘所任在地，所主在东，俗有东行南头之戏，此乃贵其为一方一境之主也。记曰：“天子无客礼，莫敢为主焉。故君适其臣，升自阼阶，不敢有其室也。”注云：“明飨君非礼也。”今之方镇刺史入本部，于令长以下，礼绝宾主，犹近君臣。至于藩镇，经管内支郡，则俱是古南面诸侯，但以使职监临，如台省之官，至外地耳。既通宴飨，则异君臣。而用古天子升阼阶之仪，昧于礼经，遂同僭拟，是不讲贯所致。又小人姑息讹谬相承，亦可笑且叹也。 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 又有书生 - Refers to another scholar or student, indicating a continuation of the narrative.
    • 读经书甚精熟 - Means 'reads Confucian classics very thoroughly,' emphasizing the scholar's expertise.
    • 不知近代事 - Means 'does not know recent events,' highlighting a lack of awareness of contemporary matters.
    • 因说骆宾王 - Means 'because he mentioned Cao Binwang,' referring to a historical figure.
    • 遂云 - Means 'then said,' introducing a quotation or statement.
    • 某识其孙李少府者，兄弟太多 - A statement by the scholar, claiming to know Cao Binwang's grandson Li Shao Fu, who has many brothers.
    • 意谓骆宾是诸王封号也 - The scholar's interpretation, thinking that Cao Binwang was a title given to various kings.
    • 今人往往说旧云 - Means 'modern people often say,' introducing a common saying or belief.
    • 准皇太子教 - Refers to a decree or order from the crown prince.
    • 不知皇太子合称令 - Means 'do not know that the crown prince should be called 'ling,' indicating a misunderstanding of proper titles.
    • 称教，乃王公也 - Means 'calling it 'jiao' is for kings and princes,' clarifying the correct usage.
    • 此皆不知根本之误也 - Means 'all these are errors due to a lack of understanding of the fundamentals,' summarizing the mistakes.
    • 人道尚右 - Means 'in human society, the right side is esteemed,' indicating a cultural preference.
    • 以右为尊 - Means 'the right side is considered honorable,' reinforcing the cultural norm.
    • 礼先宾客 - Means 'etiquette places guests first,' emphasizing the importance of hospitality.
    • 故西让客，主人在东 - Means 'therefore, the west is where guests are seated, and the host is in the east,' describing seating arrangements.
    • 盖自卑也 - Means 'this is a sign of humility,' explaining the reasoning behind the seating arrangement.
    • 今之人，或以东让客 - Means 'nowadays, some people seat guests in the east,' indicating a deviation from tradition.
    • 非礼也 - Means 'this is not proper etiquette,' criticizing the modern practice.
    • 盖缘所任在地，所主在东 - Means 'this is because the position of responsibility is in the east,' providing a reason for the modern practice.
    • 俗有东行南头之戏 - Means 'there is a custom of playing games where the east is the south end,' describing a cultural practice.
    • 此乃贵其为一方一境之主也 - Means 'this is a way of showing respect for those who are the leaders of a region or area,' explaining the significance of the custom.
    • 记曰 - Refers to a classical text or saying.
    • 天子无客礼，莫敢为主焉 - Means 'the Son of Heaven does not receive guests as equals, and no one dares to treat him as a host,' emphasizing the hierarchical nature of royal etiquette.
    • 故君适其臣，升自阼阶，不敢有其室也 - Means 'therefore, when a ruler visits his ministers, he ascends the main steps and does not enter their rooms,' describing the proper behavior.
    • 注云 - Means 'the commentary says,' introducing an explanation.
    • 明飨君非礼也 - Means 'it is not proper to entertain the ruler as a guest,' reinforcing the hierarchical nature of the etiquette.
    • 今之方镇刺史入本部 - Means 'nowadays, regional governors and prefects entering their own regions,' describing a modern practice.
    • 于令长以下，礼绝宾主 - Means 'below the level of magistrates, the distinction between host and guest is eliminated,' indicating a change in etiquette.
    • 犹近君臣 - Means 'this is still similar to the relationship between ruler and minister,' drawing a parallel.
    • 至于藩镇，经管内支郡 - Means 'as for regional commanders, who oversee subordinate counties,' describing another modern practice.
    • 则俱是古南面诸侯 - Means 'they are all ancient southern-facing feudal lords,' drawing a historical comparison.
    • 但以使职监临，如台省之官，至外地耳 - Means 'but they are only acting in the capacity of officials stationed in the provinces, like those in the central government,' explaining their role.
    • 既通宴飨，则异君臣 - Means 'since they participate in banquets, they are different from the relationship between ruler and minister,' noting a distinction.
    • 而用古天子升阼阶之仪 - Means 'but they use the ancient ritual of the Son of Heaven ascending the main steps,' criticizing the misuse of rituals.
    • 昧于礼经，遂同僭拟 - Means 'they are ignorant of the rites and thus act presumptuously,' criticizing the lack of understanding.
    • 是不讲贯所致 - Means 'this is the result of not studying and practicing,' summarizing the cause of the mistakes.
    • 又小人姑息讹谬相承，亦可笑且叹也 - Means 'and common people tolerate and perpetuate these errors, which is both laughable and lamentable,' expressing disappointment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 书生 - Refers to a scholar or student, often used in classical literature.
    • 经书 - Refers to Confucian classics, which were the core texts of Chinese education and culture.
    • 骆宾王 - A famous poet and politician from the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 李少府 - A title for a local official, often used in classical literature.
    • 皇太子 - Refers to the crown prince, the heir to the throne.
    • 令 - A title for a local official, often used in classical literature.
    • 教 - Refers to a decree or order, often used in classical literature.
    • 方镇 - Refers to regional military commanders, often used in classical literature.
    • 刺史 - Refers to a prefect, a local administrative official, often used in classical literature.
    • 令长 - Refers to a magistrate, a local administrative official, often used in classical literature.
    • 藩镇 - Refers to regional military commanders, often used in classical literature.
    • 台省 - Refers to the central government, often used in classical literature.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence introduces a scholar who is well-versed in classical texts but unaware of recent events.
    • This clause indicates that the scholar mentions Cao Binwang and then says something.
    • This statement by the scholar claims to know Cao Binwang's grandson Li Shao Fu, who has many brothers.
    • This clause explains the scholar's interpretation that Cao Binwang was a title given to various kings.
    • This clause introduces a common saying or belief among modern people.
    • This phrase refers to a decree or order from the crown prince.
    • This clause explains that the scholar does not know that the crown prince should be called 'ling.'
    • This clause clarifies that calling it 'jiao' is for kings and princes.
    • This clause summarizes the errors due to a lack of understanding of the fundamentals.
    • This clause states that in human society, the right side is esteemed.
    • This clause reinforces that the right side is considered honorable.
    • This clause emphasizes that etiquette places guests first.
    • This clause describes the seating arrangement where guests are seated in the west and the host is in the east.
    • This clause explains that this seating arrangement is a sign of humility.
    • This clause notes that some modern people seat guests in the east.
    • This clause criticizes this modern practice as improper.
    • This clause provides a reason for the modern practice, stating that the position of responsibility is in the east.
    • This clause describes a cultural practice of playing games where the east is the south end.
    • This clause explains that this practice shows respect for those who are the leaders of a region or area.
    • This clause refers to a classical text or saying.
    • This clause emphasizes that the Son of Heaven does not receive guests as equals and no one dares to treat him as a host.
    • This clause describes the proper behavior when a ruler visits his ministers.
    • This clause introduces an explanation.
    • This clause reinforces the hierarchical nature of the etiquette.
    • This clause describes a modern practice of regional governors and prefects entering their own regions.
    • This clause indicates a change in etiquette.
    • This clause draws a parallel to the relationship between ruler and minister.
    • This clause describes another modern practice of regional commanders overseeing subordinate counties.
    • This clause draws a historical comparison.
    • This clause explains their role.
    • This clause notes a distinction.
    • This clause criticizes the misuse of rituals.
    • This clause criticizes the lack of understanding.
    • This clause summarizes the cause of the mistakes.
    • This clause expresses disappointment.

    Another scholar, well-versed in Confucian classics but unaware of recent events, mentioned Cao Binwang and then said, "I know Cao Binwang's grandson Li Shao Fu, who has many brothers." The scholar thought that Cao Binwang was a title given to various kings. Modern people often say, "According to the decree of the crown prince." However, they do not know that the crown prince should be called "ling." Calling it "jiao" is for kings and princes. All these are errors due to a lack of understanding of the fundamentals.

    In human society, the right side is esteemed and considered honorable. Etiquette places guests first, so the west is where guests are seated, and the host is in the east, which is a sign of humility. Nowadays, some people seat guests in the east, which is not proper etiquette. This is because the position of responsibility is in the east, and there is a custom of playing games where the east is the south end, showing respect for those who are the leaders of a region or area.

    A classical text states, "The Son of Heaven does not receive guests as equals, and no one dares to treat him as a host. Therefore, when a ruler visits his ministers, he ascends the main steps and does not enter their rooms." The commentary explains, "It is not proper to entertain the ruler as a guest." Nowadays, regional governors and prefects entering their own regions eliminate the distinction between host and guest below the level of magistrates, which is still similar to the relationship between ruler and minister. As for regional commanders, who oversee subordinate counties, they are all ancient southern-facing feudal lords, but they are only acting in the capacity of officials stationed in the provinces, like those in the central government. Since they participate in banquets, they are different from the relationship between ruler and minister. However, they use the ancient ritual of the Son of Heaven ascending the main steps, which is the result of not studying and practicing the rites and thus acting presumptuously. Common people tolerate and perpetuate these errors, which is both laughable and lamentable. [66]

    古者三公开阁，郡守比古之侯伯，亦有阁，所以世之书题有“阁下”之称。前辈呼刺史、太守，亦曰“节下”。与宰相大僚书，往往呼“执事”，言阁下之执事人耳。刘子玄为史官，与临修宰相书，呼“足下”。韩文公与使主张仆射书，呼“执事”，即其例也。其记室本系王侯宾佐之称，他人亦非所宜。执事则指斥其左右之人，尊卑皆可通称。侍者，士庶可用之。近日官至使府御史及畿令，悉呼“阁下”。至于初命宾佐。犹呼“记室”。今则一例阁下，亦谓上下无别矣。其执事才施于举人，侍者止行于释子而已。今又布衣相呼，尽曰“阁下”。虽出于浮薄相戏，亦是名分大坏矣。又中表疏远卑行，多有座前之目，尤可惩怪。夫阁下去殿下一阶，座前降几前一等，此之乖僭，其可行耶宗従叔姑及姨舅之行，施之可也。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三公 - Refers to the highest-ranking officials in ancient Chinese government, often translated as 'the Three Excellencies.'
    • 開閣 - Means 'to open a hall' or 'to establish a hall,' referring to the establishment of an office or residence for high-ranking officials.
    • 郡守 - Refers to the governor of a prefecture, a local administrative official.
    • 比古之侯伯 - A comparative phrase meaning 'comparable to the ancient feudal lords,' indicating the status of the prefectural governor.
    • 書題有“閣下”之称 - Refers to the title '阁下' (Ge Xia), which was a respectful address used for high-ranking officials.
    • 刺史、太守 - Both refer to local administrative officials, with '刺史' being a higher-ranking position than '太守.'
    • 节下 - A respectful address used for local officials, similar to '阁下.'
    • 执事 - A term of respect meaning 'your servant' or 'your attendant,' used to address high-ranking officials.
    • 记室 - Refers to the office of a secretary or clerk, often used in the context of a noble's or official's staff.
    • 侍者 - Refers to servants or attendants, a term used for lower-ranking individuals.
    • 初命宾佐 - Refers to newly appointed assistants or aides.
    • 中表疏远卑行 - Refers to distant relatives and lower-ranking individuals.
    • 座前 - A term of respect meaning 'before you' or 'in front of you,' used for addressing someone of higher status.
    • 乖僭 - Refers to improper or excessive behavior, often used to describe actions that go beyond one's proper role.
    • 宗従叔姑及姨舅之行 - Refers to specific familial relationships, including uncles, aunts, and cousins.
    • 布衣相呼 - Refers to common people addressing each other, often used to describe informal or disrespectful behavior.
    • 浮薄相戏 - Refers to frivolous or shallow joking, often used to describe inappropriate behavior.
    • 名分大坏 - Refers to the breakdown of social norms and titles, indicating a decline in proper conduct.
    • 座前降几前一等 - Refers to the hierarchical difference between '座前' and '几前,' indicating a lesser degree of respect.
    • 无利於事 - Refers to actions that are not beneficial, often used to criticize improper behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 三公 - The highest-ranking officials in ancient Chinese government, including the Chancellor, the Minister of Works, and the Grand Commandant.
    • 郡守 - The governor of a prefecture, a local administrative official in the Han dynasty.
    • 阁下 - A respectful title used for high-ranking officials, derived from the practice of opening a hall for them.
    • 刺史、太守 - Local administrative officials, with '刺史' being a higher-ranking position than '太守.'
    • 节下 - A respectful address used for local officials, similar to '阁下.'
    • 执事 - A term of respect meaning 'your servant' or 'your attendant,' used to address high-ranking officials.
    • 记室 - The office of a secretary or clerk, often used in the context of a noble's or official's staff.
    • 侍者 - Servants or attendants, a term used for lower-ranking individuals.
    • 初命宾佐 - Newly appointed assistants or aides.
    • 中表疏远卑行 - Distant relatives and lower-ranking individuals.
    • 座前 - A term of respect meaning 'before you' or 'in front of you,' used for addressing someone of higher status.
    • 乖僭 - Improper or excessive behavior, often used to describe actions that go beyond one's proper role.
    • 宗従叔姑及姨舅之行 - Specific familial relationships, including uncles, aunts, and cousins.
    • 布衣相呼 - Common people addressing each other, often used to describe informal or disrespectful behavior.
    • 浮薄相戏 - Frivolous or shallow joking, often used to describe inappropriate behavior.
    • 名分大坏 - The breakdown of social norms and titles, indicating a decline in proper conduct.
    • 座前降几前一等 - The hierarchical difference between '座前' and '几前,' indicating a lesser degree of respect.
    • 无利於事 - Actions that are not beneficial, often used to criticize improper behavior.

    Proper Nouns

    • 三公 - The highest-ranking officials in ancient Chinese government.
    • 郡守 - The governor of a prefecture.
    • 阁下 - A respectful title used for high-ranking officials.
    • 刺史 - A local administrative official.
    • 太守 - A local administrative official.
    • 节下 - A respectful address used for local officials.
    • 执事 - A term of respect meaning 'your servant' or 'your attendant.'
    • 记室 - The office of a secretary or clerk.
    • 侍者 - Servants or attendants.
    • 初命宾佐 - Newly appointed assistants or aides.
    • 中表疏远卑行 - Distant relatives and lower-ranking individuals.
    • 座前 - A term of respect meaning 'before you' or 'in front of you.'
    • 乖僭 - Improper or excessive behavior.
    • 宗従叔姑及姨舅之行 - Specific familial relationships.
    • 布衣相呼 - Common people addressing each other.
    • 浮薄相戏 - Frivolous or shallow joking.
    • 名分大坏 - The breakdown of social norms and titles.
    • 座前降几前一等 - The hierarchical difference between '座前' and '几前.'
    • 无利於事 - Actions that are not beneficial.

    In ancient times, the Three Excellencies established halls, and the prefectural governors, comparable to the ancient feudal lords, also had halls, which is why the world's titles have the term "阁下" (Ge Xia). Formerly, local officials such as the Cishi (刺史) and Taishou (太守) were also addressed as "节下" (Jie Xia). When writing to high-ranking officials like the Chancellor, they were often addressed as "执事" (Zhi Shi), meaning "your servant" or "your attendant." Liu Zixuan, as a historian, wrote to the Chancellor and addressed him as "足下" (Zu Xia). Han Yu, in his letter to the Chief Secretary, also used "执事," following the same precedent. The term "记室" (Ji Shi) originally referred to the office of a secretary or clerk in the service of a noble or official, and it was not appropriate for others to use. "执事" (Zhi Shi) specifically refers to the people around the official, and it can be used regardless of rank. "侍者" (Shi Zhe) is a term for servants or attendants, suitable for both scholars and commoners. Recently, officials up to the level of imperial censors and county magistrates are all addressed as "阁下" (Ge Xia). As for newly appointed assistants or aides, they are still called "记室" (Ji Shi). Nowadays, everyone is addressed as "阁下" (Ge Xia), which implies a lack of distinction in status. "执事" (Zhi Shi) is only used for officials, and "侍者" (Shi Zhe) is only used for monks. Now, common people address each other as "阁下" (Ge Xia), which is a result of frivolous joking and a sign of the breakdown of proper titles. Moreover, distant relatives and lower-ranking individuals often use the term "座前" (Zuo Qian), which is particularly strange. The term "阁下" (Ge Xia) is one rank below "殿下" (Dian Xia), and "座前" (Zuo Qian) is one level lower than "几前" (Ji Qian). Such improper behavior is not acceptable. However, specific familial relationships, such as uncles, aunts, and cousins, can still use these terms appropriately. [67]

    《汉书》载张骞穷河源，言其奉使之远，实无天河之说。惟张茂先《博物志》，说近世有人居海上，每年八月，见海槎来不违时。赍一年粮，乘之到天河，见妇人织，丈夫饮牛。遣问严君平，云：“某年某月某日，客星犯牛斗，即此人也。后人相传云：得织女支机石，持以问君平。都是凭虚之说。今成都严真观有一石，俗呼为“支机石”，皆目云：当时君平留之。宝历中，余下第还家，于京洛途中，逢官差递夫舁张骞槎。先在东都禁中，今淮诏索有司取进，不知是何物也。前辈诗往往有用张骞槎者，相袭谬误矣。纵出杂书，亦不足据。 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 窮河源 - Phrase meaning 'to explore the source of the river,' often used metaphorically to denote a quest for knowledge or truth.
    • 奉使之遠 - Means 'to be sent on a distant mission,' referring to the act of being dispatched on a long journey.
    • 天河之說 - Refers to the mythological concept of the Milky Way as a celestial river.
    • 海槎 - An ancient Chinese term for a raft or boat that drifts across the sea, often used in folklore.
    • 嚴君平 - A reference to a famous Han dynasty astrologer and philosopher named Yan Junping, known for his predictions and wisdom.
    • 客星犯牛斗 - A phrase from ancient Chinese astronomy, meaning 'a guest star invades the constellations of Ox and Dipper,' which was believed to signify significant events.
    • 支機石 - A legendary stone said to be used by the Weaving Maid (织女) to support her loom, often associated with good fortune and divination.
    • 得織女支機石 - Means 'to obtain the Weaving Maid's loom-supporting stone,' a symbolic act of acquiring wisdom or divine favor.
    • 相襲謬誤 - Means 'to perpetuate errors,' indicating the transmission of incorrect information over time.
    • 寶歷中 - Refers to the Bao Li era during the Tang dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 下第還家 - Means 'to return home after failing an examination,' indicating a scholar's disappointment and retreat.
    • 京洛途中 - Refers to the journey between the capital (Chang'an) and Luoyang, two important cities in ancient China.
    • 官差遞夫舁 - Means 'official messengers carrying,' referring to the transport of items by government officials.
    • 淮詔索有司取進 - Means 'according to imperial decree, the relevant authorities were ordered to present,' indicating a formal command.
    • 前輩詩往往有用張骞槎者 - Means 'many poets of the past have used the image of Zhang Qian's raft,' highlighting a common literary motif.
    • 縱出雜書，亦不足據 - Means 'even if the stories appear in various books, they are not reliable.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 張骞 - Zhang Qian, a famous Han dynasty explorer known for his missions to Central Asia.
    • 嚴真觀 - A temple in Chengdu, Sichuan province, where the '支机石' is said to be kept.
    • 嚴君平 - Yan Junping, a Han dynasty astrologer and philosopher known for his accurate predictions.
    • 寶歷 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, indicating a specific historical period.
    • 張骞槎 - The raft or boat used by Zhang Qian, often used as a literary symbol in poetry.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《汉书》 - The *Book of Han*, a historical record compiled during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 张茂先 - Zhang Maoxian, a scholar and author of *Museum of Curiosities*.
    • 宝历 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 严真观 - A temple in Chengdu, Sichuan province.
    • 张骞槎 - The raft or boat used by Zhang Qian.

    Translation:

    The Book of Han records Zhang Qian's exploration of the river's source, stating that he was sent on a distant mission but found no evidence of the Milky Way myth. However, Zhang Maoxian's Museum of Curiosities tells a different story, describing a recent account of someone living by the sea who, every August, saw a sea raft arrive on schedule. This person brought a year's supply of food and traveled to the Milky Way, where they encountered a woman weaving and a man tending cattle. They sent a message to Yan Junping, who interpreted it as a guest star invading the constellations of Ox and Dipper, which was believed to signify significant events. Later, it was said that someone obtained the Weaving Maid's loom-supporting stone and showed it to Yan Junping. These stories are all based on fanciful accounts. Today, there is a stone in the Yan Zhen Temple in Chengdu, commonly known as the "loom-supporting stone," which is said to have been left by Yan Junping. During the Bao Li period, I returned home after failing an examination and encountered official messengers carrying Zhang Qian's raft on the journey between the capital and Luoyang. It was originally in the eastern capital's imperial palace but is now ordered to be presented according to imperial decree, though its true nature remains unknown. Many poets of the past have used the image of Zhang Qian's raft, perpetuating errors. Even if these stories appear in various books, they are not reliable. [68]

    高宗朝，改门下省为东台，中书省为西台，尚书省为文昌台，故御史台呼为南台。武后朝，御史台有左右肃政之号，当时亦谓之左右、右台，则宪府未曾有东西台之称。惟俗间呼在京为西台，东都为东台。李栖筠为御史大夫，后人不名者，呼为西台，又不知出何故事。岂以其名上栖字，遂呼之耶又呼杜门下黄裳。为当致敬，安得辄废若妖神淫祀，无名而设。苟有识者，固当远之。虽岳海镇渎，名山大川，帝王先贤，不当所立之处，不在典籍，则淫祀也。昔之为人，生无功德可称，死无节行可奖，则淫祀也。当斧之、火之，以示愚俗，又何谒而祀之哉神饭在礼宜拜受，其他则以巫觋之饷，可挥而去也。为吏宜鉴之。 [69]

    During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, the Menxia Province was renamed the Eastern Office, the Zhongshu Province became the Western Office, and the Shangshu Province was called the Wenchang Office. Consequently, the Yushi Tai (Censorate) was referred to as the Southern Office. During Wu Zetian's reign, the Censorate was given the titles "Left Censorate" and "Right Censorate," and it was also called the Left and Right Offices. Thus, the Censorate had never been known as the East or West Offices. In common parlance, the capital was called the Western Office, and the Eastern Capital was called the Eastern Office. When Li Xiuyun served as the Yushi Dafu (Grand Censor), people who did not know his name called him the Western Office, but it is unclear where this custom originated. Could it be because his name contained the character "Xi," leading to this nickname? Another example is Du Menxia Huang Shang, who should be respected, yet how can he be casually neglected? If there are those who understand, they should certainly avoid such superstitions. Even natural landmarks like mountains and rivers, or historical sites associated with emperors and sages, if they are not officially recognized or recorded, are considered superstitious practices. As for people who lived without notable virtue or died without commendable conduct, their worship is also considered superstitious. Such practices should be destroyed by cutting them down or burning them, to show the ignorant masses. Why should they be visited and worshipped? Divine offerings should be received with proper ritual, but other offerings made by shamans can be dismissed. Officials should take this as a lesson. [69]

    有士人退朝，诣其友生，见衲衣道人在坐，不怿而去。他日，谓友生曰：“公好衣毳褐之夫，何也吾不知其贤愚，且觉其臭。”友生应曰：“毳褐之臭，外也。岂甚铜乳铜乳之臭，并肩而立，接迹而趋。公外其间，曾不嫌耻，反讥余与山野有道之士游。南朝高人，以蛙鸣蒿莱胜鼓吹。吾视毳褐，愈于今之朱紫远矣！”
    　　下辈不通义理者，使之写文字，甚误。悉同一本，若宦字，多作官。荥字，多作荣，或多误著荧，髭须，多著宾，鬓发。又偏旁只较一点一画，而义全不通，若棹、掉、杨、扬、棒、捧、傅、傅、沐、沭、氏、氐、惟怅、帷帐、赐惕，又锡、钅昜、昜，若此者众矣。辨文字者，勿以为小事而不留意也。 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有士人退朝，诣其友生 - Common classical phrase indicating a scholar returning from court and visiting a friend.
    • 见衲衣道人在坐，不怿而去 - Describes a reaction to seeing a monk or Taoist in conversation, often viewed with disapproval.
    • 公好衣毳褐之夫，何也 - Question expressing curiosity about someone's preference for a specific type of clothing.
    • 吾不知其贤愚，且觉其臭 - Colloquial expression indicating a strong dislike or aversion to someone, possibly due to appearance or behavior.
    • 毳褐之臭，外也 - Metaphorical statement suggesting the external appearance of a monk's clothing is unimportant.
    • 岂甚铜乳铜乳之臭，并肩而立，接迹而趋 - Humorous or exaggerated comparison, possibly referring to the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant.
    • 南朝高人，以蛙鸣蒿莱胜鼓吹 - Reference to the appreciation of simple, natural sounds over elaborate music, a theme in classical literature.
    • 吾视毳褐，愈于今之朱紫远矣 - Statement emphasizing the superiority of simplicity and humility over ostentation.
    • 下辈不通义理者，使之写文字，甚误 - Critique of those who lack understanding of proper principles and are tasked with writing, leading to errors.
    • 悉同一本，若宦字，多作官 - Discussion of common misspellings and misunderstandings in writing.
    • 荥字，多作荣，或多误著荧，髭须，多著宾，鬓发 - Further examples of common writing errors.
    • 偏旁只较一点一画，而义全不通 - Statement highlighting the importance of precision in writing, as small differences can lead to significant misunderstandings.
    • 若棹、掉、杨、扬、棒、捧、傅、傅、沐、沭、氏、氐、惟怅、帷帐、赐惕，又锡、钅昜、昜 - List of specific examples of words that are often confused or misspelled.
    • 辨文字者，勿以为小事而不留意也 - Final admonition to take writing seriously and pay attention to details.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 士人 - Refers to scholars or literati, a key social class in classical Chinese society.
    • 友生 - A term of respect for a friend, often used in classical texts.
    • 衲衣道人 - Refers to a monk or Taoist, indicating a religious or spiritual figure.
    • 毳褐 - A type of coarse, simple clothing, often associated with monks or ascetics.
    • 铜乳 - A term that could refer to the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant, possibly used metaphorically.
    • 南朝高人 - Refers to respected figures from the Southern Dynasties, a period known for its cultural and literary achievements.
    • 朱紫 - Refers to bright, luxurious colors, often associated with wealth and status.
    • 宦字，多作官 - A common misspelling, reflecting the importance of precision in writing.
    • 荥字，多作荣 - Another example of a common misspelling.
    • 髭须，多著宾，鬓发 - Further examples of common writing errors, reflecting the importance of attention to detail.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence describes a sequence of events, but the exact nature of the interaction and the reason for the scholar's displeasure are not explicitly stated.
    • The sentence is elliptical, with the question and the response closely linked.
    • This metaphorical statement is somewhat abstract, requiring careful interpretation.
    • The sentence is humorous or exaggerated, with the comparison to the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant.
    • The sentence is a reference to a cultural theme, requiring contextual understanding.
    • The sentence is a statement of preference, requiring careful interpretation of the metaphor.
    • The sentence is a critique, requiring careful interpretation of the criticism.
    • The sentence is a discussion of common misspellings, requiring careful interpretation of the examples.
    • The sentence is a further discussion of common misspellings, requiring careful interpretation of the examples.
    • The sentence is a statement of the importance of precision, requiring careful interpretation of the metaphor.
    • The sentence is a list of specific examples, requiring careful interpretation of the examples.
    • The sentence is a final admonition, requiring careful interpretation of the advice.

    Proper Nouns

    • 士人 - Scholars or literati.
    • 友生 - A term of respect for a friend.
    • 衲衣道人 - A monk or Taoist.
    • 毳褐 - A type of coarse, simple clothing.
    • 铜乳 - A term that could refer to the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant.
    • 南朝高人 - Respected figures from the Southern Dynasties.
    • 朱紫 - Bright, luxurious colors.
    • 宦字，多作官 - A common misspelling.
    • 荥字，多作荣 - Another example of a common misspelling.
    • 髭须，多著宾，鬓发 - Further examples of common writing errors.

    A scholar returned from court and visited a friend, only to leave in displeasure upon seeing a monk or Taoist in conversation. On another day, the scholar told his friend, "Why do you associate with those who wear coarse, simple clothing? I don't know if they are wise or foolish, but I find them distasteful." The friend replied, "The distaste for their clothing is superficial. It's not as bad as the smell of copper or something similarly unpleasant, which one might encounter while standing or walking alongside. You, on the other hand, seem to associate with such people without shame, yet you criticize me for befriending those who are virtuous and reclusive. In the Southern Dynasties, the wise appreciated the natural sounds of frogs in the wild over elaborate music. To me, the simplicity of such clothing is far superior to the ostentatious colors of today."

    Furthermore, those who lack understanding of proper principles and are tasked with writing often make serious errors. For example, the character for "official" is often written as "官," and the character for "Vesuvius" is often written as "荣." Sometimes, "荧" is mistakenly used for "髭须," and "宾" for "鬓发." Even small differences in strokes can lead to significant misunderstandings, as in the cases of "棹" and "掉," "杨" and "扬," "棒" and "捧," "傅" and "傅," "沐" and "沭," "氏" and "氐," "惟怅" and "帷帐," "赐惕" and "锡," "钅昜" and "昜." There are many such examples. Those who study writing should not consider these details trivial and neglect them. [70]

    武宗皇帝庙讳炎，改两火相重。其偏旁言谈字已改为谭，淡改曾澹。其啖字，俗多不定，旧或书餤。餤音谭，《尔雅》训进，本非饭食名。世重饼啖，庖人以意相传。及下俚，凡关食物，偏旁尽従食。又啗字声不同，索前史及诸家并佛经，多作口边敢。其音与著两火同徒敢反。又《玉篇》、《切韵》，啖字是正也，著两火俗也，并徒敢反，正合作啖不疑矣。啗马即合用啗字，音徒滥反。以义言之，以物自食，谓之啖。以物喂人，谓之啗。又案《汉书 高帝纪》云：“使郦食其、陆贾往说，将啗以利。”颜监注曰：“啗者本谓食啗耳，音徒敢反，以食喂人，令其啗食，则改变为徒滥反。今以利诱之，取实为辞也。”颜监讲啗字虽未分明，亦无口傍两火之说。则明是字俗相传，而后人为姓，亦因俗耳。近日书饼啖字，至有食边口边作覃，及口边作詹者，率意而为，其误甚矣。《切韵》是寻常文书，何不置之几案旋看也。 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 武宗皇帝庙讳炎 - The temple name of Emperor Wuzong is 'Yan,' a posthumous title given to the emperor.
    • 改两火相重 - Indicates that the character for '炎' (fire) was changed to avoid using the same character twice.
    • 其偏旁言谈字已改为谭 - The character for '言谈' (speech) was changed to '谭' (Tan), likely to avoid repetition.
    • 淡改曾澹 - The character for '淡' (light) was changed to '曾澹' (Zeng Dan), again to avoid repetition.
    • 其啖字，俗多不定 - The character for '啖' (eat) is commonly used inconsistently.
    • 旧或书餤 - In the past, it was sometimes written as '餤.'
    • 餤音谭 - The pronunciation of '餤' is 'Tan.'
    • 《尔雅》训进 - According to the *Er Ya* (an ancient Chinese dictionary), '餤' means 'to advance' or 'to progress.'
    • 本非饭食名 - It is not originally a term for food.
    • 世重饼啖 - The world values '饼' (cakes) and '啖' (eating).
    • 庖人以意相传 - Cooks pass down their knowledge informally.
    • 及下俚，凡关食物，偏旁尽従食 - In common usage, all characters related to food use the radical '食' (food).
    • 又啗字声不同 - The character '啗' has a different pronunciation.
    • 索前史及诸家并佛经，多作口边敢 - Searching through historical records, various texts, and Buddhist scriptures, it is often written as '口边敢.'
    • 其音与著两火同徒敢反 - Its pronunciation is the same as '著两火' (two fires), which is pronounced '徒敢反' (tu gan fan).
    • 又《玉篇》、《切韵》，啖字是正也，著两火俗也，并徒敢反，正合作啖不疑矣 - According to *Yupian* and *Qieyun* (ancient dictionaries), '啖' is the correct form, while '著两火' is a common variant, both pronounced '徒敢反.' It should be written as '啖' without doubt.
    • 啗马即合用啗字，音徒滥反 - For '啗马' (feeding a horse), the character '啗' should be used, pronounced '徒滥反' (tu lan fan).
    • 以义言之，以物自食，谓之啖 - By definition, eating something by oneself is called '啖.'
    • 以物喂人，谓之啗 - Feeding someone else is also called '啗.'
    • 又案《汉书 高帝纪》云：“使郦食其、陆贾往说，将啗以利。” - According to the *Book of Han: Annals of Emperor Gao*, it says, 'Send Li Siqi and Lu Jia to persuade, and offer them benefits to entice them.'
    • 颜监注曰：“啗者本谓食啗耳，音徒敢反，以食喂人，令其啗食，则改变为徒滥反。今以利诱之，取实为辞也。” - The commentary by Yan Shigu says, '啗 originally means to eat, pronounced '徒敢反.' When used to feed someone, it changes to '徒滥反.' Now, it is used to entice with benefits, taking the actual meaning as the basis.'
    • 颜监讲啗字虽未分明，亦无口傍两火之说 - Although Yan Shigu's explanation is not clear, he does not mention the character '口傍两火' (two fires on the side of the mouth).
    • 则明是字俗相传，而后人为姓，亦因俗耳 - This clearly shows that the character has been passed down through common usage, and later it became a surname due to this tradition.
    • 近日书饼啖字，至有食边口边作覃，及口边作詹者，率意而为，其误甚矣 - Recently, when writing '饼啖' (cakes and eating), some people have written '食边口边作覃' (food side and mouth side as Tan) and '口边作詹' (mouth side as Zhan), which are arbitrary and very incorrect.
    • 《切韵》是寻常文书，何不置之几案旋看也 - *Qieyun* is a common reference book; why not keep it on your desk for quick reference?

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武宗皇帝 - Emperor Wuzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 庙讳 - Posthumous name given to an emperor.
    • 《尔雅》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 《汉书 高帝纪》 - The *Book of Han: Annals of Emperor Gao*.
    • 颜监 - Yan Shigu, a Tang Dynasty scholar and historian.
    • 《玉篇》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 《切韵》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary and rhyme book.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武宗皇帝 - Emperor Wuzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 《尔雅》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 《汉书 高帝纪》 - The *Book of Han: Annals of Emperor Gao*.
    • 颜监 - Yan Shigu, a Tang Dynasty scholar and historian.
    • 《玉篇》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 《切韵》 - An ancient Chinese dictionary and rhyme book.

    Emperor Wuzong's temple name is "Yan," which was changed to avoid using the same character twice. The character for "言谈" (speech) has been changed to "谭" (Tan), and "淡" (light) has been changed to "曾澹" (Zeng Dan). The character for "啖" (eat) is commonly used inconsistently, and in the past, it was sometimes written as "餤." The pronunciation of "餤" is "Tan," and according to the Er Ya (an ancient Chinese dictionary), "餤" means "to advance" or "to progress." It is not originally a term for food. The world values "饼" (cakes) and "啖" (eating), and cooks pass down their knowledge informally. In common usage, all characters related to food use the radical "食" (food). The character "啗" has a different pronunciation. Searching through historical records, various texts, and Buddhist scriptures, it is often written as "口边敢." Its pronunciation is the same as "著两火" (two fires), which is pronounced "徒敢反" (tu gan fan). According to Yupian and Qieyun (ancient dictionaries), "啖" is the correct form, while "著两火" is a common variant, both pronounced "徒敢反." It should be written as "啖" without doubt. For "啗马" (feeding a horse), the character "啗" should be used, pronounced "徒滥反" (tu lan fan). By definition, eating something by oneself is called "啖," and feeding someone else is also called "啖." According to the Book of Han: Annals of Emperor Gao, it says, "Send Li Siqi and Lu Jia to persuade, and offer them benefits to entice them." The commentary by Yan Shigu says, "啗 originally means to eat, pronounced '徒敢反.' When used to feed someone, it changes to '徒滥反.' Now, it is used to entice with benefits, taking the actual meaning as the basis." Although Yan Shigu's explanation is not clear, he does not mention the character "口傍两火" (two fires on the side of the mouth). This clearly shows that the character has been passed down through common usage, and later it became a surname due to this tradition. Recently, when writing "饼啖" (cakes and eating), some people have written "食边口边作覃" (food side and mouth side as Tan) and "口边作詹" (mouth side as Zhan), which are arbitrary and very incorrect. Qieyun is a common reference book; why not keep it on your desk for quick reference? [71]

    卷六 羽部

    　　南人长林中大树谓之有神，云近此伐木，则必神怒致祸。□□乃斧他人之树，为主所诉，官捕而鞫之，当以盗论。其家质田以赂狱吏，鬻衣以备囚粮，卒不免笞背，皆被疮而归，此乃避祸而速祸也。
    　　又有里人为邻巫所惑，而当有灾，宜谢神，乃杀家犊，酿酒，声鼓以祀。时官禁屠牛私酿，法甚峻。又当国忌，不合动乐。并犯三罪，为吏所擒。家长邻保，皆抵重罪，连及数十人，此及禳灾适所以致灾也。其愚如此。
    　　洪州优胡曹赞者，长近八尺，知书而多慧。凡诸谐戏，曲尽其能。又善为水嬉，百尺樯上不解衣，投身而下，正坐水面，若在茵席。又于水上靴而浮。或令人以囊盛之，系其囊口，浮于江上，自解其系。至于回旋出没，变易千状，见者目骇神竦，莫能测之≈有他术致之，不尔真轻生也。 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 南人長林中大樹謂之有神 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the people of the south believe that large trees in dense forests are divine.' Reflects a cultural belief in the sanctity of nature.
    • 斧他人之樹 - Literally 'chopping down someone else's tree,' implies an act of deforestation or trespassing.
    • 主所訴 - Means 'the owner complained,' indicating a legal action taken by the tree owner.
    • 官捕而鞫之 - Means 'the officials captured and interrogated him,' showing the legal process.
    • 當以盜論 - Means 'it should be treated as theft,' indicating the legal charge.
    • 質田以赂獄吏 - Means 'pledged their land to bribe the jailer,' reflecting a corrupt practice.
    • 鬻衣以備囚糧 - Means 'sold their clothes to provide food for the prisoner,' indicating extreme poverty.
    • 卒不免笞背 - Means 'eventually could not escape being beaten on the back,' indicating the physical punishment.
    • 皆被疮而归 - Means 'all returned with wounds,' indicating the physical toll of the punishment.
    • 此乃避祸而速祸也 - Means 'this is avoiding disaster only to hasten it,' indicating a paradoxical outcome.
    • 巫所惑 - Means 'bewitched by a witch,' indicating a superstitious belief.
    • 宜谢神 - Means 'should thank the gods,' indicating a ritual practice.
    • 杀家犊 - Means 'killed their family's calf,' indicating a significant sacrifice.
    • 酿酒 - Means 'brewed alcohol,' indicating a ritual preparation.
    • 声鼓以祀 - Means 'beat drums to worship,' indicating a religious ceremony.
    • 官禁屠牛私酿 - Means 'officials banned slaughtering cattle and private brewing,' indicating legal restrictions.
    • 法甚峻 - Means 'the law is very strict,' indicating the severity of the punishment.
    • 国忌 - Refers to a national mourning period.
    • 不合动乐 - Means 'it is not allowed to play music,' indicating a cultural taboo.
    • 并犯三罪 - Means 'committed three crimes,' indicating multiple violations.
    • 为吏所擒 - Means 'were captured by the officials,' indicating the legal consequences.
    • 家长邻保 - Refers to the head of the household and neighbors.
    • 皆抵重罪 - Means 'all were punished with severe crimes,' indicating the harsh legal penalties.
    • 连及数十人 - Means 'implicated dozens of people,' indicating the widespread impact.
    • 此及禳灾适所以致灾也 - Means 'this attempt to ward off disaster actually caused disaster,' indicating a paradoxical outcome.
    • 其愚如此 - Means 'their foolishness is like this,' indicating a judgment on the villagers' actions.
    • 洪州优胡曹赞者 - Refers to a famous actor named Cao Zan from Hongzhou.
    • 长近八尺 - Means 'tall, nearly eight chi,' indicating his height.
    • 知书而多慧 - Means 'educated and very intelligent,' indicating his background and abilities.
    • 凡诸谐戏 - Means 'all kinds of humorous performances,' indicating his skills.
    • 曲尽其能 - Means 'exhausted all his talents,' indicating his versatility.
    • 善为水嬉 - Means 'skilled at water entertainment,' indicating his special talent.
    • 百尺樯上不解衣 - Means 'climbed a hundred-foot mast without undressing,' indicating his physical prowess.
    • 投身而下 - Means 'jumped down,' indicating his acrobatic skill.
    • 正坐水面 - Means 'sat upright on the water,' indicating his water skills.
    • 若在茵席 - Means 'as if sitting on a mat,' indicating the smoothness of his movement.
    • 靴而浮 - Means 'floated in boots,' indicating his ability to perform in water.
    • 或令人以囊盛之 - Means 'sometimes he was put into a bag by others,' indicating a specific performance.
    • 系其囊口 - Means 'with the bag's mouth tied,' indicating the method of the performance.
    • 浮于江上 - Means 'floated on the river,' indicating the setting.
    • 自解其系 - Means 'untied himself,' indicating his skill.
    • 至于回旋出没 - Means 'as for circling and appearing and disappearing,' indicating his acrobatic movements.
    • 变易千状 - Means 'changing into a thousand forms,' indicating the variety of his performances.
    • 见者目骇神竦 - Means 'those who saw it were terrified and amazed,' indicating the audience's reaction.
    • 莫能测之 - Means 'no one could understand it,' indicating the mystery of his skills.
    • 有他术致之 - Means 'there must be some other trick,' indicating a possible explanation.
    • 不尔真轻生也 - Means 'otherwise he would truly be risking his life,' indicating the danger of his performances.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南人 - Refers to people from the southern regions of China, indicating a regional cultural difference.
    • 长林中大樹 - Refers to large trees in dense forests, indicating a natural setting.
    • 神 - Refers to divine beings, indicating a belief in the supernatural.
    • 巫所惑 - Indicates a superstitious belief in witchcraft.
    • 宜谢神 - Indicates a ritual practice of thanking the gods.
    • 杀家犊 - Indicates a significant sacrifice in a ritual.
    • 酿酒 - Indicates a ritual preparation.
    • 声鼓以祀 - Indicates a religious ceremony.
    • 官禁屠牛私酿 - Indicates legal restrictions on certain practices.
    • 法甚峻 - Indicates the severity of the law.
    • 国忌 - Refers to a national mourning period, indicating a cultural taboo.
    • 不合动乐 - Indicates a cultural taboo on playing music during mourning.
    • 洪州 - Refers to Hongzhou, a region in southern China, indicating a specific geographical context.
    • 优胡 - Refers to a type of performer, indicating a cultural context.
    • 曹赞者 - Refers to a specific individual, indicating a historical context.
    • 长近八尺 - Indicates his height, reflecting a cultural emphasis on physical attributes.
    • 知书而多慧 - Indicates his education and intelligence, reflecting a cultural value on learning.
    • 凡诸谐戏 - Indicates his skills in humorous performances, reflecting a cultural appreciation for entertainment.
    • 曲尽其能 - Indicates his versatility, reflecting a cultural value on multifaceted talents.
    • 善为水嬉 - Indicates his special talent in water entertainment, reflecting a cultural appreciation for physical skills.
    • 百尺樯上不解衣 - Indicates his physical prowess, reflecting a cultural emphasis on strength and agility.
    • 投身而下 - Indicates his acrobatic skill, reflecting a cultural appreciation for daring performances.
    • 正坐水面 - Indicates his water skills, reflecting a cultural appreciation for water-based entertainment.
    • 若在茵席 - Indicates the smoothness of his movement, reflecting a cultural appreciation for grace and elegance.
    • 靴而浮 - Indicates his ability to perform in water, reflecting a cultural appreciation for water-based entertainment.
    • 或令人以囊盛之 - Indicates a specific performance, reflecting a cultural appreciation for unique and daring acts.
    • 系其囊口 - Indicates the method of the performance, reflecting a cultural appreciation for technical skill.
    • 浮于江上 - Indicates the setting, reflecting a cultural appreciation for natural settings.
    • 自解其系 - Indicates his skill.

    Translation

    Chapter 6, Feather Department

    The people of the south believe that large trees in dense forests are divine. They say that cutting down trees near these places will surely anger the gods and bring disaster. Someone cut down someone else's tree, and the owner complained. The officials captured and interrogated him, and it was decided that he should be treated as a thief. His family pledged their land to bribe the jailer and sold their clothes to provide food for the prisoner. Eventually, they could not escape being beaten on the back and all returned with wounds. This is avoiding disaster only to hasten it.

    There were also villagers who were bewitched by a neighbor's witch and believed they would have a disaster. They should thank the gods, so they killed their family's calf, brewed alcohol, and beat drums to worship. At the time, officials banned slaughtering cattle and private brewing, and the laws were very strict. Additionally, it was a national mourning period, and playing music was not allowed. They committed three crimes and were captured by the officials. The head of the household, neighbors, and protectors were all punished with severe crimes, and dozens of people were implicated. This attempt to ward off disaster actually caused disaster. Their foolishness is like this.

    Cao Zan, a famous actor from Hongzhou, was tall, nearly eight chi, educated, and very intelligent. He excelled in all kinds of humorous performances, exhausting all his talents. He was also skilled at water entertainment, climbing a hundred-foot mast without undressing and jumping down, sitting upright on the water as if sitting on a mat. He floated in boots on the water. Sometimes he was put into a bag by others, with the bag's mouth tied, and floated on the river, untying himself. As for circling and appearing and disappearing, changing into a thousand forms, those who saw it were terrified and amazed, and no one could understand it. There must be some other trick; otherwise, he would truly be risking his life. [72]

    军中有透剑门伎。大宴日，庭中设幄数十步，若廊宇者，而编剑刃为榱栋之状。其人乘小马，至门审度，马调道端，下鞭而进，铮焉闻剑动之声。既过，而人马无伤。宣武军有小将善此伎。每飨军则为之。所获赏止于三数疋帛而已。一日，主者误漏其名，此将忿恨，诉于所管大将，得复以名入。至呈伎之际，极为调审，入数步，忽风起，马惊，触剑失序，人马皆毙于刃下。
    　　有士鬻产于外，得钱数百缗，惧川途之难赍也，祈所知纳于公藏，而持牒以归，世所谓“便换”者，置之衣囊。一日，醉，指囊示人曰：“莫轻此囊，大有好物。”盗在侧闻之。其夜，杀而取其囊，意其有金也。既开无获，投牒于水。盗为吏所捕，得其状。枢机之发，岂容易哉此所谓不密而致害也。
    　　进士郑翚说，家在高邮，有亲表卢氏，庄近水。有邻人数家，共杀一白蛇，未久，忽大震雷电雨，发洪，数家皆陷溺无遗，惟卢氏当中一家无恙。 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 透剑门伎 - A specialized martial art where performers pass through a barrier of swords.
    • 便换 - A form of financial transaction or exchange.
    • 枢机之发 - The slightest slip can lead to great harm.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宣武军 - A military unit during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 进士 - A degree obtained in the imperial examination system.
    • 高邮 - A city in Jiangsu Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 白蛇 - Often associated with folklore and mythological stories in Chinese culture.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宣武军 - A military unit during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 高邮 - A city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 郑翚 - The name of the scholar.
    • 卢氏 - The name of the relative.

    The military had a performance called the "Sword Gate Passing" art. On grand feast days, they would set up tents in the courtyard for dozens of steps, arranged like corridors and halls, and swords were woven into the shape of rafters and beams. The performer would ride a small horse, carefully measure the path at the gate, adjust the direction, lower the whip, and move forward. The sound of the swords moving could be heard. After passing, both the person and the horse remained unharmed. In the Xuanwu army, a small general was skilled in this art and performed it during military feasts. The reward he received was only a few bolts of silk.

    One day, the overseer accidentally omitted his name, and the general became angry. He complained to the commanding general and was allowed to submit his name again. At the time of the performance, he was extremely careful in preparation. He walked several steps, but suddenly the wind rose, and the horse was startled. It touched the swords and lost balance, and both the person and the horse were killed by the swords.

    There was a scholar who sold his property elsewhere and received several hundred strings of coins. Fearing the difficulty of transporting the money, he asked a trusted person to deposit it in a public treasury and carried a receipt back. This is what the world calls "convenience exchange," and he placed the receipt in his clothes bag. One day, drunk, he pointed to the bag and said to someone, "Don't underestimate this bag; it contains valuable things." A thief heard this. That night, the thief killed him and took the bag, thinking it contained gold. Upon opening, he found nothing and threw the receipt into the water. The thief was caught by officials, and his actions were discovered. The slightest slip can lead to great harm; this is what is meant by being careless and causing harm.

    The scholar Zheng Hui said, "His home was in Gaoyou. He had a relative named Lu, whose estate was near the water. Several neighboring families, together, killed a white snake. Soon after, there was a sudden thunderstorm, causing a flood. Several families were drowned without a trace, but only Lu's family in the middle remained unharmed." [73]

    窦相易直，幼时名秘。家贫，受业村学，教授叟有道术，而人不知。一日近墓，风雨暴至。学童悉归家不得，而宿于漏屋之下。寒，争附火。惟窦公寝于榻，夜深方觉。叟抚公令起曰：“窦秘君后为人臣，贵寿之极，勉力自爱也。”及德宗幸奉天日，公方举进士，亦随驾而西。乘一蹇驴，至开远门，人稠路隘，其扉将阖，公惧势不可进。闻一人叱驴，兼捶其后，得疾驰而入。顾见一黑衣卒，呼公曰：“秀才，已后莫忘此情。”及升朝，访得其子，提挈累至大官，吏中荣达。
    　　卢宾客贞白父曰老彭，有道术，兼号知人。元和初，宗人弘宣、简辞、弘正、简求，俱候焉。留坐目之甚久，命贞亦序坐。又目之曰：“一行五节度使，可谓盛矣！”卒如其言。又族子锴，初举进士，就安邑所居谒之。谓锴曰：“尔求名，大是美事。但此后十余年方得，勿以迟晚为恨。登朝亦得大美官。”锴至长庆元年，始擢第。大中十年，终庶子。 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 风雨暴至 - Sudden storm arrived; describes unexpected and severe weather.
    • 学童悉归家不得 - Students all returned home but could not; indicates poor living conditions.
    • 寒，争附火 - Cold, they competed to get close to the fire; describes the students huddling around a fire for warmth.
    • 夜深方觉 - Only woke up at midnight; indicates deep sleep and late waking.
    • 贵寿之极 - The highest rank and longevity; describes the person's future success and longevity.
    • 随驾而西 - Followed the emperor westward; describes the person's journey.
    • 蹇驴 - Slow donkey; describes the person's mode of transportation.
    • 其扉将阖 - The gate was about to close; indicates a narrow escape.
    • 叱驴 - Scold the donkey; describes the intervention that allowed the person to proceed.
    • 捶其后 - Hit it from behind; describes the intervention that allowed the person to proceed.
    • 已后莫忘此情 - Do not forget this kindness; expresses gratitude and remembrance.
    • 吏中荣达 - Achieved great success in the bureaucracy; describes the person's rise to power.
    • 知人 - Recognize people's potential; describes the person's ability to identify talent.
    • 一行五节度使 - All five will become military governors; describes the person's prediction of success.
    • 可谓盛矣 - Which is quite impressive; emphasizes the significance of the prediction.
    • 勿以迟晚为恨 - Do not regret the delay; encourages patience and perseverance.
    • 登朝亦得大美官 - You will also attain a high official position when you enter the court; describes the person's future success.
    • 始擢第 - Was promoted to the top rank; describes the person's achievement.
    • 终庶子 - Became a Shuji (a junior official); describes the person's final position.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 窦相易直 - Historical figure, likely a politician or official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang, who ruled from 779 to 805.
    • 奉天 - Place name, possibly referring to Fengtian, a city in Shaanxi province.
    • 开远门 - Gate in the capital city, likely referring to Chang'an.
    • 元和 - Era name of Emperor Dezong, used from 806 to 820.
    • 长庆 - Era name of Emperor Muzong, used from 821 to 824.
    • 大中 - Era name of Emperor Wuzong, used from 847 to 860.
    • 节度使 - Military governor in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for both military and civil administration.
    • 进士 - High-ranking civil service examination, passing which qualified one for a prestigious government position.
    • 庶子 - Junior official, typically a low-ranking member of the imperial bureaucracy.

    Dong Xiang Yizhi, when he was young, was named Secret. Coming from a poor family, he studied at a village school, where the teacher, an old man with magical arts, taught him. One day near the tomb, a sudden storm arrived. The students all returned home but could not, and stayed under a leaking roof. It was cold, and they competed to get close to the fire. Only Dong Gong slept on the bed, waking up at midnight. The old teacher patted him and said, "Later, Lord Secret will become a minister, reaching the highest rank and longevity, so strive to love yourself." On the day Emperor Dezong visited Fengtian, Dong Gong was just about to take the Jinshi exam and followed the emperor westward. Riding a slow donkey, he reached Kaiyuan Gate, where the road was crowded and narrow, and the gate was about to close, making it impossible to enter. He was afraid that he could not proceed. Hearing someone scold the donkey and hit it from behind, he was able to rush in quickly. Looking back, he saw a soldier in black, who called out, "Scholar, do not forget this kindness." When he rose to the imperial court, he found the soldier's son and helped him rise to high office, achieving great success in the bureaucracy.

    Lu Zhenbai's father, Lao Peng, had magical arts and was also known for his ability to recognize people's potential. In the early Yuanhe era, his relatives Hongxuan, Jiandici, Hongzheng, and Jianqiu all came to visit. He kept them sitting for a long time, and ordered Zhen to sit in order. He looked at them and said, "All five will become military governors, which is quite impressive!" In the end, it happened as he said. Another relative, Kai, who was just about to take the Jinshi exam, visited him at his residence in Anyi. He told Kai, "Your pursuit of fame is a great thing. But you will only achieve it in ten or more years, so do not regret the delay. You will also attain a high official position when you enter the court." Kai was promoted to the top rank in the first year of Changqing and became a Shuji (a junior official) in the tenth year of Dazhong. [74]

    张昙为汾阳王従事，家尝有怪。召术者问之，言以大祸将至，惟休退则免。昙不之信。又方宴宾，席上见血，有巫者闻之，劝其杜门不纳宾客，屏游宴。昙怒杖之。其后昙言语乖度，公颇衔之。又屡言同列间事，每独候见，多值公方宴宠姬所。不令白事，必抑门者令通。公谓其以武臣轻忽，益不平之。后因请公去所任吏，遂发怒，囚之以闻，竟毙于杖。
    　　柳员外宗元，自永州司马征至京，意望录用。一日，诣卜者问命，且告以梦曰：“余柳姓也，昨梦柳树仆地，其不吉乎？”卜者曰：“无苦，但忧为远官耳。”征其意，曰：“夫生则柳树，仆则柳木，木者，牧也。君其牧柳州乎？”卒如其言。 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 従事 - Refers to an official or attendant, often in a subordinate capacity.
    • 大祸将至 - A phrase meaning 'a great calamity is about to come,' indicating impending disaster.
    • 休退则免 - Means 'only by retiring can you avoid it,' suggesting that withdrawal is the only way to escape the calamity.
    • 乖度 - Means 'to deviate from the norm' or 'to act out of line,' implying inappropriate behavior.
    • 公颇衔之 - Means 'the official bore a grudge against him,' indicating resentment.
    • 杜门不纳宾客 - Means 'to shut oneself in and not receive guests,' suggesting isolation.
    • 屏游宴 - Means 'to refrain from feasting and entertainment,' indicating abstinence from pleasure.
    • 杖之 - Means 'to beat him with a stick,' referring to corporal punishment.
    • 独候见 - Means 'to wait and see him alone,' indicating private meetings.
    • 宠姬所 - Refers to the place where the official's favorite concubine resides.
    • 抑门者令通 - Means 'to suppress the gatekeeper and allow entry,' suggesting coercion.
    • 武臣轻忽 - Means 'to treat a military officer with contempt,' indicating disrespect.
    • 囚之以闻 - Means 'to imprison him and report it,' suggesting a formal accusation.
    • 毙于杖 - Means 'to die from beating,' indicating a violent end.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汾阳王 - Refers to a prince or nobleman, likely a high-ranking official or member of the imperial family.
    • 卜者 - Refers to a fortune-teller or diviner, a common figure in ancient Chinese society.
    • 柳员外宗元 - Refers to Liu Zongyuan, a prominent Tang Dynasty writer and philosopher.
    • 永州司马 - Refers to Liu Zongyuan's position as a deputy governor of Yongzhou, a city in Hunan Province.
    • 柳州 - Refers to Liuzhou, a city in Guangxi, where Liu Zongyuan was later appointed as governor.
    • 梦柳树仆地 - Refers to a dream where a willow tree falls over, a symbolic event that Liu Zongyuan interprets as an omen.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张昙 - Zhang Tan, the subject of the first part of the text.
    • 术者 - A diviner or fortune-teller.
    • 柳员外宗元 - Liu Zongyuan, a prominent Tang Dynasty writer and philosopher.
    • 永州司马 - Liu Zongyuan's position as a deputy governor of Yongzhou.
    • 柳州 - Liuzhou, a city in Guangxi where Liu Zongyuan was later appointed as governor.
    • 卜者 - A diviner or fortune-teller.

    Zhang Tan served as an attendant to Prince Fenyang. Once, his family experienced a strange occurrence. He summoned a diviner to inquire about it, who said that a great calamity was about to come, and only by retiring could he avoid it. Zhang Tan did not believe him. During a banquet, blood was seen on the table. A shaman heard about it and advised him to shut himself in and not receive guests, and to refrain from feasting and entertainment. Zhang Tan became angry and beat the shaman with a stick. Later, Zhang Tan's words and actions deviated from the norm, and the official bore a grudge against him.
