Zhang Tan also frequently mentioned matters concerning his colleagues, often waiting alone to see the official, who was often at his favorite concubine's residence. The official did not allow him to report matters and would suppress the gatekeeper to let him enter. The official considered Zhang Tan's behavior as treating a military officer with contempt and became even more resentful. Eventually, Zhang Tan asked the official to dismiss the official he had appointed, which infuriated the official. He imprisoned Zhang Tan and reported it, and Zhang Tan ultimately died from beating.

    Liu Zongyuan, an external scholar from Yongzhou, was summoned to the capital with the hope of being appointed to a position. One day, he visited a diviner to inquire about his fate and told him a dream: "I am of the surname Liu. Last night, I dreamed of a willow tree falling to the ground. Is this a bad omen?" The diviner said, "There is no need to worry. Just be concerned about being appointed to a distant post." When Liu Zongyuan asked for more details, the diviner explained, "When alive, it is a willow tree; when fallen, it becomes willow wood. Wood is associated with herding. Will you be appointed as the governor of Liuzhou?" Indeed, Liu Zongyuan was later appointed as the governor of Liuzhou, just as the diviner had predicted. [75]

    李凉公逢吉，未掌纶诰前，家有老婢好言梦，后多有应。李公久望除官，因访于婢。一日，婢晨至，惨然。公问其故，曰：“昨夜与郎君作梦，不是好意，不欲说。”公强之。婢曰：“梦有一人，舁一棺至堂后云：‘且置在此。’不久，即移入堂中，此梦恐非佳也。”公闻窃喜。俄尔除中书舍人。后知贡举，未毕而入相。
    　　都水使者崔绰，少年豪侠，不拘小节，天宝中，有方士过其家，崔倾财奉之，亦无所望。方士临去，留药一丸为别，崔殊不之重，埋于床下。燕蓟之乱，家人避贼，崔在后未去。忽见床下有菌，甚肥鲜，因煮而食之，杂以荤味，自此体腹轻健，至老更无疾病，月中视小字，夜食生彘。元和初犹在，年九十余卒。苏州刺史韦公集中所《赠崔都水诗》者是也。向得灵药，便能正尔服之，当已轻举矣。其次，食所化灵芝，不杂荤茹，又应反颜住世，寿不可量，盖玄中但以有寿无疾，酬好施之功而已。崔即苏州之堂妹婿也。 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 掌纶诰 - Formal title meaning 'in charge of imperial edicts,' referring to a high-ranking official position.
    • 家有老婢好言梦 - Literally means 'the household has an old maid who likes to talk about dreams,' introducing a character with a specific trait.
    • 不欲说 - Means 'does not want to say,' indicating reluctance or hesitation.
    • 梦有一人，舁一棺至堂后云 - A dream sequence where someone carries a coffin to the back of the hall, a common motif in classical Chinese literature symbolizing death or change.
    • 且置在此 - Means 'let's put it here temporarily,' indicating a temporary placement.
    • 移入堂中 - Means 'move it into the hall,' suggesting a permanent or more significant placement.
    • 窃喜 - Means 'secretly happy,' indicating a hidden or suppressed emotion.
    • 俄尔除中书舍人 - Means 'soon after being appointed as a censor in the central secretariat,' referring to a rapid promotion.
    • 知贡举 - Means 'in charge of the imperial examination,' indicating a high-ranking position in the examination system.
    • 燕蓟之乱 - Refers to a historical event, the rebellion in the Yan and Jie regions during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 不拘小节 - Means 'not bound by minor details,' describing a person who is carefree or unconventional.
    • 方士 - Refers to a Taoist magician or alchemist.
    • 留药一丸为别 - Means 'left a pill as a parting gift,' indicating a farewell gesture.
    • 殊不之重 - Means 'did not value it much,' indicating a lack of appreciation.
    • 埋于床下 - Means 'buried it under the bed,' describing the action of hiding the pill.
    • 燕蓟之乱，家人避贼，崔在后未去 - Describes the historical context of the rebellion and the character's actions during the chaos.
    • 忽见床下有菌，甚肥鲜 - Means 'suddenly saw a mushroom under the bed, very fat and fresh,' describing an unexpected discovery.
    • 因煮而食之，杂以荤味 - Means 'cooked and ate it, mixed with meat flavors,' describing the character's actions.
    • 体腹轻健 - Means 'body and stomach became light and healthy,' describing the physical effects.
    • 月中视小字 - Means 'could read small characters in the moonlight,' indicating improved vision.
    • 夜食生彘 - Means 'ate raw pork at night,' describing unusual behavior.
    • 元和初犹在，年九十余卒 - Means 'still alive at the beginning of the Yuanhe era, died at over ninety years old,' providing a historical timeline.
    • 苏州刺史韦公集中所《赠崔都水诗》者是也 - Refers to a poem by the governor of Suzhou, indicating a cultural reference.
    • 向得灵药，便能正尔服之，当已轻举矣 - Means 'if he had taken the magic pill properly, he would have already ascended to immortality,' indicating a missed opportunity.
    • 其次，食所化灵芝，不杂荤茹，又应反颜住世，寿不可量 - Means 'secondly, if he had eaten the transformed lingzhi without mixing it with meat, he should have been able to live to a very old age,' indicating another missed opportunity.
    • 盖玄中但以有寿无疾，酬好施之功而已 - Means 'the Taoist gods only rewarded him with longevity and good health for his kindness and generosity,' providing a moral lesson.
    • 崔即苏州之堂妹婿也 - Means 'Cui was the brother-in-law of the governor of Suzhou,' providing a familial connection.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李凉公逢吉 - Refers to Li Lianggong Fengji, a historical figure who held high-ranking positions in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 中书舍人 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty's central government.
    • 知贡举 - A high-ranking position in the imperial examination system, responsible for overseeing the selection of candidates.
    • 燕蓟之乱 - A historical rebellion in the Yan and Jie regions during the Tang Dynasty, indicating a period of political instability.
    • 元和初 - Refers to the early years of the Yuanhe era (806-820) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 苏州刺史韦公 - Refers to the governor of Suzhou, indicating a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔都水 - Refers to Cui Duishui, a historical figure who was the brother-in-law of the governor of Suzhou.
    • 灵芝 - A mythical fungus believed to grant immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 玄中 - Refers to the Taoist gods, indicating a religious belief system.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李凉公逢吉 - Li Lianggong Fengji, a historical figure.
    • 中书舍人 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 燕蓟之乱 - A historical rebellion in the Yan and Jie regions during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和初 - The early years of the Yuanhe era (806-820) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 苏州刺史韦公 - The governor of Suzhou, a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔都水 - Cui Duishui, a historical figure who was the brother-in-law of the governor of Suzhou.
    • 灵芝 - A mythical fungus believed to grant immortality in Chinese culture.
    • 玄中 - The Taoist gods, indicating a religious belief system.

    Li Lianggong Fengji, before he took charge of imperial edicts, had an old maid in his household who liked to talk about dreams, and many of them came true. Li had long hoped to be appointed to an official position, so he consulted the maid about it. One day, the maid came in the morning with a sad expression. Li asked her why, and she said, "Last night I had a dream about you, and it wasn't a good one. I don't want to say it." Li urged her to tell him. The maid said, "In the dream, I saw a man carrying a coffin to the back of the hall and saying, 'Let's put it here temporarily.' Soon after, it was moved into the hall. This dream doesn't seem good." Li secretly rejoiced. Soon after, he was appointed as a censor in the central secretariat. Later, he was put in charge of the imperial examination, and before he finished his duties, he entered the government.

    The water commissioner Cui Chuo was a young hero who did not adhere to minor details. During the Tianbao era, a Taoist magician passed by his home. Cui lavishly supported him, but had no expectations. As the magician was leaving, he left a pill as a parting gift. Cui did not value it much and buried it under the bed. During the rebellion in the Yan and Jie regions, his family fled from the bandits, but Cui stayed behind. Suddenly, he saw a fat and fresh mushroom under the bed, so he cooked and ate it, mixed with meat flavors. From then on, his body and stomach became light and healthy, and he had no more diseases. He could read small characters in the moonlight and ate raw pork at night. He was still alive at the beginning of the Yuanhe era and died at over ninety years old. He was the brother-in-law of the governor of Suzhou, and the governor's collection included a poem titled "Gift to Cui Dushui." If he had taken the magic pill properly, he would have already ascended to immortality. Secondly, if he had eaten the transformed lingzhi without mixing it with meat, he should have been able to live to a very old age. The Taoist gods only rewarded him with longevity and good health for his kindness and generosity. [76]

    崔相国群之镇徐州，尝以崔氏《易林》自筮。遇乾之大畜，其繇曰：“典策法书，藏在兰台。虽遭乱渎，独不遇灾。”及经王智兴之变，果除秘书监也。
    　　长庆中，鄂州里巷间人，每语，辄以牛字助之。又有一僧，自号“牛师”，乍愚乍智，人有忤之者，必云：“我兄即到，岂奈我何？”未几，而相国奇章公带平章事节度武昌军，其语乃绝。而牛师尚存。僧者，乃牛公之名也。方知将相之位，岂偶然耶先是，元和初，韩尚书皋在夏口，就加节度使，自后复为观察使。长庆三年，崔相国植従刑部尚书除观察。明年冬，牛公实来。宰臣建节镇夏口，自牛公始也。 [77]

    Cui Qun, the chancellor, was appointed to govern Xuzhou. He once used the Cui family's Yilin (Book of Changes divination text) to divinate for himself. He encountered the hexagram Qian (乾) transforming into Daxu (大畜), and the divination result said: "Important documents and legal texts, stored in the imperial library. Even if they were defiled, they alone did not suffer disaster." When the upheaval caused by Wang Zhixing occurred, he was indeed appointed as the Secretary of the Imperial Library.

    During the reign of Emperor Muzong, in the streets of Ezhou, people often added the character "niu" (牛) to their speech. There was also a monk who called himself "Niu Shi" (牛师), sometimes acting foolishly and sometimes wisely. If someone offended him, he would always say, "My brother is coming, how can you challenge me?" Soon after, Chancellor Qizhang Gong (Han Yu) was appointed as the military governor of Wuchang, and the monk's words ceased. The monk, however, still lived. The monk's name was actually "Niu Gong" (牛公). It was then realized that the position of chancellor or high-ranking military officer was not accidental. Before this, during the reign of Emperor Xianzong, Han Shangshu Gao (Han Gao) was stationed in Xiazhou (modern Wuhan) and was later appointed as the military governor. In the third year of the Changqing era, Cui Qun, the chancellor, was appointed as the military governor of Wuchang from his position as Minister of Justice. The following winter, Niu Gong (Ni Zan) came. The chancellor was appointed as the military governor of Xiazhou, and this was the first time since Niu Gong. [77]

    博陵崔子年出书一通示余曰：“刘逸淮在汴时，韩弘为右厢虞候，王某为左厢虞候，与弘相善。或谮二人取军情，将不利于刘。刘大怒，俱召诘之。弘即刘之甥，因控地碎首，大言数百，刘意稍解。王某年老股战，不能自辩。刘叱令拉坐，杖三十。时新造赤棒，头径数寸，固以筋漆，立之不仆，数五六当死矣。韩意其必死，及昏，造其家，怪无哭声。又谓其惧不敢哭，访其门卒，即言大使无恙。弘素与某熟，遂至卧内问之。王曰：‘我读《金刚经》四十年矣，今方得力。记初被坐时，见巨手如簸箕，吸然遮背。’因袒示韩，都无挞痕。韩旧不好释氏，由此始与僧往来。日自写十纸。及贵，计数百轴矣。后在中书，盛署时，有谏官因事谒见，韩方洽汗写经。谏官怪问之，韩乃具道王某事。予职在集仙，常侍柳公，常为予说。” [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 出书一通 - Produced a scroll of books, meaning he presented a collection of writings.
    • 示余曰 - A polite way of saying 'showed to me and said'.
    • 俱召詰之 - Summoned both to interrogate.
    • 因控地碎首 - Bowed down and smashed his head on the ground, a dramatic gesture of submission.
    • 大言數百 - Spoke loudly for hundreds of words.
    • 劉意稍解 - Liu's anger slightly subsided.
    • 杖三十 - Beaten with thirty strokes.
    • 固以筋漆 - Strengthened with sinew and lacquer.
    • 數五六當死矣 - After five or six strokes, it should have been fatal.
    • 怪無哭聲 - Found it strange that there was no crying.
    • 及昏 - By dusk.
    • 造其家 - Visited his home.
    • 即言大使無恙 - Who said the ambassador was fine.
    • 因袒示韓 - So revealed it to Han.
    • 都無撻痕 - There were no marks of beating.
    • 日自寫十紙 - Wrote ten sheets every day.
    • 計數百軸矣 - Calculated to be hundreds of scrolls.
    • 韓方洽汗寫經 - Han was sweating while writing the sutras.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 博陵 - A historical region in present-day Hebei Province.
    • 劉逸淮 - A historical figure, likely a military leader or official.
    • 汴 - Present-day Kaifeng, a major city during the Tang and Song dynasties.
    • 韓弘 - A historical figure, likely a military leader.
    • 虞候 - A military rank, indicating a position of authority within the army.
    • 《金剛經》 - The *Diamond Sutra*, a significant Buddhist text.
    • 釋氏 - A term for Buddhism, often used in classical Chinese texts.
    • 中書 - The Office of the Secretariat, a high-ranking government office.
    • 集仙 - The Bureau of the Immortals, a government office responsible for religious and cultural affairs.
    • 柳公 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.

    A Collection of Writings Presented to Me by Cui Zini of Boling

    Cui Zini of Boling presented a collection of writings to me and said: "When Liu Yi Huai was in Bian, Han Hong was the right flank guard, and Wang was the left flank guard. They were on good terms with each other. Someone accused the two of gaining military support, which would be detrimental to Liu. Liu became very angry and summoned both to interrogate them. Han was Liu's nephew, so he bowed down and smashed his head on the ground, speaking loudly for hundreds of words. Liu's anger slightly subsided. Wang was old and his legs trembled, unable to defend himself. Liu shouted and ordered him to be pulled down and beaten with thirty strokes. At that time, newly made red sticks, with heads several inches in diameter, strengthened with sinew and lacquer, could stand without falling. After five or six strokes, it should have been fatal. Han thought he would surely die. By dusk, he visited Wang's home, finding it strange that there was no crying. He thought Wang was afraid and dared not cry, so he asked the gatekeeper, who said the ambassador was fine. Han was familiar with Wang, so he went to his bedroom to ask him. Wang said, 'I have read the Diamond Sutra for forty years, and now it is starting to bear fruit. I remember when I was beaten, I saw a huge hand like a winnowing basket, suddenly covering my back.' So he revealed it to Han, who saw there were no marks of beating. Han used to not like Buddhism, but from this began to associate with monks, writing ten sheets every day. When he became prominent, he had written hundreds of scrolls. Later in the Office of the Secretariat, during the hot summer, a censor visited on official business. Han was sweating while writing the sutras. The censor was surprised and asked him, to which Han fully explained Wang's story. My position is in the Bureau of the Immortals, and the constant attendant Liou Gong often told me this." [78]

    王智兴在徐州，法令甚严。有防秋官健交代归，其妹婿于家中设馔以贺。自于厨中磨刀，将就生割羊脚。磨讫，持之疾行，妻兄自堂走入厨，仓卒相值，锋正中妻兄心，即死。所在擒之以告，智兴讯问，但称过误，本无恶意。智兴不之信，命斩之。刀辄自刑者手中跃出，径投于地，三换皆然。智兴异之，乃不杀。余按《广陵烈士传》曰：“刘隽，字幼节。迁宛朐令。到官二年，政治清平，为吏民所亲。时县有友人相过者，主人欢喜为具，捕犬，因误中客⊥死，平法者云：‘主人本有杀心，应当伏辜。’隽曰：‘闻许太子至孝，误不尝药，史官书弑君，曰：尽心力以事君，舍药物可也。今主人与客，本无雠恨，但欢喜为供，有亲爱饮食之意，无伤害之心，不幸而死，当以《周礼》过误平之，奈何欲用法律所失，一时两杀不辜。’王法者，拘有常例不听。隽曰：‘界有失礼之民，皆令之罪也。’解印绶去。”盖与此事相近。而徐州者，神为之辩耳。 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 防秋官健 - Guards or soldiers stationed during the autumn to defend against northern invasions.
    • 交代歸 - To hand over duty and return home, referring to the transition of guard duties.
    • 設饌以賀 - To prepare a feast to celebrate, indicating a social gathering.
    • 磨刀 - Literally 'grind the knife,' a preparatory action for slaughtering.
    • 生割羊腳 - To cut raw mutton, a common practice in preparing meat.
    • 疾行 - To walk quickly, describing the man's hurried movement.
    • 倉卒相值 - To meet unexpectedly in a hurry, describing the sudden encounter.
    • 鋒正中妻兄心，即死 - The blade struck his sister-in-law's brother in the heart, and he died immediately, a literal description of the fatal incident.
    • 擒之以告 - To capture him and report, indicating the arrest and reporting of the incident.
    • 訊問 - To interrogate, describing the official questioning of the suspect.
    • 本無惡意 - No ill intent, the suspect's claim of innocence.
    • 命斬之 - Ordered to be executed, the punishment decided by Wang Zhixing.
    • 刀輒自刑者手中躍出，徑投於地 - The knife leaped out of the executioner's hand and fell to the ground, a supernatural event.
    • 三換皆然 - Three times it happened the same way, indicating the repeated supernatural occurrence.
    • 異之 - Found it strange, Wang Zhixing's reaction to the supernatural event.
    • 乃不殺 - Then decided not to kill, Wang Zhixing's final decision.
    • 廣陵烈士傳 - A historical record or biography, likely a local or regional history.
    • 劉隽，字幼節 - A historical figure named Liu Jun, with the courtesy name Youjie.
    • 遷宛朐令 - Appointed as the magistrate of Wanqu, indicating his official position.
    • 政治清平 - Governance was clear and peaceful, describing his administration.
    • 為吏民所親 - Was beloved by officials and people, indicating his popularity.
    • 友人相過者 - A friend who came to visit, describing a social visit.
    • 主人歡喜為具 - The host prepared a feast with joy, indicating hospitality.
    • 捕犬，因誤中客 - Caught a dog, which accidentally hit the guest, describing the accidental injury.
    • 平法者 - Legal experts or officials responsible for interpreting and applying the law.
    • 盡心力以事君，舍藥物可也 - To serve the ruler with all one's heart and strength, and to avoid taking medicine is acceptable, a reference to a historical anecdote.
    • 界有失禮之民，皆令之罪也 - If there are people who lose their manners within the jurisdiction, it is the magistrate's fault, a statement of responsibility.
    • 解印綬去 - Removed his seal and resigned, indicating his resignation.
    • 蓋與此事相近 - This is similar to the current case, a comparison between historical and contemporary events.
    • 神為之辯耳 - It was the gods who intervened, a supernatural explanation for the events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 徐州 - Refers to the city of Xuzhou, an important city in ancient China, known for its strategic location and historical significance.
    • 王智兴 - Refers to Wang Zhixing, a historical figure who served as the governor of Xuzhou.
    • 防秋 - Refers to the practice of strengthening defenses during the autumn to prepare for potential invasions, a common military strategy in ancient China.
    • 廣陵烈士傳 - Refers to a historical record or biography, likely a local or regional history, which provides context for the comparison.
    • 劉隽，字幼节 - Refers to Liu Jun, a historical figure known for his fair and just governance.
    • 宛朐 - Refers to the county of Wanqu, a place where Liu Jun served as the magistrate.
    • 尽心力以事君，舍药物可也 - Refers to a historical anecdote about the Prince of Chu, who refused to take medicine to avoid appearing weak in front of his father, leading to his death.
    • 界有失礼之民，皆令之罪也 - Refers to the principle of a magistrate's responsibility for the behavior of people within his jurisdiction.

    Proper Nouns

    • 徐州 - The city of Xuzhou.
    • 王智兴 - Wang Zhixing, the governor of Xuzhou.
    • 广陵烈士传 - A historical record or biography.
    • 刘隽，字幼节 - Liu Jun, a historical figure.
    • 宛朐 - The county of Wanqu.
    • 许太子 - The Prince of Chu, a historical figure.

    Wang Zhixing, while serving as the governor of Xuzhou, enforced strict laws. One day, a guard who had just handed over his duty and was returning home, prepared a feast to celebrate with his sister-in-law. He was grinding a knife in the kitchen to prepare for cutting raw mutton. After finishing, he quickly took the knife and walked away. His sister-in-law's brother entered the kitchen from the hall, and they met unexpectedly. The blade struck him in the heart, and he died immediately. The incident was reported, and Wang Zhixing interrogated the suspect, who claimed that it was an accidental mistake and that he had no ill intent. Wang Zhixing did not believe him and ordered his execution. However, the knife repeatedly leaped out of the executioner's hand and fell to the ground, happening three times in a row. Surprised by this supernatural event, Wang Zhixing decided not to kill him.

    According to the Guangling Lishi Zhuan (Biographies of Heroes from Guangling), it is recorded: "Liu Jun, courtesy name Youjie, was appointed as the magistrate of Wanqu. Upon taking office for two years, his governance was clear and peaceful, and he was beloved by officials and people. Once, a friend visited the county, and the host prepared a feast with joy. During the feast, a dog was caught, which accidentally hit the guest and caused his death. Legal experts said, 'The host originally had murderous intent and should be held responsible.' Liu Jun replied, 'I have heard that the Prince of Chu was filial and refused to take medicine, which led to his death. The historian recorded it as a case of killing the ruler, saying, "To serve the ruler with all one's heart and strength, and to avoid taking medicine is acceptable." In this case, the host and the guest had no enmity; the host merely prepared a feast with joy and had no intention of harming anyone. Unfortunately, the guest died. According to the Rituals of Zhou, this should be treated as an accidental error. Why use the law to punish both parties unjustly?' The legal system, however, was rigid and would not listen. Liu Jun said, 'If there are people who lose their manners within my jurisdiction, it is my fault.' He then removed his seal and resigned."

    This case is similar to the one in Xuzhou, where the gods intervened to make the judgment. [79]

    汉州开元寺，有菩萨像。自顶及焰光坐趺，都是一段青石。洁腻可爱，雕琢极工，高数尺，会昌毁寺时，佛像多遭摧折刓缺，惟此不伤丝毫。及再立寺，僧振古宝而置放西廊。余与京大德知玄法事西川従事扬仁赡同谒，杨深于释氏，好古之士也。赡敬弥日，而玄心精识多闻，话其本末云：“先是匠人得此石异之，虔心镌刻，殆忘餐寝。有美女常器食给之。其人运思在像，都无邪思。久之，怠而妄心生，女乃不至。饥渴既逼，兼毒厉匝体，遂悟是天女。因焚香叩首，悔谢切至，女复来，其病立愈。而像即成。亦尝有记录，因毁寺失其传焉。寺今再立矣。” [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 漢州開元寺 - Refers to the Kaiyuan Temple in Han Prefecture, a specific historical location.
    • 菩薩像 - A statue of a Bodhisattva, a key figure in Buddhism.
    • 自頂及焰光坐趺，都是一段青石 - Describes the statue as being made from a single piece of blue-green stone, from the top to the flame-like halo and the seated posture.
    • 潔腻可愛 - A vivid description of the statue's surface, meaning 'clean, smooth, and lovely.'
    • 雕琢極工 - Indicates that the carving is extremely meticulous and skillful.
    • 高數尺 - Means 'several feet tall,' indicating the statue's size.
    • 會昌毀寺時 - Refers to the period during the Kaiyuan era when many Buddhist temples were destroyed, a historical event.
    • 佛像多遭摧折刓缺 - Means that many Buddha statues were damaged or broken during this period.
    • 惟此不傷 - Indicates that this particular statue remained undamaged.
    • 及再立寺 - Means 'when the temple was rebuilt.'
    • 僧振古寶而置放西廊 - Describes how the monks preserved the statue as an ancient treasure and placed it in the west corridor.
    • 余與京大德知玄法事西川従事揚仁赡同谒 - A complex sentence introducing the narrator and other individuals who visited the temple together.
    • 楊深于釋氏 - Means that Yang was deeply knowledgeable about Buddhism.
    • 好古之士也 - Indicates that Yang was also a scholar who appreciated ancient things.
    • 赡敬弥日 - Means that Yang showed deep respect throughout the day.
    • 玄心精识多闻 - Describes the monk Xuan as having a profound mind, sharp intellect, and extensive knowledge.
    • 話其本末云 - Means 'he spoke about the origin and details, saying.'
    • 先是匠人得此石异之 - Means 'previously, a craftsman found this stone to be extraordinary.'
    • 虔心镌刻 - Indicates that the craftsman carved the statue with great reverence.
    • 殆忘餐寝 - Means 'almost forgetting to eat or sleep,' emphasizing the craftsman's dedication.
    • 有美女常器食给之 - Describes a beautiful woman who regularly provided food for the craftsman.
    • 其人运思在像，都无邪思 - Means 'the craftsman's thoughts were entirely focused on the statue, with no impure thoughts.'
    • 久之，怠而妄心生 - Indicates that after a long time, the craftsman became lazy and his mind wandered.
    • 女乃不至 - Means 'the woman then stopped coming.'
    • 饥渴既逼，兼毒厉匝体 - Describes the craftsman's suffering from hunger and thirst, and the pain spreading throughout his body.
    • 遂悟是天女 - Means 'he then realized that the woman was a celestial maiden.'
    • 因焚香叩首，悔谢切至 - Describes the craftsman's act of burning incense, bowing, and deeply repenting.
    • 女复来，其病立愈 - Means 'the woman returned, and his illness was instantly cured.'
    • 而像即成 - Indicates that the statue was completed.
    • 亦尝有记录，因毁寺失其传焉 - Means 'there was also a record, but it was lost due to the temple's destruction.'
    • 寺今再立矣 - Means 'the temple has now been rebuilt.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢州開元寺 - A specific temple in Han Prefecture, historically significant in the context of Buddhism in China.
    • 會昌毀寺 - Refers to the Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution during the Kaiyuan era (841-846), when many Buddhist temples were destroyed.
    • 菩薩像 - A central figure in Buddhism, representing compassion and enlightenment.
    • 釋氏 - A term used to refer to Buddhism or Buddhists.
    • 好古之士 - Refers to scholars who appreciate and study ancient texts and artifacts.
    • 天女 - A celestial maiden in Buddhist mythology, often associated with purity and divine intervention.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The long descriptive sentence '漢州開元寺，有菩薩像。自頂及焰光坐趺，都是一段青石。潔腻可愛，雕琢極工，高數尺，會昌毀寺時，佛像多遭摧折刓缺，惟此不傷' must be read as a single unit, detailing the statue's appearance and its survival during the temple's destruction.
    • The clause '及再立寺，僧振古寶而置放西廊' describes the monks' actions after the temple's rebuilding, linking the statue's preservation to its placement.
    • The complex sentence '余與京大德知玄法事西川従事揚仁赡同谒' introduces the narrator and other visitors, requiring careful parsing to maintain clarity.
    • The clause '楊深于釋氏，好古之士也' provides background on Yang's knowledge and interests, linking his character to the context of the visit.
    • The clause '赡敬弥日，而玄心精识多闻，话其本末云' describes Yang's behavior and the monk Xuan's knowledge, setting the stage for the story that follows.
    • The clause '先是匠人得此石异之，虔心镌刻，殆忘餐寝' introduces the craftsman's dedication and the supernatural element of the story.
    • The clause '有美女常器食给之，其人运思在像，都无邪思' describes the craftsman's focus and the mysterious woman's role.
    • The clause '久之，怠而妄心生，女乃不至，饥渴既逼，兼毒厉匝体，遂悟是天女' narrates the craftsman's downfall and his realization of the woman's divine nature.
    • The clause '因焚香叩首，悔谢切至，女复来，其病立愈，而像即成' describes the craftsman's repentance and the statue's completion.
    • The clause '亦尝有记录，因毁寺失其传焉，寺今再立矣' provides historical context and concludes the narrative with the temple's rebuilding.

    Proper Nouns

    • 漢州開元寺 - The Kaiyuan Temple in Han Prefecture.
    • 會昌毀寺 - The Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution during the Kaiyuan era.
    • 楊仁赡 - The name of the scholar who visited the temple.
    • 知玄 - The name of the monk who provided the story.
    • 天女 - A celestial maiden in Buddhist mythology.

    The Kaiyuan Temple in Han Prefecture houses a statue of a Bodhisattva. From the top of the head to the flame-like halo and the seated posture, the entire statue is carved from a single piece of blue-green stone. Its surface is clean, smooth, and lovely, with exquisite craftsmanship, standing several feet tall. During the Kaiyuan era when many Buddhist temples were destroyed, most Buddha statues suffered damage or were broken, but this one remained unscathed. When the temple was rebuilt, the monks preserved it as an ancient treasure and placed it in the west corridor. I, along with the esteemed monk Zhi Xuan, who was in charge of religious affairs in the Western Sichuan region, and Yang Renzan, a scholar who was deeply knowledgeable about Buddhism and appreciated ancient things, visited the temple together. Yang showed deep respect throughout the day, while Zhi Xuan, with his profound mind, sharp intellect, and extensive knowledge, recounted the story: "Previously, a craftsman found this stone to be extraordinary and carved the statue with great reverence, almost forgetting to eat or sleep. A beautiful woman would regularly provide food for him. His thoughts were entirely focused on the statue, with no impure thoughts. After a long time, he became lazy and his mind wandered, and the woman then stopped coming. He suffered from hunger and thirst, and pain spread throughout his body, and he then realized that the woman was a celestial maiden. He burned incense, bowed, and deeply repented, and the woman returned, and his illness was instantly cured. The statue was then completed. There was also a record of this event, but it was lost due to the temple's destruction. The temple has now been rebuilt." [80]

    元和、长庆中，两京闾巷间相见，多云：“合是阿舅。”及太和以来，文宗俗崇树外戚，而诈称国舅者数辈，竟不得其真，合是之说，果有验矣。
    　　余年小，在江汉，尝与群儿戏。以竹｛韦｝为枪，鸟翎饰其上，裂纸为旌旗，作战斗之像，相向云杀。俄尔立定，又云再杀。不数年，宪宗剪除群寇，蔡、齐二巨猾，相次夷灭，再杀之应也。
    　　太和初，京师有轻薄徒，取贡士姓名，以义理编饰为词，号为“举人露布”。九年冬，就戮者，多出自文儒。
    　　太和初，王潜为荆南节度使。无故有白马驰入府门而毙，僵卧塞涂。是岁潜卒。此近马祸也。
    　　进士陈存能为古歌诗，而命蹇。主司每欲与第，临时皆有故，不果，许尚书孟容旧相知，知举日，万方欲为申屈。将试前夕，宿宗人家。宗人为具入试食物，兼备晨食，请存偃息以候时。五更后，怪不起，就寝呼之，不应。前视之，已中风不能言也。 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元和、长庆中 - Refers to the reign periods of Emperor Xianzong (元和) and Emperor Muzong (长庆) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 合是阿舅 - A colloquial expression meaning 'it should be your uncle,' used as a greeting or reference to family relations.
    • 诈称国舅 - Means 'to falsely claim to be the emperor's brother-in-law.'
    • 竟不得其真 - Means 'finally, the true one could not be found.'
    • 合是之说，果有验矣 - Means 'the saying 'it should be your uncle' indeed proved true.'
    • 群儿戏 - Means 'children playing' or 'children's games.'
    • 以竹｛韦｝为枪 - Means 'to make spears out of bamboo strips.'
    • 鸟翎饰其上 - Means 'to decorate the top with bird feathers.'
    • 裂纸为旌旗 - Means 'to tear paper into flags.'
    • 作战斗之像 - Means 'to make a representation of battle.'
    • 相向云杀 - Means 'to face each other and say 'kill.''
    • 俄尔立定 - Means 'soon after, they stood still.'
    • 再杀 - Means 'kill again.'
    • 宪宗剪除群寇 - Means 'Emperor Xianzong eliminated the bandits.'
    • 蔡、齐二巨猾 - Refers to the powerful warlords of Cai and Qi.
    • 相次夷灭 - Means 'were subsequently annihilated.'
    • 举人露布 - Means 'a public announcement by scholars.'
    • 就戮者 - Means 'those who were executed.'
    • 文儒 - Refers to scholars and literati.
    • 轻薄徒 - Means 'idle and frivolous people.'
    • 贡士姓名 - Means 'the names of the scholars who passed the imperial exams.'
    • 义理编饰为词 - Means 'to compile and arrange words according to principles of righteousness.'
    • 王潜为荆南节度使 - Means 'Wang Qian was the military governor of Jingnan.'
    • 无故有白马驰入府门而毙 - Means 'a white horse suddenly rushed into the government office and died.'
    • 僵卧塞涂 - Means 'it lay stiff and blocking the road.'
    • 是岁潜卒 - Means 'that year, Wang Qian died.'
    • 进士陈存能为古歌诗 - Means 'the Jinshi Chen Cun was able to compose ancient-style poems.'
    • 命蹇 - Means 'having bad luck.'
    • 主司 - Refers to the examiners or officials in charge of the imperial exams.
    • 第 - Means 'to pass the exam and be ranked.'
    • 临时皆有故 - Means 'at the time, there were always reasons preventing him from passing.'
    • 许尚书孟容旧相知 - Means 'Xu Shang, the former prime minister, was an old acquaintance.'
    • 知举日 - Means 'on the day of the exam.'
    • 万方欲为申屈 - Means 'thousands of people wanted to appeal on his behalf.'
    • 将试前夕 - Means 'the night before the exam.'
    • 宗人家 - Means 'at a friend's house.'
    • 具入试食物 - Means 'to prepare food for the exam.'
    • 兼备晨食 - Means 'also to prepare breakfast.'
    • 偃息以候时 - Means 'to rest and wait for the time.'
    • 五更后 - Means 'after the fifth watch of the night.'
    • 就寝呼之 - Means 'to call him while he was asleep.'
    • 不应 - Means 'he did not respond.'
    • 前视之 - Means 'to look at him from the front.'
    • 中风不能言 - Means 'he was paralyzed and could not speak.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元和、长庆 - Reign periods of Emperor Xianzong (806-820) and Emperor Muzong (820-824) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 太和 - Reign period of Emperor Wenzong (827-840) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 两京 - Refers to the two capitals of Chang'an (Xi'an) and Luoyang.
    • 闾巷间 - Refers to the common people and their daily lives.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蔡、齐 - Refers to the regions of Cai and Qi, which were centers of rebellion during the late Tang Dynasty.
    • 国舅 - Refers to the emperor's brother-in-law.
    • 进士 - Refers to scholars who passed the highest level of the imperial exams.
    • 贡士 - Refers to scholars who passed the provincial exams and were eligible for the imperial exams.
    • 文儒 - Refers to scholars and literati.
    • 轻薄徒 - Refers to idle and frivolous people who often engage in mischief.
    • 举人露布 - A public announcement or proclamation by scholars, often used to spread news or rumors.
    • 王潜 - A historical figure who served as the military governor of Jingnan during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 荆南 - A region in present-day Hubei Province.
    • 节度使 - A high-ranking military and civil official in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陈存 - A historical figure known for his literary abilities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元和 - Reign period of Emperor Xianzong (806-820).
    • 长庆 - Reign period of Emperor Muzong (820-824).
    • 太和 - Reign period of Emperor Wenzong (827-840).
    • 文宗 - Emperor Wenzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宪宗 - Emperor Xianzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蔡 - A region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 齐 - A region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 国舅 - The emperor's brother-in-law.
    • 进士 - Scholars who passed the highest level of the imperial exams.
    • 贡士 - Scholars who passed the provincial exams and were eligible for the imperial exams.
    • 文儒 - Scholars and literati.
    • 轻薄徒 - Idle and frivolous people.
    • 举人露布 - A public announcement by scholars.

    During the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (Yuanhe) and Emperor Muzong (Changqing), when people in the two capitals met in the alleys and streets, they often said, "It should be your uncle." By the time of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), the emperor favored his relatives, and several people falsely claimed to be the emperor's brother-in-law, but none of them were the true one. The saying "It should be your uncle" indeed proved true.

    When I was young, living by the Yangtze River, I often played with children. We made spears out of bamboo strips and decorated them with bird feathers. We tore paper into flags and made representations of battle, facing each other and saying "kill." Soon after, we stood still and said "kill again." Within a few years, Emperor Xianzong eliminated the bandits, and the powerful warlords of Cai and Qi were subsequently annihilated. The saying "kill again" came true.

    At the beginning of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), there were idle and frivolous people in the capital who took the names of scholars who had passed the imperial exams and compiled and arranged words according to principles of righteousness, calling it a "public announcement by scholars." In the ninth year of his reign, those who were executed were mostly scholars and literati.

    At the beginning of Emperor Wenzong's reign (Tahe), Wang Qian was the military governor of Jingnan. Without any reason, a white horse suddenly rushed into the government office and died, lying stiff and blocking the road. That year, Wang Qian died. This was a near miss with a horse disaster.

    The Jinshi Chen Cun was able to compose ancient-style poems, but he had bad luck. The examiners often wanted to pass him, but at the time, there were always reasons preventing him from passing. Xu Shang, the former prime minister, was an old acquaintance, and on the day of the exam, thousands of people wanted to appeal on his behalf. The night before the exam, he stayed at a friend's house. The friend prepared food for the exam and also breakfast, asking Chen Cun to rest and wait for the time. After the fifth watch of the night, it was strange that he did not get up. They called him while he was asleep, but he did not respond. Looking at him from the front, he was already paralyzed and could not speak. [81]

    进士郑滂，在名场岁久，流辈多已崇达，常有后时之叹。一夕忽梦及第，而与韦周方同年。当时韦氏先期举人，无周方之名者，益闷闷。太和元年秋，移举洛中，时韦景方居守，尚书族弟也。赴举过陕。尚书时廉察陕郊，诘景方曰：“我名弘景，汝兄弘方，汝名景方，兄弟各分吾名一字名之，殊无义也。”遂更名周方，滂闻之，极喜曰：“吾及第有望矣。”四年，周方果同年焉。滂登朝，至殿中侍御史。前宣成观察大夫郑常说此事。大夫，即滂之再従弟也。子溥又自说应举时，曾梦看及第榜，榜上但见大书“凤”字。大中元年冬，求解凤翔，偶看本府乡贡士纸之首，便是“凤”字。至东都试缑山月闻王子晋吹笙诗，坐侧诸诗，悉有“凤”字。明年，果登第焉。子溥，郑公之子。 [82]

    Scholar-official Vang Pang had spent many years in the examination system without success, and his peers had long since achieved high positions, often leaving him with a sense of regret for being behind them. One night, he suddenly dreamed that he had passed the examination and was in the same year as Wei Zhoufang. At the time, Wei Zhoufang was among the expected candidates, but his name did not appear on the list, which only deepened Vang Pang's frustration.

    In the first year of the Tahe era (827 AD) during the Tang Dynasty, Vang Pang moved to Luoyang to take the examination. Wei Jingfang, a cousin of a high-ranking official, was in charge of the region. As Vang Pang passed through Shaanxi, the official, who was then inspecting the area, questioned Wei Jingfang: "My name is Hongjing, your brother is Hongfang, and you are Jingfang. The brothers each took one character from my name, which lacks meaning." Wei Jingfang then changed his name to Zhoufang. Upon hearing this, Vang Pang was extremely pleased, saying, "I now have hope of passing the examination!"

    Four years later, Zhoufang indeed passed the examination in the same year as Vang Pang. Vang Pang then entered the imperial bureaucracy and rose to the position of Vice-Chancellor of the Palace Secretariat. Zheng Chang, a former governor of Xuancheng, often recounted this story. Zheng Chang was Vang Pang's second cousin.

    Zibu, Zheng Chang's son, also spoke of his own experience. When he was preparing for the examination, he had a dream in which he saw the list of successful candidates, and prominently displayed on the list was the character "凤" (phoenix). In the first year of the Dazhong era (847 AD), he requested a transfer to Fengxiang. Coincidentally, the first page of the examination paper he saw also bore the character "凤". Later, when he was tested in Luoyang on a poem about Wang Zijun playing the sheng (a traditional Chinese wind instrument), all the poems around him also contained the character "凤". The following year, he indeed passed the examination. Zibu was the son of Zheng Gong. [82]

    大中九年，沈询侍郎以中书舍人知举。其登第门生李彬父丛为万年令。同年有起居者之会，仓部李郎中蠙时在座，因戏诸进士曰：“今日极盛，蠙与贤座主同年。”时右司李郎中従晦，又在座戏蠙曰：“殊未耳！小生与贤座主同年，如何？”谓郴州柳侍郎也。众皆以为异。是日，数公皆诣宾客。冯尚书审，则又柳公座主杨相国之同年，与坐嗟叹。侍读谏议漳说。 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 同年有起居者之会 - A gathering of people who passed the same year’s examinations, often including those who were promoted to the position of '起居' (historiographer).
    • 殊未耳 - A colloquial expression meaning 'not yet' or 'not really.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大中九年 - The year 855, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 中书舍人 - A high-ranking official in the imperial government responsible for drafting important documents and serving as an examiner for the imperial examinations.
    • 登第 - Passing the imperial examinations and becoming a scholar-official.
    • 同年 - People who passed the same year’s examinations, often forming a social network.
    • 起居 - Historiographers who recorded the daily activities and decrees of the emperor.
    • 仓部 - The Ministry of Revenue, responsible for financial matters.
    • 冯尚书审 - A high-ranking official, likely a Minister of Personnel.
    • 杨相国 - A high-ranking official, likely a chancellor.
    • 侍读谏议漳 - A scholar-official who served as a tutor and advisor to the emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沈询 - A name of a person.
    • 李彬 - A name of a person.
    • 李丛 - A name of a person.
    • 李蠙 - A name of a person.
    • 李従晦 - A name of a person.
    • 柳侍郎 - A title indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 漳 - A name of a person.

    In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Shen Xun, a Vice Director of the Secretariat, served as the chief examiner for the imperial examinations. His successful candidate, Li Bin, whose father was Li Cong, served as the magistrate of Wannian County. At a gathering of those who had passed the same year’s examinations and were now historiographers, Cangbu Li Langzhong Bian was present and joked with the successful candidates, saying, "Today is truly splendid, as I and my esteemed examiner are from the same year." At that time, Yousi Li Langzhong Conghui, who was also present, teased Li Bian, saying, "Not yet! I and my esteemed examiner are from the same year, how about that?" He referred to Liu Shilang, the Vice Director of the Ministry of Rites, from Chenzhou. Everyone found this unusual. On that day, several officials visited their guests. Feng Shangshu Shen, who was also present, was a fellow year-examinee of Liu Shilang, the Chancellor Yang, and they sat together, lamenting the situation. The Tutor and Advisor Zhang commented on it. [83]

    河南孔尹温裕任补阙日，谏讨党项事，贬郴州司马。久之得堂兄尚书温业书，报云：“宪府欲取尔作侍御史。”日望敕下。忽又得书云：“宰相以右史处之。”皆无音耗。一日，有鹊喜于庭，直若语状，孙稚拜且祝云：“愿早得官。”鹊既飞去，坠下方寸纸，有“补阙”二字，极异之。无几，却除此官。
    　　郑又自说，早承相国武都公知奖。当时为大理司直，常叹滞淹。会张謩欲除太常博士，李公云：“郑司直久屈，必请举自代。”旋遇萧赞服阕，且要与官，诸坐遂以萧为博士。前此，有大云寺僧宝锐者，知人休咎。因问之，锐曰：“司直朝官终得，中间且合为数郡，如何便得？”既而以侍御史历作河阳、浙西、淮南，累至检校郎中，方除比部员外郎。锐师说事，亦不常中，此又极验者，以阴骘要显前定之事耳。 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 補阙 - A title of an official in the Tang dynasty's imperial court, responsible for correcting omissions and errors in the emperor's edicts.
    • 堂兄 - Refers to a brother of one's father or mother.
    • 尚书 - A high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 侍御史 - Another title of an official, responsible for overseeing the imperial court and advising the emperor.
    • 右史 - A title of an official responsible for recording the emperor's daily activities.
    • 检校郎中 - A title indicating a temporary or provisional appointment to the rank of Langzhong (a mid-level official).
    • 比部员外郎 - A title of an official in the Ministry of Revenue, responsible for auditing financial records.
    • 阴骘 - A concept in Chinese philosophy referring to the idea that one's actions in life can affect their fate or destiny.
    • 显前定之事 - Refers to the belief that certain events or outcomes are predetermined or fated.
    • 阴骘要显前定之事 - Refers to the idea that one's actions in life can affect their fate or destiny.
    • 河南孔尹温裕 - A proper noun referring to a person named Wen Yu, who held the title of Yin (a type of official) in Henan province.
    • 党项 - A reference to the Tangut people, an ethnic group that lived in the northwest region of China during the Tang dynasty.
    • 郴州司马 - A title of an official in the Tang dynasty's provincial administration, responsible for military affairs.
    • 宰相 - The highest-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy, often serving as the emperor's chief advisor.
    • 太常博士 - A title of an official in the Ministry of Rites, responsible for overseeing religious ceremonies and rituals.
    • 萧赞服阕 - A reference to a person named Xiao Zan who had completed mourning for a deceased relative.
    • 大云寺僧宝锐 - A proper noun referring to a Buddhist monk named Bao Rui, who lived at the Dàyún Temple.
    • 知人休咎 - Refers to the ability to predict one's fortune or misfortune.

    During the time when Kong Yinwen, the Yin of Henan, served as a Buque (an official responsible for correcting omissions and errors in the emperor's edicts), he advised against the campaign against the Tanguts (a reference to the Tangut people). As a result, he was demoted to the position of Sima (an official responsible for military affairs) in Chenzhou. After a long period of time, he received a letter from his elder brother, Wenye, who was a high-ranking official. The letter stated, "The Constitutional Court intends to appoint you as a Shiyushi (an official responsible for overseeing the imperial court and advising the emperor)." He eagerly awaited the imperial decree. Suddenly, he received another letter which said, "The prime minister has appointed you as the You Shi (an official responsible for recording the emperor's daily activities)." However, he did not receive any news regarding the appointment. One day, a magpie appeared in the courtyard, chirping as if speaking. His grandson, Zhi, bowed and prayed, "May I obtain an official position soon." The magpie flew away and dropped a small piece of paper, which had the words "Buque" written on it. This was considered extremely strange. Not long after, he was appointed to the position of Buque again.

    Kong Yinwen also mentioned that he had been known and praised by the Duke of Wudu, a high-ranking official. At that time, he served as a Sizhi (an official in the imperial court) at Dali Temple. He often lamented his stagnation in his career. Coincidentally, Zhang Mo, another official, wanted to be appointed as the Taichang Boshi (an official in the Ministry of Rites). Li Gong, a high-ranking official, said, "Zheng Sizhi has been suppressed for a long time. I will definitely recommend him to take his place." Soon after, Xiao Zan, who had completed mourning for a deceased relative, was also appointed to an official position. The other officials then appointed Xiao as the Boshi. Before this, there was a Buddhist monk named Bao Rui from Dàyún Temple, who could predict one's fortune or misfortune. Kong Yinwen asked him about his own future, and Bao Rui said, "As a Sizhi, you will eventually obtain an official position in the imperial court. However, you will first have to serve as an official in several counties. How could you obtain it immediately?" Later, he was appointed as a Shiyushi and served in Heyang, Zhe Xi, and Huainan. He was promoted to the position of Jianjiao Langzhong (a mid-level official) and finally appointed as the Bibu Yuanwailang (an official in the Ministry of Revenue). Bao Rui's predictions were not always accurate, but this particular one was extremely precise, as it involved the concept of Yin Zhi (the idea that one's actions in life can affect their fate or destiny) and the belief in predetermined outcomes. [84]

    王蒙者，与赵门下憬，布衣之旧，常知其吏才。及公入相，蒙自前吉州新淦令来谒。公见喜极，给恤甚厚，将擢为御史。时宪僚数至少。德宗甚难于除授。而赵公秉政，其言多行，蒙坐待绣衣之拜矣。一日，偶诣慈恩僧寺占色者，忘其名。蒙问早晚得官，僧曰：“观君之色，殊未见喜兆，此后若干年，当得一边上御史。”蒙大笑而归。数日，宰臣对，赵公乘间奏曰：“御史府阙人太多，就中监察尤为要者，臣欲选择三数人。”德宗曰：“非不欲补其阙员，此官须得孤直茂实者充选，料卿祗应取轻薄后生朝中子弟耳。此不如不置。”赵公曰：“臣之愚见，正如圣虑，欲于录事参军、县令中求之。 [85]

    Wang Meng, who had been a close friend of Zhao's subordinate Jing from their days as commoners, was well aware of Jing's administrative talents. When Zhao entered the prime minister's office, Wang Meng, who had previously served as the magistrate of Xin Gan in Ji Prefecture, came to pay his respects. Upon seeing Wang Meng, Zhao was extremely pleased and provided him with generous support, planning to promote him to the position of Censor. At the time, there were very few officials in the Censorate. Emperor Dezong found it difficult to appoint new members. However, Zhao, who held power, often had his proposals accepted, and Wang Meng was eagerly awaiting his promotion to the Censorate. One day, Wang Meng visited the Ci'en Temple to consult a fortune-teller whose name he had forgotten. He asked when he would be promoted to an official position. The fortune-teller observed Wang Meng's face and said, "Your complexion shows no signs of good fortune. It will be several years before you become a Censor on the side." Wang Meng laughed and returned home. A few days later, during a court session, Zhao took the opportunity to propose, "The Censorate has too many vacancies, and the position of Inspector is particularly important. I would like to select three or four people for this role." Emperor Dezong replied, "I am not unwilling to fill the vacancies, but this position requires someone who is upright, honest, and virtuous. I suspect you will only choose young, inexperienced officials from the imperial court. It would be better not to appoint anyone at all." Zhao responded, "Your Majesty, my humble opinion aligns with your wise consideration. I intend to look for suitable candidates among the clerks and county magistrates." [85]

    ”上大喜曰：“如此即朕之意，卿有人未？”公因荐二人，其一即蒙也。上曰：“且将状来。”公既出，逢裴延龄，进以度支次对。问公曰：“相公奏何事称意，喜色充溢？”公不之对。延龄愠骂而去，云：“看此老兵，所请得行否？”既见上，奏事毕，因问曰：“赵憬向论请何事？”上曰：“赵憬极公心。”因说御史事。延龄曰：“此大不可，陛下何故信之且赵憬身为宰相，岂谙州县官续效向二人又不为人所称说，憬何由自知之必私也。复至，陛下但诘其所自即知矣。”他日上阁，问云：“卿何心知此二人？”公曰：“一是故人，一与臣微亲，谙熟之。”上无言。他日，延龄又入。上曰：“赵憬所请，果如卿料。”遂寝不行。蒙却归故林，而赵公薨于相位。后数年，连帅奏为従事，得假御史焉。 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 上大喜曰 - Indicates that the emperor was greatly pleased.
    • 如此即朕之意 - Means 'if it is like this, it is my intention.'
    • 卿有人未 - A colloquial way of asking, 'Do you have anyone in mind?'
    • 且将状来 - Means 'bring the petition here.'
    • 相公奏何事称意 - Means 'What did the chancellor propose that pleased you?'
    • 喜色充溢 - Describes a face full of joy.
    • 不之对 - Indicates that the person did not answer directly.
    • 愠骂而去 - Means 'left in anger, cursing.'
    • 看此老兵 - A derogatory term for an old soldier or veteran.
    • 所请得行否 - Means 'Will what he requested be carried out?'
    • 极公心 - Means 'extremely impartial' or 'very fair-minded.'
    • 复至 - Means 'when he comes again.'
    • 诘其所自 - Means 'to question where he got the information.'
    • 何心知此二人 - Means 'Why did you know these two people?'
    • 故人 - Means 'old friend.'
    • 微亲 - Means 'distant relative' or 'slight connection.'
    • 谙熟之 - Means 'to be well acquainted with them.'
    • 遂寝不行 - Means 'was thus put aside and not carried out.'
    • 却归故林 - Means 'returned to his old place' or 'went back to his roots.'
    • 薨于相位 - Means 'died in the position of chancellor.'
    • 连帅奏为从事 - Means 'the governor recommended him as an official.'
    • 得假御史焉 - Means 'he obtained the position of a temporary censor.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上 - Refers to the emperor.
    • 赵憬 - A chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 裴延龄 - Another official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his controversial policies.
    • 蒙 - Likely refers to a person recommended by Sui Jing, possibly a military officer.
    • 御史 - Refers to a censor, a high-ranking official responsible for monitoring the actions of other officials.

    Proper Nouns

    • 上 - The emperor.
    • 赵憬 - Chancellor Zhao Jing.
    • 裴延龄 - Official Pei Yanliang.
    • 蒙 - A person recommended by Sui Jing.
    • 御史 - Censor.

    The emperor was greatly pleased and said, "If it is like this, it is my intention. Do you have anyone in mind?" Sui Jing then recommended two people, one of whom was Meng. The emperor said, "Bring the petition here." After Sui Jing left, he encountered Pei Yanliang, who asked him, "What did the chancellor propose that pleased you? Your face is full of joy." Sui Jing did not answer directly. Pei Yanliang left in anger, cursing, "Look at this old veteran, will what he requested be carried out?" When Sui Jing saw the emperor again, he presented his report and the emperor asked, "What did Chancellor Zhao Jing propose that pleased you?" The emperor said, "Chancellor Zhao Jing is extremely impartial." He then explained the matter of the censor. Pei Yanliang said, "This is a great mistake. Why do you trust him? As a chancellor, Zhao Jing cannot possibly know about the county officials' merits and demerits. He must have a private reason for recommending them. When he comes again, just question him about where he got the information, and you will know." On another day, the emperor asked, "Why did you know these two people?" Sui Jing said, "One is an old friend, and the other is a distant relative. I am well acquainted with them." The emperor remained silent. Later, Pei Yanliang returned, and the emperor said, "What Zhao Jing requested, indeed, as you suspected. It was thus put aside and not carried out." Meng returned to his old place, and Chancellor Zhao Jing died in his position. Several years later, the governor recommended him as an official, and he obtained the position of a temporary censor. [86]

    相国崔公慎由廉察浙西。左目眦生赘，如息肉，欲蔽瞳人，视物极碍，诸医方无验。一日，淮南判官杨员外牧，自吴中越职，馔召于中堂。因话扬州有穆中善医眼，来为白府主，请遗书崔相国铉，令致之。崔公许诺。后数日，得书云：“穆生性粗疏，恐不可信。有谭简者，用心精审，胜穆甚远。”遂致以来。既见，白崔公曰：“此立可去。但能安神不挠，独断于中，则必效矣。”崔公曰：“如约，虽妻子必不使知。”谭简又曰：“须用九日晴明，亭午于静处疗之，若其日果能遂心，更无忧矣。”是时月初也。至六七日间，忽阴雨甚，谭生极有忧色。至八九大开霁，问崔公：“饮酒多少？”崔公曰：“户虽至小，亦可引满。”谭生大喜。初公将决意用谭之医，惟语大将中善医者沈师象，师象赞成其事。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 相国 - High-ranking official title, equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 廉察 - Term for conducting investigations or inspections.
    • 浙西 - Refers to the western part of Zhejiang province.
    • 左目眦生赘 - Describes a growth or tumor on the left eyelid.
    • 如息肉 - Compares the growth to a polyp or benign tumor.
    • 蔽瞳人 - Means 'obscures the pupil,' indicating that the growth interferes with vision.
    • 视物极碍 - Means 'extremely obstructs vision,' emphasizing the severity of the condition.
    • 诸医方无验 - Means 'all medical treatments have been ineffective.'
    • 淮南判官杨员外牧 - Refers to Yang Wai Mu, an official from Huainan.
    • 越职 - Means 'exceeding one's duties' or 'acting beyond one's official capacity.'
    • 馔召于中堂 - Means 'invited to a feast in the main hall.'
    • 穆中善医眼 - Refers to Mu Zhong, a skilled ophthalmologist.
    • 来为白府主 - Means 'to come and report to the governor.'
    • 请遗书崔相国铉 - Means 'to request a letter to be sent to Chancellor Cui Xuan.'
    • 立可去 - Means 'can be removed immediately.'
    • 安神不挠 - Means 'to remain calm and undisturbed.'
    • 独断于中 - Means 'to make a decisive decision internally.'
    • 必效矣 - Means 'will definitely be effective.'
    • 妻子 - Refers to wife and children.
    • 谭简 - Refers to Tan Jian, another ophthalmologist.
    • 用心精审 - Means 'to be meticulous and thorough.'
    • 胜穆甚远 - Means 'far superior to Mu.'
    • 致以来 - Means 'to bring him here.'
    • 亭午 - Means 'midday' or 'noon.'
    • 静处疗之 - Means 'to treat in a quiet place.'
    • 遂心 - Means 'to go as planned' or 'to succeed.'
    • 大将中善医者沈师象 - Refers to Shen Shixiang, a skilled physician among the military leaders.
    • 赞成其事 - Means 'to support the plan.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 相国崔公慎由 - Refers to Cui Shenyu, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔相国铉 - Refers to Cui Xuan, another high-ranking official.
    • 穆中 - A skilled ophthalmologist mentioned in the text.
    • 谭简 - Another skilled ophthalmologist who is recommended.
    • 沈师象 - A skilled physician among the military leaders.
    • 唐代官制 - The text reflects the administrative structure and medical practices of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 中医传统 - The text highlights the use of traditional Chinese medicine and the importance of skilled physicians.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sequence '左目眦生赘，如息肉，欲蔽瞳人，视物极碍' describes the condition of the eye in detail.
    • The clause '诸医方无验' indicates the ineffectiveness of previous treatments.
    • The sentence '淮南判官杨员外牧，自吴中越职，馔召于中堂' introduces Yang Wai Mu and his actions.
    • The clause '谭简又曰：“须用九日晴明，亭午于静处疗之，若其日果能遂心，更无忧矣。”' provides specific instructions for the treatment.
    • The sentence '至六七日间，忽阴雨甚，谭生极有忧色' describes the weather and its impact on the treatment.
    • The clause '至八九大开霁' indicates the weather clearing up.
    • The dialogue '问崔公：“饮酒多少？”崔公曰：“户虽至小，亦可引满。”' shows the interaction between Tan Jian and Cui Shenyu.
    • The sentence '初公将决意用谭之医，惟语大将中善医者沈师象，师象赞成其事' explains the final decision and the support from Shen Shixiang.

    Proper Nouns

    • 相国崔公慎由 - Cui Shenyu, a high-ranking official.
    • 崔相国铉 - Cui Xuan, another high-ranking official.
    • 穆中 - Mu Zhong, a skilled ophthalmologist.
    • 谭简 - Tan Jian, another skilled ophthalmologist.
    • 沈师象 - Shen Shixiang, a skilled physician among the military leaders.
    • 淮南判官杨员外牧 - Yang Wai Mu, an official from Huainan.
    • 浙西 - The western part of Zhejiang province.
    • 唐代 - The Tang Dynasty.

    Chancellor Cui Shenyu, a high-ranking official, conducted investigations in the western part of Zhejiang. On his left eyelid, a growth resembling a polyp had formed, threatening to obscure the pupil and severely obstructing his vision. All previous medical treatments had been ineffective. One day, Yang Wai Mu, a judge from Huainan, acted beyond his official capacity and invited Cui Shenyu to a feast in the main hall. During the feast, Yang Wai Mu mentioned that there was a skilled ophthalmologist named Mu Zhong in Yangzhou who could treat such conditions. He suggested writing to Chancellor Cui Xuan to request Mu Zhong's assistance. Cui Shenyu agreed to this proposal. A few days later, they received a letter from Cui Xuan stating, "Mu Zhong is rather rough and may not be reliable. There is another ophthalmologist named Tan Jian who is meticulous and thorough, far superior to Mu." They then brought Tan Jian to see Cui Shenyu. Upon meeting, Tan Jian said, "This condition can be removed immediately. However, you must remain calm and undisturbed, making a decisive decision internally, and it will definitely be effective." Cui Shenyu replied, "I will ensure that, even my wife and children, will not know about this." Tan Jian added, "We need nine days of clear weather, and the treatment should take place at midday in a quiet place. If the day goes as planned, there will be no further worries." At the time, the weather was cloudy. After six or seven days, it suddenly rained heavily, and Tan Jian looked very worried. By the eighth or ninth day, the weather cleared up. Tan Jian asked Cui Shenyu, "How much can you drink?" Cui Shenyu replied, "Even if the room is small, I can still drink to the brim." Tan Jian was very pleased. Initially, Cui Shenyu had decided to use Tan Jian's medical skills and only informed the skilled physician Shen Shixiang among the military leaders, who supported the plan. [87]

    是日引谭生于使宅北楼，惟师象与一小竖随行，左右更无人知者。谭生请公饮酒数杯，端坐无思，俄而谭生以手微扪所患曰：“殊小事耳。”初觉似拔之，虽痛亦忍。又闻动剪刀声。白公曰：“此地稍暗，请移往中庭。”象与小竖扶公而至于庭。坐既定，闻栉焉有声。先是，谭生请好绵数两染绛。至是，以绛绵拭病处，兼傅以药，遂不甚痛。谭生请公开眼，看所赘肉，大如小指，坚如干筋，遂命投之江中。方遣报夫人及子弟。谭生立以状报淮南，崔相国复书云：“自发医后，忧疑颇甚。及闻痊愈，神思方安。”后数日，而征诏至金陵。嗟夫！向若杨君不遇，谭生不至，公心不断，九日不晴，征诏遽来，归期是切，碍其目疾，位当废矣，安得秉钧入辅，为帝股肱此数事足验玄助。而公作相之后，谭生已逝，又何命之太薄也！ [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 是日 - Means 'on that day' or 'that day,' indicating a specific time.
    • 引 - Means 'to invite' or 'to lead,' suggesting an act of bringing someone to a place.
    • 惟师象与一小竖随行 - '惟' means 'only' or 'just,' '师象' is a person's name, and '一小竖' means 'a small servant' or 'a young page.'
    • 左右更无人知者 - '左右' means 'the people around' or 'those nearby,' and '更无人知者' means 'no one else knew.'
    • 数杯 - Means 'a few cups,' referring to a small amount of wine.
    • 端坐无思 - Means 'sitting upright without thinking,' suggesting a state of calm and concentration.
    • 俄而 - Means 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter.'
    • 殊小事耳 - Means 'a very minor matter indeed,' downplaying the severity of the issue.
    • 初觉似拔之 - Means 'at first it felt like pulling it out,' describing the initial sensation.
    • 虽痛亦忍 - Means 'although it hurt, it was bearable.'
    • 动剪刀声 - Means 'the sound of scissors moving,' indicating the use of scissors.
    • 此地稍暗，请移往中庭 - Means 'this place is a bit dark, please move to the central courtyard.'
    • 栉焉有声 - Means 'the sound of combing,' describing the act of combing.
    • 先是，谭生请好绵数两染绛 - '先是' means 'previously' or 'before that,' '谭生' is a person's name, '好绵' means 'good cotton,' '数两' means 'several pieces,' and '染绛' means 'dyed red.'
    • 以绛绵拭病处，兼傅以药 - Means 'to wipe the affected area with the red cotton and apply medicine to it.'
    • 遂不甚痛 - Means 'and it was no longer very painful.'
    • 谭生请公开眼，看所赘肉 - Means 'Tan Sheng asked White to open his eyes and see the protruding flesh.'
    • 大如小指，坚如干筋 - Means 'as big as a small finger, hard as dried tendons.'
    • 遂命投之江中 - Means 'then ordered it to be thrown into the river.'
    • 方遣报夫人及子弟 - Means 'then sent someone to inform his wife and children.'
    • 谭生立以状报淮南，崔相国复书云 - '立' means 'immediately,' '状' means 'report,' '淮南' is a place name, and '崔相国' is a person's name.
    • 自发医后，忧疑颇甚 - Means 'after sending for the doctor, he was very worried and anxious.'
    • 及闻痊愈，神思方安 - Means 'upon hearing that he had recovered, his mind finally calmed.'
    • 后数日，而征诏至金陵 - Means 'a few days later, the imperial summons arrived in Jinling.'
    • 嗟夫 - An interjection expressing regret or lament.
    • 向若杨君不遇，谭生不至，公心不断，九日不晴，征诏遽来，归期是切，碍其目疾，位当废矣 - '向若' means 'if only,' '杨君' is a person's name, '不遇' means 'did not meet,' '谭生不至' means 'Tan Sheng did not arrive,' '公心不断' means 'the official's resolve did not waver,' '九日不晴' means 'it did not clear up for nine days,' '征诏遽来' means 'the imperial summons came suddenly,' '归期是切' means 'the deadline for returning was pressing,' '碍其目疾' means 'because of his eye disease,' and '位当废矣' means 'his position would have been abolished.'
    • 安得秉钧入辅，为帝股肱 - Means 'how could he hold power and assist the emperor as a trusted advisor.'
    • 此数事足验玄助 - Means 'these several events sufficiently demonstrate divine assistance.'
    • 而公作相之后，谭生已逝，又何命之太薄也 - Means 'but after he became chancellor, Tan Sheng had already passed away, how thin his fate was indeed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 使宅北楼 - Refers to a specific location in a residence, likely a place of importance or privacy.
    • 白公 - Refers to Bai Juyi, a famous Tang dynasty poet and statesman.
    • 谭生 - Refers to Tan Sheng, a person involved in the story.
    • 杨君 - Refers to Yang Jun, another person involved in the story.
    • 崔相国 - Refers to Cui Xiangguo, a high-ranking official.
    • 淮南 - Refers to Huainan, a region in China.
    • 金陵 - Refers to Jinling, the ancient name for Nanjing, the capital of the Tang dynasty.
    • 征诏 - Refers to an imperial summons or decree.
    • 目疾 - Refers to an eye disease, which was a significant concern in ancient China.
    • 秉钧入辅 - Refers to holding power and assisting the emperor as a trusted advisor.
    • 帝股肱 - Refers to being a trusted and influential advisor to the emperor.
    • 玄助 - Refers to divine assistance or intervention.
    • 相 - Refers to a high-ranking official, often equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.

    Proper Nouns

    • 白公 - Bai Juyi, a famous Tang dynasty poet and statesman.
    • 谭生 - Tan Sheng, a person involved in the story.
    • 杨君 - Yang Jun, another person involved in the story.
    • 崔相国 - Cui Xiangguo, a high-ranking official.
    • 淮南 - Huainan, a region in China.
    • 金陵 - Jinling, the ancient name for Nanjing, the capital of the Tang dynasty.
    • 目疾 - An eye disease, which was a significant concern in ancient China.
    • 秉钧入辅 - Holding power and assisting the emperor as a trusted advisor.
    • 帝股肱 - Being a trusted and influential advisor to the emperor.
    • 玄助 - Divine assistance or intervention.
    • 相 - A high-ranking official, often equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.

    On that day, Tan Sheng was invited to the northern tower of the residence, accompanied only by Shixiang and a young page. No one else knew about it. Tan Sheng offered White several cups of wine, and they sat upright without speaking. Soon after, Tan Sheng gently touched the affected area and said, "It's a very minor matter indeed." At first, it felt like pulling it out, though it was painful, it was bearable. Then, the sound of scissors moving could be heard. White said, "This place is a bit dark; please move to the central courtyard." Shixiang and the young page helped White to the courtyard. Once seated, the sound of combing could be heard. Previously, Tan Sheng had requested several pieces of good cotton dyed red. Now, he wiped the affected area with the red cotton and applied medicine to it, and it was no longer very painful. Tan Sheng asked White to open his eyes and see the protruding flesh, which was as big as a small finger and hard as dried tendons. Then, he ordered it to be thrown into the river. Tan Sheng immediately sent someone to inform his wife and children. He then reported to Huainan, and Cui Xiangguo replied, "After sending for the doctor, I was very worried and anxious. Upon hearing that he had recovered, my mind finally calmed." A few days later, the imperial summons arrived in Jinling. Alas! If only Yang Jun had not met, if Tan Sheng had not arrived, if the official's resolve had not wavered, if it had not cleared up for nine days, if the imperial summons had come suddenly, if the deadline for returning had been pressing, because of his eye disease, his position would have been abolished. How could he hold power and assist the emperor as a trusted advisor? These several events sufficiently demonstrate divine assistance. But after he became chancellor, Tan Sheng had already passed away, how thin his fate was indeed! [88]

    是日引谭生于使宅北楼，惟师象与一小竖随行，左右更无人知者。谭生请公饮酒数杯，端坐无思，俄而谭生以手微扪所患曰：“殊小事耳。”初觉似拔之，虽痛亦忍。又闻动剪刀声。白公曰：“此地稍暗，请移往中庭。”象与小竖扶公而至于庭。坐既定，闻栉焉有声。先是，谭生请好绵数两染绛。至是，以绛绵拭病处，兼傅以药，遂不甚痛。谭生请公开眼，看所赘肉，大如小指，坚如干筋，遂命投之江中。方遣报夫人及子弟。谭生立以状报淮南，崔相国复书云：“自发医后，忧疑颇甚。及闻痊愈，神思方安。”后数日，而征诏至金陵。嗟夫！向若杨君不遇，谭生不至，公心不断，九日不晴，征诏遽来，归期是切，碍其目疾，位当废矣，安得秉钧入辅，为帝股肱此数事足验玄助。而公作相之后，谭生已逝，又何命之太薄也！

    On that day, Tan Sheng was invited to the northern tower of the residence, accompanied only by Shixiang and a young page. No one else knew about it. Tan Sheng offered White several cups of wine, and they sat upright without speaking. Soon after, Tan Sheng gently touched the affected area and said, "It's a very minor matter indeed." At first, it felt like pulling it out, though it was painful, it was bearable. Then, the sound of scissors moving could be heard. White said, "This place is a bit dark; please move to the central courtyard." Shixiang and the young page helped White to the courtyard. Once seated, the sound of combing could be heard. Previously, Tan Sheng had requested several pieces of good cotton dyed red. Now, he wiped the affected area with the red cotton and applied medicine to it, and it was no longer very painful. Tan Sheng asked White to open his eyes and see the protruding flesh, which was as big as a small finger and hard as dried tendons. Then, he ordered it to be thrown into the river. Tan Sheng immediately sent someone to inform his wife and children. He then reported to Huainan, and Cui Xiangguo replied, "After sending for the doctor, I was very worried and anxious. Upon hearing that he had recovered, my mind finally calmed." A few days later, the imperial summons arrived in Jinling. Alas! If only Yang Jun had not met, if Tan Sheng had not arrived, if the official's resolve had not wavered, if it had not cleared up for nine days, if the imperial summons had come suddenly, if the deadline for returning had been pressing, because of his eye disease, his position would have been abolished. How could he hold power and assist the emperor as a trusted advisor? These several events sufficiently demonstrate divine assistance. But after he became chancellor, Tan Sheng had already passed away, how thin his fate was indeed!
